**(a)** Calculate the **outlet temperature of water** and the **LMTD**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **overall heat transfer coefficient** and the **required inner pipe length**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Water outlet temperature and LMTD**

Heat duty:

$$\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{8000}{3600} = 2.222 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q = 2.222 \times 2200 \times (150 - 80) = 2.222 \times 2200 \times 70 = 342{,}222 \text{ W}$$

Water outlet temperature (with $\dot{m}_c = 12000/3600 = 3.333$ kg/s):

$$T_{c,o} = 25 + \dfrac{342{,}222}{3.333 \times 4180} = 25 + 24.56 = 49.56 \text{ °C}$$

Terminal temperature differences (counter-current):

$$\Delta T_1 = 150 - 49.56 = 100.44 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ °C}$$

LMTD:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{100.44 - 55}{\ln(100.44 / 55)} = \dfrac{45.44}{\ln(1.8262)} = \dfrac{45.44}{0.6020} = 75.48 \text{ °C}$$

> **Answer (a):** $T_{c,o} = 49.56$ °C; LMTD = **75.48 °C**.

**Part (b) — Overall coefficient and length**

$$\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + \dfrac{D_o \cdot \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$$

Term 1:

$$\dfrac{0.0334}{0.0266 \times 800} = \dfrac{0.0334}{21.28} = 1.570 \times 10^{-3}$$

Term 2:

$$\ln(0.0334 / 0.0266) = \ln(1.2556) = 0.2276$$

$$\dfrac{0.0334 \times 0.2276}{2 \times 45} = \dfrac{0.007601}{90} = 8.45 \times 10^{-5}$$

Term 3:

$$\dfrac{1}{3000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

Sum:

$$\dfrac{1}{U_o} = (1.570 + 0.0845 + 0.333) \times 10^{-3} = 1.988 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$U_o = 503.0 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

Required area:

$$A_o = \dfrac{342{,}222}{503.0 \times 75.48} = \dfrac{342{,}222}{37{,}966} = 9.01 \text{ m}^2$$

Required length:

$$L = \dfrac{9.01}{\pi \times 0.0334} = \dfrac{9.01}{0.1049} = 85.9 \text{ m}$$

> **Answer (b):** $U_o = 503.0$ W/m²·K; Length = **85.9 m** (rounded to **9 standard 5-m hairpins** = 90 m actual).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Calculation of the Size of Double Pipe" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $D_o$ for $A_o$ calculation** — Many students use $D_i$ for the area formula but report $U_o$ in the denominator. This is **inconsistent** and leads to **2–3 marks lost**. Always verify that the **area basis matches the U basis**.
>
> 2. **Using the Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)** instead of LMTD — This is a **conceptual error** worth at least **1 full mark deduction** and signals weak fundamentals.
>
> 3. **Neglecting pipe wall resistance** — Even when the problem says "neglect fouling," the wall conduction term $\dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w}$ should still be included. **Auto-zero** for omitting it.
>
> 4. **Swapping $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** — The LMTD is **insensitive to assignment order** (numerator and denominator both invert), but examiners may still mark wrong if the labels in the final answer do not match the figure.
>
> 5. **Not rounding up the number of hairpins** — A calculated length of 78.6 m requiring 7.86 hairpins **must be rounded up to 8 hairpins**. Rounding down is a common loss of **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kcal/h, BTU/hr, and SI units (W) within the same solution leads to **partial marking** at best. KTU strictly requires SI units throughout.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heat Duty First** — Always start with $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ or $Q = \dot{m} \lambda$ before any other calculation.
- **LMTD is the heart** of double pipe design — never use arithmetic mean, and always state whether the configuration is **co-current** or **counter-current** before writing the LMTD formula.
- **Counter-current is thermodynamically superior** — it allows the cold outlet to exceed the hot outlet temperature in theory (the "cross-over" or "temperature cross" limit).
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** is the sum of **five resistances** in series: inside convection, inside fouling, wall conduction, outside fouling, outside convection.
- **Area basis consistency is critical** — $U_i$ goes with $A_i$; $U_o$ goes with $A_o$. The most common KTU convention is the **outer area basis**.
- **Standard pipe schedules** — Always use **IPS Schedule 40** standard sizes for both inner and outer pipes. The "1-2" and "1.5-3" hairpin configurations are the most common.
- **Length is converted to hairpins** — Each standard hairpin is **2 effective pipe lengths** (an inner and a return path). With 5-m straight pipe segments, each hairpin contributes **10 m** of effective length.
- **Correction Factor F** — For a true counter-current double pipe exchanger, **F = 1**. It only becomes less than 1 for multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers. KTU Module 1 questions on double pipe normally **omit** the F-factor or set it to unity.
- **Check assumption validity** — At least one terminal temperature difference $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ must be **positive**; if either is zero or negative, a "temperature cross" has occurred and the LMTD method **fails** (you must use the $\varepsilon$-NTU method instead).
- **Final answer must be in SI units** with appropriate engineering rounding (typically 3–4 significant figures).
- **Reinforce with a quick "back-of-the-envelope" check**: for liquid-liquid service, $U$ typically lies in the range **200–600 W/m²·K**; for condensing steam, **1000–2500 W/m²·K**. If your computed $U$ falls wildly outside this band, **recheck your arithmetic**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Drop Estimation in Double Pipe Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Drop Estimation in Double Pipe Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pressure drop estimation in double pipe flow** refers to the systematic engineering calculation of the mechanical energy loss experienced by a fluid as it flows through a concentric double-pipe (pipe-within-pipe) heat exchanger or transport line. The total pressure drop is the sum of the **frictional pressure drop** (due to viscous dissipation along the pipe length and in the annular region), the **frictional pressure drop due to fittings, bends, and bends of the hairpin type**, and the **momentum pressure changes** due to sudden contractions, sudden expansions, and entrance/exit effects.

Mathematically, the general momentum-mechanical energy balance for steady, incompressible, isothermal flow through a horizontal double-pipe section yields:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{fittings} + \Delta P_{contraction} + \Delta P_{expansion} + \Delta P_{elevation}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master the distinction between **Fanning friction factor ($f_F$)** and **Darcy friction factor ($f_D = 4 f_F$)**, because Indian textbooks and Western textbooks use different conventions. KTU Board valuation accepts both, but **explicitly state which convention you are using** in the first line of your solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition - Reynolds Number (Re):** The dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow system. It is the single most important parameter that determines whether the flow is **laminar (Re < 2100)**, **transitional (2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000)**, or **turbulent (Re > 4000)** inside a pipe.

The equivalent Reynolds number for the **annular region** uses the **equivalent (hydraulic) diameter** of the annulus rather than the actual outer pipe diameter:

$$Re_{annulus} = \frac{D_{e,annulus} \cdot v_{annulus} \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \frac{D_o - D_i}{D_o - D_i}\cdot\frac{D_o - D_i \cdot v_{annulus} \cdot \rho}{\mu}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **drinking a thick milkshake through a very long straw** versus drinking water through the same straw. The milkshake requires you to suck much harder, right? The pressure drop in the double-pipe flow is the engineering version of this "sucking harder" phenomenon.

Now, picture a **two-lane traffic tunnel**: the cars enter a narrow inner tunnel (the inner pipe) OR they travel through the gap between the inner and outer tunnel walls (the annulus). The **friction factor** represents the "road roughness" – a smooth asphalt road has low friction, but a gravel road causes cars to slow down (high friction, high pressure drop). The **Reynolds number** represents how fast the cars are moving – slow-moving bumper-to-bumper traffic (laminar) behaves very differently from fast-moving traffic (turbulent).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Pressure drop estimation is **CRITICAL** in:
> 1. **Pump and compressor sizing** – you need to know how much mechanical energy the pump must supply to overcome friction losses.
> 2. **Heat exchanger design** – excessive pressure drop means high pumping cost; insufficient pressure drop may signal poor fluid distribution.
> 3. **Pipeline network design** – long-distance oil, gas, and water pipelines require precise pressure drop calculations to determine the number of booster pump stations.
> 4. **Process safety** – sudden pressure drops can cause flashing of liquids, cavitation in pumps, or loss of cooling in emergency systems.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravitational acceleration | $g_c$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water at 4 °C | $\rho_{water}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\mu_{water}$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\nu_{water}$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Darcy-to-Fanning friction factor ratio | $f_D / f_F$ | **4** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Decomposition

The estimation of pressure drop in a double-pipe system follows a strict logical hierarchy. Each step depends on the result of the previous step.

### Step 1: Geometric Definition of the Flow Cross-Section
- **Inner pipe flow** (fluid inside the inner pipe):
  - Cross-sectional area: $A_i = \dfrac{\pi D_i^2}{4}$
  - Hydraulic diameter: $D_{e,i} = D_i$ (simple circular pipe)
- **Annular flow** (fluid in the space between inner and outer pipe):
  - Cross-sectional area: $A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)$
  - Hydraulic (equivalent) diameter: $D_{e,ann} = D_o - D_i$

### Step 2: Calculation of Mass Flow Rate or Volumetric Flow Rate
The student is typically given a duty (e.g., heat duty $Q$ in Watts) and must back-calculate the flow rate using the energy balance:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m} = \frac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

The volumetric flow rate and the superficial velocity follow:

$$Q_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \quad \text{and} \quad v = \frac{Q_{vol}}{A_{cross-section}}$$

### Step 3: Determination of Flow Regime via Reynolds Number
- Calculate $Re = \dfrac{D_e \cdot v \cdot \rho}{\mu}$ using the appropriate equivalent diameter.
- Classify: **Laminar** ($Re < 2100$), **Transitional** ($2100 \le Re \le 4000$), **Turbulent** ($Re > 4000$).

### Step 4: Selection of Appropriate Friction Factor Correlation
- **Laminar Flow:** $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (Darcy), or $f_F = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ (Fanning)
- **Turbulent Flow:** Use an **explicit** or **implicit** correlation:
  - **Blasius equation** (smooth pipes, $Re < 10^5$): $f_D = \dfrac{0.3164}{Re^{0.25}}$
  - **Petukhov equation** (smooth pipes, $3000 < Re < 5 \times 10^6$): $f_D = (0.790 \ln Re - 1.64)^{-2}$
  - **Colebrook-White equation** (rough pipes, implicit): $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -2 \log\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f_D}}\right)$
  - **Haaland equation** (explicit approximation of Colebrook): $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{Re}\right]$

### Step 5: Application of Darcy-Weisbach Equation
$$\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 4 f_F \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

### Step 6: Inclusion of Minor Losses
$$\Delta P_{minor} = \sum K_i \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Where $K_i$ is the **loss coefficient** for each fitting (sudden contraction, sudden expansion, elbows, tees, etc.).

### Step 7: Assembly of Total Pressure Drop
$$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_f + \Delta P_{fittings} + \Delta P_{contraction} + \Delta P_{expansion}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" matters:** The friction factor encapsulates the **microscopic interaction** between fluid molecules and the pipe wall (viscous shear) and the **macroscopic turbulent eddies** that exchange momentum across streamlines. The K-loss coefficients represent **localized flow disturbances** where the streamlines deviate abruptly from straight, parallel paths (e.g., at sudden contractions, the flow contracts to a **vena contracta** before re-expanding, dissipating energy in the process).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ | Circular cross-sectional area | Single pipe |
| 2 | $A_{ann} = \pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2)/4$ | Annular cross-section area | Double-pipe annulus |
| 3 | $D_{e,ann} = D_o - D_i$ | Equivalent (hydraulic) diameter | Annulus |
| 4 | $Re = D_e v \rho / \mu$ | Reynolds number | All internal flows |
| 5 | $f_D = 64/Re$ | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | $Re < 2100$ |
| 6 | $f_F = 16/Re$ | Fanning friction factor (laminar) | $Re < 2100$ |
| 7 | $f_D = 0.3164 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | Blasius (smooth) | $4000 < Re < 10^5$ |
| 8 | $f_D = (0.790 \ln Re - 1.64)^{-2}$ | Petukhov (smooth) | $3000 < Re < 5 \times 10^6$ |
| 9 | $1/\sqrt{f_D} = -2 \log[\varepsilon/(3.7D) + 2.51/(Re\sqrt{f_D})]$ | Colebrook (rough, implicit) | Turbulent, rough |
| 10 | $1/\sqrt{f_D} = -1.8 \log[(\varepsilon/(3.7D))^{1.11} + 6.9/Re]$ | Haaland (rough, explicit) | Turbulent, rough |
| 11 | $\Delta P_f = 4 f_F (L/D_e) (\rho v^2/2)$ | Fanning-based pressure drop | All flows |
| 12 | $\Delta P_f = f_D (L/D_e) (\rho v^2/2)$ | Darcy-based pressure drop | All flows |
| 13 | $\Delta P_{minor} = \sum K_i (\rho v^2/2)$ | Minor losses from fittings | Localized losses |
| 14 | $K_{contraction} = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$ | K-value, sudden contraction | Contraction losses |
| 15 | $K_{expansion} = (1 - A_2/A_1)^2$ | K-value, sudden expansion | Expansion losses |
| 16 | $K_{entrance} = 0.5$ | Sharp-edged entrance | Inlet loss |
| 17 | $K_{exit} = 1.0$ | Exit to large reservoir | Outlet loss |
| 18 | $f_{ann,lam} = 16/Re_{ann}$ | Annular laminar friction factor | $Re_{ann} < 2100$ |
| 19 | $\dot{m} = Q / (C_p \cdot \Delta T)$ | Mass flow from heat duty | Energy balance |
| 20 | $v = \dot{m} / (\rho \cdot A)$ | Superficial velocity | Mass conservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL UNIT RULE:** When using $\rho v^2 / 2$ in SI units, the pressure drop is in **Pascals (Pa)**. If using $v^2 / (2 g_c)$ in CGS or FPS units, the result is in **kgf/cm²** or **lbf/in²**. **Always show the unit conversion** in KTU board exams; marks are awarded for dimensional discipline.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pressure drop calculation chain is the **backbone of process equipment design**:

1. **Cost optimization:** Pumping cost is proportional to $\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P$. A 10% overestimation of pressure drop can lead to significant excess capital expenditure (oversized pump, thicker pipe walls, larger motor).

2. **Equipment rating:** Heat exchangers, reactors, distillation columns, and absorbers all have **maximum allowable pressure drops** (typically 0.5–1.5 bar for liquid streams, 0.1–0.3 bar for gas streams). Exceeding these limits forces the designer to re-rate the equipment.

3. **System integration:** In a chemical plant, multiple process units are connected by piping. The total pressure drop across the plant determines the **discharge pressure of upstream pumps** and the **suction pressure of downstream compressors**. A small error in a single pipe's $\Delta P$ can cascade through the entire plant.

4. **Safety relief valve sizing:** Pressure drop in long pipe runs must be considered when sizing PSVs (pressure safety valves) to ensure they receive adequate inlet pressure to fully open during a fire case or overpressure event.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Darcy-Weisbach Equation from the Mechanical Energy Balance

The mechanical energy balance for steady, incompressible flow between points 1 and 2 in a horizontal pipe is:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + \hat{W}_s + \hat{F}$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2$ = upstream and downstream pressures (Pa)
- $v_1, v_2$ = upstream and downstream velocities (m/s)
- $\hat{W}_s$ = shaft work done ON the fluid per unit mass (J/kg)
- $\hat{F}$ = friction loss per unit mass (J/kg)

For a constant-diameter horizontal pipe, $v_1 = v_2$ and $\hat{W}_s = 0$ (no pump). The pressure drop is therefore equal to the friction loss:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \hat{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = \rho \hat{F}$$

The friction loss per unit mass is empirically correlated with the **kinetic energy per unit mass** ($v^2/2$) and a **dimensionless friction factor** ($f$):

$$\hat{F} = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2}$$

Substituting this back into the energy balance:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2} = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

This is the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** in its Fanning friction factor form. If we let $f_D = 4 f$, we obtain the Darcy form:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}}$$

### 3.1.1 Numerical Demonstration: Fully Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** Water at 20 °C flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter $D = 0.05$ m and length $L = 100$ m at a volumetric flow rate $Q_v = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. The pipe roughness is $\varepsilon = 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m. Calculate the pressure drop using (a) Fanning convention and (b) Darcy convention. Use the Haaland equation for friction factor.

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $D = 0.05$ m
- $L = 100$ m
- $Q_v = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\varepsilon = 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area and velocity.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q_v}{A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2546 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \frac{D v \rho}{\mu} = \frac{(0.05)(0.2546)(1000)}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 12{,}705$$

Since $Re = 12{,}705 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3: Calculate the relative roughness.**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 9.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Darcy friction factor using the Haaland equation.**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re}\right]$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\frac{9.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{12{,}705}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(2.486 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{1.11} + 5.431 \times 10^{-4}\right]$$

Calculating the first term inside the logarithm:

$$\left(2.486 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{1.11} = e^{1.11 \cdot \ln(2.486 \times 10^{-4})} = e^{1.11 \cdot (-8.299)} = e^{-9.212}$$

$$\left(2.486 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{1.11} = 9.99 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

Adding the two terms inside the logarithm:

$$1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.431 \times 10^{-4} = 6.431 \times 10^{-4}$$

Taking the base-10 logarithm:

$$\log(6.431 \times 10^{-4}) = \log(6.431) + \log(10^{-4}) = 0.8082 - 4 = -3.1918$$

Multiplying by -1.8:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \times (-3.1918) = 5.745$$

Therefore:

$$\sqrt{f_D} = \frac{1}{5.745} = 0.1741 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_D = 0.0303$$

**Step 5 (a): Calculate the pressure drop using the Fanning convention.**

First, convert the Darcy friction factor to the Fanning friction factor:

$$f_F = \frac{f_D}{4} = \frac{0.0303}{4} = 7.575 \times 10^{-3}$$

Now apply the Fanning-based pressure drop equation:

$$\Delta P_f = 4 f_F \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$= 4 \times 7.575 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{1000 \times (0.2546)^2}{2}$$

$$= 4 \times 7.575 \times 10^{-3} \times 2000 \times \frac{1000 \times 0.06482}{2}$$

$$= 60.6 \times 32.41$$

$$\Delta P_f = 1964 \text{ Pa} = 1.964 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 (b): Calculate the pressure drop using the Darcy convention directly.**

$$\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.0303 \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{1000 \times 0.06482}{2}$$

$$= 0.0303 \times 2000 \times 32.41$$

$$\Delta P_f = 1964 \text{ Pa} = 1.964 \text{ kPa}$$

Both conventions yield the **same numerical answer** (as they must, by definition), confirming the consistency of the calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent (Hydraulic) Diameter for the Annulus

Consider fluid flowing through the **annular region** between an outer pipe of diameter $D_o$ and an inner pipe of diameter $D_i$. The hydraulic diameter is defined as:

$$D_e = \frac{4 A_{cross-section}}{Wetted Perimeter}$$

For the annulus, the cross-sectional area available for flow is:

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$$

The wetted perimeter consists of **both** the inner and outer pipe walls in contact with the fluid:

$$P_{wetted} = \pi D_o + \pi D_i = \pi(D_o + D_i)$$

Substituting these into the definition of hydraulic diameter:

$$D_{e,ann} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{\pi(D_o + D_i)} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_o + D_i}$$

Using the algebraic identity $D_o^2 - D_i^2 = (D_o - D_i)(D_o + D_i)$:

$$D_{e,ann} = \frac{(D_o - D_i)(D_o + D_i)}{D_o + D_i} = D_o - D_i$$

$$\boxed{D_{e,ann} = D_o - D_i}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Friction Factor Correlations

| Correlation | Type | Flow Regime | Smooth/Rough | Range of Validity | Solvability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_D = 64/Re$ | Explicit | Laminar | Either | $Re < 2100$ | Direct |
| Blasius: $f_D = 0.3164/Re^{0.25}$ | Explicit | Turbulent | Smooth | $4000 < Re < 10^5$ | Direct |
| Petukhov: $f_D = (0.79 \ln Re - 1.64)^{-2}$ | Explicit | Turbulent | Smooth | $3000 < Re < 5 \times 10^6$ | Direct |
| Colebrook-White: $1/\sqrt{f_D} = -2 \log[\varepsilon/(3.7D) + 2.51/(Re\sqrt{f_D})]$ | **Implicit** | Turbulent | Rough | Turbulent, all $\varepsilon/D$ | **Iterative** |
| Haaland: $1/\sqrt{f_D} = -1.8 \log[(\varepsilon/(3.7D))^{1.11} + 6.9/Re]$ | Explicit | Turbulent | Rough | Turbulent, all $\varepsilon/D$ | Direct |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The Colebrook equation is **implicit** (the friction factor appears on both sides). It cannot be solved by simple algebraic manipulation. The student must either (a) use the explicit Haaland equation as an approximation, or (b) solve Colebrook by iteration (Newton-Raphson, bisection, or successive substitution). The Board expects the **Haaland equation** to be the default choice in board exams for turbulent, rough pipe flow.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical K-Values for Common Fittings

| Fitting Type | K-Value (loss coefficient) |
|---|---|
| 90° standard elbow | 0.9 |
| 90° long-radius elbow | 0.45 |
| 45° elbow | 0.4 |
| Tee, line flow | 0.6 |
| Tee, branch flow | 1.8 |
| Globe valve, fully open | 10.0 |
| Gate valve, fully open | 0.17 |
| Ball valve, fully open | 0.05 |
| Check valve, swing type | 2.5 |
| Sharp-edged entrance | 0.5 |
| Re-entrant (Borda) entrance | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Projecting exit | 1.0 |
| Sudden contraction ($A_2/A_1 = 0.5$) | $K \approx 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1) \approx 0.25$ |
| Sudden expansion ($A_2/A_1 = 0.5$) | $K = (1 - A_2/A_1)^2 = 0.25$ |
| Sudden expansion ($A_2/A_1 = 0.0$, i.e., exit) | $K = 1.0$ |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Pressure Drop Due to Sudden Expansion

When fluid flows from a smaller pipe (area $A_1$, velocity $v_1$) into a larger pipe (area $A_2$, velocity $v_2$), the streamlines diverge and a substantial amount of kinetic energy is dissipated in the form of intense turbulence and eddy formation in the **recirculation zone** between the vena contracta and the pipe wall.

Applying a macroscopic momentum balance across the expansion section (control volume between sections 1 and 2):

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \dot{m}(v_2 - v_1)$$

Where $P_1$ is the pressure acting on the annular area $(A_2 - A_1)$ plus the pressure at the inlet, but for simplicity the analysis yields:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho v_2 (v_2 - v_1) \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

Using continuity, $\dot{m} = \rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2$, and rearranging:

$$\Delta P_{exp} = \rho v_1^2 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2} - 1\right) \cdot (-1)$$

This simplifies to the celebrated **Borda-Carnot equation**:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{exp} = \frac{\rho (v_1 - v_2)^2}{2} = \frac{\rho v_1^2}{2} \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2}$$

Defining $K_{exp} = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2 = (1 - \beta^2)^2$ where $\beta = A_1/A_2$:

$$\Delta P_{exp} = K_{exp} \cdot \frac{\rho v_1^2}{2}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Pressure Drop Calculation Sequence

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical decision sequence** that a chemical engineering designer must follow when estimating the pressure drop in a double-pipe system. This is a block-level functional flow rather than a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Process Inputs Duty Q Temperature dT Cp rho mu"] --> nodeB["Calculate Mass Flow Rate mdot = Q / Cp dT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Identify Geometry Inner pipe OR Annulus"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute Equivalent Diameter De = Di OR De = Do - Di"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute Cross Section Area A = pi De sq / 4"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Velocity v = mdot / rho A"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Reynolds Number Re = De v rho / mu"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Re less than 2100"}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI["Laminar Flow fD = 64 / Re"]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ{"2100 less than Re less than 4000"}
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeK["Transitional Flow Interpolate or use Sieder-Tate"]
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeL["Turbulent Flow Check Roughness epsilon / De"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Compute Friction Drop dPf using Darcy Weisbach"]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeN{"epsilon = 0 Smooth pipe"}
    nodeN -- YES --> nodeO["Use Blasius OR Petukhov Equation"]
    nodeN -- NO --> nodeP["Use Haaland OR Colebrook White Equation"]
    nodeO --> nodeM
    nodeP --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeQ["Sum Minor Losses dPm = sum Ki rho v sq / 2"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Add Contraction Expansion Entrance Exit dPc dPex"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Total dP = dPf + dPm + dPc + dPex"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Check against allowable dP Compare with pump duty"]
    nodeT --> nodeU{"Within limits"}
    nodeU -- YES --> nodeV["Design Accepted Finalize Equipment Specs"]
    nodeU -- NO --> nodeW["Iterate Reduce L Increase D OR reduce flow"]
    nodeW --> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger with Pressure Drop Contributions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["INLET Reservoir Tank"] --> node2["Entrance Loss K = 0.5"]
    node2 --> node3["Straight Pipe Section 1 frictional drop"]
    node3 --> node4["Bend 1 hairpin elbow K = 0.9"]
    node4 --> node5["Straight Pipe Section 2 frictional drop"]
    node5 --> node6["Bend 2 hairpin elbow K = 0.9"]
    node6 --> node7["Straight Pipe Section 3 frictional drop"]
    node7 --> node8["Exit Loss K = 1.0"]
    node8 --> node9["OUTLET Downstream Equipment"]
    node3 -.-> node10["Total dP = Sum of all 7 contributions"]
    node4 -.-> node10
    node5 -.-> node10
    node6 -.-> node10
    node7 -.-> node10
    node2 -.-> node10
    node8 -.-> node10
    node10 --> node11["Pump Sizing Compressor Rating"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram: Friction Factor Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Reynolds Number and Roughness Known"] --> nodeQ1{"Re less than 2100"}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeAns1["LAMINAR: fD = 64 / Re Independent of epsilon"]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2{"epsilon equals 0 OR smooth pipe"}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeQ3{"Re less than 100000"}
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeAns2["HAALAND explicit OR COLEBROOK iterative"]
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeAns3["BLASIUS: fD = 0.3164 / Re pow 0.25"]
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeAns4["PETUKHOV: fD equals 0.79 ln Re minus 1.64 all pow negative 2"]
    nodeAns1 --> nodeFinal["Insert fD into Darcy Weisbach"]
    nodeAns2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeAns3 --> nodeFinal
    nodeAns4 --> nodeFinal
    nodeFinal --> nodeEnd["Compute dPf in Pascals"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: Annular vs Inner Pipe Flow Routing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTop["Hot Fluid Stream"] --> nodeQ1{"Service Type Heating OR Cooling"}
    nodeQ1 -- HEATING --> nodeA1["Hot fluid flows through ANNULUS Higher dP allowable"]
    nodeQ1 -- COOLING --> nodeA2["Hot fluid flows through INNER PIPE Better film coefficient"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeGeom1["De = Do - Di"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeGeom2["De = Di"]
    nodeGeom1 --> nodeVel1["v = mdot / rho Aann"]
    nodeGeom2 --> nodeVel2["v = mdot / rho Api"]
    nodeVel1 --> nodeRe1["Re = De v rho / mu"]
    nodeVel2 --> nodeRe2["Re = Di v rho / mu"]
    nodeRe1 --> nodeFF1["Select Friction Factor Correlation"]
    nodeRe2 --> nodeFF2["Select Friction Factor Correlation"]
    nodeFF1 --> nodeDP1["Compute dPf Annulus"]
    nodeFF2 --> nodeDP2["Compute dPf Inner Pipe"]
    nodeDP1 --> nodeTot["Total dP = dPann + dPinner + minor losses"]
    nodeDP2 --> nodeTot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The KTU 2024 syllabus for "Pressure Drop Estimation in Double Pipe Flow" emphasizes the **cumulative** nature of pressure drop. Each fitting (hairpin bend, tee, valve) and each length of straight pipe contributes additively to the total $\Delta P$. The functional flow diagrams above map the **decision logic** rather than the physical geometry, which is more useful for solving board examination numerical problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the equivalent (hydraulic) diameter for fluid flow in the annular region of a concentric double-pipe heat exchanger. State its significance in pressure drop estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

The equivalent (hydraulic) diameter for the annular region of a concentric double-pipe heat exchanger is defined as four times the ratio of the cross-sectional area available for flow to the wetted perimeter:

$$D_{e,ann} = \frac{4 A_{ann}}{P_{wetted}} = \frac{4 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{\pi(D_o + D_i)} = D_o - D_i$$

**Significance:**
1. It allows the use of standard pipe-flow friction factor correlations (which were developed for circular pipes) for non-circular geometries such as the annulus.
2. It is the characteristic length used in the Reynolds number to determine the flow regime in the annulus.
3. It appears in the Darcy-Weisbach equation for the calculation of the frictional pressure drop in the annulus.

**Key Constants:** None required; purely a geometric definition.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the Darcy friction factor and the Fanning friction factor. What is the numerical relationship between them?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Darcy Friction Factor ($f_D$) | Fanning Friction Factor ($f_F$) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | European engineering tradition | American chemical engineering tradition |
| Numerical value | 4 times the Fanning factor | 1/4 of the Darcy factor |
| Darcy-Weisbach form | $\Delta P = f_D (L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ | $\Delta P = 4 f_F (L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ |
| Laminar value (smooth pipe) | $64/Re$ | $16/Re$ |
| Typical use | Mechanical, civil, aerospace | Chemical, process engineering |

**Numerical Relationship:**

$$\boxed{f_D = 4 f_F \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f_F = \frac{f_D}{4}}$$

The Darcy friction factor is **4 times larger** than the Fanning friction factor because the Fanning convention defines friction as a shear stress at the wall (which is 1/4 of the total pressure drop per unit length), while the Darcy convention defines it as the head loss per unit length.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A chemical plant uses a concentric double-pipe heat exchanger to cool 8000 kg/h of an organic liquid (specific heat $C_p = 2.5$ kJ/kg·K) from 90 °C to 50 °C using cooling water. Cooling water enters the inner pipe at 25 °C and exits at 45 °C, with a mass flow rate of 6000 kg/h. The inner pipe has an outer diameter of 60 mm (wall thickness 4 mm), and the outer pipe has an inner diameter of 100 mm. Both pipes are commercial steel with roughness $\varepsilon = 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m. The straight length of each pass is 3 m, and there are 2 hairpin bends (90° standard elbows, $K = 0.9$ each). Assume the inner pipe is smooth ($\varepsilon \approx 0$) and neglect any elevation change.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The mass flow rate of the organic liquid in the annulus [2 marks]
- (ii) The Reynolds number and flow regime for both streams [4 marks]
- (iii) The friction factor for both streams using appropriate correlations [4 marks]
- (iv) The total pressure drop on the cooling water side (inner pipe) [4 marks]

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 0.1 m carries oil ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.005$ Pa·s) at a velocity of 1.5 m/s. The pipe is 50 m long and is fitted with two 90° standard elbows ($K = 0.9$ each), a fully open globe valve ($K = 10$), and exits into a large tank.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The Reynolds number and the friction factor [3 marks]
- (ii) The total frictional pressure drop including minor losses [4 marks]

---

### Model Answer to Question A (a)

**Given Data (Inner Pipe / Cooling Water Side):**
- $\dot{m}_{cw} = 6000$ kg/h = $1.667$ kg/s
- $D_i = 60 - 2(4) = 52$ mm = $0.052$ m (inner diameter of inner pipe)
- $D_o^{outer} = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m (inner diameter of outer pipe)
- $L = 3$ m per pass, 2 passes → $L_{total} = 6$ m
- Number of hairpin bends = 2
- $K_{elbow} = 0.9$
- $\rho_{water, 35°C} \approx 994$ kg/m³
- $\mu_{water, 35°C} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s (at mean temperature 35 °C)
- $C_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Calculate the mass flow rate of the organic liquid (annulus side).**

Energy balance on the heat exchanger:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot C_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw} = \dot{m}_{org} \cdot C_{p,org} \cdot \Delta T_{org}$$

$$Q = 1.667 \times 4.18 \times (45 - 25) = 1.667 \times 4.18 \times 20 = 139.4 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_{org} = \frac{Q}{C_{p,org} \cdot \Delta T_{org}} = \frac{139.4}{2.5 \times (90 - 50)} = \frac{139.4}{100} = 1.394 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate velocities and Reynolds numbers.**

For the inner pipe (cooling water):

$$A_{i} = \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.052)^2}{4} = 2.124 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_{cw} = \frac{\dot{m}_{cw}}{\rho_{cw} \cdot A_i} = \frac{1.667}{994 \times 2.124 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.7896 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re_{cw} = \frac{D_i v_{cw} \rho_{cw}}{\mu_{cw}} = \frac{0.052 \times 0.7896 \times 994}{7.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 56{,}634$$

Since $Re_{cw} > 4000$, the cooling water flow is **turbulent**.

For the annulus (organic liquid):

Assuming organic liquid properties: $\rho_{org} = 850$ kg/m³, $\mu_{org} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (assumed reasonable values):

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1^2 - 0.052^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.0027)}{4} = 5.736 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$D_{e,ann} = D_o - D_i = 0.1 - 0.052 = 0.048 \text{ m}$$

$$v_{org} = \frac{\dot{m}_{org}}{\rho_{org} \cdot A_{ann}} = \frac{1.394}{850 \times 5.736 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2858 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re_{ann} = \frac{D_{e,ann} v_{org} \rho_{org}}{\mu_{org}} = \frac{0.048 \times 0.2858 \times 850}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4665$$

Since $Re_{ann} > 4000$, the organic flow is also **turbulent**, but just barely.

**Step 3: Calculate the friction factors.**

For the inner pipe (turbulent, smooth): Use Blasius equation.

$$f_{D,cw} = \frac{0.3164}{Re_{cw}^{0.25}} = \frac{0.3164}{(56{,}634)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.3164}{15.43} = 0.0205$$

For the annulus (turbulent): Use Blasius (assuming smooth wall):

$$f_{D,ann} = \frac{0.3164}{(4665)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.3164}{8.252} = 0.03834$$

**Step 4: Calculate the total pressure drop on the cooling water side.**

$$\Delta P_{f,cw} = f_{D,cw} \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D_i} \cdot \frac{\rho_{cw} v_{cw}^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.0205 \times \frac{6}{0.052} \times \frac{994 \times (0.7896)^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.0205 \times 115.4 \times \frac{994 \times 0.6234}{2}$$

$$= 2.366 \times 309.8$$

$$= 733.0 \text{ Pa} = 0.733 \text{ kPa}$$

Minor losses from 2 hairpin bends:

$$\Delta P_{minor,cw} = 2 \times K_{elbow} \times \frac{\rho_{cw} v_{cw}^2}{2} = 2 \times 0.9 \times 309.8 = 557.6 \text{ Pa}$$

Total pressure drop on the cooling water side:

$$\Delta P_{total,cw} = 733.0 + 557.6 = 1290.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.29 \text{ kPa}$$

### Model Answer to Question A (b)

**Given Data:**
- $D = 0.1$ m, $L = 50$ m
- $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.005$ Pa·s
- $v = 1.5$ m/s
- 2 elbows with $K = 0.9$ each, 1 globe valve with $K = 10$, exit loss $K = 1.0$

**Step 1: Calculate the Reynolds number and friction factor.**

$$Re = \frac{D v \rho}{\mu} = \frac{0.1 \times 1.5 \times 850}{0.005} = 25{,}500$$

Since $Re > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

Using Blasius (assuming smooth pipe):

$$f_D = \frac{0.3164}{(25{,}500)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.3164}{12.65} = 0.0250$$

**Step 2: Calculate the frictional pressure drop.**

$$\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.0250 \times \frac{50}{0.1} \times \frac{850 \times (1.5)^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.0250 \times 500 \times 956.25 = 11{,}953 \text{ Pa} = 11.95 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the minor losses.**

$$\Delta P_{minor} = (2 \times 0.9 + 10 + 1.0) \times \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$= (1.8 + 10 + 1.0) \times 956.25$$

$$= 12.8 \times 956.25 = 12{,}240 \text{ Pa} = 12.24 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Total pressure drop.**

$$\Delta P_{total} = 11.95 + 12.24 = 24.19 \text{ kPa}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper - Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation for frictional pressure drop in a straight horizontal pipe. State clearly the assumptions made. Show that the Fanning and Darcy friction factor conventions differ by a factor of **exactly 4** for the same physical flow situation. [7 marks]

**(b)** A fluid ($\rho = 1200$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows at a volumetric flow rate of $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s through a 25 mm diameter commercial steel pipe ($\varepsilon = 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m) of length 30 m.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The Reynolds number, flow regime, and friction factor using the Colebrook equation (solve iteratively) [4 marks]
- (ii) The pressure drop across the pipe length [3 marks]

---

### Model Answer to Question B (a)

**Derivation of Darcy-Weisbach Equation:**

The mechanical energy balance for steady, incompressible, isothermal flow through a horizontal control volume between sections 1 and 2 is:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g_c} + \frac{v_1^2}{2 g_c} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g_c} + \frac{v_2^2}{2 g_c} + z_2 + h_f$$

In SI units where $g_c = 1$:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + h_f$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow.
2. Incompressible fluid.
3. Isothermal flow.
4. Horizontal pipe (no elevation change: $z_1 = z_2$).
5. Constant pipe diameter ($v_1 = v_2$).
6. No shaft work (no pump or turbine in the segment).
7. Newtonian fluid behavior.

Under these assumptions, the equation reduces to:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \rho h_f$$

The friction head loss per unit mass is empirically correlated with the **dimensionless friction factor** and the **kinetic energy per unit mass**:

$$h_f = 4 f_F \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = 4 f_F \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}}$$

**Proof of the 4:1 Ratio:**

The **Fanning** convention defines the friction factor using the **wall shear stress** $\tau_w$:

$$f_F = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2}$$

The **Darcy** convention defines the friction factor using the **total pressure drop per unit length**:

$$f_D = \frac{\Delta P / L}{\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 / D}$$

For a horizontal pipe, a force balance on the fluid element yields:

$$\Delta P \cdot A = \tau_w \cdot P_{wetted} \cdot L = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L$$

So:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

Substituting into the Darcy definition:

$$f_D = \frac{4 \tau_w L / D}{L \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 / D} = \frac{4 \tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2} = 4 f_F$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_D = 4 f_F \quad \text{(QED, the ratio is exactly 4)}}$$

### Model Answer to Question B (b)

**Step 1: Calculate the velocity and Reynolds number.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q_v}{A} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.3056 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{D v \rho}{\mu} = \frac{0.025 \times 0.3056 \times 1200}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 6112$$

Since $Re = 6112 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 2: Calculate relative roughness.**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.025} = 1.84 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Solve Colebrook equation iteratively.**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -2 \log\left[\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f_D}}\right]$$

**Iteration 1:** Initial guess: $f_D = 0.03$ (typical for turbulent flow).

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.03}} = 5.774$$

RHS: $-2 \log\left[\frac{1.84 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{6112 \times \sqrt{0.03}}\right]$

$$= -2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.359 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \log[2.856 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \times (-2.544) = 5.088$$

$f_D = (1/5.088)^2 = 0.03864$

**Iteration 2:** Try $f_D = 0.03864$, $\sqrt{f_D} = 0.1966$.

RHS: $-2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.51/(6112 \times 0.1966)]$

$$= -2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.090 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \log[2.587 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \times (-2.587) = 5.174$$

$f_D = (1/5.174)^2 = 0.03735$

**Iteration 3:** Try $f_D = 0.03735$, $\sqrt{f_D} = 0.1933$.

RHS: $-2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.51/(6112 \times 0.1933)]$

$$= -2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.124 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \log[2.621 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \times (-2.582) = 5.163$$

$f_D = (1/5.163)^2 = 0.03752$

**Iteration 4:** Try $f_D = 0.03752$, $\sqrt{f_D} = 0.1937$.

RHS: $-2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.51/(6112 \times 0.1937)]$

$$= -2 \log[4.973 \times 10^{-4} + 2.121 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \log[2.618 \times 10^{-3}]$$

$$= -2 \times (-2.582) = 5.164$$

Converged at $f_D \approx 0.0375$.

**Cross-check with Haaland equation (explicit, expected to match within ~1.5%):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\frac{1.84 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{6112}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log[(4.973 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 1.129 \times 10^{-3}]$$

Calculating $(4.973 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11}$:

$$= e^{1.11 \cdot \ln(4.973 \times 10^{-4})} = e^{1.11 \cdot (-7.607)} = e^{-8.444} = 2.146 \times 10^{-4}$$

RHS: $-1.8 \log[2.146 \times 10^{-4} + 1.129 \times 10^{-3}] = -1.8 \log[1.344 \times 10^{-3}]$

$$= -1.8 \times (-2.872) = 5.169$$

$f_D = (1/5.169)^2 = 0.0374$

This is in excellent agreement with the Colebrook iterative solution of $f_D = 0.0375$.

**Step 4: Calculate the pressure drop.**

$$\Delta P = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.0375 \times \frac{30}{0.025} \times \frac{1200 \times (0.3056)^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.0375 \times 1200 \times 56.04$$

$$= 2522 \text{ Pa} = 2.52 \text{ kPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing Fanning and Darcy conventions:** A common student error is to use $f_F = 16/Re$ (laminar Fanning) and then apply the Darcy formula $\Delta P = f_D (L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ without converting. This yields a result that is **4 times too small**. Always state the convention explicitly in the first step.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert the inner pipe diameter:** If the wall thickness of the inner pipe is given (e.g., 4 mm), the **hydraulic diameter is the INNER diameter of the inner pipe**, not the outer diameter. Many students mistakenly use $D_o^{inner} = 60$ mm instead of $D_i^{inner} = 60 - 2(4) = 52$ mm.
> 3. **Mis-identifying the equivalent diameter for the annulus:** The equivalent diameter of the annulus is $D_o - D_i$, **NOT** $D_o + D_i$ (which would be the wetted perimeter divided by $\pi$). The "+" sign is a common transcription error.
> 4. **Iterative solution convergence:** The Colebrook equation must be solved by iteration. The Haaland equation is the **explicit, closed-form** alternative that KTU examiners prefer for board exams. Do not attempt to solve Colebrook algebraically — it is impossible.
> 5. **Neglecting minor losses:** A common mistake is to compute only the straight-pipe friction and ignore elbows, valves, contractions, and expansions. For short pipe runs with many fittings, minor losses can **exceed** straight-pipe friction.
> 6. **Unit consistency:** Mixing $\rho$ in g/cm³ with $v$ in m/s yields an erroneous $\Delta P$. Always use $\rho$ in **kg/m³** for SI calculations, and convert carefully if cgs or FPS units are used.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Pressure Drop Estimation in Double Pipe Flow:**

### Conceptual Foundations
- [x] Pressure drop = **mechanical energy dissipated as heat** due to viscous and turbulent friction.
- [x] Pressure drop is **additive** for series components: straight pipes, fittings, contractions, expansions, and elevation changes.
- [x] The Reynolds number is the **master switch** that determines the friction factor correlation.

### Key Geometric Definitions
- [x] **Inner pipe flow:** $D_e = D_i$ (inner diameter of the inner pipe).
- [x] **Annular flow:** $D_{e,ann} = D_o - D_i$ (outer pipe ID minus inner pipe OD).
- [x] **Annular area:** $A_{ann} = \pi(D_o^2 - D_i^2)/4$.
- [x] **Inner pipe area:** $A_i = \pi D_i^2 / 4$.

### Friction Factor Conventions
- [x] **Fanning friction factor:** $f_F = 16/Re$ for laminar flow.
- [x] **Darcy friction factor:** $f_D = 64/Re$ for laminar flow.
- [x] **Ratio:** $f_D = 4 f_F$ (always, in any flow regime).
- [x] **Darcy-Weisbach:** $\Delta P = f_D (L/D) (\rho v^2/2)$.
- [x] **Fanning version:** $\Delta P = 4 f_F (L/D) (\rho v^2/2)$.

### Friction Factor Correlations (Turbulent Flow)
- [x] **Blasius** (smooth, $Re < 10^5$): $f_D = 0.3164/Re^{0.25}$.
- [x] **Petukhov** (smooth, $Re < 5 \times 10^6$): $f_D = (0.79 \ln Re - 1.64)^{-2}$.
- [x] **Colebrook** (rough, implicit, iterative).
- [x] **Haaland** (rough, explicit, preferred for KTU exams).

### Loss Coefficients (K-Values) — Must-Memorize Numbers
- [x] Sharp-edged entrance: $K = 0.5$.
- [x] Exit to large reservoir: $K = 1.0$.
- [x] Sudden contraction: $K = 0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$.
- [x] Sudden expansion: $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ (Borda-Carnot).
- [x] 90° standard elbow: $K = 0.9$.
- [x] Globe valve fully open: $K = 10$.
- [x] Gate valve fully open: $K = 0.17$.

### Calculation Workflow (Always Follow This Order)
1. Compute mass flow rate from energy balance (if duty is given).
2. Compute cross-sectional area and velocity.
3. Compute Reynolds number.
4. Identify flow regime and select correlation.
5. Compute friction factor.
6. Compute straight-pipe $\Delta P_f$.
7. Sum all minor losses.
8. Report total $\Delta P$ with units.

### Engineering Judgement Calls
- [x] If $\Delta P_{total} > 50$ kPa for liquid service, consider a larger pipe diameter.
- [x] If $Re < 2100$ (laminar), use the Hagen-Poiseuille equation: $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v / D^2$ (this is equivalent to $f_D = 64/Re$).
- [x] In transitional flow ($2100 < Re < 4000$), the friction factor is **uncertain** — apply a safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0 on the calculated $\Delta P$.
- [x] For gas flow, account for **compressibility** if $\Delta P / P > 0.10$ (10% rule of thumb).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Design of Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger: Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermal Design of Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger: Constructional Features ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is a type of indirect-contact, recuperative heat transfer equipment in which one fluid flows through a bundle of tubes enclosed within an outer pressure vessel (the shell), while the second fluid flows over the outside of the tubes inside the shell. Heat transfer between the two fluids occurs through the tube wall, with the two streams kept physically separated. The STHE is the most widely used heat exchanger in the chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, power, and HVAC industries because of its robustness, ability to handle high pressures and temperatures, ease of fabrication, and adaptability to a wide range of operating conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Constructional features covered under Module 1 typically include (a) major parts (shell, channels, tubes, tube sheets, baffles, nozzles), (b) TEMA classification, (c) types of STHE (fixed tube sheet, U-tube, floating head), and (d) tube layout, pitch, and material selection. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a multi-storey apartment building where each floor has water pipes running through it. The **outer building** acts as the **shell**; the **pipes inside each flat** are the **tubes** carrying hot water; the **walls separating the flats from the common corridor** are the **tube sheets** that hold the pipes in place; and the **horizontal slabs crossing the corridor at intervals** are the **baffles** that force the corridor air to zigzag past each flat. Heat from the hot water pipes transfers to the cooler corridor air. The key insight is that the STHE provides a large surface area (thousands of tubes) packed into a compact shell, with **baffles** that force the shell-side fluid to repeatedly cross the tube bundle, dramatically boosting the heat transfer coefficient. ### 1.3 Key Standard Codes Used - **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) – the most commonly followed standard for STHE design. - **ASME Section VIII, Division 1** – for pressure vessel design and fabrication. - **IS 4503** (Indian Standard) – specification for STHE. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard SI Units used throughout this module:** > - Tube length, shell diameter: **m (metre)** > - Tube outer/inner diameter: **mm (millimetre)** > - Heat duty: **W (Watt)** or **kW** > - Heat transfer coefficient: **W/m²·K** > - Pressure: **Pa** or **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Major Components of a Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger A STHE is made up of several parts, each serving a specific function. The following list covers all components that the KTU examiner expects a student to know by name and function. - **Shell:** The outer pressure vessel that confines the shell-side fluid and houses the tube bundle. Cylindrical, with end flanges or channels bolted on. Common shell diameters: 100 mm to 3000 mm. - **Tubes:** The heat transfer surface. Standard outside diameters are **6.35 mm (¼")**, **9.53 mm (⅜")**, **12.7 mm (½")**, **19.05 mm (¾")**, **25.4 mm (1")**, and **38.1 mm (1½")**. Lengths: 1 m to 6 m or more. - **Tube Sheets (Tubesheets):** Thick circular plates in which the tubes are expanded or welded. They separate the tube-side fluid from the shell-side fluid. They are the heart of any STHE because they must withstand both pressure and thermal stress. - **Channels (Heads / Bonnets):** End covers bolted to the shell via the channel flange. They contain the inlet and outlet nozzles for the tube-side fluid. May be removable (floating head) or fixed. - **Baffles:** Plates installed inside the shell, perpendicular to the tubes, to support the tubes, increase turbulence of the shell-side fluid, and direct the flow path. Types include segmental, double-segmental, disc-and-donut, helical, and orifice baffles. - **Nozzles:** Pipe connections for the inlet and outlet of both fluids, usually four in number (2 for shell-side, 2 for tube-side). Positioned on the shell and channel. - **Pass Partitions (Channel Dividers):** Plates inside the channel that divide the flow into multiple tube-side passes (1-2, 2-4, 4-8 passes). - **Impingement Plate / Rod:** A protective plate placed opposite a shell-side inlet nozzle to prevent direct high-velocity jet erosion of the tubes. - **Expansion Joint / Bellows:** Used in fixed tube-sheet exchangers to absorb the differential thermal expansion between the shell and the tube bundle. - **Tie Rods and Spacers:** Long studs that hold the baffles and tube support plates at the correct spacing inside the shell. - **Drain and Vent Connections:** Small nozzles for venting air during hydrotest and draining condensate. ### 2.2 TEMA Classification (T-E-M-A Designation) TEMA classifies STHE into three main categories based on the **front end (channel)**, **shell type**, and **rear end (head)**. This is universally tested in KTU exams. | Letter | Position | Meaning | Common Configurations | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | T | Front End | **T**ube-side removable bundle; channel integral with tubesheet | T E A, T E M, T E U | | U | Front End | **U**-tube bundle | U E A, U E M, U E U | | S | Front End | **S**ingle fixed tubesheet | S E A, S E M | | E | Shell | **E**-shell, one pass shell | T E A, U E A | | F | Shell | **F**-shell, two pass shell with longitudinal baffle | T F A, U F A | | A | Rear End | **A** channel and cover, removable | T E A, U E A | | M | Rear End | **M** channel and cover with **B**on**N**et, removable | T E M, U E M | | U | Rear End | **U**-tube bundle return | T E U, U E U | | W | Rear End | **W** packed floating head with lantern ring | T E W | | X | Rear End | **X** cross-floating head | T E X | | P | Rear End | **P** outside-packed floating head | T E P | | D | Rear End | **D** split-ring floating head | T E D | | S | Rear End | **S**lippery floating head (sleeve) | T E S | > [!NOTE] > **Most commonly used STHE type in industry and frequently asked in KTU exams:** **Fixed Tube Sheet (TEMA: AES, BEM, AEM)** and **U-Tube (TEMA: BEU, BEW)**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Definitions | Symbol | Definition | Formula / Value | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | $d_o$ | Tube outer diameter | given / selected | m | | $d_i$ | Tube inner diameter | $d_i = d_o - 2 t$ | m | | $t$ | Tube wall thickness | selected as per ASME B31.3 | m | | $P_T$ | Tube pitch (centre-to-centre distance) | $P_T = 1.25 d_o$ (triangular) or $1.5 d_o$ (square) | m | | $\theta$ | Tube layout angle | $30°, 60°, 90°, 45°$ | degrees | | $D_s$ | Shell inside diameter | $D_s = P_T (N_T / K_1)^{1/n_t}$ | m | | $N_T$ | Total number of tubes | calculated from heat duty | – | | $K_1, n_t$ | Tube count constants | depend on $P_T/d_o$ and layout | – | | $L_{tc}$ | Centre-to-centle baffle spacing (window segment cut) | 20% to 45% of $D_s$ | m | | $B$ | Baffle spacing | $0.2 D_s \leq B \leq D_s$ | m | | $h_{io}$ | Outside-to-inside coefficient correction | $h_{io} = h_i \cdot (d_i / d_o)$ | W/m²·K | | $L_{eff}$ | Effective tube length | $L - 2 \cdot \text{end\;thickness}$ | m | | $A_o$ | Outside heat transfer area | $A_o = \pi d_o L N_T$ | m² | | $A_i$ | Inside heat transfer area | $A_i = \pi d_i L N_T$ | m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU exam tip:** Always use the **outside heat transfer area $A_o$** as the reference area unless the question explicitly states otherwise. The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is conventionally based on $A_o$. ### 2.4 Tube Layout Patterns Tubes can be arranged on the tube sheet in four standard patterns, which affect the number of tubes that fit in a given shell diameter. | Layout | Symbol | Pitch Formula | Tubes per Unit Area | Cleanability | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Triangular (30°) | △ | $P_T = 1.25 d_o$ | High | Poor | | Rotated Square (45°) | ◇ | $P_T = 1.25 d_o$ | Moderate | Excellent | | Square (90°) | ▢ | $P_T = 1.5 d_o$ | Low | Excellent | | Rotated Triangular (60°) | △ | $P_T = 1.25 d_o$ | High | Moderate | > [!NOTE] > **Standard industrial choice:** **Triangular pitch at 30°** (most compact) and **Square pitch at 90°** (easiest to clean mechanical scale). Most STHEs use triangular pitch because it gives the maximum heat transfer area per unit shell volume. ### 2.5 Baffle Types and Functions Baffles are critical for shell-side performance. They: 1. **Support the tubes** against vibration and sagging over long spans. 2. **Direct the shell-side flow** in a cross-flow pattern over the tube bundle, increasing velocity and turbulence. 3. **Increase heat transfer coefficient** by forcing the fluid to change direction. 4. **Create a pressure drop** that must be calculated (Kern's or Bell-Delaware method). | Baffle Type | Construction | Use Case | |:---|:---|:---| | Segmental (Single) | A chord cut from a disc | Most common; 20–45% cut | | Double Segmental | Two smaller chords on opposite sides | Lower pressure drop | | Disc-and-Donut | Alternating discs and donuts | High heat transfer, used for viscous fluids | | Helical (Spiral) | Continuous helical plate | Used for fouling slurries | | Orifice (Baffle with Hole) | Segment with central hole | When tubes are omitted for flow distribution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Baffle cut (%) is critical:** > - **<20%** → very high velocity, high pressure drop, vibration risk > - **20–25%** → optimal (industry default) > - **25–45%** → lower pressure drop, more bypassing > - **>45%** → ineffective crossflow, dead zones ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Shell Diameter from Tube Count The number of tubes that can fit into a shell of inside diameter $D_s$ depends on the tube pitch $P_T$ and the layout pattern. The KTU syllabus expects students to use the **Kern's tube count correlation** or the **Hewitt / TEMA constant method**. **Step 1: Define the tube count constant** For a given tube layout and pitch ratio $P_T / d_o$, the constants $K_1$ and $n_t$ are tabulated (or found in TEMA tables). **Step 2: Express total tube count** $$ N_T = K_1 \left( \frac{D_s}{P_T} \right)^{n_t} $$ **Step 3: Rearrange for shell diameter** $$ \left( \frac{D_s}{P_T} \right)^{n_t} = \frac{N_T}{K_1} $$ $$ \frac{D_s}{P_T} = \left( \frac{N_T}{K_1} \right)^{1/n_t} $$ $$ \boxed{D_s = P_T \left( \frac{N_T}{K_1} \right)^{1/n_t}} $$ **Step 4: Tabulated values of $K_1$ and $n_t$** | $P_T / d_o$ | Layout | $K_1$ | $n_t$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1.25 | Triangular (30°) | 0.249 | 2.207 | | 1.25 | Rotated Square (45°) | 0.175 | 2.291 | | 1.5 | Square (90°) | 0.155 | 2.291 | **Step 5: Numerical illustration** *Suppose* $N_T = 200$ tubes, triangular pitch with $P_T / d_o = 1.25$, $d_o = 19.05$ mm (¾" tube). First compute the pitch: $$ P_T = 1.25 \times 19.05 \text{ mm} = 23.81 \text{ mm} = 0.02381 \text{ m} $$ Now use the formula: $$ D_s = 0.02381 \left( \frac{200}{0.249} \right)^{1/2.207} $$ $$ \frac{200}{0.249} = 803.21 $$ $$ 803.21^{1/2.207} = e^{\ln(803.21)/2.207} = e^{6.689/2.207} = e^{3.030} = 20.69 $$ $$ D_s = 0.02381 \times 20.69 = 0.4926 \text{ m} \approx 492.6 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard practice:** Round $D_s$ up to the nearest standard shell size (e.g., 500 mm) and re-verify the number of tubes. This is the iterative step the KTU examiner will expect. ### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Tube Length vs. Tubesheet Thickness Tubes are expanded (or welded) into the tubesheet. The portion of the tube inside the tubesheet is **not** active for heat transfer. Therefore, the effective tube length for heat transfer is: $$ L_{eff} = L - 2 \cdot t_s $$ where $t_s$ is the tubesheet thickness. A typical tubesheet thickness for a 500 mm shell with design pressure of 10 bar would be: $$ t_s = \frac{D_s}{2} \sqrt{\frac{0.4 P}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}} $$ This is the **ASME Section VIII, Div 1 UG-55(g)** formula. Let us derive and apply it. **Step 1: Write the design equation for an unbraced circular plate with uniform pressure** $$ t_s = d \sqrt{\frac{C P}{S E}} $$ where: - $d$ = effective diameter of the gasket face - $C$ = a constant depending on edge fixing (0.4 for tubesheet welded to shell) - $P$ = design pressure - $S$ = allowable stress - $E$ = joint efficiency **Step 2: Substitute the commonly used form** For a tubesheet welded to the shell flange: $$ t_s = d \sqrt{\frac{0.4 P}{S E}} $$ **Step 3: Numerical example** Given $D_s = 500$ mm, $P = 1.0$ MPa, $S = 137$ MPa (SA 240 Gr 304 at 200°C), $E = 1.0$: $$ t_s = 500 \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 1.0}{137 \times 1.0}} = 500 \sqrt{0.00292} = 500 \times 0.0540 = 27.0 \text{ mm} $$ Adopt $t_s = 30$ mm (rounded up). **Step 4: Effective tube length** If the total tube length is $L = 3$ m: $$ L_{eff} = 3 - 2(0.030) = 2.94 \text{ m} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Outside Heat Transfer Area The reference area used for the overall heat transfer coefficient is conventionally the **outside tube area**: $$ A_o = N_T \pi d_o L_{eff} $$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. The heat transfer rate equation is $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$. 2. TEMA standardises $A$ to be based on $d_o$ because the outside area is the actual installed surface, and tube bundles are quoted by their outside area. 3. Each tube contributes $\pi d_o L_{eff}$ area. 4. With $N_T$ tubes, multiply. **Worked example (continuing Section 3.1):** With $N_T = 200$, $d_o = 19.05$ mm, $L_{eff} = 2.94$ m: $$ A_o = 200 \times \pi \times 0.01905 \times 2.94 $$ $$ A_o = 200 \times 3.1416 \times 0.01905 \times 2.94 $$ $$ A_o = 200 \times 0.1759 = 35.18 \text{ m}^2 $$ > [!NOTE] > This $A_o$ will be used in Module 2 (LMTD method) and Module 3 (effectiveness-NTU method) to size the exchanger. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of STHE Type | Criterion | Fixed Tube Sheet | U-Tube | Floating Head | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | Thermal expansion handling | Poor (needs bellows) | Excellent | Excellent | | Mechanical cleaning of shell | Not possible | Not possible | Possible | | Mechanical cleaning of tubes | Possible (straight tubes) | Not possible (curved) | Possible | | Bundle removal | Not possible | Possible | Possible | | Operating pressure | Low–Medium | High | High | | Operating temperature | Low–Medium | High | High | | Recommended service | Clean liquids, low ΔT | High pressure gas, high ΔT | Fouling, dirty service | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 weightage:** Selection justification of STHE type based on fluid properties, pressure, temperature, and fouling tendency is a **3-mark direct question** and a **7-mark sub-part** in 14-mark problems. ### 3.5 Conversion Factors (Must Memorize for KTU Exam) | Quantity | Conversion | |:---|:---| | 1 inch | 25.4 mm | | 1 ft | 0.3048 m | | 1 bar | 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa | | 1 kcal/h | 1.163 W | | 1 BTU/(h·ft²·°F) | 5.678 W/(m²·K) | | 1 poise | 0.1 Pa·s | | 1 stokes | 10⁻⁴ m²/s | | 1 ton of refrigeration | 3.517 kW | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger The following Mermaid diagram maps the constructional architecture and flow interaction of a STHE. The diagram follows the safe identifier rule (alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords) and uses clean uppercase alphanumeric labels. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef shell fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef tube fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef support fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef fluid fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef nozzle fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 inletHot[("HOT FLUID INLET
Tube Side Nozzle T1")]:::nozzle inletCold[("COLD FLUID INLET
Shell Side Nozzle S1")]:::nozzle outletHot[("HOT FLUID OUTLET
Tube Side Nozzle T2")]:::nozzle outletCold[("COLD FLUID OUTLET
Shell Side Nozzle S2")]:::nozzle channel1["FRONT CHANNEL
Head with Pass Partitions
2 Tube Side Passes"]:::support tubesheet1["FRONT TUBESHEET
Welded / Expanded Joint"]:::support tubeBundle["TUBE BUNDLE
Straight or U Tubes
Material SS 304 / CS / Cu-Ni"]:::tube shellWall["SHELL
Cylindrical Pressure Vessel
Confines Shell Side Fluid"]:::shell baffleA["SEGMENTAL BAFFLE 1
25 percent Cut"]:::support baffleB["SEGMENTAL BAFFLE 2
25 percent Cut"]:::support baffleC["SEGMENTAL BAFFLE 3
25 percent Cut"]:::support baffleD["SEGMENTAL BAFFLE 4
25 percent Cut"]:::support baffleE["SEGMENTAL BAFFLE 5
25 percent Cut"]:::support tubesheet2["REAR TUBESHEET

For Hydrotest and Operation"]:::nozzle inletHot --> channel1 channel1 --> tubesheet1 tubesheet1 --> tubeBundle tubeBundle --> tubesheet2 tubesheet2 --> channel2 channel2 --> outletHot inletCold --> impinge impinge --> shellWall shellWall --> baffleA baffleA --> baffleB baffleB --> baffleC baffleC --> baffleD baffleD --> baffleE baffleE --> outletCold tRods -.-> baffleA tRods -.-> baffleB tRods -.-> baffleC tRods -.-> baffleD tRods -.-> baffleE ventDrain -.-> shellWall ``` ### 4.2 TEMA-Type Selection Decision Tree The following flowchart guides the **KTU exam answer for type selection** — a recurring question in Part A and Part B. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef step fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decision fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef result fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 startA["START: Identify Service Conditions
Pressure, Temperature, Fouling, Cleanability"]:::step q1{"Fouling Tendency

of Shell Required"}:::decision q4{"Operating Pressure
Above 70 bar"}:::decision res1["FIXED TUBE SHEET

Compact, High Area
Pitch 1.25 do"]:::layout a2["ROTATED SQUARE 45 DEG
Good Cleanability

Pitch 1.5 do"]:::layout a4["ROTATED TRIANGULAR 60 DEG
