Pitch 1.25 do"]:::layout a1 --> a2 a2 --> a3 a3 --> a4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why no physical drawings?** Physical STHE drawings require 3D rendering. The Mermaid functional architecture block diagrams above clearly show (a) how the parts interact, (b) the flow path of both fluids, and (c) the decision logic for type selection. This is sufficient for the KTU Module-1 constructional-features section. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q1. List any six major components of a shell and tube heat exchanger and state the function of each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Shell** – outer pressure vessel that confines the shell-side fluid and houses the tube bundle. 2. **Tubes** – provide the heat transfer surface; one fluid flows inside, the other outside. 3. **Tubesheets** – thick plates that hold the tubes and separate the two fluid streams. 4. **Baffles** – support the tubes, increase turbulence, and direct cross-flow of the shell-side fluid. 5. **Channels (Heads)** – end covers for tube-side fluid inlet/outlet. 6. **Nozzles** – pipe connections for both fluid streams to enter and leave the exchanger. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2. What is the difference between a fixed tube-sheet and a U-tube heat exchanger? When is each preferred? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Fixed Tube-Sheet | U-Tube | |:---|:---|:---| | Tube shape | Straight | Bent in U | | Thermal expansion | Cannot expand freely; needs bellows | U-bend absorbs expansion | | Bundle removal | Not possible | Possible | | Cost | Lowest | Moderate | | Shell cleaning | Not possible | Not possible | | Tube cleaning | Possible | Not possible (curved) | **Preferred:** Fixed tube-sheet for **clean service** with **low ΔT**; U-tube for **high pressure, high temperature, dirty service on the shell side**, and when **differential thermal expansion is large**. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Dec 2023]** > **Q3 (a). With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a shell and tube heat exchanger. List the major parts and explain the function of each. (7 Marks)** > > **Q3 (b). A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to be designed with the following data: heat duty 800 kW, hot fluid inlet/outlet = 90/60 °C, cold fluid inlet/outlet = 30/50 °C, overall U = 500 W/m²·K. Calculate the LMTD and the required heat transfer area. State any assumptions made. (7 Marks)** --- #### **Question 3(a) Model Solution (7 Marks)** 1. **Construction (3 marks):** A STHE consists of a cylindrical shell fitted with two end channels connected by a tube bundle. The tubes are held in tubesheets. Baffles are placed inside the shell to support the tubes and force cross-flow of the shell-side fluid. Pass partitions in the channels divide the tube side into multiple passes. Nozzles are provided for the inlet and outlet of both fluids. 2. **Working (2 marks):** The hot fluid flows through the tubes (tube-side), while the cold fluid flows through the shell, across the tube bundle. Heat transfers from the hot fluid through the tube wall to the cold fluid. Baffles force the shell-side fluid to flow perpendicular to the tubes, increasing turbulence and heat transfer coefficient. 3. **Parts and functions (2 marks):** Shell, tubes, tubesheets, baffles, channels, nozzles, pass partitions, impingement plate, tie rods — all explained (refer Section 2.1). #### **Question 3(b) Model Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $Q = 800$ kW = $800{,}000$ W; $U = 500$ W/m²·K. **Step 1: Hot fluid:** $T_1 = 90$ °C, $T_2 = 60$ °C → $\Delta T_h = 30$ °C. **Step 2: Cold fluid:** $t_1 = 30$ °C, $t_2 = 50$ °C → $\Delta T_c = 20$ °C. **Step 3: Calculate LMTD.** $$ \Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 90 - 50 = 40 \text{ °C} $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ °C} $$ $$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{40 - 30}{\ln(40/30)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.333)} = \frac{10}{0.2877} = 34.76 \text{ °C} $$ **Step 4: Apply correction factor.** For 1-2 STHE with $P = (t_2 - t_1)/(T_1 - t_1) = (50-30)/(90-30) = 0.333$ and $R = (T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1) = (90-60)/(50-30) = 1.5$. From LMTD correction factor charts, $F = 0.94$ (assume as the problem does not ask for the chart reading explicitly, accept $F = 1$ unless mentioned). **Step 5: Area calculation.** $$ A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{800{,}000}{500 \times 34.76} = \frac{800{,}000}{17{,}380} = 46.04 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Final Answer:** $A \approx 46$ m². Use $F = 0.94$ to get corrected area $A_c = A/F = 49$ m². > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to apply the **LMTD correction factor $F$** for multi-pass exchangers. Forgetting $F$ yields an **under-designed exchanger** (too small area). The KTU examiner will deduct **2 marks** if $F$ is omitted. Always check the flow arrangement (1-2, 2-4) and use the Bowman-Fairbanks chart or the analytical expression for $F$ given in Kern's textbook. --- ### 5.3 ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, May 2024]** > **Q4 (a). Explain the TEMA classification of shell and tube heat exchangers. With a labelled sketch, describe the construction of a U-tube heat exchanger. (7 Marks)** > > **Q4 (b). In a shell and tube heat exchanger, the shell inside diameter is 500 mm, the tube outside diameter is 19.05 mm, and a triangular pitch of 1.25 times the tube diameter is used. Determine the number of tubes that can be accommodated in the shell. (7 Marks)** #### **Question 4(a) Model Solution (7 Marks)** 1. **TEMA classification (3 marks):** TEMA designates STHEs by three letters: front-end type, shell type, rear-end type. Front ends are **T** (removable bundle), **U** (U-tube), **S** (fixed tubesheet). Shells are **E** (one pass) or **F** (two pass). Rear ends are **A**, **M**, **U**, **W**, **X**, **P**, **D**, **S**. For example, **TEMA BEM** denotes a bonnet-rear-end, E-shell, M-channel exchanger. 2. **U-tube construction (4 marks):** - The tubesheet at one end is welded to the shell and channel; the U-bend is a single 180° return. - The bundle is removable. - The shell-side flow is directed by segmental baffles. - The bundle is not partitioned; the shell side is single-pass. - Differential thermal expansion is absorbed by the U-bend. #### **Question 4(b) Model Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $D_s = 500$ mm, $d_o = 19.05$ mm, triangular pitch with $P_T / d_o = 1.25$. **Step 1: Compute the pitch.** $$ P_T = 1.25 \times 19.05 = 23.8125 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2: Look up the tube count constants for triangular pitch, 1.25 ratio.** From the table: $K_1 = 0.249$ and $n_t = 2.207$. **Step 3: Apply the tube count formula.** $$ N_T = K_1 \left( \frac{D_s}{P_T} \right)^{n_t} $$ $$ \frac{D_s}{P_T} = \frac{500}{23.8125} = 21.0 $$ $$ N_T = 0.249 \times (21.0)^{2.207} $$ $$ (21.0)^{2.207} = e^{2.207 \ln 21.0} = e^{2.207 \times 3.0445} = e^{6.717} = 826.0 $$ $$ N_T = 0.249 \times 826.0 = 205.7 $$ **Step 4: Round down to the nearest whole tube count, considering tube layout practicalities.** $$ \boxed{N_T \approx 200 \text{ tubes (typical for a 500 mm shell, 19.05 mm tubes, triangular pitch)}} $$ **Practical check:** Always consult the TEMA standard tube count table for the exact number; the value calculated here is an estimate from the Kern correlation. Actual TEMA tables give 202 tubes for a 508 mm shell, 19.05 mm tubes, 23.81 mm triangular pitch. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common errors in tube count problems: > 1. Using the wrong $K_1$ and $n_t$ — always verify the layout (triangular vs. square) and the pitch ratio before reading values. > 2. Forgetting to convert all dimensions to **the same units** (mm or m). Mixing units is the #1 source of KTU mark loss. > 3. For multi-pass exchangers, the actual number of tubes in operation must be a **multiple of the number of passes** (e.g., 2N for 2-pass). If $N_T$ is not an even multiple, increase $D_s$ and re-iterate. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 — Constructional Features of Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger: MUST-KNOW POINTS** - ✅ STHE = **shell + tube bundle + tubesheets + baffles + channels + nozzles** — name all six major parts in exam answers. - ✅ **TEMA classification:** Memorise the three-letter code (T-U-S for front, E-F for shell, A-M-U-W-X-P-D-S for rear). The most commonly used TEMA types in industry: **AES, BEM, AEL, BEU, BEW**. - ✅ **Fixed tube sheet** = lowest cost, no bundle removal, requires expansion bellows for large ΔT. - ✅ **U-tube** = handles thermal expansion, bundle removable, cannot clean U-bend mechanically. - ✅ **Floating head** = highest cost, full cleanability of both sides, used in dirty/fouling service. - ✅ **Tube pitch** = 1.25 × $d_o$ (triangular) or 1.5 × $d_o$ (square). - ✅ **Standard tube OD:** 6.35, 9.53, 12.7, 19.05, 25.4, 38.1 mm. - ✅ **Baffle cut:** 20–25% for optimum, 20–45% acceptable, <20% or >45% problematic. - ✅ **Tubesheet thickness** is governed by **ASME Section VIII, Div 1, UG-55** (unbraced plate formula with constant 0.4 for welded joint). - ✅ **Shell diameter** is calculated from the tube count using **Kern's correlation** with tabulated $K_1$ and $n_t$. - ✅ **Heat transfer area** $A_o = N_T \pi d_o L_{eff}$, based on outside tube diameter. - ✅ **LMTD correction factor $F$** must be applied for multi-pass arrangements; do not assume $F = 1$. - ✅ **Materials of construction:** Carbon steel (CS) for shell, SS 304/316 for tubes in corrosive service, cupro-nickel for seawater, titanium for chlorides, Hastelloy for strong acids. - ✅ **TEMA T-E-A, U-E-A, S-E-A** are the three fundamental single-pass configurations to draw in the exam. - ✅ **Number of tube-side passes must be even** (1, 2, 4, 6, 8) for flow continuity. - ✅ **Impingement plate** is mandatory when shell-side fluid contains abrasive particles or where the inlet velocity exceeds a critical value to prevent tube erosion. - ✅ **Tie rods** and **spacers** hold the baffle assembly rigid; without them, baffles would float. - ✅ **Conversion factors:** 1 inch = 25.4 mm, 1 bar = 100 kPa, 1 kcal/h = 1.163 W.

topics.title: Baffles and Pass Partition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Baffles and Pass Partition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Baffles — The Formal Definition

In a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger (STHE)**, **baffles** are internal plate-like structures installed inside the shell, perpendicular to the tube axis, with precisely cut openings that allow the tubes to pass through them. They are welded or held by tie rods and spacers to the shell's internal surface.

Their principal functions are:

1. **Mechanical Support:** They hold the tube bundle in position, preventing tube sagging, vibration, and damage caused by fluid-induced tube resonance (a phenomenon called **fluid-elastic instability**).
2. **Flow Direction Modification:** They force the shell-side fluid to flow **across the tube bank** in a zigzag (sinusoidal) pattern, rather than flowing parallel to the tubes. This dramatically increases the shell-side heat transfer coefficient.
3. **Velocity Augmentation:** By reducing the free-flow area between adjacent baffles, they increase the shell-side fluid velocity, which improves the heat transfer coefficient (h_s) and reduces fouling.
4. **Tube Bundle Stabilization:** They prevent flow-induced tube vibration that could lead to fatigue failure of the tubes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Syllabus Terminology):**
> *"Baffles are segmented plates inserted into the shell of a shell-and-tube exchanger to support the tubes, to direct the shell-side fluid into a cross-flow pattern across the tube bundle, and to increase the turbulence and effective heat transfer coefficient on the shell side."*

## 1.2 Pass Partition — The Formal Definition

A **pass partition** (also called a **partition plate** or **pass divider**) is a flat metal plate welded longitudinally along the inside of the shell or inside the channel/bonnet, which divides the flow area into a number of parallel flow paths called **passes**.

- **Tube-side pass partition:** Located inside the **channel/bonnet** (not the shell). It forces the tube-side fluid to make U-turns at the headers, creating multiple tube passes (1-2, 1-4, 1-6, 1-8 passes).
- **Shell-side pass partition:** Located inside the **shell**, running longitudinally parallel to the tubes. It creates multiple shell-side passes by splitting the shell into compartments (e.g., 2-shell-pass, divided-flow, or split-flow designs).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Crowd-Control Rails in a Subway Station**
>
> Imagine a crowded subway platform with people trying to walk from one end to the other. Without any guidance, they bunch up, push chaotically, and waste energy. Now install **baffle rails** (the metal barriers) at intervals across the platform — people are forced to walk in organized zigzag lanes rather than a single chaotic mass. They move faster (higher velocity), get distributed evenly, and reach their destination more efficiently.
>
> - The **baffle** = the crowd-control rail
> - The **shell-side fluid** = the commuters
> - The **tubes** = the escalators they must pass by
> - **Cross-flow contact** = each commuter brushes past many escalators, exchanging "heat" (or in our case, thermal energy) with each
>
> Similarly, a **pass partition** is like a dividing wall in a hallway that forces you to walk down one side, U-turn at the end, and walk back on the other side. This is how the fluid makes **multiple passes** to extract more heat.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Maximum allowable unsupported tube span** for steel tubes: typically **50 × tube outer diameter (d_o)** for vulnerable alloys like **aluminum** or **copper alloys** (per **TEMA** standards).
- **Standard baffle cut (%):** **20% to 45%** (with 25% being the most common default).
- **Minimum baffle spacing:** $\dfrac{D_s}{5}$ or **2 inches (50.8 mm)**, whichever is greater.
- **Maximum baffle spacing:** $D_s$ (the shell inside diameter).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Baffles

Baffles are classified primarily by their geometric cut pattern:

### (a) Segmental Baffles (Most Common — Used in 90% of Industrial STHEs)

A **segmental baffle** is created by cutting a segment off a circular disc of diameter slightly less than the shell inner diameter $D_s$. The cut can be:

- **Horizontal segmental cut** (cut from top/bottom) → flow direction is horizontal across the tube bundle.
- **Vertical segmental cut** (cut from side) → used in **vertical** heat exchangers.

The **baffle cut** is expressed as a percentage:

$$B_c \, (\%) = \dfrac{h_{cut}}{D_s} \times 100$$

where $h_{cut}$ is the height of the segment removed and $D_s$ is the shell inside diameter.

**Baffle cut rules (TEMA):**
- $B_c < 20\%$ → Very low cut, induces high velocity but very high pressure drop (rarely used).
- $B_c = 20\% \text{ to } 30\%$ → Standard for general service.
- $B_c = 45\%$ → Maximum recommended (otherwise "dead zones" of stagnant fluid form in the cross-flow window).
- $B_c > 45\%$ → **NOT recommended**; forms low-velocity stagnant zones → severe fouling.

### (b) Disc-and-Donut Baffle

Alternating **disc** (solid circular plate) and **donut** (annular ring) baffles. Used for **high-pressure** services with dirty/process fluids. Provides good support but induces complex flow patterns.

### (c) Orifice Baffle

A plate with a single large circular or rectangular hole in the center. The flow is forced through the orifice, creating high local velocity and turbulence. Used in **low-fouling** applications.

### (d) Rod Baffles

A series of parallel rods arranged in a criss-cross lattice replacing solid plates. Used in **low-pressure-drop** applications and to reduce **vibration-induced tube failure**.

## 2.2 Baffle Spacing ($L_B$)

The **baffle spacing** $L_B$ is the center-to-center distance between two adjacent baffles. It determines:

- The cross-flow velocity of the shell-side fluid
- The unsupported tube span (mechanical integrity)
- The shell-side pressure drop
- The heat transfer coefficient

**Step-by-step logic for baffle spacing selection:**

1. Compute the shell-side mass flow rate $\dot{m}_s$ and physical properties ($\rho_s$, $\mu_s$).
2. Estimate the **optimum shell-side velocity** $u_{s,opt}$ (typically **0.5 to 1.5 m/s** for liquids; **5 to 30 m/s** for gases).
3. Calculate the **cross-flow area** $A_s$ between two baffles.
4. Solve for $L_B$ from the cross-flow area equation.
5. Verify mechanical and pressure-drop constraints.

## 2.3 Pass Partition Arrangements

The **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** letter coding system describes shell-side flow:

| Code | Configuration | Description |
|:-----|:--------------|:------------|
| **TEMA E** | One-pass shell | Single shell-side pass, cross-flow |
| **TEMA F** | Two-pass shell | Longitudinal partition creating 2 shell passes |
| **TEMA G** | Split flow | Shell divided into two halves longitudinally |
| **TEMA H** | Double split flow | Three longitudinal partitions |
| **TEMA J** | Divided flow | Inlet and outlet on same shell side, opposite ends of central partition |
| **TEMA K** | Kettle reboiler | Single shell with enlarged section for vapor-liquid separation |
| **TEMA X** | Cross flow | Pure cross-flow with no baffles in window zones |

Tube-side passes are denoted numerically: **1-2** (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes), **2-4**, **1-4**, **2-8**, etc.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **highest-yield** formula set for KTU 2024 Board Exams on baffle and pass partition design.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $B_c = \dfrac{h_{cut}}{D_s} \times 100$ | Baffle cut percentage | \% |
| 2 | $A_s = \bigl(D_s - N_{t,d} \cdot d_o \bigr) \cdot L_B$ | Approximate cross-flow area between baffles (where $N_{t,d}$ is the number of tubes in the diameter row of the central tube plane) | m² |
| 3 | $u_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s \cdot A_s}$ | Shell-side cross-flow velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $G_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{A_s}$ | Shell-side mass flux | kg/(s·m²) |
| 5 | $L_{B,min} = \max \left( \dfrac{D_s}{5}, \, 2 \text{ in} \right)$ | Minimum baffle spacing (TEMA) | m |
| 6 | $L_{B,max} = D_s$ | Maximum baffle spacing (TEMA) | m |
| 7 | $L_{B,opt} = 0.4 \, D_s \text{ to } 0.6 \, D_s$ | Optimum baffle spacing for heat transfer | m |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{s} = f \cdot \dfrac{G_s^2 \cdot (N_{B}+1) \cdot D_s}{2 \rho_s}$ | Shell-side pressure drop (Kern's method) | Pa |
| 9 | $D_e = \dfrac{4 \cdot [P_T^2 - (\pi d_o^2 / 4)]}{\pi d_o / 2}$ | Equivalent diameter for square pitch | m |
| 10 | $N_B = \dfrac{L_{tube,eff}}{L_B} - 1$ | Number of baffles in the shell | — |
| 11 | $Re_s = \dfrac{G_s \cdot d_o}{\mu_s}$ | Shell-side Reynolds number (based on tube outer diameter) | — |
| 12 | $\text{Pressure drop correction factor} = F_s$ | Function of baffle cut, used in Kern method | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Crude Preheat Trains:** Multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers with 25% segmental baffles are the workhorses of petroleum refineries.
- **Power Plant Condensers:** Use large-diameter STHEs with cross-flow (TEMA X) or two-pass shells (TEMA F) for steam condensation.
- **LNG & Cryogenic Heat Exchangers:** Brazed-aluminium or wound-spiral exchangers use helical baffles to combat thermal stresses.
- **Chemical Reactors with External Cooling:** Kettle reboilers (TEMA K) use shell-side pass partitions to separate vapor from liquid phases.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cross-Flow Area ($A_s$) for a Single Baffle Window

The **cross-flow area** between two adjacent baffles (also called the "window" area) governs the shell-side velocity and the convective heat transfer coefficient.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem)

- Shell inside diameter: $D_s = 0.6$ m
- Tube outer diameter: $d_o = 0.025$ m (25 mm)
- Tube pitch: $P_T = 0.03175$ m (1.25 × $d_o$, square pitch)
- Number of tubes in the central tube row: $N_{t,d} = 14$
- Baffle spacing (assumed): $L_B = 0.30$ m

### Step 1: Write the Geometric Approximation

The shell-side fluid flows in the **gap between the outer tube limit and the shell wall** (the cross-flow window). The cross-sectional area available for cross-flow is:

$$A_s \approx (D_s - N_{t,d} \cdot d_o) \cdot L_B$$

This approximation assumes the central tube plane is fully populated with tubes in the cross-flow path.

### Step 2: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= (0.6 - 14 \times 0.025) \times 0.30 \\[6pt]
&= (0.6 - 0.35) \times 0.30 \\[6pt]
&= 0.25 \times 0.30 \\[6pt]
A_s &= 0.075 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Shell-Side Velocity

If the shell-side mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_s = 12$ kg/s and shell-side density is $\rho_s = 850$ kg/m³ (oil, for example):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_s &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s \cdot A_s} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{12}{850 \times 0.075} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{12}{63.75} \\[6pt]
u_s &= 0.1882 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering check:** $u_s = 0.19$ m/s is on the **low end** of the typical liquid range (0.5 to 1.5 m/s). The designer would reduce $L_B$ to increase velocity.

### Step 4: Recalculate with Optimized $L_B$

Using $L_{B,opt} = 0.5 \cdot D_s = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.30$ m. The value is acceptable, but the velocity is low. Let us try $L_B = 0.20$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= (0.6 - 0.35) \times 0.20 = 0.05 \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
u_s &= \dfrac{12}{850 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{12}{42.5} = 0.282 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Still on the lower side. With $L_B = 0.15$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= (0.6 - 0.35) \times 0.15 = 0.0375 \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
u_s &= \dfrac{12}{850 \times 0.0375} = 0.376 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verify Baffle Spacing Constraints

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{B,min} &= \max \left( \dfrac{D_s}{5}, \, 2 \text{ in} \right) = \max(0.12 \text{ m}, \, 0.0508 \text{ m}) = 0.12 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
L_{B,max} &= D_s = 0.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $L_B = 0.15$ m satisfies both constraints. Final selection: $L_B = 0.15$ m.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Baffles ($N_B$)

Given the effective tube length (between channel and bonnet) $L_{tube,eff} = 4.0$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_B &= \dfrac{L_{tube,eff}}{L_B} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4.0}{0.15} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= 26.67 - 1 \\[6pt]
N_B &= 25.67 \rightarrow 26 \text{ baffles (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for $N_B$:** The formula $N_B = L_{tube,eff}/L_B - 1$ gives the number of **internal** baffles. Some textbooks (Kern) use $N_B + 1$ as the number of **cross-flow passes**, which appears in the pressure drop formula.

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Diameter for Square Tube Pitch

The shell-side heat transfer and friction factor correlations (Kern, Bell-Delaware) use the **equivalent diameter** $D_e$, which represents the hydraulic diameter of the flow passage between tubes.

### Square Pitch Formula

For a **square** pitch arrangement (tube centers at $P_T$ apart in orthogonal directions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \dfrac{4 \cdot A_{flow}}{P_{wetted}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \cdot [P_T^2 - (\pi d_o^2 / 4)]}{\pi d_o / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation with $P_T = 0.03175$ m, $d_o = 0.025$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T^2 &= (0.03175)^2 = 1.0081 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{4} &= \dfrac{\pi \times (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
P_T^2 - \dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{4} &= 1.0081 \times 10^{-3} - 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 5.172 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
\pi d_o / 2 &= \pi \times 0.025 / 2 = 0.03927 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
D_e &= \dfrac{4 \times 5.172 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03927} = \dfrac{2.0688 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03927} \\[6pt]
D_e &= 0.05268 \text{ m} \approx 52.7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $D_e$ is then used to compute the shell-side Reynolds number $Re_s = G_s \cdot D_e / \mu_s$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Baffle Type Selection

| Application | Recommended Baffle | Baffle Cut | Why |
|:------------|:-------------------|:-----------|:----|
| General liquid-liquid service | Segmental | 25% | Balanced ΔP & h_s |
| High-fouling service (e.g., cooling tower water) | Segmental | 35–45% | Reduces ΔP, allows cleaning |
| Condensing vapor | Segmental | 25% | Standard practice |
| Boiling (kettle) | Segmental or no baffles in vapor zone | 25% | Avoids dryout |
| High-pressure gas | Orifice or rod | 35–45% | Lower ΔP |
| Cryogenic / very low ΔP | Rod baffles | N/A | Helical flow, minimal ΔP |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define baffle cut. Why is a baffle cut of 25% considered optimal in segmental baffle design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **baffle cut** is defined as the ratio of the height of the segment removed from the baffle disc to the shell inside diameter, expressed as a percentage:

$$B_c \, (\%) = \dfrac{h_{cut}}{D_s} \times 100$$

A baffle cut of **25%** is considered optimal because:

1. It provides an excellent balance between **shell-side velocity** (high enough to enhance the heat transfer coefficient) and **shell-side pressure drop** (within allowable limits).
2. It avoids the formation of **stagnant dead zones** in the cross-flow window that would otherwise occur at baffle cuts above 45%.
3. It yields a **tube-side pressure drop** and a **heat transfer coefficient** that are well within the operating envelope for most industrial services.
4. TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) standards recognize 20%–30% as the **standard baffle cut range** for general service.

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):**
- Definition of baffle cut with formula → 1 mark
- Two reasons for 25% optimality → 1 mark
- Reference to TEMA standards → 1 mark

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between tube-side and shell-side pass partitions with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tube-Side Pass Partition | Shell-Side Pass Partition |
|:--------|:-------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Location** | Inside the channel/bonnet header | Inside the shell, longitudinal direction |
| **Geometry** | Flat plate welded across the channel | Flat plate welded along the shell length |
| **Function** | Forces tube-side fluid to reverse direction and make multiple tube passes | Forces shell-side fluid to reverse direction and make multiple shell passes |
| **Configurations** | 1-2, 1-4, 2-4, 2-8 passes | TEMA E (1 shell pass), TEMA F (2 shell passes) |
| **Design Intent** | Increase tube-side velocity and heat transfer coefficient | Approach true counter-current flow; increase LMTD |
| **Example** | A 1-2 STHE has 1 shell pass and 2 tube passes | A 2-4 STHE has 2 shell passes and 4 tube passes |

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):**
- Any 3 correct distinguishing points with examples → 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to be designed to cool oil (shell side) using cooling water (tube side). The following data are given:**

- Shell inside diameter $D_s = 540$ mm
- Tube outer diameter $d_o = 25$ mm
- Tube pitch (triangular pitch 30°): $P_T = 31.75$ mm
- Number of tubes $N_t = 120$
- Shell-side mass flow rate $\dot{m}_s = 18$ kg/s
- Shell-side density $\rho_s = 880$ kg/m³
- Effective tube length $L_{tube,eff} = 3.0$ m
- Baffle cut $B_c = 25\%$

**(a)** Calculate the **optimum baffle spacing** $L_B$ and the **number of baffles** $N_B$ in the shell, using Kern's approximation. Justify whether your selection satisfies TEMA mechanical constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **shell-side cross-flow velocity** and the **equivalent diameter** for a 30° triangular pitch. Comment on whether the velocity is acceptable for liquid service. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Optimum Baffle Spacing Criterion (Kern's Method)**

For an **optimum heat transfer** design with reasonable pressure drop, Kern's method suggests:

$$L_{B,opt} = 0.5 \, D_s$$

$$
L_{B,opt} = 0.5 \times 0.540 = 0.270 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Verify TEMA Mechanical Constraints**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{B,min} &= \max \left( \dfrac{D_s}{5}, \, 2 \text{ in} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \max \left( \dfrac{0.540}{5}, \, 0.0508 \right) \\[6pt]
&= \max \left( 0.108 \text{ m}, \, 0.0508 \text{ m} \right) = 0.108 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
L_{B,max} = D_s = 0.540 \text{ m}
$$

Since $0.108 \le 0.270 \le 0.540$, the **TEMA constraints are satisfied**. ✓

**Step 3: Number of Baffles**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_B &= \dfrac{L_{tube,eff}}{L_B} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3.0}{0.270} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= 11.11 - 1 \\[6pt]
N_B &= 10.11 \rightarrow 11 \text{ baffles (round up to integer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer for Part (a):**
- $L_B = 0.270$ m (270 mm)
- $N_B = 11$ baffles
- TEMA mechanical constraints are satisfied.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Cross-Flow Area (Kern's Approximation)**

Number of tubes in the central diameter row $N_{t,d}$ is estimated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{t,d} &\approx \dfrac{D_s}{P_T} = \dfrac{0.540}{0.03175} = 17.0 \text{ (round to 17 tubes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-flow area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= (D_s - N_{t,d} \cdot d_o) \cdot L_B \\[6pt]
&= (0.540 - 17 \times 0.025) \times 0.270 \\[6pt]
&= (0.540 - 0.425) \times 0.270 \\[6pt]
A_s &= 0.115 \times 0.270 = 0.03105 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Shell-Side Cross-Flow Velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_s &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s \cdot A_s} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{18}{880 \times 0.03105} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{18}{27.324} \\[6pt]
u_s &= 0.659 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Equivalent Diameter (30° Triangular Pitch)**

For a 30° triangular pitch arrangement, the equivalent diameter is:

$$
D_e = \dfrac{4 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} P_T^2 - \dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{8} \right]}{\dfrac{\pi d_o}{2}}
$$

Calculating each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} P_T^2 &= 0.8660 \times (0.03175)^2 = 0.8660 \times 1.0081 \times 10^{-3} = 8.730 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{8} &= \dfrac{\pi \times (0.025)^2}{8} = 2.454 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Numerator area} &= 8.730 \times 10^{-4} - 2.454 \times 10^{-4} = 6.276 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
\pi d_o / 2 &= 0.03927 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
D_e &= \dfrac{4 \times 6.276 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03927} = \dfrac{2.510 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03927} \\[6pt]
D_e &= 0.0639 \text{ m} \approx 63.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Comment on Velocity**

The shell-side velocity $u_s = 0.659$ m/s falls within the **typical recommended range for liquids (0.5 to 1.5 m/s)**. This is acceptable for oil service. If the calculated value were below 0.3 m/s, the designer would need to reduce $L_B$ to increase the velocity. If it were above 2.0 m/s, the pressure drop would become excessive.

**Final Answer for Part (b):**
- $u_s = 0.659$ m/s (acceptable for liquid service)
- $D_e = 63.9$ mm (for 30° triangular pitch)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**A 1-shell-pass, 2-tube-pass heat exchanger is to be designed. The shell-side fluid is a hydrocarbon vapor and the tube-side fluid is cooling water.**

**(a)** Explain the **functions of baffles** in detail. With the help of neat sketches, describe the construction of **segmental**, **disc-and-donut**, and **orifice** baffles. State two advantages and one disadvantage of segmental baffles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **longitudinal shell-side pass partition** is to be installed in a 1.2 m shell to convert a TEMA E (1-pass shell) into a TEMA F (2-pass shell) configuration. Discuss the design considerations:  
(i) Why is a true 2-4 configuration achieved by combining a 2-pass shell with 4-pass tubes (not by 2-pass shell alone)?  
(ii) What is the LMTD correction factor ($F_T$) implication?  
(iii) Mention two situations where a 2-pass shell is preferred over a 1-pass shell. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Functions of Baffles:**

1. **Support the tubes** against sagging and vibration-induced failure.
2. **Direct the shell-side fluid** into a cross-flow pattern across the tube bundle.
3. **Increase shell-side velocity** in the cross-flow window, thereby increasing the heat transfer coefficient $h_s$.
4. **Reduce fouling** by preventing stagnant zones in the tube bank.
5. **Improve mixing** of the shell-side fluid through repeated directional changes.

**Baffle Types (Neat Sketch Description):**

- **Segmental Baffle:** A circular plate of diameter slightly less than $D_s$, with a chord-cut segment removed. The cut can be horizontal (top/bottom) or vertical (side). **Most common** in industry.
- **Disc-and-Donut Baffle:** Alternating **disc** (solid circular plate fitting against all tubes) and **donut** (annular ring fitting the shell wall) baffles. Used in high-pressure, dirty services.
- **Orifice Baffle:** A plate with a single large circular hole in the center. Creates a high-velocity jet across the tube bank, used for low-fouling applications.

**Segmental Baffles — Advantages and Disadvantages:**

| Advantages | Disadvantage |
|:-----------|:-------------|
| Simple and inexpensive to manufacture | High shell-side pressure drop |
| Easy to install and replace | Induces significant shell-side leakage |
| Standard TEMA geometry; well-documented correlations | Vibration of tubes in unsupported spans |

**Marks Distribution (Part a):**
- Functions of baffles (any 4) → 3 marks
- Sketch descriptions of 3 types → 3 marks
- Advantages/disadvantage → 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Why 2-4 Configuration?**

A 2-pass shell alone (TEMA F) is **not sufficient** to achieve true counter-current flow because:

- The shell-side flow is divided into 2 passes, but the tube-side flow may still be in 1 pass (parallel flow with the first shell pass → co-current, then opposite with the second → counter-current).
- The **2-4 configuration** (2 shell passes + 4 tube passes) provides **true mechanical counter-current** arrangement. The cold fluid flows opposite to the hot fluid in each of the 4 tube passes, maximizing the **log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)**.
- It also increases the **tube-side velocity** (because the same total mass flow is distributed over fewer tubes per pass), boosting the tube-side $h_i$.

**(ii) LMTD Correction Factor $F_T$ Implication:**

- For 1 shell pass with multiple tube passes (e.g., 1-2, 1-4), the LMTD correction factor $F_T$ is **less than 1.0**, often significantly so when the temperature crosses are wide.
- Adding a second shell pass (2-4) brings $F_T$ **closer to 1.0** because each shell pass experiences a smaller temperature cross.
- $F_T$ approaching 1.0 means **higher effective LMTD** → less heat transfer area required → lower capital cost.

**(iii) Two Situations Where 2-Pass Shell is Preferred:**

1. **Wide Temperature Cross Applications:** When the hot fluid inlet temperature is close to the cold fluid outlet temperature (or vice versa), a 1-pass shell with multiple tube passes yields a very low $F_T$ (< 0.75). A 2-pass shell corrects this.
2. **High Heat Recovery Targets:** In feedwater heaters and refinery preheat trains, the design target is to **approach the pinch temperature**. A 2-pass shell allows LMTD correction to approach 1.0, making the design thermodynamically efficient.

**Marks Distribution (Part b):**
- (i) Explanation of 2-4 with reasons → 2 marks
- (ii) $F_T$ implication with logic → 3 marks
- (iii) Two situations → 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$ in the baffle count formula:** Students often write $N_B = L_{tube,eff}/L_B$ (without $-1$). The correct formula is $N_B = L_{tube,eff}/L_B - 1$. This mistake costs 1 full mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing tube-side and shell-side pass partitions:** Tube-side partitions are in the **channel/bonnet**; shell-side partitions are in the **shell**. Mixing these up loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Using Bc > 45%:** Examiners will deduct marks if you propose a baffle cut > 45%. Always justify your choice with TEMA standard ranges.
>
> 4. **Using $D_s$ directly as $D_e$:** The equivalent diameter is **NOT** the shell diameter. Use the correct formula with $P_T$ and $d_o$. A common error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring dimensional units:** Always write $L_B$ in metres, not mm, in formulas. Examiners are strict about unit consistency.
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting "1-2" notation:** "1-2" means **1 shell pass, 2 tube passes**, NOT 12 passes. This is a frequent conceptual error.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to round up $N_B$:** $N_B$ must always be an **integer**; round UP to ensure the baffle spacing does not exceed $L_{B,max}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Baffles and Pass Partition)**

- **Baffle:** Internal plate in the shell that supports tubes, increases turbulence, and directs cross-flow.
- **Pass Partition:** Plate in the channel/bonnet (tube-side) or shell (shell-side) that creates multiple fluid passes.
- **Baffle Cut (Bc):** $B_c = h_{cut}/D_s \times 100$. Standard range: **20%–45%**, optimal: **25%**.
- **Baffle Spacing (LB):** Min = $D_s/5$ or 2 inches; Max = $D_s$; Optimum = 0.4 to 0.6 $D_s$.
- **Number of Baffles (NB):** $N_B = L_{tube,eff}/L_B - 1$ (always round up).
- **Cross-Flow Area (As):** $A_s = (D_s - N_{t,d} \cdot d_o) \cdot L_B$.
- **Shell-Side Velocity (us):** $u_s = \dot{m}_s / (\rho_s \cdot A_s)$. Typical liquids: 0.5 to 1.5 m/s.
- **Equivalent Diameter (De):** Square pitch: $D_e = 4(P_T^2 - \pi d_o^2/4)/(\pi d_o/2)$; Triangular pitch: similar formula with $\sqrt{3}/2$ factor.
- **TEMA Configurations:** E (1-pass), F (2-pass), G (split), H (double split), J (divided), K (kettle), X (cross).
- **Notation "1-2":** Means 1 shell pass and 2 tube passes (not 12).
- **Tube-Side Pass Partition:** Located in the **channel/bonnet header**.
- **Shell-Side Pass Partition:** Located in the **shell**, runs longitudinally.
- **Standard TEMA Limits:** $B_c \le 45\%$, $L_B \le D_s$, $L_B \ge D_s/5$.
- **Mechanical Rule:** $L_B$ should be ≤ 50 $d_o$ for steel tubes (vulnerable alloys have lower limits).
- **True Counter-Current:** Achieved by **2-pass shell + multi-pass tubes** (e.g., 2-4 or 2-6).
- **LMTD Correction Factor ($F_T$):** Closer to 1.0 for 2-pass shells, maximizing effective LMTD.
- **Most Common Industrial Baffle:** Segmental baffle, 25% cut.
- **Vibration Risk:** Large $L_B$ leads to tube sagging and flow-induced vibration; use rod baffles if needed.
- **Baffle Leakage:** Always accounted for by the $F_s$ correction factor in Kern's pressure drop formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TEMA Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** TEMA Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TEMA Nomenclature** refers to the standardized system of designation, classification, and terminology developed by the **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** for **shell-and-tube heat exchangers**. It provides a universal "engineering language" to describe the mechanical construction, front head, shell type, and rear head configuration of any heat exchanger using a **three-letter alphanumeric code** (e.g., **AEL**, **BEM**, **CFU**).

The standard was first published in **1941** (TEMA, 10th Edition, 2018) and is internationally recognized as the benchmark for petrochemical, chemical, and process industry heat exchanger specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBCHT504, Module 1):** Students must be able to **identify, sketch, and describe** each TEMA component, decode the three-letter designation, and select the appropriate **TEMA class (R, C, or B)** based on the severity of service.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine buying a car. You don't describe it as "metal box with four wheels and a trunk." Instead, you use a specific model name like **"Honda City ZX CVT"** — instantly, any mechanic worldwide knows the **body type**, **engine class**, and **transmission variant**.

**TEMA nomenclature works the exact same way for heat exchangers.**

A TEMA code like **"AEM"** tells any engineer in the world:
- **A** → Removable channel (bonnet) at the **front**
- **E** → **One-pass shell**
- **M** → Fixed tubesheet at the **rear** (with bonnet)

So instead of a 500-word description, a single three-letter code conveys complete mechanical construction.

---

## 1.3 TEMA Service Classes – The Three Tiers

TEMA classifies heat exchangers into **three service classes** based on operating severity:

| Class | Service Type | Application Severity |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Class R** | Petroleum & Related Processing | **Most Severe** – High pressure, flammable, toxic |
| **Class C** | Commercial & General Process | **Moderate** – Standard industrial use |
| **Class B** | Chemical Process Service | **Least Severe** – Light-duty chemical service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Standard Metric:** TEMA's nominal diameter notation uses the **diameter in inches**, and design pressures are typically rated in **psig** (pounds per square inch gauge) in the base standard, though SI conversions are universally applied in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.4 Universal TEMA Three-Letter Designation

$$
\boxed{\text{TEMA Code} = \underbrace{X_1}_{\text{Front Head}} - \underbrace{X_2}_{\text{Shell Type}} - \underbrace{X_3}_{\text{Rear Head}}}
$$

**Example:** **BEM** ⇒ Bonnet (front) + One-pass shell + Fixed tubesheet bonnet (rear)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Letter Logic Decoded

Each letter in the TEMA code is **not arbitrary** — it follows a **systematic hierarchy** describing the exchanger from **tube-side inlet → shell-side flow path → tube-side outlet**.

### Step 1: First Letter (X₁) — Front Head / Stationary Head

This is the **inlet** end where the tube-side fluid enters. The fluid is contained in a **channel** or **bonnet**.

| Code | Front Head Type | Mechanical Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **A** | Channel (Removable Bonnet) | Removable cover bolted to a stationary channel barrel |
| **B** | Bonnet (Integral Cover) | Cover is integral with the channel; entire head is removable |
| **C** | Channel Integral with Tubesheet | Tubesheet is welded to the shell; bundle cannot be removed |
| **D** | Special High-Pressure Closure | Forged integral high-pressure design (Class R only) |
| **N** | Channel Integral, Gasketed | Like C, but with a gasket joint (allows cleaning) |

### Step 2: Second Letter (X₂) — Shell Type

Defines the **shell-side flow geometry** — how the shell-side fluid is routed and baffled.

| Code | Shell Type | Flow Path |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **E** | One-Pass Shell | Single shell pass, fluid enters one end, exits the other |
| **F** | Two-Pass Shell | Longitudinal baffle divides the shell into two passes |
| **G** | Split Flow | Inlet splits into two parallel streams |
| **H** | Double Split Flow | Two inlets, two outlets, central nozzle |
| **J** | Divided Flow | Single inlet splits → rejoin → single outlet (boilers) |
| **K** | Kettle Reboiler | Expanded shell for vapor disengagement |
| **X** | Cross Flow | Pure cross-flow (used in U-tube and special applications) |

### Step 3: Third Letter (X₃) — Rear Head / Channel Cover

This is the **outlet** end for the tube-side fluid.

| Code | Rear Head Type | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **L** | Fixed Tubesheet (Like A) | Removable channel, fixed tubesheet |
| **M** | Fixed Tubesheet (Like B) | Fixed tubesheet, removable bonnet |
| **N** | Fixed Tubesheet (Like C/N) | Tubesheet welded to shell, no expansion allowance |
| **P** | Outside Packed Floating Head | Floating head with packing box outside shell |
| **S** | Floating Head with Backing Device | Sealed floating head (no packing exposure) |
| **T** | Pull-Through Floating Head | Floating head sized to pass through shell |
| **U** | U-Tube Bundle | Hairpin tubes, no rear tubesheet |
| **W** | Externally Sealed Floating Head | Floating head with external bellows seal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The TEMA code directly dictates:
> - **Thermal expansion accommodation** (fixed vs. floating tubesheet)
> - **Mechanical cleanability** (removable bundle vs. fixed)
> - **Cost and fabrication complexity** (U-tubes are cheaper, floating heads are expensive)

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Standard / Equation | KTU Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| TEMA Designation | $\text{Code} = X_1X_2X_3$ (3-letter) | Decode/encode any exchanger |
| TEMA Class (R/C/B) | Selected per service severity | Mandatory specification |
| Number of Tube Passes | $N_p = 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 \ldots$ (even) | Heat transfer & pressure drop |
| Shell Diameter Correlation | $D_s \approx f(D_o, \text{pitch}, \text{layout})$ | Mechanical design |
| Shell-Side Velocity | $G_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{A_s}$ | Used in HT and ΔP |
| Tube-Side Velocity | $G_t = \dfrac{\dot{m}_t}{\frac{N_t}{N_p} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2}$ | Used in HT and ΔP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse the TEMA class letters (R, C, B) with the three-letter mechanical designation (A, B, C, D, E, F, …).** They are independent classification systems. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 2.3 Why This System Exists — Real-World Engineering Utility

In a refinery with **10,000+ heat exchangers**, using free-text descriptions would cause:
- Procurement delays
- Fabrication ambiguity
- Costly misinterpretation

TEMA's three-letter code allows an **engineer in Kerala to spec a heat exchanger that a fabricator in Mumbai can build without ambiguity.** This is why TEMA is mandated by:
- **ASME BPVC Section VIII** (boiler and pressure vessel code)
- **API 660** (for refinery applications)
- **IS 4503** (Indian Standard, BIS equivalent)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Systematic Derivation: Decoding a TEMA Code

### Demonstration: Decoding "AEL"

$$
\text{TEMA Code} = X_1X_2X_3 = \mathbf{A\,E\,L}
$$

**Step 1: Decode First Letter — `A`**
- The letter 'A' corresponds to a **Channel (Removable Bonnet)** front head.
- This means the tube-side fluid enters through a **removable cover** bolted to a stationary channel barrel.
- The tubesheet is **clamped between channel and shell flanges** — so the **tube bundle is removable** for cleaning.

**Step 2: Decode Second Letter — `E`**
- The letter 'E' corresponds to a **One-Pass Shell**.
- The shell-side fluid makes **a single pass** from inlet nozzle → outlet nozzle.
- Cross baffles (segmental) are used to direct flow across the tube bundle for higher heat transfer.

**Step 3: Decode Third Letter — `L`**
- The letter 'L' corresponds to a **Fixed Tubesheet rear head** (mechanically analogous to the A-type).
- Tubes are **straight, fixed at both ends** — no thermal expansion flexibility.
- Suitable for **clean service only** (no fouling, no thermal cycling).

**Step 4: Final Classification**
- This is an **AEL-type fixed tubesheet, one-pass shell heat exchanger** — the **simplest and cheapest** configuration in TEMA.

---

### Demonstration: Decoding "BEM"

$$
\text{TEMA Code} = X_1X_2X_3 = \mathbf{B\,E\,M}
$$

**Step 1: `B`** → Bonnet (Integral Cover) at front head. The cover is **welded** to the channel; the entire head is removable as a unit.

**Step 2: `E`** → One-pass shell.

**Step 3: `M`** → Fixed tubesheet at the rear (mechanically analogous to B-type).

**Step 4: Result** → Cheaper than AEL because the cover-channel weld eliminates one gasket joint.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | **AEL** | **AEM** | **BEM** | **CFU** | **AKT** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Front head | Channel (removable) | Channel (removable) | Bonnet (integral) | Channel (integral) | Channel (removable) |
| Shell type | One pass | One pass | One pass | Two pass | One pass (kettle) |
| Rear head | Fixed TS (A) | Fixed TS (B) | Fixed TS (B) | U-tube | Pull-through floating |
| Bundle removable? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Thermal expansion handled? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (U-loop) | ✅ Yes (floating) |
| Typical application | Clean, low-T services | Lightly fouling | General process | Dirty, hot services | Kettle reboilers |
| Relative cost | Low | Low–Medium | Low | Medium | High |

---

## 3.3 Logical Derivation: Selection of TEMA Class (R, C, or B)

The selection follows a **decision-tree logic** based on operating conditions:

$$
\text{Class} = \begin{cases}
\mathbf{R} & \text{if } P > 3000 \text{ psig} \;\; \text{or} \;\; T > 650^\circ\text{F} \;\; \text{or flammable/toxic} \\
\mathbf{C} & \text{if } 150 < P \le 3000 \text{ psig} \;\; \text{or moderate duty} \\
\mathbf{B} & \text{if } P \le 150 \text{ psig} \;\; \text{or low-pressure chemical service}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Justification of thresholds (as per TEMA 9th/10th Edition):**
> - The **R/C boundary at 3000 psig** arises from the practical limit of forged carbon-steel flanges in refinery hydrocracker service.
> - The **B/C boundary at 150 psig** corresponds to standard pipe-flange class ratings.
> - These thresholds are **not universal constants**; they are **service-based conventions** and may be adjusted in project specifications.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Tube Pass Selection Logic

The number of tube passes is derived from the **trade-off between heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop**:

$$
h_t \propto \left(\frac{\dot{m}_t}{N_p}\right)^{0.8} \quad \text{;} \quad \Delta P_t \propto N_p^{1.8} \cdot L
$$

**Logic steps:**
1. Increasing $N_p$ (tube passes) → tube-side velocity $u_t$ rises → $h_t$ increases (good for HT).
2. But $\Delta P_t$ rises **exponentially** with $N_p^{1.8}$ — exceeding allowable $\Delta P$ is the binding constraint.
3. **Standard KTU convention:** Start with $N_p = 2$, then increase to 4 only if heat duty is critically under-met.
4. **Even number rule:** Tube passes are **always even** so that the fluid returns to the front head.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Listing of All TEMA Codes (Examiner's Quick Reference)

$$
\boxed{\text{All possible codes} = \{X_1\} \times \{X_2\} \times \{X_3\} = 5 \times 7 \times 8 = 280 \text{ combinations}}
$$

But **only ~40 combinations are industrially common**. Below is the complete authoritative list:

| Front ($X_1$) | Shell ($X_2$) | Rear ($X_3$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A, B, C, D, N | E, F, G, H, J, K, X | L, M, N, P, S, T, U, W |

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Common Industrial Codes} = \{ &\text{AEL, AEM, AEP, AES, AET, AEU, AEW,} \\
&\text{BEM, BES, BET, BEU,} \\
&\text{CEL, CEM, CES,} \\
&\text{NEN, NEN, NEN,} \\
&\text{AKU, AKT, BKT,} \\
&\text{CFU, BFM, AFS,} \\
&\text{DEM, DEN} \;\}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: TEMA Three-Letter Code Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["TEMA Designation XYZ"] --> nodeB["X1 - Front Head"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["X2 - Shell Type"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["X3 - Rear Head"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["A - Channel Bonnet"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["B - Bonnet Integral"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["C - Channel Integral"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["D - High Pressure"]
    nodeB --> nodeB5["N - Channel Integral Gasketed"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["E - One Pass"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["F - Two Pass"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["G - Split Flow"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["H - Double Split"]
    nodeC --> nodeC5["J - Divided Flow"]
    nodeC --> nodeC6["K - Kettle Reboiler"]
    nodeC --> nodeC7["X - Cross Flow"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["L - Fixed TS Like A"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["M - Fixed TS Like B"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["N - Fixed TS Like C"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["P - Outside Packed"]
    nodeD --> nodeD5["S - Floating Backing"]
    nodeD --> nodeD6["T - Pull Through"]
    nodeD --> nodeD7["U - U Tube"]
    nodeD --> nodeD8["W - External Seal"]

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB
    style nodeC fill:#90EE90
    style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: TEMA Service Class Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["Select Service Class"] --> nodeQ1{"Pressure greater than 3000 psig OR Temperature above 650 F OR Flammable Toxic"}

    nodeQ1 -- "YES" --> nodeR["Class R - Petroleum Refinery"]
    nodeQ1 -- "NO" --> nodeQ2{"Pressure 150 to 3000 psig OR Moderate Duty"}

    nodeQ2 -- "YES" --> nodeC["Class C - Commercial General"]
    nodeQ2 -- "NO" --> nodeB["Class B - Chemical Process"]

    style nodeStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeR fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#FFA500
    style nodeB fill:#90EE90
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functionality Map of TEMA Nomenclature Use

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInput["Process Data: P, T, Fluid, Duty"] --> nodeDecide["Class Selection R C B"]
    nodeDecide --> nodeDesignate["Choose Three Letter Code"]
    nodeDesignate --> nodeFAB["Fabricator Builds Unit"]
    nodeDesignate --> nodeINSP["Inspector Verifies Code"]
    nodeDesignate --> nodePROC["Procurement Standardizes Spec"]

    nodeFAB --> nodeOutput["Heat Exchanger Unit"]
    nodeINSP --> nodeOutput
    nodePROC --> nodeOutput

    style nodeInput fill:#E0FFFF
    style nodeDecide fill:#FFD700
    style nodeDesignate fill:#98FB98
    style nodeOutput fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note on Physical Drawings:** In a printed KTU study resource, each TEMA type (AEL, BEM, CFU, AKT, etc.) would be accompanied by a hand-drawn longitudinal cross-section showing the **tubesheet**, **shell**, **channel cover**, **baffles**, and **tube layout**. These physical schematics are standardized in the TEMA Standards publication and are not reproducible as Mermaid graphs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define TEMA. What are the three TEMA service classes, and how are they selected?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **TEMA** stands for the **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association**, an international trade body that publishes standards for shell-and-tube heat exchangers. The three TEMA service classes are:
> 1. **Class R** – Petroleum refinery and related severe service (P > 3000 psig or T > 650 °F or flammable/toxic fluids).
> 2. **Class C** – Commercial and general process applications (moderate duty).
> 3. **Class B** – Chemical process service (light duty, P < 150 psig).
>
> **Selection logic:** Based on operating **pressure, temperature, and fluid hazard** (toxicity/flammability). Class R is the most stringent; Class B is the least.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Write the full TEMA designation for a heat exchanger with: removable channel front head, one-pass shell, and a fixed tubesheet with integral cover at the rear.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Front head: Removable channel → Letter **A**
- Shell type: One pass → Letter **E**
- Rear head: Fixed tubesheet with integral cover (like B-type) → Letter **M**

$$
\boxed{\text{TEMA Code} = \mathbf{AEM}}
$$

**Brief explanation:** This is a clean-service, low-cost exchanger with a removable bundle, suitable for moderate thermal expansion only.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a)** *Explain, with neat sketches, the TEMA designations for the **front head** and **rear head** of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. List all five front-head and eight rear-head types with their distinguishing features.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A refinery requires a heat exchanger for a hydrocarbon stream at **50 bar (≈ 725 psig)** and **380 °C (≈ 716 °F)**. The fluid is **flammable**. Recommend the appropriate TEMA **service class** and justify. Also, suggest a suitable **TEMA three-letter code** for a unit requiring thermal expansion handling and a removable bundle for periodic cleaning.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Front Head Types ($X_1$):**

| Code | Type | Distinguishing Feature |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **A** | Channel (Removable Bonnet) | Removable flat or dished cover bolted to a channel barrel; tubesheet clamped between channel and shell flanges. Bundle is removable. |
| **B** | Bonnet (Integral Cover) | Cover is integral with the channel; entire head is removed as a unit. Cheaper than A (eliminates one gasket). |
| **C** | Channel Integral with Tubesheet | Tubesheet is welded to the shell. Bundle **cannot** be removed. |
| **D** | Special High-Pressure Closure | Forged, integral high-pressure design, Class R only. |
| **N** | Channel Integral, Gasketed | Like C, but with a gasketed joint for occasional cleaning. |

**Rear Head Types ($X_3$):**

| Code | Type | Distinguishing Feature |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **L** | Fixed TS (like A) | Fixed tubesheet, removable channel cover. No expansion allowance. |
| **M** | Fixed TS (like B) | Fixed tubesheet, removable bonnet. |
| **N** | Fixed TS (like C) | Tubesheet welded to shell — non-removable. |
| **P** | Outside Packed Floating Head | Floating head with packing exposed to atmosphere. Cheapest floating design. |
| **S** | Floating Head with Backing Device | Sealed floating head; no packing exposed. |
| **T** | Pull-Through Floating Head | Floating head can be pulled through the shell for full bundle access. |
| **U** | U-Tube Bundle | Hairpin tubes — natural expansion handling, cheapest floating design, but inner tubes cannot be mechanically cleaned. |
| **W** | Externally Sealed Floating Head | Floating head sealed with external bellows; used in toxic service. |

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
[A-Type Front Head]                  [L/M/N-Type Rear Head]

| Cover | — Channel | TS | Shell | TS | Channel | Cover |
| (gasketed) |  (welded) |  (flange)  |
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Service Class Selection**

Given: P = 50 bar = **725 psig**; T = 380 °C = **716 °F**; fluid is **flammable**.

$$
\text{Checking thresholds: } P < 3000 \text{ psig} \;\; \text{but} \;\; T > 650^\circ\text{F} \;\; \text{and fluid is flammable}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Class R** is selected because the **fluid is flammable**, which is the binding criterion under TEMA rules — flammable service automatically escalates the unit to **Class R** regardless of pressure/temperature.

**Step 2: Three-Letter Code Selection**

Requirements:
- ✅ Thermal expansion handling → **floating head** required (rules out L, M, N)
- ✅ Removable bundle for periodic cleaning → U-tubes cannot be mechanically cleaned → **rules out U**
- ✅ Flammable service → Packed floating head not ideal (packing leak risk) → **S or T recommended**

$$
\boxed{\text{Recommended Code} = \mathbf{AES} \;\;\text{or}\;\; \mathbf{AET}}
$$

**AET** is the most robust: A-type front (removable channel) + E-shell (one pass) + T-rear (pull-through floating head) — full bundle accessibility, full thermal expansion, suitable for Class R flammable service.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *With the help of a clear sketch, explain the construction of an **AKT-type** heat exchanger. Why is this configuration specifically used for **kettle reboilers**?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the TEMA codes **AEL, AEM, BEM, and CFU** in terms of (i) bundle removal capability, (ii) thermal expansion handling, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) relative cost.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**AKT Construction:**

The **AKT** code denotes:
- **A** → Removable channel (bonnet) front head
- **K** → Kettle-type shell (expanded diameter, vapor disengagement space)
- **T** → Pull-through floating head at the rear

**Sketch (textual):**

```
VAPOR OUT
     |
     |____  Expanded Shell (K-type)
     |    \_____
     |          \  Vapor Space
     |           \_____
[Tubesheet A] [Tubes] [Tubesheet T]  [Floating Head]
     |                                    |
   LIQUID IN                          LIQUID OUT
```

**Why AKT for Kettle Reboilers:**

1. The **K-shell** is a **diameter-expanded shell** that acts as an integral vapor-liquid separator (vapor disengagement zone above the tube bundle).
2. The **T-rear (pull-through floating head)** allows **complete bundle withdrawal** for inspection and cleaning — critical because reboiler tubes foul with deposits from boiling.
3. The **A-front** allows the channel cover to be removed for tube-side inspection.
4. Combination of **A + K + T** gives full access to **both ends** of the tube bundle.

**Application:** Used in distillation column reboilers where the liquid boils on the shell side and vapor must be separated from entrained liquid before returning to the column.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

| Comparison Criterion | **AEL** | **AEM** | **BEM** | **CFU** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **(i) Bundle removable?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (U-tube has no rear TS) |
| **(ii) Thermal expansion handled?** | ❌ No (fixed TS) | ❌ No (fixed TS) | ❌ No (fixed TS) | ✅ Yes (U-loop self-compensates) |
| **(iii) Typical application** | Clean, low-T services | Lightly fouling, clean services | General process, low cost | Dirty, hot, high-fouling services |
| **(iv) Relative cost** | **Lowest** | Low | Low | **Moderate–High** |

**Key Engineering Insights:**

- **AEL, AEM, BEM** are all **fixed tubesheet** designs — cheap but **cannot handle thermal expansion** and are limited to **clean services**.
- **CFU** uses a **U-tube bundle** — handles expansion naturally and accommodates **fouling on the shell side**, but the **inside of U-tubes cannot be mechanically cleaned**.
- **AEM vs. BEM:** AEM has a gasketed channel cover (more flanges, easier to open); BEM has a welded bonnet (cheaper, fewer leak paths).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not mix up TEMA Class letters (R, C, B) with mechanical designators (A, B, C, D…).** Writing "Class B" when the question asks for "front-head type" will fetch **zero marks**.
> 2. **Always justify your class selection** with the *binding criterion* (pressure, temperature, OR fluid hazard) — not a vague "because it's hot."
> 3. **Sketch clarity matters:** A neat, labeled TEMA sketch is worth **2–3 marks** by itself. Show tubesheet, channel, shell, cover, and nozzles clearly.
> 4. **For floating-head codes (P, S, T, W), mention the thermal expansion mechanism explicitly** — examiners specifically look for this understanding.
> 5. **The letter "F"** in TEMA means **two-pass shell**, NOT fixed tubesheet. **The letter "M"** means fixed tubesheet (rear), NOT "moving."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- ✅ **TEMA = Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association** (USA, founded 1939, first standard 1941).
- ✅ **Three-letter code structure:** $X_1$ (front head) – $X_2$ (shell) – $X_3$ (rear head).
- ✅ **Five front head types:** A, B, C, D, N.
- ✅ **Seven shell types:** E, F, G, H, J, K, X.
- ✅ **Eight rear head types:** L, M, N, P, S, T, U, W.
- ✅ **Three service classes:** R (severe, refinery), C (commercial, moderate), B (light, chemical).
- ✅ **Fixed tubesheet codes (L, M, N):** No thermal expansion, clean service only, lowest cost.
- ✅ **Floating head codes (P, S, T, W):** Handle thermal expansion, suitable for fouling services.
- ✅ **U-tube code (U):** Handles expansion, but inner U-bend **cannot be mechanically cleaned**.
- ✅ **K-shell (K):** Used for **kettle reboilers**; provides vapor disengagement space.
- ✅ **A-Front + T-Rear (AT):** Most accessible configuration — full bundle pull-through.
- ✅ **Class R is mandatory** for **flammable, toxic, or high-pressure (> 3000 psig) service**, regardless of temperature.
- ✅ **Tube passes are always even** (2, 4, 6, 8…); odd numbers are not used in TEMA design.
- ✅ **The most common KTU exam question:** "Explain the TEMA designation of the following heat exchanger" or "Recommend a TEMA type for service X."
- ✅ **Remember the difference:** TEMA mechanical code (e.g., AEL) ≠ TEMA service class (R, C, B). Both must be specified for procurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing of Fluids and Calculation of Heat Transfer Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Routing of Fluids and Calculation of Heat Transfer Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Routing of Fluids — Definition

**Routing of fluids** in chemical plant design refers to the systematic engineering process of determining the optimal physical path, pipe diameter, pipe material, fittings (elbows, tees, reducers), valves, and supporting hardware through which process fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or two-phase mixtures) are transported between unit operations such as reactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, pumps, compressors, and storage tanks.

The primary engineering deliverables of fluid routing are:

- **Pipe diameter (ID)** selected based on permissible velocity, pressure drop, and capital cost trade-off.
- **Pipe wall thickness (Schedule)** selected based on internal pressure (using ASME B31.3 / B31.1), corrosion allowance, and material strength.
- **Equipment count** — number and type of fittings, valves, and instrumentation.
- **Total pressure drop (ΔP)** to size pumps/compressors.
- **Line list / piping isometric drawings / Plot Plan / Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID).**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Routing of fluids essentially integrates **fluid mechanics** (Bernoulli/Darcy-Weisbach equation), **process economics** (optimum pipe diameter), and **mechanical design** (pipe wall thickness, supports). Mastery of this topic is tested in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.2 Calculation of Heat Transfer Area — Definition

The **heat transfer area** of a heat exchanger is the effective interfacial surface through which thermal energy is exchanged between the hot and cold fluid streams. The required area $A$ is calculated from the fundamental rate equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat duty (W or kW),
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K),
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²),
- $\Delta T_m$ = mean temperature driving force (K), corrected with the LMTD correction factor $F$ when applicable.

The calculation sequence involves: (1) Energy balance to find $Q$, (2) Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD), (3) Overall $U$ from individual film coefficients and fouling resistances, (4) Determination of $A$, and (5) Mechanical sizing of the shell, tube bundle, baffles, and nozzles.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Routing of Fluids — "The City Water Supply Analogy"
Imagine water flowing from an overhead tank (source) to your home (destination). A chemical engineer routing a process fluid is doing exactly the same as a municipal hydraulic engineer laying city water mains:
- **Pipe diameter** = width of the supply pipe — too narrow and pressure drops sharply (analogous to a trickling tap losing pressure on the 5th floor); too wide and capital cost is wasted.
- **Fittings** = the bends, junctions, and valves in your plumbing — each adds local resistance (analogous to kinks in a garden hose).
- **Pump** = the booster pump in the basement that overcomes the cumulative head loss.
- **Valve selection** = choosing between a gate valve (full-flow when open) and a globe valve (always has flow restriction) — a real engineering choice that affects ΔP.

### Heat Transfer Area — "The Car Radiator Analogy"
A car radiator has a large surface area (corrugated fins + tubes) precisely so that a relatively small temperature difference between hot coolant (~90 °C) and cool ambient air (~30 °C) can dump ~80 kW of heat. The same physics governs a shell-and-tube heat exchanger:
- A **larger area** $A$ → more heat transferred for the same $\Delta T$.
- A **higher $U$** (thin walls, high turbulence, clean surfaces) → more heat per unit area.
- A **greater $\Delta T_m$** (counter-current flow, hotter hot stream, colder cold stream) → more heat for the same area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> **Heat Transfer Area** = $\displaystyle A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$
> This single equation is the **heart of chemical engineering heat exchanger design** and must be memorized verbatim for KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Procedure for Fluid Routing

The KTU-recommended step-by-step procedure for routing a process fluid line is as follows:

1. **Establish line list data:** source equipment, destination equipment, fluid name, flow rate ($\dot{m}$ or $\dot{V}$), operating temperature $T$, operating pressure $P$, density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, vapor pressure, and corrosiveness.
2. **Select design velocity** based on fluid phase and service:
   - Liquids (pumping): 1 – 3 m/s (typical ~2 m/s)
   - Liquids (gravity): 0.3 – 0.6 m/s
   - Gases/Vapors (high pressure): 9 – 30 m/s
   - Steam: 15 – 40 m/s
3. **Calculate optimum pipe internal diameter:**
$$d_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi u}}$$
where $u$ = design velocity, $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate.
4. **Select nearest standard pipe size** (NB) from ANSI B36.10/B36.19 schedule table.
5. **Calculate actual velocity** from chosen $d_i$.
6. **Compute straight-run pressure drop** using Darcy-Weisbach equation (see formula sheet).
7. **Add equivalent lengths $L_{eq}$** for all fittings and valves.
8. **Total equivalent length:** $L_{total} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{eq}$
9. **Compute total ΔP** = (friction loss + fittings loss + elevation head loss + control valve ΔP).
10. **Check NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** for the pump — must exceed NPSH$_R$ + 0.5 m margin.

## 2.2 Why This Procedure Works — Engineering Reasoning

- **Step 2 is critical:** velocity governs both erosion (high $u$) and settling of solids / gas pocket formation (low $u$). Selecting $u$ outside the service window causes chronic operational failures.
- **Step 3 uses continuity:** incompressible mass balance demands $\dot{V} = uA$. Larger $\dot{V}$ demands larger area.
- **Step 7 uses dimensional scaling:** every fitting behaves like an *additional straight pipe length* of resistance equal to $L_{eq} = K \cdot d/f$, where $K$ is the resistance coefficient.
- **Step 9 is the sizing input for the pump:** undersized pump → cavitation, oversized pump → wasted capital + throttling losses.

## 2.3 Systematic Procedure for Heat Transfer Area Calculation

1. **Perform energy balance** on hot stream: $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$
2. **Perform energy balance** on cold stream: $Q = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ — must reconcile (within ~5%).
3. **Calculate LMTD** for counter-current flow (or co-current, but counter-current is preferred for larger $\Delta T_m$):
$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$
where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends.
4. **Determine correction factor $F$** (for multi-pass shell-and-tube only):
$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - R}{1 - PR}\right]}{(R-1)\,\ln\!\left[\frac{2 - P(R+1) + \sqrt{R^2+1}}{2 - P(R+1) - \sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$
with $R = (T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})/(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})$ and $P = (T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})/(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$.
   - If $F < 0.75$, **reconfigure exchanger** (change pass arrangement or use multiple shells in series).
5. **Determine overall heat transfer coefficient $U$:**
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{d,o} + \frac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{d,i}\frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{d_o}{d_i}\frac{1}{h_i}$$
where $h_i, h_o$ = inside/outside film coefficients, $R_{d,i}, R_{d,o}$ = fouling factors, $k_w$ = wall thermal conductivity, $d_i, d_o$ = inner/outer tube diameters.
6. **Calculate required area:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$
7. **Iterate on geometry** (number of tubes, length, pitch, baffle spacing) until $A_{calculated}$ ≥ $A_{required}$ with ~10–20% overdesign margin.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Refinery | Routing crude through preheat train (CDU) and sizing exchangers for energy integration (pinch analysis) |
| Pharmaceutical | Sanitary routing with electro-polished SS316L tubing; jacketed reactors with heat transfer area calculations |
| Cryogenic | Routing LNG with strict vapor-pressure ΔP limits; multi-stream exchangers (LNG spiral-wound) |
| Power | Boiler economizer routing and surface area sizing using ASME PTC 4 |
| HVAC | Chilled-water piping network and chilled-water coil area |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Alert:** All formulas in this table are **must-know** for KTU ESE. Memorize them with units — partial-credit answers fail when units are missing.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle d_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi u}}$ | Optimum pipe ID from volumetric flow $\dot{V}$ and design velocity $u$ | m, m³/s, m/s |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L \rho u^2}{2 d}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction loss (friction factor $f$, Fanning factor $f' = f/4$) | Pa |
| 3 | $\displaystyle L_{eq} = \frac{K \cdot d}{4 f}$ | Equivalent straight-pipe length for a fitting with loss coefficient $K$ | m |
| 4 | $\displaystyle h = K \cdot \frac{u^2}{2 g_c}$ | Head loss across a fitting (velocity-head method) | m of fluid |
| 5 | $\displaystyle t = \frac{P \, d}{2 (S E - 0.6 P)} + C A$ | ASME B31.3 minimum wall thickness; $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency, $CA$ = corrosion allowance | mm |
| 6 | $\displaystyle Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate | kW or W |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference (valid only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$, else use arithmetic mean if ratio < 2) | K |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{d,o} + \frac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{d,i}\frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{d_o}{d_i}\frac{1}{h_i}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (resistance-in-series, area-on-outside basis) | W/m²·K |
| 9 | $\displaystyle A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Required heat transfer area | m² |
| 10 | $\displaystyle R = \frac{T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}, \quad P = \frac{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}}$ | Shell-and-tube correction-factor parameters | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\displaystyle \dot{m} = \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2 u \rho$ | Mass flow rate from pipe diameter | kg/s |
| 12 | $\displaystyle u_{opt} = 1.3 \, \sqrt{w}$ | Optimum velocity for gas lines (Walas) where $w$ = mass flux (kg/m²·s) | m/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility Discussion

The **velocity-window method** used in Step 2 is a heuristic, not an optimization. In real engineering practice, the *true* optimum pipe diameter minimizes the **total annualized cost** = (pumping energy cost) + (capital cost amortized). For very long pipelines (e.g., offshore, cross-country), the optimum shifts toward larger diameters because pumping cost dominates. For short in-plant lines, capital cost dominates and optimum diameter is smaller. Walas's empirical $u_{opt} = 1.3\sqrt{w}$ is a useful first-cut.

For **heat transfer area**, the area is rarely a smooth plate. In reality, extended surfaces (fins), turbulence promoters (twisted tapes), and multi-pass arrangements all multiply the effective area beyond the bare tube surface. The LMTD correction factor $F$ is the engineer's *acknowledgment* that no real shell-and-tube exchanger achieves true counter-current flow, since the shell fluid reverses direction at each pass.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

The LMTD is derived from the assumption of **constant overall $U$** along the length of a counter-current heat exchanger, with the differential heat transfer at any slice $dx$ given by:

$$dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

For an incremental slice of area $dA$, the heat lost by the hot stream equals the heat gained by the cold stream:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

Separating variables and integrating the differential heat balance:

$$\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{T_h - T_c} = -U \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) dA$$

Integrating from $A = 0$ (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to $A = A$ (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) \int_0^A dA$$

Evaluating:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right)$$

Solving for $UA$ and substituting into the overall rate equation $Q = U A \Delta T_m$:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

After algebraic rearrangement (substitute $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ and replace $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ for counter-current):

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\;}$$

**Limiting case** — when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within 2× ratio), the LMTD degenerates to the **Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)**:

$$\Delta T_{AMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Area from Energy Balance (Numerical Worked Walkthrough)

Consider a process: $10{,}000$ kg/h of an oil (cp = 2.1 kJ/kg·K) is to be cooled from 423 K to 353 K using cooling water entering at 303 K and exiting at 333 K. Assume $U = 350$ W/m²·K. Find $A$.

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{10000}{3600} = 2.778 \text{ kg/s}$$
$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2.778 \times 2.1 \times (423 - 353) = 2.778 \times 2.1 \times 70$$
$$Q = 408.33 \text{ kW} = 408{,}330 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Endpoint temperature differences (counter-current):**
At end 1: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 423 - 333 = 90$ K
