At end 2: $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 353 - 303 = 50$ K

**Step 3 — LMTD:**
$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{90 - 50}{\ln(90/50)} = \frac{40}{\ln 1.8} = \frac{40}{0.5878} = 68.05 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Required area:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{408{,}330}{350 \times 68.05} = \frac{408{,}330}{23{,}816.5} \approx 17.15 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply 15% overdesign margin:**
$$A_{design} = 1.15 \times 17.15 \approx 19.7 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 20 \text{ m}^2$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Overall U from Individual Resistances (Network Analogy)

Heat transfer is **analogous to electrical conduction** (Ohm's law: $V = I R$ ↔ $\Delta T = q R_{th}$). The thermal resistance of any layer is $R_{th} = \Delta x / (k A)$.

For a tube carrying inside fluid at coefficient $h_i$ with outside fluid at coefficient $h_o$, the tube wall (thickness $t = (d_o - d_i)/2$) and two fouling layers $R_{d,i}, R_{d,o}$ create five resistances **in series**:

**Inside convective:** $R_i = 1 / (h_i \cdot A_i) = 1 / (h_i \cdot \pi d_i L)$
**Inside fouling:** $R_{d,i} = R_{d,i} / A_i$ (per unit area on **inside** basis)
**Wall conduction:** $R_w = \ln(d_o/d_i) / (2 \pi k_w L)$
**Outside fouling:** $R_{d,o} = R_{d,o} / A_o$ (per unit area on **outside** basis)
**Outside convective:** $R_o = 1 / (h_o \cdot A_o) = 1 / (h_o \cdot \pi d_o L)$

Summing and converting to a common basis (per unit **outside** area):

$$\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{R_{d,i}}{A_i} + \frac{\ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 \pi k_w L} + \frac{R_{d,o}}{A_o} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

Using $A_i / A_o = d_i / d_o$ and dividing through by $A_o$:

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + \frac{R_{d,i} d_o}{d_i} + \frac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{d,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

The fouling factor has units of $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K} / \text{W}$ (resistance per unit area). TEMA publishes typical fouling values: cooling water ~ $0.0002$ m²K/W, light organics ~ $0.0002$, crude oil ~ $0.0004$–$0.001$, etc.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Combined Routing + Pressure Drop

A pump delivers $50$ m³/h of water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1$ cP) through 80 m of horizontal straight pipe plus four 90° standard elbows, two gate valves (fully open), and one check valve. Pipe: 3-inch Schedule 40 steel ($d_i = 77.93$ mm, roughness $\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm). Estimate ΔP.

**Step 1 — Velocity:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.07793)^2 = 4.769 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$u = \frac{50/3600}{4.769 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.01389}{4.769 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho u d}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.91 \times 0.07793}{0.001} = 2.27 \times 10^5$$
Fully turbulent.

**Step 3 — Relative roughness:**
$$\varepsilon/d = 0.046 / 77.93 = 5.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Friction factor** (Colebrook-White or Moody chart):
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon/d}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$$
Iterating gives $f \approx 0.0188$.

**Step 5 — Equivalent lengths** (from Crane TP-410):
- 90° standard elbow: $L_{eq}/d \approx 30$ → $L_{eq} = 30 \times 0.07793 = 2.34$ m each × 4 = 9.36 m
- Gate valve (open): $L_{eq}/d \approx 8$ → $0.62$ m each × 2 = 1.24 m
- Check valve: $L_{eq}/d \approx 100$ → $7.79$ m

Total: $L_{total} = 80 + 9.36 + 1.24 + 7.79 = 98.39$ m

**Step 6 — Pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach):**
$$\Delta P = f \frac{L}{d} \frac{\rho u^2}{2} = 0.0188 \times \frac{98.39}{0.07793} \times \frac{1000 \times 2.91^2}{2}$$
$$\Delta P = 0.0188 \times 1262.7 \times 4234.1 \approx 100{,}500 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.005 \text{ bar}$$

This ΔP (plus static head + control valve ΔP + nozzle losses) is then used to size the pump.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Co-current vs Counter-current vs Multi-pass

| Configuration | $\Delta T_m$ value | $F$ factor | When Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|
| Counter-current (1-1) | Maximum LMTD | $F = 1.0$ | Always preferred when thermally feasible |
| Co-current (parallel) | Lower LMTD | $F = 1.0$ | When one fluid viscosity changes drastically (e.g., condensation) |
| 1-2 shell-and-tube | Lower than counter | $F = 0.7$ – $0.9$ typical | Industrial default (mechanical feasibility) |
| 2-4 shell-and-tube | Even lower | $F = 0.6$ – $0.8$ | High-pressure drop applications |
| Cross-flow | Use separate chart | $F$ from graph | Compact heat exchangers (plate-fin) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-pass heat exchangers never achieve true counter-current.** A 1-2 exchanger has $F \leq 0.9$ typically; if $F < 0.75$, the engineer must either (i) change to a 2-4 arrangement with two shells in series, or (ii) increase the number of shells in series to push $F$ back above 0.75. The total $F$ for $N$ shells in series with 1-2 each is computed as $F_N = [F_1 (1/F_1 + 1) - 1] / [R]$, but for design exam purposes the simpler approximation $F_N \approx F_1^{0.6N}$ may be used (Kern's method).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Fluid Routing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Line Service - fluid, flow, T, P] --> B[Select Design Velocity from Service Window]
    B --> C[Calculate Optimum Pipe ID using Continuity Equation]
    C --> D[Select Nearest Standard Pipe Schedule]
    D --> E[Recompute Actual Velocity and Verify Range]
    E --> F[Estimate Straight-Run Friction Loss using Darcy-Weisbach]
    F --> G[Inventory All Fittings and Valves]
    G --> H[Compute Equivalent Length for Each Fitting]
    H --> I[Sum Total Equivalent Length]
    I --> J[Add Static Head and Control Valve Losses]
    J --> K{Total DP exceeds Pump Budget?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Upsize Pipe Diameter or Reduce Fittings]
    L --> D
    K -->|No| M[Verify NPSH Available exceeds NPSH Required]
    M --> N{Adequate NPSH Margin?}
    N -->|No| O[Reroute Suction Line - shorter, larger, fewer fittings]
    O --> F
    N -->|Yes| P[Finalize Line List and Isometric Drawing]
    P --> Q[Pass to Mechanical Design for Wall Thickness and Support]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Heat Transfer Area Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Define Process Streams - hot in/out T, cold in/out T, mass flow, cp] --> T[Energy Balance on Hot Stream - find Q]
    T --> U[Energy Balance on Cold Stream - verify Q within 5 percent]
    U --> V[Choose Exchanger Configuration - 1-2 or 2-4]
    V --> W[Calculate Endpoint Temperature Differences DT1 and DT2]
    W --> X[Compute LMTD from DT1 and DT2]
    X --> Y[Compute R and P parameters]
    Y --> Z[Read or Compute Correction Factor F]
    Z --> AA{F is greater than 0.75?}
    AA -->|No| AB[Reconfigure Exchanger or Add Shells in Series]
    AB --> V
    AA -->|Yes| AC[Determine Film Coefficients hi and ho via Sieder-Tate or Dittus-Boelter]
    AC --> AD[Select Fouling Factors from TEMA tables]
    AD --> AE[Compute Overall U from Resistance-in-Series Network]
    AE --> AF[Calculate Required Area A = Q over U times F times LMTD]
    AF --> AG[Apply 10 to 20 percent Overdesign Margin]
    AG --> AH[Round Up to Standard Shell Diameter and Tube Length]
    AH --> AI[Detail Mechanical Design - baffles, nozzles, supports]
    AI --> AJ[End]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Resistance-in-Series Thermal Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ho[Outside Film Resistance<br/>1 slash ho] --> Rdo[Outside Fouling Resistance<br/>Rdo]
    Rdo --> Wall[Wall Conduction Resistance<br/>ln do slash di over 2 kw]
    Wall --> Rdi[Inside Fouling Resistance<br/>Rdi times do slash di]
    Rdi --> Hi[Inside Film Resistance<br/>do slash di times 1 over hi]
    Hi --> Tho[Hot Fluid Outlet Temperature]
    Tho -.delta T driving force.-> Ho
```

The driving temperature difference $\Delta T_{m,corrected} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ acts across this entire network in series, producing the rate $Q$.

## 4.4 Schematic — Standard Pipe Schedule Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Compute Required ID for Optimum Velocity] --> P2{Calculated ID exceeds 24 inches?}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Use Extra Strong Schedule - higher wall thickness]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Match against ANSI B36.10 Nominal Pipe Size Table]
    P4 --> P5{Operating Pressure exceeds Schedule 40 rating?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Upsize to Schedule 80 or 160]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Default to Schedule 40 - Standard Wall]
    P6 --> P8[Apply ASME B31.3 Wall Thickness Formula]
    P7 --> P8
    P3 --> P8
    P8 --> P9{Computed t minus Corrosion Allowance exceeds Schedule t?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Re-select Higher Schedule or Higher Material Grade]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Finalize Pipe Spec]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematic representations are adapted from **process flow logic** — physical P&IDs would require CAD-based isometric drawings that are outside the scope of mermaid rendering. The above block diagrams convey the **decision flow** that a chemical engineer follows during design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define fluid routing in a chemical plant. List the four primary engineering deliverables produced by the fluid routing exercise.**

**Model Answer:**
Fluid routing is the engineering activity of determining the physical path, size, and accessories for transporting process fluids between equipment in a chemical plant.

The four primary deliverables are:
1. **Pipe diameter (ID)** selected for optimum velocity.
2. **Pipe wall thickness / schedule** based on internal pressure (ASME B31.3) and corrosion allowance.
3. **Equipment count** — fittings, valves, supports, and instrumentation.
4. **Total pressure drop (ΔP)** to size the driver (pump/compressor).

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: State the fundamental equation for heat transfer area in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Define every term and its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
$$\boxed{A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Heat transfer area | m² |
| $Q$ | Heat duty (rate) | W (or kW) |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) |
| $F$ | LMTD correction factor (multi-pass) | dimensionless |
| $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 1 questions appear as **Question 1 or 2** in the ESE paper. Both Question A and Question B below are **independent** full-module options (internal choice). Attempt **one** of them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**A horizontal pipe line of 50 m carries 30 m³/h of an oil (ρ = 900 kg/m³, μ = 0.05 Pa·s) from a pump to a storage tank. The line has two 90° standard elbows, one gate valve (fully open), and the discharge elevation is 8 m above the pump centerline. The pipe is 2-inch Schedule 40 steel (ID = 52.48 mm, ε = 0.046 mm).**

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the pipe friction loss and equivalent-length contributions, then determine the total head loss.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the pump discharge pressure is 4 bar (gauge), estimate the pressure available at the tank inlet. Comment on whether this margin is adequate.**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Velocity:**
$$\dot{V} = \frac{30}{3600} = 0.008333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05248)^2 = 2.164 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$u = \frac{0.008333}{2.164 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.85 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**
$$Re = \frac{900 \times 3.85 \times 0.05248}{0.05} = 3636$$
This is **laminar** ($Re < 2300$ is laminar; actually $Re \approx 3636$ is transitional — let me re-check). For Re = 3636, it is in the transitional zone; for design safety assume **fully turbulent** and use Colebrook.

> **Valuation note:** In the transition zone, designers conservatively take the higher friction factor. If we treat as fully turbulent:

$$\varepsilon/d = 0.046/52.48 = 8.77 \times 10^{-4}$$

Using Colebrook (or Swamee-Jain explicit):
$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon/d}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$
$$f = \frac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(2.37 \times 10^{-4} + 3.39 \times 10^{-3})]^2} = \frac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(3.63 \times 10^{-3})]^2} = \frac{0.25}{(-2.44)^2}$$
$$f \approx 0.042$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent lengths** (Crane TP-410 / Perry's):
- 90° standard elbow: $L_{eq}/d = 30$ → $L_{eq} = 30 \times 0.05248 = 1.574$ m each × 2 = 3.149 m
- Gate valve (open): $L_{eq}/d = 8$ → $L_{eq} = 8 \times 0.05248 = 0.420$ m

**Step 4 — Total equivalent length:**
$$L_{total} = 50 + 3.149 + 0.420 = 53.57 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Friction head loss:**
$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{d} \cdot \frac{u^2}{2g} = 0.042 \times \frac{53.57}{0.05248} \times \frac{3.85^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$
$$h_f = 0.042 \times 1020.7 \times 0.755 = 32.36 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 6 — Total head (friction + elevation):**
$$h_{total} = h_f + \Delta z = 32.36 + 8 = 40.36 \text{ m of oil}$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Convert head to pressure:**
$$\Delta P_{loss} = \rho g h = 900 \times 9.81 \times 40.36 = 356{,}338 \text{ Pa} = 3.56 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Tank inlet pressure:**
$$P_{tank} = P_{pump} - \Delta P_{loss} = 4.0 - 3.56 = 0.44 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on adequacy:**
A residual pressure of 0.44 bar at the tank inlet is **adequate for typical storage tank inlet conditions** (which usually only require 0.2–0.5 bar to overcome nozzle loss and maintain positive pressure against vapor backflow). However, if the tank is pressurized or if there is a downstream flow control valve, additional margin is needed.

**Recommendation:** Increase pump discharge to 5 bar (gauge) to maintain a **20% margin** for unforeseen fittings, fouling, or future flow increases. Alternatively, **upsize the pipe** to reduce friction loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget to add the elevation head** to the friction head — this costs 2 marks.
> 2. Students forget to **convert head (m) to pressure (Pa)** before subtracting from gauge pressure — this costs 1 mark.
> 3. Students use **inconsistent ρ in continuity vs Reynolds** — define ρ once and use throughout.
> 4. Don't forget that the **static head** (8 m) is on the *discharge side* — it adds to the pump load, it does not subtract.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger cools 8000 kg/h of an organic liquid (cp = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) from 450 K to 350 K using cooling water entering at 300 K and exiting at 320 K. The overall heat transfer coefficient (based on outside tube area) is U = 500 W/m²·K.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD), correction factor F, and required heat transfer area.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the cooling water flow rate is increased such that its outlet temperature becomes 315 K, recalculate the area and comment on the operational trade-off.**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**
$$\dot{m} = 8000 / 3600 = 2.222 \text{ kg/s}$$
$$Q = 2.222 \times 2.5 \times (450 - 350) = 2.222 \times 2.5 \times 100 = 555.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Verify with water side:**
$$Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} \Delta T_w \Rightarrow \dot{m}_w = \frac{555.6}{4.18 \times 20} = 6.646 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — LMTD (counter-current, for 1-2 use counter-current as basis):**
- End 1 (hot in / cold out): $\Delta T_1 = 450 - 320 = 130$ K
- End 2 (hot out / cold in): $\Delta T_2 = 350 - 300 = 50$ K

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{130 - 50}{\ln(130/50)} = \frac{80}{\ln 2.6} = \frac{80}{0.9555} = 83.73 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Correction factor parameters:**
$$R = \frac{T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}} = \frac{100}{20} = 5.0$$
$$P = \frac{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}} = \frac{20}{150} = 0.1333$$

**Step 5 — F for 1-2 from Bowman-Müller-Akkers correlation (or standard chart):**

For $R = 5.0, P = 0.1333$, 1-2 exchanger: $F \approx 0.94$ (interpolating from TEMA chart).

**Step 6 — Required area:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{555{,}600}{500 \times 0.94 \times 83.73} = \frac{555{,}600}{39{,}353} = 14.12 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Add 15% overdesign:** $A_{design} = 1.15 \times 14.12 = 16.24 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow$ round to **17 m²**.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — New cooling water outlet temperature** = 315 K. Water ΔT = 315 − 300 = 15 K. Required water mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{w,new} = \frac{555.6}{4.18 \times 15} = 8.86 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — New endpoint ΔT:**
- $\Delta T_1 = 450 - 315 = 135$ K
- $\Delta T_2 = 350 - 300 = 50$ K

$$\Delta T_{LMTD,new} = \frac{135 - 50}{\ln(135/50)} = \frac{85}{\ln 2.7} = \frac{85}{0.9933} = 85.57 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — New $P$:**
$$P_{new} = \frac{15}{150} = 0.1$$
$R$ remains 5.0. $F_{new}$ (1-2 exchanger) ≈ 0.96.

**Step 4 — New area:**
$$A_{new} = \frac{555{,}600}{500 \times 0.96 \times 85.57} = \frac{555{,}600}{41{,}073} = 13.53 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Trade-off analysis:**
| Aspect | Original | Modified |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling water ΔT | 20 K | 15 K |
| Water mass flow | 6.65 kg/s | 8.86 kg/s |
| LMTD | 83.7 K | 85.6 K |
| $F$ | 0.94 | 0.96 |
| Area | 14.1 m² | 13.5 m² |
| **% Area reduction** | — | **~4.3%** |
| **Pumping cost** | Lower | **Higher (33% more water)** |

**Conclusion:** Increasing cooling water flow yields only marginal (~4%) area reduction at the cost of significantly higher pumping cost. The optimal cooling water outlet temperature is around **330 K (ΔT ~30 K)** to balance capital (area) vs operating (pumping) costs. This is the classic **heat exchanger optimization trade-off**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply LMTD by correction factor $F$** — this alone costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up co-current and counter-current endpoint pairs**: students often write $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ (wrong). The correct counter-current pairings are: end 1 = hot in with cold out, end 2 = hot out with cold in.
> 3. **Using $F = 1$ for multi-pass** without justification — at least state the assumption.
> 4. **Units:** $Q$ must be in W (not kW) when $U$ is in W/m²·K. Convert kW → W before dividing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Routing of Fluids
- [ ] Velocity window for liquids ≈ **1–3 m/s**, gases ≈ **9–30 m/s** (service-dependent).
- [ ] Optimum pipe ID from continuity: $d_i = \sqrt{4\dot{V}/(\pi u)}$.
- [ ] Darcy-Weisbach: $\Delta P_f = 4 f L \rho u^2 / (2 d)$ — Fanning factor = $f/4$.
- [ ] Equivalent length: $L_{eq} = K d / (4 f)$ — K values from **Crane TP-410** or Perry's.
- [ ] Standard fittings $L_{eq}/d$: 90° elbow ≈ 30, tee (line) ≈ 20, tee (branch) ≈ 60, gate valve (open) ≈ 8, globe valve (open) ≈ 340, check valve ≈ 100.
- [ ] Total pump head = friction + fittings + elevation + nozzle + control valve.
- [ ] NPSH$_A$ > NPSH$_R$ + **0.5 m margin** (cavitation prevention).
- [ ] ASME B31.3 wall thickness: $t = PR/(SE - 0.6P) + CA$.
- [ ] **Always** verify calculated velocity against service window after upsizing.

### Heat Transfer Area
- [ ] Energy balance: $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ — **always reconcile both sides**.
- [ ] LMTD: $\Delta T_{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- [ ] AMTD used when $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2 < 2$.
- [ ] LMTD correction factor $F$ from Bowman-Müller-Akkers; reject $F < 0.75$.
- [ ] Multi-shell: $F_N = [F_1(1/F_1 + 1) - 1]/R$ or Kern's $F_N \approx F_1^{0.6 N}$.
- [ ] Resistance-in-series: $\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{d,o} + \frac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{d,i}\frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{d_o}{d_i}\frac{1}{h_i}$.
- [ ] Fouling factors from **TEMA** tables (cooling water ≈ 0.0002 m²K/W, crude ≈ 0.001).
- [ ] Area equation: $A = Q/(U F \Delta T_{LMTD})$.
- [ ] Apply **10–20% overdesign margin** for uncertainties in $U$ and fouling.
- [ ] Round area up to nearest standard shell diameter (e.g., 12", 16", 20", 24").
- [ ] Counter-current > Co-current in $\Delta T_m$ unless viscosity swing demands co-current.

### Common KTU Mistake Themes (Repeated Every Year)
1. **Forgetting units conversion** between kW and W.
2. **Mixing Fanning and Darcy friction factors** — they differ by factor of 4.
3. **Wrong endpoint ΔT pairings** in LMTD.
4. **Ignoring the correction factor $F$** — straight LMTD only valid for counter-current 1-1.
5. **Not using energy balance** to first find $Q$ before calculating area.
6. **Forgetting elevation/static head** in pump-head calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Tube Bundle Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Tube Bundle Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Estimation of Tube Bundle Size

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tube Bundle**: A precisely arranged cluster of parallel tubes (cylindrical conduits) enclosed within a cylindrical shell, forming the active heat-transfer surface of a **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)**. The **Tube Bundle Size** refers to the geometric envelope that defines the heat-transfer inventory of the exchanger — namely, the **number of tubes (N_t)**, **tube length (L)**, **tube outer diameter (d_o)**, **tube pitch (p_t)**, **bundle diameter (D_b)**, and the resulting **shell inside diameter (D_s)**.

The estimation procedure of the tube bundle size is the *very first sizing step* in the mechanical design of a shell-and-tube exchanger, performed *after* the heat-duty, LMTD, and overall heat-transfer coefficient (U_o) have been established from the process-side energy balance.

### Sub-Components that Define a "Tube Bundle"

| Term | Symbol | Typical Engineering Range |
|---|---|---|
| Number of tubes | $N_t$ | 10 – 5000+ |
| Tube length | $L$ | 1 m – 12 m |
| Tube outside diameter | $d_o$ | **19.05 mm (¾")** and **25.4 mm (1")** are TEMA standards |
| Tube pitch | $p_t$ | $1.25 \, d_o \le p_t \le 1.5 \, d_o$ |
| Pitch ratio | $p_R = p_t / d_o$ | 1.25 – 1.5 (TEMA minimum) |
| Bundle diameter | $D_b$ | Computed from layout |
| Shell inside diameter | $D_s$ | $D_b$ + baffle/shell clearances |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Drinking Straws in a Soda Can" Model**
>
> Imagine you have a tin can (the *shell*) and you want to fit as many drinking straws (the *tubes*) inside it so that a hot drink flows through the shell around the straws while a cold drink flows *through* the straws. The "tube bundle" is simply the geometric packing arrangement of those straws. The questions an engineer asks are:
> 1. **How many straws do I need?** (→ Number of tubes $N_t$)
> 2. **How long should the straws be?** (→ Tube length $L$)
> 3. **How tightly can I pack them — touching, or with gaps for cleaning?** (→ Pitch $p_t$)
> 4. **What is the smallest can that still holds all the straws?** (→ Bundle diameter $D_b$)
> 5. **And once I add the necessary wall clearances, what is the final can size?** (→ Shell diameter $D_s$)
>
> Just as you would arrange straws in either a *triangular* (densest) or *square* (cleaner) pattern, the engineer has layout choices governed by TEMA standards.

This estimation step is *deterministic* — it depends only on duty ($Q$), $U_o$, geometry, and standard layout tables. It precedes the more iterative thermal-hydraulic rating step that follows in Module 2 (Kern's or Bell-Delaware method).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Flow of Tube Bundle Estimation

The estimation proceeds in **four sequential stages**. Each stage unlocks the next.

**Stage 1 — Decide tube geometry (d_o, p_t, layout pattern)**

This is a *design choice*, made from process constraints (fouling, pressure drop, mechanical cleaning) and TEMA standards. TEMA prescribes standard tubes of **19.05 mm (¾")** and **25.4 mm (1")** OD, with BWG (Birmingham Wire Gauge) chosen for wall thickness based on pressure.

**Stage 2 — Compute the heat-transfer area required**

$$A_{req} \;=\; \frac{Q}{U_o \, \Delta T_{LMTD} \, F}$$

where $F$ is the LMTD correction factor for multi-pass exchangers (1-shell, 2-shell pass).

**Stage 3 — Compute the *number of tubes* $N_t$**

Rearranging the surface area identity $A = \pi d_o L N_t$:

$$N_t \;=\; \frac{A_{req}}{\pi \, d_o \, L}$$

The tube length $L$ is itself a *design choice* (typically 2.44 m, 3.66 m, 4.88 m, 6.10 m, or 7.32 m — multiples of 8 ft or 12 ft sections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always round $N_t$ **up** to a value that fits a TEMA-standard layout table (counting rule), because you cannot physically install a fractional tube.

**Stage 4 — Compute the *bundle diameter* $D_b$**

The bundle diameter is derived from the tube count and the chosen pitch geometry:

$$D_b \;=\; d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{K} \right)^{1/n}$$

where the constants $K$ and $n$ are layout-specific and tabulated below.

**Stage 5 — Compute the *shell diameter* $D_s$**

$$D_s \;=\; D_b \;+\; \text{(outer tube limit to shell ID clearance)}$$

A practical engineering shortcut, fully compatible with KTU Module-1 expectations, is:

$$D_s \;=\; d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{K} \right)^{1/n} \;+\; C$$

where $C$ is the shell-clearance term (TEMA: $C \approx 1.5 \, d_o$ for triangular pitch with full baffles, or 0 for a tight pull-through floating-head).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises **every formula** required to answer a KTU Module-1 question on tube bundle size estimation. Internal pipe symbols are replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Constants / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{ph} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{pc}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Energy balance |
| 2 | LMTD (no correction) | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counterflow |
| 3 | Corrected LMTD | $\Delta T_{corr} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $F$ from TEMA charts |
| 4 | Required area | $A_{req} = \dfrac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{corr}}$ | $U_o$ from Table (Module 1) |
| 5 | Number of tubes (continuous) | $N_t^{*} = \dfrac{A_{req}}{\pi d_o L}$ | Round up |
| 6 | Triangular pitch bundle dia. | $D_b = d_o \left( \dfrac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2}$ | $K = 0.866,\ n = 2$ |
| 7 | Square pitch (90°) bundle dia. | $D_b = d_o \left( \dfrac{N_t}{1.0} \right)^{1/2}$ | $K = 1.0,\ n = 2$ |
| 8 | Rotated square (45°) bundle dia. | $D_b = d_o \left( \dfrac{N_t}{1.0} \right)^{1/2}$ | $K \approx 0.957$ (sometimes) |
| 9 | Shell ID (pull-through) | $D_s = D_b$ | Floating head |
| 10 | Shell ID (fixed tubesheet, TEMA C) | $D_s = D_b + 0.8 \, p_t$ | TEMA Standards |
| 11 | TEMA minimum pitch ratio | $p_R = p_t / d_o \ge 1.25$ | Mandatory mechanical clearance |
| 12 | Tube count — triangular, 1-pass | $N_t = 3a^2 + 3a + 1$ | $a$ = side of inscribed hexagon |
| 13 | Tube count — triangular, 2-pass | $N_t = 6a^2 + 6a + 2$ | $a$ even, horizontal split |
| 14 | Tube count — triangular, 4-pass | $N_t = 12a^2 + 6a + 2$ |  |
| 15 | Tube count — triangular, 6-pass | $N_t = 18a^2 + 6a + 2$ |  |
| 16 | Tube count — triangular, 8-pass | $N_t = 24a^2 + 6a + 2$ |  |
| 17 | Tube count — square, 1-pass | $N_t = a^2 + (a-1)^2$ (a odd); $N_t = a^2 + a^2 - a$ (a even) | $a$ = tubes per row |
| 18 | Tube count — square, 2-pass | $N_t = 2a^2$ | Horizontal split |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Why this matters in the chemical-process industry (CPI):
> - **Capex control**: $N_t$ and $L$ together determine the *fabrication cost* — wrong sizing wastes thousands of kilograms of tube metal.
> - **Plot-space fit**: $D_s$ must match the *pipe rack* and *vessel* bay dimensions.
> - **Maintainability**: pitch-ratio and layout dictate *whether a brush can pass between tubes* (mechanical cleaning).
> - **Mechanical integrity**: tube-pitch minimums prevent *vibration-induced fatigue failure* (TEMA RCB 4.51).
> - **Module 2 linkage**: the very $D_s$ and $N_t$ you compute here become inputs to the Kern / Bell-Delaware shell-side $h_s$ and $\Delta P_s$ calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstration

## 3.1 Derivation of Bundle Diameter for a Triangular-Pitch Layout (30° arrangement)

### 3.1.1 Geometric Starting Point

Consider a bundle of $N_t$ tubes, each of outside diameter $d_o$, laid out on a **triangular pitch** $p_t$. The cross-section of the bundle is a hexagon of *inscribed-circle diameter* $D_b$.

A bundle of $N_t$ tubes, each occupying a regular hexagonal cell of area $A_{cell}$, fits inside the hexagonal envelope:

$$A_{bundle} \;=\; \frac{\pi D_b^2}{4} \;\;,\qquad A_{cell} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\, p_t^2$$

The total cells inside the hexagon, equal to the number of tubes (one tube per cell, central tube present):

$$N_t \;=\; \frac{A_{bundle}}{A_{cell}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D_b^2 / 4}{\sqrt{3}\, p_t^2 / 2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{D_b^2}{p_t^2}$$

Using the identity $\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} = 0.866$ (true to 4 significant figures):

$$N_t \;=\; 0.866 \left( \frac{D_b}{p_t} \right)^2$$

### 3.1.2 Inverting the Relation

$$\left( \frac{D_b}{p_t} \right)^2 \;=\; \frac{N_t}{0.866} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{D_b}{p_t} \;=\; \left( \frac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{\;D_b \;=\; p_t \left( \frac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; d_o \cdot p_R \left( \frac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2}\;}$$

where $p_R = p_t / d_o$ is the **pitch ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical aside**: $K = 0.866$ comes purely from the geometry of 60° equilateral triangles. For a **square pitch**, the corresponding packing constant is $K = 1.0$ (because $\pi/4 = 0.7854$ for squares, then re-scaled, and the net of the empirical Kern constant is 1.0 — see Kern's *Process Heat Transfer*, 1950).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Triangular-Pitch Tube-Counting Formulas (Number of Passes)

A triangular-pitched bundle with a *central tube* forms a hexagonal array. Define $a$ as the number of tubes along one **side** of the largest inscribed hexagon (not counting the central tube on that side).

**1-pass (no split):**

Layer 0 (center) = 1 tube
Layer 1 = 6 tubes
Layer 2 = 12 tubes
...
Layer $k$ = $6k$ tubes

$$N_t \;=\; 1 + 6 \sum_{k=1}^{a} k \;=\; 1 + 6 \cdot \frac{a(a+1)}{2} \;=\; 3a^2 + 3a + 1$$

**2-pass (horizontal split, $a$ even):** The two halves each contain a triangle of side $a$ plus an extra column.

Upper half: $1 + 6(1) + 6(2) + \ldots + 6(a/2) = 1 + 3(a/2)(a/2+1)$
Lower half: same as upper
Total: $2 \cdot [1 + 3 \cdot (a/2)(a/2+1)] = 2 + \frac{3a^2}{2} + \frac{3a}{2}$

Simplifying to the canonical form:

$$N_t \;=\; 6a^2 + 6a + 2 \quad \text{(2-pass, a even)}$$

**4, 6, 8 passes:** each additional pair of pass partitions removes $N/2p$ tubes from the formula's first coefficient, giving the family:

$$N_t \;=\; 6\,p\,a^2 \;+\; 6a \;+\; 2 \quad \text{(p-pass, a multiple of 2p)}$$

This is the form most often quoted in KTU answer keys and is reproduced in **Row 13–16 of the Formula Sheet (Section 2.2)**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU-Style 14-Mark

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — to be reproduced in the exam**:
>
> *"A 1-shell, 2-tube-pass heat exchanger is required to handle a duty of $Q = 1.2 \times 10^6$ W. Hot fluid enters at 150 °C, leaves at 90 °C; cold fluid enters at 30 °C, leaves at 70 °C. Overall coefficient $U_o = 850 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. LMTD correction factor $F = 0.96$. Tubes are 25.4 mm OD, 2.44 m long, arranged on triangular pitch with $p_t = 31.75$ mm. Determine: (a) number of tubes, (b) bundle diameter, (c) shell diameter."*

### Step 1 — Compute LMTD

Hot end: $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 70 = 80 \text{ °C}$
Cold end: $\Delta T_2 = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ °C}$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} \;=\; \frac{80 - 60}{\ln(80/60)} \;=\; \frac{20}{\ln(1.333)} \;=\; \frac{20}{0.2877} \;=\; 69.5 \;°C$$

### Step 2 — Correct the LMTD

$$\Delta T_{corr} \;=\; F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD} \;=\; 0.96 \times 69.5 \;=\; 66.7 \;°C$$

### Step 3 — Required heat-transfer area

$$A_{req} \;=\; \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{corr}} \;=\; \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{850 \times 66.7} \;=\; 21.18 \;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Continuous number of tubes

$$N_t^{*} \;=\; \frac{A_{req}}{\pi d_o L} \;=\; \frac{21.18}{\pi \times 0.0254 \times 2.44} \;=\; \frac{21.18}{0.1947} \;=\; 108.8$$

Round **up** to the nearest 2-pass triangular count. The 2-pass triangular table is $6a^2 + 6a + 2$:

| $a$ | $N_t$ |
|---|---|
| 3 | 74 |
| 4 | 122 |
| 5 | 182 |

The next count above 108.8 is **122 tubes** at $a = 4$.

$$\boxed{\;N_t \;=\; 122\;}$$

### Step 5 — Bundle diameter (triangular pitch, $K = 0.866$)

$$D_b \;=\; d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; 0.0254 \times \left( \frac{122}{0.866} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; 0.0254 \times \sqrt{140.88}$$

$$D_b \;=\; 0.0254 \times 11.87 \;=\; 0.3015 \;\text{m} \;=\; 301.5 \;\text{mm}$$

### Step 6 — Shell diameter (TEMA-class C, fixed tubesheet)

$$D_s \;=\; D_b + 0.8 \, p_t \;=\; 301.5 + 0.8 \times 31.75 \;=\; 301.5 + 25.4 \;=\; 326.9 \;\text{mm}$$

Round to the **nearest standard pipe size**: 350 mm (14" nominal).

$$\boxed{\;D_s \;\approx\; 350 \;\text{mm (rounded to standard)}\;}$$

### 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis (Why the layout choice matters)

| Layout | $K$ | $D_b$ for 122 tubes, 25.4 mm OD | Relative D_b |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triangular (30°) | 0.866 | 301.5 mm | 1.000 (smallest) |
| Square (90°) | 1.000 | 327.5 mm | 1.086 |
| Rotated square (45°) | 0.957 | 320.7 mm | 1.064 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design insight**: a triangular layout gives the *most compact* shell — saving material — at the cost of poorer mechanical cleaning. A square (or rotated-square) layout allows a brush to pass *between every row* and is chosen for *fouling service* (crude pre-heat, refinery bottoms, slurries).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tube Bundle Estimation Procedure

The following Mermaid flow shows the *causal dependency* of every step. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `stepA`, `box1`), and labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting, in compliance with the rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[STEP 1: PROCESS DATA Q, T HOT, T COLD, M, CP] --> stepB[STEP 2: COMPUTE Q AND LMTD]
    stepB --> stepC[STEP 3: APPLY LMTD CORRECTION FACTOR F]
    stepC --> stepD[STEP 4: OBTAIN UO FROM TEMA TABLE]
    stepD --> stepE[STEP 5: COMPUTE A REQ]
    stepE --> stepF[STEP 6: CHOOSE DO, L, LAYOUT, PITCH]
    stepF --> stepG[STEP 7: COMPUTE NT CONTINUOUS]
    stepG --> stepH[STEP 8: ROUND UP TO NEAREST TEMA COUNT]
    stepH --> stepI[STEP 9: COMPUTE BUNDLE DIAMETER DB]
    stepI --> stepJ[STEP 10: COMPUTE SHELL DIAMETER DS]
    stepJ --> stepK[STEP 11: SELECT STANDARD SHELL PIPE SIZE]
    stepK --> stepL[OUTPUT: NT, L, DO, PT, DB, DS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Triangular vs Square vs Rotated-Square Layout

The diagram below depicts, *as a block-architecture mapping*, the three TEMA pitch patterns. The middle block shows how each layout's $K$ constant and counting-rule formula feed into the bundle-diameter calculator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PITCH_CHOICE[LAYOUT PATTERN DECISION]
        L1[TRIANGULAR 30 DEGREE PITCH MOST COMPACT]:::A
        L2[SQUARE 90 DEGREE PITCH CLEANABLE]:::B
        L3[ROTATED SQUARE 45 DEGREE PITCH CLEANABLE PLUS COMPACT]:::C
    end

    L1 --> R1[FORMULA NT EQUALS 6PA SQUARE PLUS 6A PLUS 2]
    L2 --> R2[FORMULA NT EQUALS 2A SQUARE FOR 2 PASS]
    L3 --> R3[FORMULA NT EQUALS 2A SQUARE MINUS 2A FOR 2 PASS]

    R1 --> CALC[BUNDLE DIAMETER DB CALCULATOR]
    R2 --> CALC
    R3 --> CALC

    CALC --> OUT[OUTPUT DB AND DS]

    classDef A fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#0b3d91,color:#0b3d91;
    classDef B fill:#d8f0db,stroke:#1d6f3a,color:#1d6f3a;
    classDef C fill:#fde6cc,stroke:#9a4d00,color:#9a4d00;
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Sectional View of a Triangular-Pitched Bundle (ASCII)

A 1-pass, $a = 3$ triangular bundle (so $N_t = 3(9) + 3(3) + 1 = 37$ tubes) looks like this on cross-section. Each `O` is a tube, and the central tube is on the bundle axis.

```
                    O
                  O   O
                O   O   O
                  O   O
                    O
                  O   O
                O   O   O
                  O   O
                    O
```

The **inscribed circle of this hexagon** is the bundle diameter $D_b$. The six outer corner vertices lie on the circle of diameter $D_b$.

## 4.4 Mermaid — Inputs vs Outputs of the Bundle-Size Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT FROM MODULE 1 PRIOR STEPS]
        I1[Q KILOWATT]:::I
        I2[UO WATT PER M2 K]:::I
        I3[TLMTD CORR KELVIN]:::I
        I4[TUBE OD MILLIMETRE]:::I
        I5[TUBE LENGTH METRE]:::I
    end

    subgraph MIDDLE[CALCULATION ENGINE]
        M1[AREA EQ NT TIMES PI TIMES DO TIMES L]:::M
        M2[BUNDLE DIA EQ DO TIMES NT OVER 0.866 RAISED TO 0.5]:::M
        M3[SHELL DIA EQ DB PLUS 0.8 PT]:::M
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[OUTPUTS TO MODULE 2 KERN AND BELL DELAWARE]
        O1[NT NUMBER OF TUBES]:::O
        O2[DB BUNDLE DIAMETER]:::O
        O3[DS SHELL DIAMETER]:::O
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    I4 --> M1
    I5 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    I4 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    O1 --> M1
    M3 --> O2
    M3 --> O3

    classDef I fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#1a3a6e,color:#0e2447;

```

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Tube Bundle Size**
> 1. **Do not** forget the LMTD correction factor $F$ for 1-shell / multi-tube-pass exchangers. Examiners routinely dock 1–2 marks for using uncorrected LMTD.
> 2. **Do not** round $N_t$ to the *nearest* integer — always round **up** to the next TEMA-countable layout. The TEMA table values ($6a^2 + 6a + 2$ etc.) are the *only* physically installable counts.
> 3. **Do not** use $K = 0.866$ for a square-pitch layout — use $K = 1.0$. Mixing up the layouts is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Always state the assumed TEMA shell type** (fixed tubesheet, U-tube, floating head) because it controls the $D_s = D_b + C$ clearance term.
> 5. **Pitch ratio** $p_R$ must be $\ge 1.25$ (TEMA minimum). If your chosen pitch is below this, the answer is *mechanically invalid*.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the term "tube bundle" as used in shell-and-tube heat exchangers. List the geometric parameters that fully describe a tube bundle size.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tube bundle** is the structured cluster of parallel tubes mounted between the two tubesheets of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger, through which one of the process fluids flows while the other fluid is confined to the shell side and flows across the bundle. The bundle's geometry is fully defined by the following six parameters: (i) number of tubes $N_t$, (ii) tube outer diameter $d_o$, (iii) tube wall thickness (BWG), (iv) tube length $L$, (v) tube pitch $p_t$ and pitch ratio $p_R = p_t / d_o$, and (vi) tube-layout pattern (triangular 30°, square 90°, or rotated square 45°). The bundle diameter $D_b$ and shell diameter $D_s$ are *derived* from these six.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**State TEMA's recommendation for the minimum tube-pitch ratio $p_R = p_t / d_o$. Justify why this minimum is enforced.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) prescribes a **minimum pitch ratio $p_R \ge 1.25$** for all shell-and-tube exchangers. This minimum is enforced for two reasons:

1. **Mechanical cleaning**: a pitch ratio of 1.25 ensures a clearance of at least $0.25\, d_o$ between adjacent tube OD surfaces, which is just sufficient to admit a mechanical cleaning brush or hydrodynamic sand slurry.
2. **Structural integrity**: closer pitches create *tube-to-tube* contact zones and *flow-induced vibration* (acoustic resonance) failures, both of which are mitigated by the minimum-pitch rule (TEMA RCB 4.51).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> A 1-shell-pass, 2-tube-pass heat exchanger is to be designed. Hot oil ($\dot{m}_h = 5$ kg/s, $c_{ph} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) enters at 160 °C and leaves at 100 °C. Cooling water enters at 30 °C and leaves at 50 °C. The estimated overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U_o = 700 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Tubes of OD $d_o = 19.05$ mm (0.75") on triangular pitch $p_t = 23.8$ mm and tube length $L = 3.0$ m are to be used. LMTD correction factor $F = 0.94$.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the number of tubes required, the bundle diameter, and the shell inside diameter. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Re-design using a square (90°) pitch with the same pitch ratio, and comment on the resulting shell diameter and cleanability. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Triangular layout, 7 marks**

**Step 1: Heat duty**

$$Q \;=\; \dot{m}_h c_{ph} \Delta T_h \;=\; 5 \times 2.1 \times (160-100) \;=\; 5 \times 2.1 \times 60 \;=\; 630 \;\text{kW} \;=\; 6.3 \times 10^5 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2: LMTD**

$$\Delta T_1 = 160 - 50 = 110 \;°C \quad,\quad \Delta T_2 = 100 - 30 = 70 \;°C$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} \;=\; \frac{110 - 70}{\ln(110/70)} \;=\; \frac{40}{\ln(1.5714)} \;=\; \frac{40}{0.45199} \;=\; 88.5 \;°C$$

**Step 3: Corrected LMTD**

$$\Delta T_{corr} \;=\; 0.94 \times 88.5 \;=\; 83.2 \;°C$$

**Step 4: Required area**

$$A_{req} \;=\; \frac{6.3 \times 10^5}{700 \times 83.2} \;=\; 10.82 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5: Continuous tube count**

$$N_t^{*} \;=\; \frac{A_{req}}{\pi d_o L} \;=\; \frac{10.82}{\pi \times 0.01905 \times 3.0} \;=\; \frac{10.82}{0.1795} \;=\; 60.3$$

**Step 6: Round up to TEMA count (2-pass triangular: $6a^2 + 6a + 2$)**

- $a = 3$: $N_t = 6(9) + 6(3) + 2 = 54 + 18 + 2 = 74$ ✓ (next above 60.3)

$$\boxed{\;N_t = 74\;}$$

**Step 7: Bundle diameter (triangular, $K = 0.866$)**

$$D_b \;=\; d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{0.866} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; 0.01905 \times \left( \frac{74}{0.866} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{85.45}$$

$$D_b \;=\; 0.01905 \times 9.243 \;=\; 0.1761 \;\text{m} \;=\; 176.1 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 8: Shell ID (TEMA C, fixed tubesheet, $C = 0.8 p_t$)**

$$D_s \;=\; D_b + 0.8 p_t \;=\; 176.1 + 0.8 \times 23.8 \;=\; 176.1 + 19.04 \;=\; 195.1 \;\text{mm}$$

Standard pipe: **8" nominal (200 mm)** is the closest commercial size.

$$\boxed{\;D_s \;\approx\; 200 \;\text{mm (next standard size)}\;}$$

**Part (b) — Re-design with square pitch, 7 marks**

Square-pitch (90°) layout uses $K = 1.0$. The 2-pass count is $N_t = 2a^2$.

We need a count $\ge 60.3$ from the *square* family:

- $a = 6$: $N_t = 2(36) = 72$ ✓

$$\boxed{\;N_t = 72 \text{ (square pitch, 2-pass)}\;}$$

**Bundle diameter (square, $K = 1.0$):**

$$D_b \;=\; d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{1.0} \right)^{1/2} \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{72} \;=\; 0.01905 \times 8.485 \;=\; 0.1616 \;\text{m} \;=\; 161.6 \;\text{mm}$$

**Shell ID (square layout, same $C = 0.8 p_t$):**

$$D_s \;=\; 161.6 + 0.8 \times 23.8 \;=\; 161.6 + 19.04 \;=\; 180.6 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 200 \;\text{mm (standard)}$$

**Comment:**

| Aspect | Triangular (Part a) | Square (Part b) |
|---|---|---|
| Tubes | 74 | 72 |
| Bundle dia. $D_b$ | 176.1 mm | 161.6 mm |
| Shell dia. $D_s$ (computed) | 195.1 mm | 180.6 mm |
| Shell dia. $D_s$ (rounded) | 200 mm | 200 mm |
| **Mechanical cleaning** | **Impossible** between every tube — only on the central axes | **Possible** along both axes — full brush access |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical remark**: Although the triangular layout gives a larger *bundle* diameter, both round to the same *commercial shell* size of 200 mm. In practice, the engineer chooses the layout based on **fouling severity**, not on the marginal $D_s$ difference.

---

### **Question B** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> A chemical reactor's overhead condenser must condense $3.5$ kg/s of a hydrocarbon vapour ($\lambda = 320$ kJ/kg) using cooling water entering at 25 °C and leaving at 45 °C. Vapour condenses at 90 °C (isothermal). Estimate the tube-bundle size if $U_o = 950$ W/m²·K, LMTD correction factor $F = 0.85$, with 19.05 mm OD tubes, 4.88 m long, on a triangular pitch of 1.25 × $d_o$.
>
> **(a)** Determine $N_t$, $D_b$, and $D_s$ for a 1-pass triangular layout. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Now re-do the calculation for a 4-pass triangular layout and state which layout is preferred for a *pure vapour* condensing service. Justify. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 1-pass triangular, 7 marks**

**Step 1: Heat duty**

$$Q \;=\; \dot{m} \lambda \;=\; 3.5 \times 320 \;=\; 1120 \;\text{kW} \;=\; 1.12 \times 10^6 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2: LMTD** (condensing at $T_{sat} = 90$ °C)

$$\Delta T_1 = 90 - 25 = 65 \;°C \quad,\quad \Delta T_2 = 90 - 45 = 45 \;°C$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} \;=\; \frac{65 - 45}{\ln(65/45)} \;=\; \frac{20}{\ln(1.4444)} \;=\; \frac{20}{0.3677} \;=\; 54.4 \;°C$$

**Step 3: Corrected LMTD**

$$\Delta T_{corr} \;=\; 0.85 \times 54.4 \;=\; 46.2 \;°C$$

**Step 4: Required area**

$$A_{req} \;=\; \frac{1.12 \times 10^6}{950 \times 46.2} \;=\; 25.5 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5: Continuous tube count**

$$N_t^{*} \;=\; \frac{25.5}{\pi \times 0.01905 \times 4.88} \;=\; \frac{25.5}{0.2920} \;=\; 87.3$$

**Step 6: Round up to 1-pass triangular count ($N_t = 3a^2 + 3a + 1$)**

- $a = 4$: $N_t = 3(16) + 3(4) + 1 = 48 + 12 + 1 = 61$ ✗ (below 87.3)
- $a = 5$: $N_t = 3(25) + 3(5) + 1 = 75 + 15 + 1 = 91$ ✓

$$\boxed{\;N_t = 91\;}$$

**Step 7: Bundle diameter (triangular, $K = 0.866$)**

$$D_b \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{91/0.866} \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{105.08} \;=\; 0.01905 \times 10.251$$

$$D_b \;=\; 0.1953 \;\text{m} \;=\; 195.3 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 8: Shell ID** (TEMA C, $C = 0.8 p_t$, $p_t = 1.25 \times 19.05 = 23.8$ mm)

$$D_s \;=\; 195.3 + 0.8 \times 23.8 \;=\; 195.3 + 19.04 \;=\; 214.3 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 250 \;\text{mm (10" std.)}$$

**Part (b) — 4-pass triangular, 7 marks**

The 4-pass triangular count is $N_t = 12a^2 + 6a + 2$ with $a$ divisible by 4.

- $a = 4$: $N_t = 12(16) + 6(4) + 2 = 192 + 24 + 2 = 218$ ✗ (above 87.3)
- $a = 2$: $N_t = 12(4) + 6(2) + 2 = 48 + 12 + 2 = 62$ ✗ (below 87.3)
- $a = 3$: $a$ must be a multiple of 4 (and $\ge 2p$ for even partitioning) — $a = 3$ is *not* divisible by 4 and would give an uneven tube partition. *Note*: Some KTU tables relax this to "any even a" for 4-pass; the conventional rule is $a$ divisible by $2p = 4$. Hence we use $a = 4$, giving $N_t = 218$.

Alternatively, with 2-pass, $N_t = 6a^2 + 6a + 2$ ($a$ even):

- $a = 4$: $N_t = 6(16) + 6(4) + 2 = 96 + 24 + 2 = 122$ ✓
- $a = 3$: not allowed (must be even)

**For 4-pass strictly**: $N_t = 218$ at $a = 4$ — this is the *only* valid 4-pass triangular count above 87.3.

$$D_b \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{218/0.866} \;=\; 0.01905 \times \sqrt{251.7} \;=\; 0.01905 \times 15.87 \;=\; 0.3023 \;\text{m}$$

$$D_s \;=\; 0.3023 + 0.0190 \;=\; 0.3213 \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 350 \;\text{mm (14" std.)}$$

**Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Metric | 1-pass (Part a) | 4-pass (Part b) |
|---|---|---|
| Tubes | 91 | 218 |
| Bundle dia. | 195.3 mm | 302.3 mm |
| Shell dia. (rounded) | 250 mm | 350 mm |
| Length | 4.88 m | 4.88 m |
| **Total heat-transfer area** | $A = \pi (0.01905)(4.88)(91) = 26.6 \text{ m}^2$ | $A = \pi (0.01905)(4.88)(218) = 63.7 \text{ m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferred layout for condensing service**: **1-pass triangular** is preferred for *pure vapour condensation* (and indeed for any *isothermal hot side*) because:
> 1. Condensation coefficient $h_{hot}$ on the shell side is extremely high; the controlling resistance is on the *cooling-water* (tube) side.
> 2. Multi-pass tube layouts complicate water-side velocity control and pressure drop without adding heat-transfer benefit.
> 3. Lower tube count (91 vs 218) and a smaller shell (250 vs 350 mm) translates to a **~50% material-cost saving**.
> 4. Multi-pass layouts are *only* justified when the tube-side $h$ is limiting and higher tube-side velocity is needed (e.g., viscous oils, gases).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — must memorise before Module 1 exam**

- ✅ **Tube bundle size = {$N_t$, $d_o$, $L$, $p_t$, layout, $D_b$, $D_s$**}
- ✅ **TEMA tubes**: **19.05 mm (¾")** and **25.4 mm (1")** are the *only* two tube ODs you need to consider unless the problem specifies otherwise.
- ✅ **Pitch ratio** $p_R = p_t / d_o \ge 1.25$ — TEMA's hard minimum; do not violate.
- ✅ **Triangular pitch** $K = 0.866$, $n = 2$ — densest layout.
- ✅ **Square pitch (90°)** $K = 1.0$, $n = 2$ — cleanable.
- ✅ **Rotated square (45°)** $K \approx 0.957$ — cleanable + compact.
- ✅ **Bundle-diameter formula**: $\displaystyle D_b = d_o \left( \frac{N_t}{K} \right)^{1/n}$
- ✅ **TEMA 2-pass triangular count**: $N_t = 6a^2 + 6a + 2$ with $a$ *even* — commit this to memory.
- ✅ **TEMA 1-pass triangular count**: $N_t = 3a^2 + 3a + 1$ — $a$ = side of inscribed hexagon.
- ✅ **Tube-counting rule**: always round $N_t$ **up** to the next TEMA count, *never* down.
- ✅ **Shell ID formula (TEMA C, fixed tubesheet)**: $D_s = D_b + 0.8 \, p_t$.
- ✅ **Shell ID formula (floating head, pull-through)**: $D_s = D_b$ (tight fit).
- ✅ **Area-of-triangle rule**: $\Delta T_{corr} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ with $F$ from TEMA F-chart for 1-shell-pass, $N$-tube-pass configuration.
- ✅ **Heat duty sources**: sensible heat $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ *or* latent heat $Q = \dot{m} \lambda$ *or* both.
- ✅ **Standard tube lengths**: **2.44 m (8 ft), 3.05 m (10 ft), 3.66 m (12 ft), 4.88 m (16 ft), 6.10 m (20 ft), 7.32 m (24 ft)**.
- ✅ **Standard commercial shell sizes**: based on NPS pipe — **200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000 mm**, etc.
- ✅ **Layout selection rule of thumb**: *clean fluids → triangular*; *fouling fluids → square or rotated-square*.
- ✅ **TEMA classes**: B (bonnet), C (fixed tubesheet), R (removable bundle), etc. — the class controls the clearance term $C$ in $D_s = D_b + C$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-mile exam advice**:
> 1. **Show units throughout** — KTU examiners *will* deduct marks for missing units.
> 2. **Tabulate your givens** at the start of the solution — it makes partial-mark recovery easier.
> 3. **Cross-check $p_R \ge 1.25$** as a final step.
> 4. **Cite TEMA** whenever you use a layout constant or counting rule — earns a "professional practice" mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient Using Bell's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient Using Bell's Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bell's Method** (more rigorously known as the **Bell–Delaware Method**) is a sophisticated engineering analysis procedure developed by **K. J. Bell** in the early 1960s and subsequently refined at the **University of Delaware** (under the Heat Transfer Research, Inc. — HTRI — consortium). It is the industry-standard semi-empirical correlation framework used to estimate the **true operating shell-side heat transfer coefficient** ($h_o$) and **shell-side pressure drop** ($\Delta P_s$) in a **segmentally baffled shell-and-tube heat exchanger (STHE)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PBCHT504 – Chemical Engineering Design-I, Module-1), Bell's Method provides a rigorous bridge between the *idealized* tube-bank correlations (e.g., Colburn, Kern) and the *real* heat exchanger behaviour, which is dominated by **leakage, bypass, and cross-flow induced by segmental baffles**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** The overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$) of a shell-and-tube exchanger must be evaluated by combining the tube-side coefficient ($h_i$) from standard internal-flow correlations (Dittus–Boelter, Sieder–Tate) with the **shell-side coefficient ($h_o$) obtained via Bell's method**. The fouling factors $R_{d,i}$ and $R_{d,o}$ are then added to yield the design $U_D$.

The fundamental design relationship for the overall heat transfer coefficient is:

$$\frac{1}{U_D} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_{io}} \;+\; R_{d,i} \;+\; R_{w} \;+\; R_{d,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}$$

where $R_w = x_w / k_w$ is the conduction resistance of the tube wall, and $h_{io} = h_i \cdot (d_i / d_o)$ is the inside coefficient referred to the outside tube surface area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Air-Conditioning" Intuition

Imagine a large open-plan office in which cool air must be delivered uniformly to every desk. The **conditioner (tube-side fluid)** blows cool air into a network of ceiling ducts (the **tube bundle**). However, the air first has to traverse the *open office space* (the **shell side**) before reaching the desks. Now consider:

- **Segmental baffles** are like *half-walls (gaps)* installed across the office. They force the air to *zig-zag* rather than going straight — this is **cross-flow**. The zig-zag pattern dramatically improves cooling (just like baffles improve heat transfer).
- However, some air **slips through the gaps** between the baffle and the shell wall (**baffle-shell leakage**, $J_L$) — wasting cooling capacity.
- Some air **slips between tubes and baffle holes** (**tube-to-baffle leakage**, $J_L$).
- Some air **bypasses the tube bundle entirely** by flowing in the gap between the tube bundle and the shell wall (**bundle bypass stream**, $J_B$) — analogous to the air sneaking past the desks.
- The bundle is rarely perfectly packed, so some lanes have different velocities (**unequal lane distribution**, $J_S$).
- When the fluid cools down (or heats up) strongly across the bundle, its viscosity changes, creating **laminar adverse temperature gradient** effects ($J_R$).

Bell's Method is essentially a **"correction-factor accounting system"** that starts from the *ideal* tube-bank coefficient ($h_{ideal}$) and then multiplies by a series of penalties ($J_C, J_B, J_L, J_S, J_R$) — each one representing a real-world inefficiency of the shell-side flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bell's Method Logic in One Sentence:**
> $$h_o \;=\; h_{ideal} \times J_C \times J_B \times J_L \times J_S \times J_R$$
> Each $J \leq 1$ because real exchangers always perform *worse* than the ideal cross-flow tube bank.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Reference Range |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Segmental baffle cut (fraction of shell ID) | $B_c$ | **0.20 – 0.45** (typical 0.25) |
| Baffle spacing | $l_B$ | **0.2 × D_s – 1.0 × D_s** |
| Tube pitch ratio | $p_t / d_o$ | **1.25 – 1.50** |
| Tube layout angle | $\theta$ | **30° (triangular)** or **90° (square)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** In Bell's Method derivations, the **Standard is $h_o$ referenced to the outer tube surface area** ($A_o = N_t \pi d_o L$). Always use $d_o$ consistently for the shell-side area balance.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The Bell's Method executes in a strict eight-stage logic flow. Each stage feeds numerical values into the next.

### Stage 1 — Establish the Exchanger Geometry
Define (or read from the problem statement):
- Shell inside diameter: $D_s$
- Outer/inner tube diameters: $d_o$, $d_i$
- Number of tubes: $N_t$
- Tube pitch: $p_t$
- Tube layout angle: $\theta$ (30°, 60°, 45°, 90°)
- Number of tube passes: $N_p$
- Baffle cut: $B_c$ (e.g., 25% means $B_c = 0.25$)
- Baffle spacing (centre-to-centre): $l_B$
- Number of baffles: $N_b$
- Tube length between tube sheets: $L$

### Stage 2 — Compute Cross-Flow Area ($S_m$) and Shell Equivalent Diameter ($D_e$)

The **mid-shell cross-flow area** between the shell wall and the tube bundle envelope is:

$$S_m \;=\; l_B \bigl(D_s - D_{otl}\bigr)$$

where $D_{otl}$ is the outer tube-limit diameter (diameter of the circle enclosing all tubes). For a typical 4-tube-pass layout, $D_{otl}$ is read from TEMA tables based on $N_t$, $d_o$, $p_t$, and layout.

The **shell equivalent diameter** (for the cross-flow section) is:

$$D_e \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot \text{(cross-flow area open to flow)}}{\text{wetted perimeter of tubes in the cross-flow stream}}$$

For a triangular pitch: $D_e = \dfrac{4(\sqrt{3}/4 \, p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2 / 8)}{\pi d_o / 2}$

For a square pitch: $D_e = \dfrac{4(p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2/4)}{\pi d_o}$

### Stage 3 — Determine Shell-Side Mass Velocity and Reynolds Number

$$G_s \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_s}{S_m}$$

$$\text{Re}_s \;=\; \frac{G_s \cdot D_e}{\mu_s}$$

### Stage 4 — Ideal Cross-Flow Coefficient ($h_{ideal}$)

Use the **Colburn-type** correlation for an ideal tube bank:

$$h_{ideal} \;=\; j_H \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$$

where $j_H$ is the **Colburn factor**, dependent on $\text{Re}_s$ and tube layout:

- **Laminar** ($\text{Re}_s < 10$): $j_H \approx 1.4 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$
- **Turbulent** ($\text{Re}_s > 10\,000$): $j_H \approx 0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$ (triangular) or $0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$ (square, with constants)
- **Transition**: Use TEMA tabulated values or Delaware chart fits.

### Stage 5 — Bell's Correction Factors (The "Penalty Stack")

Each factor is **bounded between 0 and 1**, derived from the geometric ratios of the exchanger.

| Factor | Physical Meaning | Key Inputs |
|---|---|---|
| $J_C$ | Baffle cut effect | $B_c$ ratio, $l_B / D_s$ |
| $J_B$ | Bundle bypass stream | Clearance ratios $F_{bp}, F_{sb}$ |
| $J_L$ | Combined leakage (tube-baffle + baffle-shell) | Clearance areas $S_{tb}, S_{sb}$ |
| $J_S$ | Non-uniform lane distribution | $F_{bp}$, $\text{Re}_s$, pitch |
| $J_R$ | Adverse temperature gradient (laminar flow only) | $\Delta T$ across bundle, $\text{Re}_s$ |

### Stage 6 — Shell-Side Heat Transfer Coefficient (Corrected)

$$h_o \;=\; h_{ideal} \cdot J_C \cdot J_B \cdot J_L \cdot J_S \cdot J_R$$

### Stage 7 — Tube-Side Coefficient ($h_i$)

If not given, compute from:

$$h_i \;=\; 0.023 \cdot \frac{k_i}{d_i} \cdot \text{Re}_i^{0.8} \cdot \text{Pr}_i^{n}$$

with $n = 0.4$ for heating and $0.3$ for cooling (Sieder–Tate / Dittus–Boelter).

### Stage 8 — Assemble the Overall Coefficient

$$\frac{1}{U_D} \;=\; \frac{d_o}{h_i d_i} \;+\; R_{d,i} \;+\; \frac{x_w}{k_w} \;+\; R_{d,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and memorize this table for the KTU ESE. All these equations are board-favorite derivations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-flow area | $S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl})$ | m² |
| 2 | Equivalent diameter (triangular pitch) | $D_e = \dfrac{4(\sqrt{3}/4 \, p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2/8)}{\pi d_o/2}$ | m |
| 3 | Equivalent diameter (square pitch) | $D_e = \dfrac{4(p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2/4)}{\pi d_o}$ | m |
| 4 | Shell-side mass velocity | $G_s = \dot{m}_s / S_m$ | kg/(m²·s) |
| 5 | Shell-side Reynolds number | $\text{Re}_s = G_s D_e / \mu_s$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Prandtl number | $\text{Pr}_s = c_{ps} \mu_s / k_s$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Ideal cross-flow coefficient | $h_{ideal} = j_H \cdot (k_s / D_e) \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 8 | Bell's corrected $h_o$ | $h_o = h_{ideal} \, J_C \, J_B \, J_L \, J_S \, J_R$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 9 | Overall $U$ referenced to $A_o$ | $1/U_D = 1/h_{io} + R_{d,i} + x_w/k_w + R_{d,o} + 1/h_o$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 10 | Inside coefficient referred to outer area | $h_{io} = h_i \cdot (d_i / d_o)$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 11 | Baffle cut ratio | $B_c = h_B / D_s$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Bell's $J_C$ (baffle cut) | $J_C = 0.55 + 0.72 B_c$ (approx., $B_c \le 0.5$) | dimensionless |
| 13 | Number of effective tubes in cross-flow | $N_{tc} = D_s (1 - 2 B_c) / p_t$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Shell-side pressure drop | $\Delta P_s = \Delta P_{cross} + \Delta P_{window}$ | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note on Pipes for Tables:** All vertical bars and absolute values in the formulas above use the standard mathematical vertical bar; within markdown tables, we use $\vert$ so the table pipe `|` is never mistaken for a column separator.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bell's Method is the **de facto design standard** in:

- **HTRI** commercial heat exchanger design software (Xchanger Suite)
- **Process industries**: refinery crude preheat trains, ethylene cracking furnaces, ammonia plants
- **Power engineering**: feedwater heaters, condensers, and air-cooled auxiliary exchangers
- **Process safety**: rating existing exchangers for new operating conditions

The shell-side heat transfer coefficient is the **single largest contributor** to exchanger underperformance in the field. Bell's Method is the only practical, design-office-friendly procedure that captures real geometric losses with acceptable accuracy (typically ±20%), and is therefore universally tested in chemical engineering board examinations worldwide — including **KTU**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Shell-Side Coefficient using Bell's Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation below is presented in **complete algebraic detail** — every intermediate quantity is named, every substitution is justified. This is the type of step-by-step working that earns **full marks** in the KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

We are given a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with the following data:

- Shell ID: $D_s$
- Tube OD / ID: $d_o / d_i$
- Tube pitch: $p_t$ (triangular, $30°$ layout)
- Tube count: $N_t$
- Baffle cut: $B_c$ (e.g., 0.25)
- Centre-to-centre baffle spacing: $l_B$
- Number of tube passes: $N_p$

The first geometric quantity required is the **outer tube limit diameter** $D_{otl}$, which for a triangular pitch, $N_p$ passes, and $N_t$ tubes is read from TEMA tables (or from the bundle-envelope correlation):

$$D_{otl} \;\approx\; d_o \left(\frac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1}$$

where $K_1$ and $n_1$ are empirical bundle constants (e.g., for 2-pass triangular, $K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 1.8$ approximately).

### Step 2 — Mid-Shell Cross-Flow Area

The cross-flow stream must squeeze through the gap between the shell wall and the tube bundle, twice per baffle space:

$$S_m \;=\; l_B \bigl(D_s - D_{otl}\bigr)$$

This is the **effective flow area** perpendicular to the cross-flow direction. All shell-side momentum and heat-transfer correlations reference this area.

### Step 3 — Equivalent Diameter $D_e$

For a **triangular ($30°$) pitch**:

$$D_e \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot A_{cell,open}}{P_{wetted}} \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot (\sqrt{3}/4 \, p_t^2 \;-\; \pi d_o^2 / 8)}{\pi d_o / 2}$$

Simplify the numerator: $\sqrt{3}/4 \cdot p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2 / 8 = 0.4330 \, p_t^2 - 0.3927 \, d_o^2$

Simplify the denominator: $\pi d_o / 2 = 1.5708 \, d_o$

$$\boxed{\;D_e \;=\; \frac{1.7321 \, p_t^2 - 1.5708 \, d_o^2}{1.5708 \, d_o} \;=\; \frac{1.1027 \, p_t^2}{d_o} - d_o\;}$$

For a **square ($90°$) pitch**:

$$\boxed{\;D_e \;=\; \frac{4(p_t^2 - \pi d_o^2 / 4)}{\pi d_o} \;=\; \frac{1.2732 \, p_t^2}{d_o} - d_o\;}$$

### Step 4 — Mass Velocity and Reynolds Number

$$G_s \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_s}{S_m}$$

$$\text{Re}_s \;=\; \frac{G_s \, D_e}{\mu_s}$$

The physical meaning: $G_s$ is the mass flow rate of shell fluid per unit area of cross-flow gap; $\text{Re}_s$ tells us whether the flow is laminar, transition, or turbulent — and selects the appropriate $j_H$.

### Step 5 — Ideal Heat Transfer Coefficient

For **turbulent flow** ($\text{Re}_s > 10\,000$):

$$j_H \;=\; 0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4} \quad (\text{triangular, }30°)$$

$$j_H \;=\; 0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.6} \quad (\text{square, }90°, \text{inline})$$

For **laminar** ($\text{Re}_s < 100$): $j_H = 1.4 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$

Substitute into:

$$h_{ideal} \;=\; j_H \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3} \;=\; j_H \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$$

For $\text{Re}_s > 10\,000$, triangular:

$$h_{ideal} \;=\; 0.33 \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$$

### Step 6 — Bell's Correction Factor $J_C$ (Baffle Cut)

From the empirical Bell–Delaware correlation (curve-fit form):

$$J_C \;=\; 0.55 \;+\; 0.72 \, B_c \quad \text{for} \quad B_c \le 0.5$$

For $B_c = 0.25$: $J_C = 0.55 + 0.72(0.25) = 0.55 + 0.18 = 0.73$

### Step 7 — Bell's Correction Factor $J_B$ (Bundle Bypass)

$$J_B \;=\; \exp\bigl[-1.25 \, F_{bp} \, (1 + 2 \sqrt{F_{sb}})\, (1 - \text{Re}_s/10^5)^{?}\bigr]$$

A standard simplified working form used in KTU board problems:

$$J_B \;\approx\; \exp\bigl[-C_1 \, F_{bp}\,(1 - \sqrt{F_{sb}})\bigr]$$

with $C_1 \approx 1.5$ and $F_{bp}, F_{sb}$ being the bypass-shell and bypass-tube clearance fractions.

### Step 8 — Bell's Correction Factor $J_L$ (Leakage)

$$J_L \;=\; 1 \;-\; \beta_1 \bigl(S_{tb}/S_m + S_{sb}/S_m\bigr) \;+\; \beta_2 \bigl(\text{terms}\bigr)$$

The dominant term reduces $J_L$ from 1 toward 0.5–0.9 depending on how poorly the baffles fit.

### Step 9 — Bell's $J_S$ and $J_R$

- $J_S$ corrects for non-uniform flow distribution between tube rows; depends on $\text{Re}_s$ and pitch ratio.
- $J_R$ is unity for $\text{Re}_s > 100$ and drops below 1 only for laminar flow with large property variations.

### Step 10 — Assembly of $h_o$

$$h_o \;=\; h_{ideal} \cdot J_C \cdot J_B \cdot J_L \cdot J_S \cdot J_R$$

### Step 11 — Tube-Side Coefficient

If $h_i$ is not given, compute from Dittus–Boelter:

$$h_i \;=\; 0.023 \cdot \frac{k_i}{d_i} \cdot \text{Re}_i^{0.8} \cdot \text{Pr}_i^{0.4} \quad (\text{cooling})$$

Convert to outer-area basis:

$$h_{io} \;=\; h_i \cdot \frac{d_i}{d_o}$$

### Step 12 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Clean / Design)

$$\frac{1}{U_D} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_{io}} \;+\; R_{d,i} \;+\; \frac{x_w \, d_o}{k_w \, d_{m,\ln}} \;+\; R_{d,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}$$

where $d_{m,\ln}$ is the **log-mean tube diameter** for wall conduction (often approximated by $d_{m,\ln} = (d_o - d_i)/\ln(d_o/d_i)$).

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Illustrative)

**Given:** A 1-2 shell-and-tube exchanger, shell ID $D_s = 0.6$ m, $d_o = 25$ mm, $d_i = 20$ mm, triangular pitch $p_t = 32$ mm, $N_t = 200$, $l_B = 0.20$ m, $B_c = 0.25$, $\dot{m}_s = 8$ kg/s, $k_s = 0.6$ W/(m·K), $\mu_s = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $c_{ps} = 4180$ J/(kg·K). Find $h_o$ and $U_D$ for clean conditions with $h_i = 1500$ W/(m²·K).

### Solution Walk-Through

**Step A — Geometric Inputs:**
$D_s = 0.6$ m; $d_o = 0.025$ m; $p_t = 0.032$ m.

**Step B — Outer tube limit:**
For triangular pitch, $N_t = 200$ ⇒ $D_{otl} \approx 0.45$ m (from TEMA or correlation).

**Step C — Cross-flow area:**
$$S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl}) = 0.20 \times (0.60 - 0.45) = 0.20 \times 0.15 = 0.030 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step D — Equivalent diameter (triangular):**
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \, p_t^2}{d_o} - d_o = \frac{1.1027 \times (0.032)^2}{0.025} - 0.025$$
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \times 0.001024}{0.025} - 0.025 = \frac{0.001129}{0.025} - 0.025 = 0.04517 - 0.025 = 0.02017 \; \text{m}$$

**Step E — Mass velocity and Reynolds number:**
$$G_s = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{S_m} = \frac{8}{0.030} = 266.67 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$
$$\text{Re}_s = \frac{G_s D_e}{\mu_s} = \frac{266.67 \times 0.02017}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.378}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 13\,445$$

**Step F — Prandtl number:**
$$\text{Pr}_s = \frac{c_{ps} \mu_s}{k_s} = \frac{4180 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.6} = \frac{1.672}{0.6} = 2.787$$

**Step G — Ideal heat transfer coefficient:**
Using triangular turbulent $j_H = 0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$:
$$j_H = 0.33 \times (13\,445)^{-0.4} = 0.33 \times (13\,445^{-0.4})$$
$$\log_{10}(13\,445) = 4.1286 \;\Rightarrow\; -0.4 \times 4.1286 = -1.6514$$
$$\Rightarrow 13\,445^{-0.4} = 10^{-1.6514} = 0.02233$$
$$j_H = 0.33 \times 0.02233 = 0.00737$$
$$h_{ideal} = j_H \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$$
$$\text{Pr}_s^{1/3} = 2.787^{1/3} = 1.404$$
$$h_{ideal} = 0.00737 \times \frac{0.6}{0.02017} \times 13\,445 \times 1.404$$
$$h_{ideal} = 0.00737 \times 29.75 \times 13\,445 \times 1.404$$
$$= 0.00737 \times 29.75 = 0.2193$$
$$0.2193 \times 13\,445 = 2948.6$$
$$2948.6 \times 1.404 = 4139.8 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step H — Bell's correction factors (assumed):**
- $J_C = 0.73$ (baffle cut)
- $J_B = 0.90$ (bundle bypass)
- $J_L = 0.85$ (leakage)
- $J_S = 0.95$ (non-uniform)
- $J_R = 1.00$ (turbulent, no penalty)

$$h_o = 4139.8 \times 0.73 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 1.00$$
$$h_o = 4139.8 \times 0.530$$
$$h_o \approx 2194 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step I — Overall coefficient (clean):**
$$h_{io} = h_i \cdot (d_i / d_o) = 1500 \times (0.020 / 0.025) = 1200 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$
$$\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{1200} + \frac{1}{2194} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} + 4.558 \times 10^{-4} = 1.289 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$U_C = \frac{1}{1.289 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 776 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step J — Adding fouling + wall resistance** (e.g., $R_{d,i} = 0.0002$, $R_{d,o} = 0.0002$, $x_w/k_w = 0.0001$ m²·K/W):
$$\frac{1}{U_D} = 1.289 \times 10^{-3} + 0.0002 + 0.0001 + 0.0002 = 1.789 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$U_D = \frac{1}{1.789 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 559 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Observation:** The clean overall coefficient (~776 W/m²·K) drops by ~28% after fouling and wall resistance are included — this is the **design margin** every chemical engineer must report.
