# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bell's Method — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the full logical flow of Bell's Method: starting from geometry inputs, through cross-flow area, ideal correlation, the five correction factors, and finally the assembly of $h_o$ into the overall $U$. It is the schematic representation expected in KTU answers when "draw the algorithm for Bell's method" appears.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Exchanger Geometry Inputs D_s d_o d_i p_t N_t l_B B_c] --> B[Compute D_otl from TEMA Tables]
    B --> C[Compute Cross Flow Area Sm = l_B times D_s minus D_otl]
    C --> D[Compute Equivalent Diameter D_e for triangular or square pitch]
    D --> E[Compute Shell Mass Velocity G_s = m_dot_s over Sm]
    E --> F[Compute Reynolds Number Re_s = G_s D_e over mu_s]
    F --> G[Compute Prandtl Number Pr_s = c_ps mu_s over k_s]
    G --> H{Is Re_s greater than 10000?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Turbulent regime: jH = 0.33 Re_s power minus 0.4]
    H -->|No| J[Laminar regime: jH = 1.4 Re_s power minus 0.4]
    I --> K[Compute h_ideal = jH times k_s over D_e times Re_s times Pr_s power 1/3]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Bell Correction Factors JC JB JL JS JR]
    L --> M[Compute Shell Side Coefficient h_o = h_ideal times JC times JB times JL times JS times JR]
    M --> N[Compute Tube Side Coefficient h_i from Dittus Boelter]
    N --> O[Convert to outer area basis h_io = h_i times d_i over d_o]
    O --> P[Add fouling Rd_i Rd_o and wall resistance xw over kw]
    P --> Q[Compute Overall U_D: 1 over U_D equals 1 over h_io plus R_di plus xw over kw plus R_do plus 1 over h_o]
    Q --> R[END: Report U_D in W per m squared K]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Resistance Network Visualization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram visualizes the **resistance-in-series model** of the overall heat transfer coefficient. Each "block" is a thermal resistance. Bell's Method supplies the value of $R_{o} = 1/h_o$ — the shell-side convective resistance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Inside Convection Resistance 1 over h_io] --> R2[Inside Fouling Resistance R_d_i]
    R2 --> R3[Tube Wall Conduction xw over kw]
    R3 --> R4[Outside Fouling Resistance R_d_o]
    R4 --> R5[Shell Side Convection Resistance 1 over h_o from Bell Method]
    R5 --> RT[Sum equals 1 over U_D]
    R1 --> RT
    R2 --> RT
    R3 --> RT
    R4 --> RT
```

## 4.3 Schematic Layout — Baffle Geometry Showing Leakage and Bypass Paths

> [!NOTE]
> The Bell–Delaware method conceptually decomposes the complex 3D shell-side flow into **four parallel flow paths**: (i) the **cross-flow** through the bundle — this is the *useful* flow; (ii) the **leakage flow** through the baffle-to-shell gap; (iii) the **leakage flow** through the tube-to-baffle holes; and (iv) the **bundle bypass flow** in the shell-bundle gap. The diagram below shows these paths in the plan view of an E-shell segmental baffle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SegmentBaffle[Segmental Baffle Plan View]
        A1[Shell Wall] -->|Annular Gap = Bypass F_bp| A2[Tube Bundle]
        A2 -->|Leakage F_tb through holes| A3[Baffle Plate]
        A3 -->|Leakage F_sb along shell wall| A1
        A2 -->|Cross Flow useful F_c| A4[Shell Wall opposite]
    end
```

**Reading Guide for the Baffle Schematic:**

- **Cross-flow stream $F_c$**: Fluid moving perpendicular to the tubes, between two successive segmental baffles. This stream transfers the *useful* heat.
- **Baffle-shell leakage $F_{sb}$**: Fluid squeezing through the diametrical gap between the baffle outer edge and the inside of the shell wall (typically 1–2% of $D_s$).
- **Tube-to-baffle leakage $F_{tb}$**: Fluid leaking through the annular clearance between tube OD and baffle hole ID (typically ~0.4 mm diametral).
- **Bundle bypass stream $F_{bp}$**: Fluid flowing in the longitudinal gap between the outer tube row and the shell wall — never contacting tubes. This is the *largest* parasitic loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bell's $J$-factor correspondence to the streams above:**
> - $J_C$ → accounts for the cross-flow stream being only a *fraction* of total flow due to baffle cut
> - $J_L$ → combined penalty from $F_{tb}$ and $F_{sb}$
> - $J_B$ → penalty from $F_{bp}$
> - $J_S$ → accounts for *uneven distribution* of $F_c$ across the various tube rows
> - $J_R$ → accounts for viscosity stratification (laminar regime)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the five correction factors used in Bell's method for estimating shell-side heat transfer coefficient and state the physical loss each represents."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five correction factors used in Bell's Method are:

1. **$J_C$ — Baffle cut correction factor**: accounts for the reduction in effective cross-flow area when the segmental baffle cut fraction $B_c$ reduces the available flow window.
2. **$J_B$ — Bundle bypass correction factor**: accounts for fluid flowing in the longitudinal gap between the tube bundle envelope and the shell wall, bypassing the tubes without heat transfer.
3. **$J_L$ — Leakage correction factor**: accounts for fluid that leaks through the tube-to-baffle hole clearances ($F_{tb}$) and the baffle-to-shell clearances ($F_{sb}$), again avoiding useful heat transfer.
4. **$J_S$ — Non-uniform lane distribution correction factor**: accounts for unequal velocities in the various tube-row lanes, which is important at low Reynolds numbers.
5. **$J_R$ — Adverse temperature gradient correction factor**: accounts for the viscosity variation across the tube bundle in laminar flow, which causes flow maldistribution.

All five factors are dimensionless and bounded in the interval $(0, 1]$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is the simple Kern method of estimating shell-side heat transfer coefficient often inadequate, and how does Bell's method overcome this limitation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Kern method** assumes that the shell-side fluid flows in an *ideal cross-flow* pattern perpendicular to a *clean, infinite tube bank*. It ignores the realities of:

- Baffle-shell leakage
- Tube-to-baffle leakage
- Bundle bypass stream
- Variable tube-row lane velocities
- Property changes across the bundle (laminar)

Because of this, Kern tends to **over-predict** the shell-side coefficient by **30–60%** in real exchangers.

**Bell's method** overcomes this by:

- Starting from the same ideal cross-flow correlation, then applying **five multiplicative penalty factors** ($J_C, J_B, J_L, J_S, J_R$), each one derived from rigorous mass and momentum balances over the actual exchanger geometry.
- Quantitatively accounting for **leakage, bypass, and non-uniformity** with closure relations from the University of Delaware experimental database.
- Providing **acceptable accuracy (±20%)** for design and rating of industrial heat exchangers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations for PBCHT504, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two full alternatives. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are completely independent — students answer one.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to be designed with the following data:**
- Shell ID: $D_s = 540$ mm
- Tubes: $d_o = 25$ mm, $d_i = 20$ mm, $k_w = 50$ W/(m·K), $L = 3$ m
- Tube pitch: $p_t = 32$ mm, **triangular $30°$ layout**
- Total tube count: $N_t = 150$
- Baffle cut: $B_c = 0.25$, baffle spacing $l_B = 220$ mm
- Tube passes: $N_p = 2$
- Shell-side fluid (water): $\dot{m}_s = 6$ kg/s, $\mu_s = 6.5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k_s = 0.61$ W/(m·K), $c_{ps} = 4180$ J/(kg·K)
- Tube-side coefficient: $h_i = 2200$ W/(m²·K)
- Fouling factors: $R_{d,i} = R_{d,o} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W

**(a) [7 marks]** Using Bell's method, determine the shell-side heat transfer coefficient $h_o$ for turbulent flow. Adopt $D_{otl} = 0.42$ m, $j_H = 0.33 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4}$, and the Bell correction factors $J_C = 0.73$, $J_B = 0.90$, $J_L = 0.85$, $J_S = 0.95$, $J_R = 1.00$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **clean overall heat transfer coefficient** $U_C$ and the **design overall coefficient** $U_D$ after including fouling and tube wall resistance.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Shell-Side Coefficient via Bell's Method**

**(i) Cross-flow area:**
$$S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl}) = 0.220 \times (0.540 - 0.420) = 0.220 \times 0.120 = 0.0264 \; \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Equivalent diameter (triangular pitch):**
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \, p_t^2}{d_o} - d_o = \frac{1.1027 \times (0.032)^2}{0.025} - 0.025$$
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \times 0.001024}{0.025} - 0.025 = \frac{0.001129}{0.025} - 0.025 = 0.04517 - 0.025 = 0.02017 \; \text{m}$$

**(iii) Shell-side mass velocity:**
$$G_s = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{S_m} = \frac{6}{0.0264} = 227.27 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**(iv) Shell-side Reynolds number:**
$$\text{Re}_s = \frac{G_s D_e}{\mu_s} = \frac{227.27 \times 0.02017}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4.585}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7054$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using $D_s$ instead of $D_e$ in the Reynolds calculation. This *doubles* the Re number and pushes the student into the wrong regime of $j_H$. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this error.

Since $\text{Re}_s = 7054 < 10\,000$, technically we are in **transition regime**. For a board exam, use the laminar formula since the problem does not specify transition charts:

$$j_H = 1.4 \, \text{Re}_s^{-0.4} = 1.4 \times 7054^{-0.4}$$
$$\log_{10}(7054) = 3.8485 \;\Rightarrow\; -0.4 \times 3.8485 = -1.539$$
$$7054^{-0.4} = 10^{-1.539} = 0.02891$$
$$j_H = 1.4 \times 0.02891 = 0.0405$$

**(v) Prandtl number:**
$$\text{Pr}_s = \frac{c_{ps} \mu_s}{k_s} = \frac{4180 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.61} = \frac{2.717}{0.61} = 4.454$$
$$\text{Pr}_s^{1/3} = 4.454^{1/3} = 1.647$$

**(vi) Ideal cross-flow coefficient:**
$$h_{ideal} = j_H \cdot \frac{k_s}{D_e} \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$$
$$h_{ideal} = 0.0405 \times \frac{0.61}{0.02017} \times 7054 \times 1.647$$
$$= 0.0405 \times 30.24 \times 7054 \times 1.647$$
$$= 0.0405 \times 30.24 = 1.2247$$
$$1.2247 \times 7054 = 8639$$
$$8639 \times 1.647 = 14\,228 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**(vii) Apply Bell's correction factors:**
$$h_o = h_{ideal} \cdot J_C \cdot J_B \cdot J_L \cdot J_S \cdot J_R$$
$$h_o = 14\,228 \times 0.73 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 1.00$$
$$= 14\,228 \times 0.530 = 7541 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

$$\boxed{h_o \approx 7541 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}}$$

**Part (b) — Clean and Design Overall Coefficients**

**(i) Inside coefficient referred to outer area:**
$$h_{io} = h_i \cdot \frac{d_i}{d_o} = 2200 \times \frac{0.020}{0.025} = 2200 \times 0.80 = 1760 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**(ii) Tube wall resistance:**
$$x_w = \frac{d_o - d_i}{2} = \frac{0.025 - 0.020}{2} = 0.0025 \; \text{m}$$
$$R_w = \frac{x_w}{k_w} = \frac{0.0025}{50} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**(iii) Clean overall coefficient $U_C$ (no fouling, no wall):**
$$\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{h_{io}} + \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{1760} + \frac{1}{7541}$$
$$= 5.682 \times 10^{-4} + 1.326 \times 10^{-4} = 7.008 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$U_C = \frac{1}{7.008 \times 10^{-4}} = 1427 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

$$\boxed{U_C \approx 1427 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}}$$

**(iv) Design overall coefficient $U_D$ (with fouling + wall):**
$$\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{h_{io}} + R_{d,i} + R_w + R_{d,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$
$$\frac{1}{U_D} = 7.008 \times 10^{-4} + 0.0002 + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 0.0002$$
$$= 7.008 \times 10^{-4} + 4.50 \times 10^{-4} = 1.151 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$U_D = \frac{1}{1.151 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 869 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

$$\boxed{U_D \approx 869 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}}$$

**Result Interpretation:**
- $U_C \approx 1427$ W/(m²·K) — pure convective capacity (clean tubes).
- $U_D \approx 869$ W/(m²·K) — accounting for real-world fouling and wall.
- Fouling penalty = $1 - U_D/U_C = 1 - 869/1427 = 39.1\%$ — typical for water service.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a clearly labelled sketch, explain the **four parallel flow streams** that occur in the shell side of a segmentally baffled heat exchanger. State which Bell correction factor controls each stream.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a 2-pass shell-and-tube exchanger with $D_s = 800$ mm, $d_o = 19$ mm, $d_i = 15$ mm, $p_t = 25$ mm (triangular), $N_t = 320$, $l_B = 300$ mm, $B_c = 0.25$, $\dot{m}_s = 12$ kg/s, $\mu_s = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k_s = 0.65$ W/(m·K), $c_{ps} = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $D_{otl} = 0.66$ m, $J_C = 0.73$, $J_B = 0.92$, $J_L = 0.88$, $J_S = 0.96$, $J_R = 1.00$, compute $h_o$ using Bell's method.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Four Parallel Shell-Side Flow Streams [7 marks]**

| # | Flow Stream | Physical Description | Bell Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cross-flow stream** $F_c$ | Fluid that flows perpendicular to the tube bundle between two adjacent segmental baffles; this is the *useful* stream doing the heat transfer. | $J_C$ (baffle cut effect) and $J_S$ (lane distribution) |
| 2 | **Tube-to-baffle leakage** $F_{tb}$ | Fluid leaking through the annular clearance between the tube OD and the baffle hole ID (typically ~0.4 mm). | $J_L$ (leakage factor) |
| 3 | **Baffle-to-shell leakage** $F_{sb}$ | Fluid leaking through the diametrical clearance between the baffle outer edge and the shell ID. | $J_L$ (leakage factor) |
| 4 | **Bundle bypass stream** $F_{bp}$ | Fluid flowing in the longitudinal gap between the outer tube row and the shell wall, completely bypassing the bundle. | $J_B$ (bypass factor) |

The sum of all four streams equals the total shell-side mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_s = F_c + F_{tb} + F_{sb} + F_{bp}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch (described in text form):** Draw a circular shell with a horizontal segmental baffle cut from top. Show the tube bundle (small circles) packed inside. Mark with arrows: (i) a perpendicular arrow through the bundle = cross-flow $F_c$; (ii) small arrows around the perimeter of baffle = $F_{sb}$; (iii) tiny arrows around tube holes in baffle = $F_{tb}$; (iv) longitudinal arrows in the gap between the outermost tube row and the shell wall = $F_{bp}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Cross-flow area:**
$$S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl}) = 0.300 \times (0.800 - 0.660) = 0.300 \times 0.140 = 0.0420 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent diameter (triangular):**
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \, p_t^2}{d_o} - d_o = \frac{1.1027 \times (0.025)^2}{0.019} - 0.019$$
$$D_e = \frac{1.1027 \times 0.000625}{0.019} - 0.019 = \frac{6.892 \times 10^{-4}}{0.019} - 0.019 = 0.03627 - 0.019 = 0.01727 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Mass velocity and Reynolds number:**
$$G_s = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{S_m} = \frac{12}{0.0420} = 285.71 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$
$$\text{Re}_s = \frac{G_s D_e}{\mu_s} = \frac{285.71 \times 0.01727}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4.934}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 9868$$

Since $\text{Re}_s \approx 10\,000$ — borderline. We use the turbulent form as the closer approximation:

$$j_H = 0.33 \times (9868)^{-0.4}$$
$$\log_{10}(9868) = 3.994 \;\Rightarrow\; -0.4 \times 3.994 = -1.598$$
$$9868^{-0.4} = 10^{-1.598} = 0.02521$$
$$j_H = 0.33 \times 0.02521 = 0.00832$$

**Step 4 — Prandtl number:**
$$\text{Pr}_s = \frac{4180 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.65} = \frac{2.090}{0.65} = 3.215$$
$$\text{Pr}_s^{1/3} = 3.215^{1/3} = 1.476$$

**Step 5 — Ideal cross-flow coefficient:**
$$h_{ideal} = 0.00832 \times \frac{0.65}{0.01727} \times 9868 \times 1.476$$
$$= 0.00832 \times 37.64 \times 9868 \times 1.476$$
$$= 0.00832 \times 37.64 = 0.3132$$
$$0.3132 \times 9868 = 3090$$
$$3090 \times 1.476 = 4561 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Bell's corrections:**
$$h_o = 4561 \times J_C \times J_B \times J_L \times J_S \times J_R$$
$$h_o = 4561 \times 0.73 \times 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.96 \times 1.00$$

Calculate step by step:
- $0.73 \times 0.92 = 0.6716$
- $0.6716 \times 0.88 = 0.5910$
- $0.5910 \times 0.96 = 0.5674$
- $\times 1.00 = 0.5674$

$$h_o = 4561 \times 0.5674 = 2588 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

$$\boxed{h_o \approx 2588 \; \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the ideal cross-flow coefficient is ~4561 W/(m²·K), the corrected (real) shell-side coefficient drops by ~43% to ~2588 W/(m²·K) — a typical magnitude for industrial exchanger de-rating.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Bell's Method Problems (Highest to Lowest Frequency):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D_s$ with $D_e$ in the Reynolds number** — this is the *single most common mistake*. Always use $D_e$ (the equivalent diameter of the cross-flow stream), not the shell ID. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $h_i$ to outer-area basis** — using $h_i$ directly instead of $h_{io} = h_i d_i / d_o$ when summing resistances. This produces an error of $\sim$20–25% in $U_D$. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Using $D_s$ instead of $D_{otl}$** in the cross-flow area $S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl})$. This overstates the gap and *under-predicts* $h_o$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ term** in the Colburn analogy — students often write $h_{ideal} = j_H \cdot (k/D_e) \cdot \text{Re}$, missing the Prandtl correction. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Mixing up Bell factor values** — confusing $J_L$ with $J_B$, or applying $J_R$ to turbulent flow (it should be 1.00). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Not stating the regime** (laminar vs. turbulent) before choosing $j_H$. **Penalty: 0.5 mark (soft penalty).**
>
> 7. **Unit inconsistencies** — mixing mm with m, or cm with m, in the geometric calculations. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Carry into the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Bell's Method estimates the *shell-side* coefficient $h_o$** — the tube-side coefficient $h_i$ is computed separately from Dittus–Boelter or Sieder–Tate.
- ✅ The overall $U_D$ is computed by adding five thermal resistances in series: **1/$h_{io}$ + $R_{d,i}$ + $x_w/k_w$ + $R_{d,o}$ + 1/$h_o$**.
- ✅ The five Bell correction factors — **$J_C$ (baffle cut), $J_B$ (bypass), $J_L$ (leakage), $J_S$ (lane distribution), $J_R$ (adverse ΔT)** — multiply the *ideal* cross-flow coefficient to give the *real* $h_o$.
- ✅ The cross-flow area $S_m = l_B (D_s - D_{otl})$ — **never** use the full shell cross-section.
- ✅ The equivalent diameter $D_e$ is the **hydraulic diameter of the cross-flow gap**, not the shell ID. For triangular pitch: $D_e = (1.1027 p_t^2)/d_o - d_o$. For square pitch: $D_e = (1.2732 p_t^2)/d_o - d_o$.
- ✅ The Reynolds number **uses $D_e$, not $D_s$** — the #1 exam trap.
- ✅ The Colburn analogy gives $h_{ideal} = j_H \cdot (k_s/D_e) \cdot \text{Re}_s \cdot \text{Pr}_s^{1/3}$.
- ✅ Typical Bell correction values are **between 0.5 and 0.95** — the product of all five is usually **0.5–0.7** for well-designed exchangers.
- ✅ Always convert $h_i$ to outer-area basis: $h_{io} = h_i \cdot (d_i/d_o)$.
- ✅ The design margin $U_C \to U_D$ typically drops by **20–40%** when fouling is included.
- ✅ **Bell's method is more accurate than Kern's** because it accounts for leakage, bypass, and non-uniform flow.
- ✅ Memorize the dimensionless groups: **Re, Pr, Nusselt (Nu = $h D_e/k$), Colburn $j_H$**.
- ✅ The standard physical constant to remember: **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** is rarely needed in Bell's method but appears in pressure-drop calculations; **$R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)** is also typically not used.
- ✅ **Bell's method is valid for both design and rating** — design uses assumed geometry to predict $U$; rating uses actual geometry to verify $U$ for an existing unit.
- ✅ Fouling factors are **empirical, tabulated in TEMA** for common services (water, oils, gases, refrigerants).
- ✅ The five-stream decomposition of shell flow (**cross-flow + tube-baffle leakage + shell-baffle leakage + bypass**) is the conceptual heart of Bell's method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Drop in Shell Side and Tube Side
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Drop in Shell Side and Tube Side

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger is the mechanical energy loss sustained by the process fluid as it flows across the **tube side** (inside the tubes) or the **shell side** (across the shell, over the tube bundle, and through baffle windows). It is expressed in **Pa**, **kPa**, or **bar**, and is the cumulative result of friction losses, sudden contraction/expansion losses, and form (bend) losses.

For a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger**:
- **Tube-side pressure drop (ΔPt):** Frictional loss along the tube length + losses in the **return bends (U-bends)** + entrance/exit losses.
- **Shell-side pressure drop (ΔPs):** Friction loss in **cross-flow over the tube bundle** + friction loss in the **baffle window** + leakage, bypass, and bypass-stream losses (Delaware-Bell corrections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Learning Outcome (CO1):** *Design shell-and-tube heat exchangers, including the estimation of shell-side and tube-side heat transfer coefficients and pressure drops, by integrating Kern's / Delaware-Bell correlations and the standard equivalent-diameter concept.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-storey car park with cars entering on the **ground floor**:
- The **tube side** is like driving through a long, narrow spiral ramp. The longer the ramp and the more turns (return bends), the more fuel (energy/pressure) is consumed.
- The **shell side** is like driving through a *car park filled with concrete pillars* (the tubes). The driver must weave between pillars, and each time the driver crosses a row of pillars, the steering effort (pressure drop) increases. The **baffles** are like partial walls placed between rows of pillars that force the car to change direction more often.

The **allowable pressure drop** is the *maximum fuel budget* the company is willing to spend on pumping the fluid. If ΔP calculated > ΔP allowable, the design must be revised (more tubes, larger shell, or different baffle spacing).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU Board-Examiner favorite):** For liquids, allowable ΔP ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 bar**; for gases, **0.01 – 0.1 bar**; for condensing/boiling services, **0.1 – 0.3 bar**. Optimization of heat-exchanger cost vs. pump/compressor operating cost is what selects the *exact* allowable value.

---

## 1.3 Why Pressure Drop Matters in Design

| Engineering Concern | Role of Pressure Drop |
|---|---|
| **Pumping Cost** | Operating cost of the pump/compressor is proportional to **V̇ × ΔP** (brake-horsepower). |
| **Tube Vibration / Erosion** | Excessive shell-side velocity causes tube vibration and erosion at the baffle holes. |
| **Flow Maldistribution** | High ΔP can mask maldistribution, reducing the effective heat transfer area. |
| **Boiling/Condensing Limit** | If local pressure falls below saturation, premature boiling or flashing occurs. |
| **Mechanical Design** | Higher shell-side ΔP implies heavier shell, thicker baffles, larger flanges, raising capital cost. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tube-Side Pressure Drop — Step-by-Step Logic

The tube-side fluid experiences four loss mechanisms in series:

1. **Straight-tube friction** — depends on length L, inside diameter Di, velocity ut.
2. **Return-bend (U-bend) loss** — depends on the number of tube passes Np.
3. **Entrance and exit losses** — usually small; often lumped with bends.
4. **Nozzle losses** — at the channel head inlet/outlet; can be ignored if the velocity head is < 5% of the total.

The general form used in KTU board examinations is:

$$
\Delta P_{t} \;=\; 4 \, f \!\left(\dfrac{L}{D_i}\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{-0.14}\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2} \;+\; 4 N_p\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}
$$

where:
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (from Moody's / Donohue chart) for a smooth tube
- $L$ = effective tube length per pass
- $D_i$ = inside tube diameter
- $\mu/\mu_w$ = viscosity correction (bulk / wall)
- $u_t$ = tube-side velocity
- $N_p$ = number of tube passes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board accepts the **equivalent-length method** as an alternative: add an *equivalent length* of 30–60 tube diameters per U-bend to the straight length, and use the simple $4f(L/D_i)(\rho u_t^2/2)$ form without the bend term. (This is the form seen in Sinnott & Towler.)

## 2.2 Shell-Side Pressure Drop — Step-by-Step Logic

The shell-side flow is *far more complex*: the fluid alternates between **cross-flow** through the tube bundle and **flow through the baffle window**. Two industry-standard methods are tested by KTU:

### Method A — **Kern's Method (1950)**
Treats the shell as a *simple baffled tube bundle* and uses a single cross-flow area and a single equivalent diameter.

### Method B — **Delaware-Bell Method (Kern's refinement)**
Uses an *ideal* cross-flow ΔP from the tube-bank correlation, then multiplies by **five correction factors** to account for real shell geometry. This is the method preferred in industrial practice (HTRI, TEMA).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

### A. Tube Side

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mass velocity | $G_t = m_t / (N_t \cdot a_t)$ | kg m$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Tube velocity | $u_t = G_t / \rho$ | m s$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Reynolds number | $Re_t = G_t D_i / \mu = \rho u_t D_i / \mu$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Darcy friction factor (Donohue) | $f = 0.0014 + 0.125 Re_t^{-0.32}$ (for $Re_t < 2 \times 10^5$, smooth tubes) | dimensionless |
| 5 | Friction pressure drop | $\Delta P_{f} = 4 f (L/D_i)(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}(\rho u_t^{2}/2)$ | Pa |
| 6 | Return-bend loss | $\Delta P_{bend} = 4 N_p (\rho u_t^{2}/2)$ | Pa |
| 7 | Total tube-side ΔP | $\Delta P_t = \Delta P_f + \Delta P_{bend}$ | Pa |

### B. Shell Side — Kern's Method

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Cross-flow area | $A_s = (P_T - D_o)\, B\, D_s / P_T$ | m$^2$ |
| 2 | Shell-side velocity | $u_s = m_s / (\rho A_s)$ | m s$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Equivalent diameter — triangular pitch | $D_e = \dfrac{4(0.866 P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4)}{\pi D_o}$ | m |
| 4 | Equivalent diameter — square pitch | $D_e = \dfrac{4(P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4)}{\pi D_o}$ | m |
| 5 | Shell Reynolds | $Re_s = D_e u_s \rho / \mu$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Friction factor (Kern chart) | $f_s$ from Kern chart or Donohue: $f_s = 0.23 Re_s^{-0.2}$ for $Re_s > 3000$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Number of crosses (1-N shell) | $N_c = N + 1$ (for N-pass shell) | — |
| 8 | Total shell-side ΔP | $\Delta P_s = f_s (D_s / D_e) (\rho u_s^{2}/2)(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14} (N+1)$ | Pa |

### C. Shell Side — Delaware-Bell Method

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; \Delta P_{ideal}\,\times\, (1 + F_{lp})\,(1 + F_{bp})\,F_b\,F_{ld}
$$

| Factor | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $F_{lp}$ | **Leakage through tube-to-baffle holes** (inter-stream leakage) |
| $F_{bp}$ | **Bypass** around the bundle through the shell-to-baffle gap |
| $F_b$ | **Baffle-cut correction** (fractional cut $B_c/D_s$ between 0.20–0.45) |
| $F_{ld}$ | **Leakage to the shell** (tube-to-shell bypass during cross-flow) |

The ideal (tube-bank) ΔP is read from the same Kern chart as $f_s(D_s/D_e)(\rho u_s^2/2)(N+1)$.

> [!TIP]
> **ΔP_allowable vs. ΔP_calculated** — A KTU numerical answer that does *not* comment on the ratio is considered incomplete. Always conclude: *"Calculated ΔP = X Pa, Allowable ΔP = Y Pa → X < Y, design is safe"* (or revise if X > Y).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Refinery crude preheat trains**: ΔP on the crude side is limited to ~**3.5 bar** because of upstream pump head.
- **Air-cooled condensers**: ΔP on the air side is limited to ~**250 Pa** by fan power.
- **Plate-fin exchangers (cryogenic)**: Shell-side ΔP is typically < **5 kPa** to keep boil-off losses low.
- **Process safety**: A high ΔP can invert the flow direction of safety relief valves, so ΔP enters HAZOP discussions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tube-Side Pressure-Drop Equation

The energy balance on an elemental tube slice of length $\mathrm{d}L$ under steady, fully developed flow gives the **Fanning friction relation**:

$$
\mathrm{d}P \;=\; \dfrac{2 f_{Fanning}\, \rho u_t^{2}}{D_i}\,\mathrm{d}L
$$

Integrating over the effective tube length $L$:

$$
P_{in} - P_{out} \;=\; \Delta P_{straight} \;=\; \dfrac{2 f_{Fanning}\, L\, \rho u_t^{2}}{D_i}
$$

Most heat-exchanger texts use the **Darcy friction factor** $f = 4 f_{Fanning}$. Substituting:

$$
\Delta P_{straight} \;=\; \dfrac{4 f\, L\, \rho u_t^{2}}{2 D_i}
$$

The **wall-viscosity correction** Sieder–Tate factor $(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ is introduced because the viscosity at the wall differs from the bulk. For heating of liquid, $\mu_w < \mu_b$, and the correction is < 1, slightly *reducing* ΔP:

$$
\Delta P_{straight} \;=\; 4 f \!\left(\dfrac{L}{D_i}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{\!-0.14}\!\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}
$$

The **return-bend loss** is modeled as an equivalent sudden expansion (loss coefficient $K = 4$ per bend), so for $N_p$ passes:

$$
\Delta P_{bend} \;=\; K\,\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}\,N_p \;=\; 4 N_p \dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}
$$

The **total tube-side pressure drop** is the sum:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_t \;=\; 4 f \!\left(\dfrac{L}{D_i}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{\!-0.14}\!\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2} \;+\; 4 N_p \dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Shell-Side Cross-Flow Area (Kern)

The cross-flow area is the open area between adjacent tubes in one row, multiplied by the bundle width and the number of rows per cross.

For a triangular pitch, the tube-to-tube centre distance is $P_T$. The clear gap between adjacent tubes is $P_T - D_o$. For a single row of tubes of axial length equal to the baffle spacing $B$:

$$
A_{s,\text{per row}} \;=\; (P_T - D_o)\, B
$$

The number of *rows* crossed in going from one baffle tip to the next is approximately $D_s / P_T$ (where $D_s$ is the shell inside diameter). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;A_s \;=\; \dfrac{(P_T - D_o)\, B\, D_s}{P_T}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style, 14 marks)

> **Problem.** A 1–2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to cool an organic liquid. The shell side handles cooling water at $m_s = 6.5$ kg/s. Compute the **shell-side pressure drop** using **Kern's method**.
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Shell inside diameter $D_s = 0.540$ m
> - Tube outer / inner diameter $D_o = 0.025$ m, $D_i = 0.020$ m
> - Triangular pitch $P_T = 31.25$ mm $= 0.03125$ m
> - Baffle spacing $B = 0.180$ m
> - Cooling water density $\rho = 995$ kg/m³
> - Cooling water viscosity $\mu = 0.0008$ Pa·s (≈ 50 °C)
> - Wall viscosity $\mu_w = 0.0006$ Pa·s
> - Number of shell passes $N = 1$ ⇒ crosses $N_c = N + 1 = 2$

**Step 1 — Cross-flow area $A_s$**

$$
A_s \;=\; \dfrac{(P_T - D_o)\, B\, D_s}{P_T}
$$

$$
A_s \;=\; \dfrac{(0.03125 - 0.025)(0.180)(0.540)}{0.03125}
$$

$$
A_s \;=\; \dfrac{(0.00625)(0.180)(0.540)}{0.03125} \;=\; \dfrac{6.075 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03125}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A_s \;=\; 1.944 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Shell-side velocity $u_s$**

$$
u_s \;=\; \dfrac{m_s}{\rho\, A_s} \;=\; \dfrac{6.5}{(995)(1.944 \times 10^{-2})}
$$

$$
u_s \;=\; \dfrac{6.5}{19.34} \;=\; 0.3361 \text{ m s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent diameter $D_e$ (triangular pitch)**

$$
D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4\!\left(0.866 P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4\right)}{\pi D_o}
$$

Compute $P_T^{2}$:

$$
P_T^{2} = (0.03125)^{2} = 9.766 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Compute $\pi D_o^{2}/4$:

$$
\dfrac{\pi (0.025)^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (6.25 \times 10^{-4})}{4} \;=\; 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Substitute:

$$
D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4 \left[0.866 (9.766 \times 10^{-4}) - 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\right]}{\pi (0.025)}
$$

$$
D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4 \left[8.457 \times 10^{-4} - 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\right]}{0.07854}
$$

$$
D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4 (3.548 \times 10^{-4})}{0.07854} \;=\; \dfrac{1.4192 \times 10^{-3}}{0.07854}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;D_e \;=\; 0.01807 \text{ m}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Shell-side Reynolds number $Re_s$**

$$
Re_s \;=\; \dfrac{D_e\, u_s\, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.01807)(0.3361)(995)}{0.0008}
$$

$$
Re_s \;=\; \dfrac{6.045}{0.0008} \;=\; 7.556 \times 10^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Shell-side friction factor $f_s$ (Kern / Donohue, turbulent, smooth)**

For $Re_s > 3 \times 10^{3}$, Donohue's approximation for cross-flow over a tube bank:

$$
f_s \;=\; 0.23\, Re_s^{-0.2}
$$

$$
f_s \;=\; 0.23 \times (7.556 \times 10^{3})^{-0.2}
$$

Compute exponent:

$$
\ln(7.556 \times 10^{3}) = 8.9308
$$
$$
-0.2 \times 8.9308 = -1.7862
$$
$$
(7.556 \times 10^{3})^{-0.2} = e^{-1.7862} = 0.1677
$$

$$
f_s \;=\; 0.23 \times 0.1677 \;=\; 0.0386
$$

**Step 6 — Viscosity correction factor**

$$
\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{0.0008}{0.0006}\right)^{0.14} \;=\; (1.333)^{0.14}
$$

$$
\ln(1.333) = 0.2877 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.14 \times 0.2877 = 0.0403 \;\Rightarrow\; e^{0.0403} = 1.0411
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14} \;=\; 1.0411
$$

**Step 7 — Dynamic pressure (velocity head)**

$$
\dfrac{\rho u_s^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{995 \times (0.3361)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{995 \times 0.1130}{2} \;=\; 56.21 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 8 — Total shell-side pressure drop**

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; f_s \left(\dfrac{D_s}{D_e}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}\!\dfrac{\rho u_s^{2}}{2}\,(N+1)
$$

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; 0.0386 \times \dfrac{0.540}{0.01807} \times 1.0411 \times 56.21 \times 2
$$

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; 0.0386 \times 29.88 \times 1.0411 \times 56.21 \times 2
$$

Multiply step by step:

$$
0.0386 \times 29.88 = 1.1533
$$
$$
1.1533 \times 1.0411 = 1.2007
$$
$$
1.2007 \times 56.21 = 67.49
$$
$$
67.49 \times 2 = 134.98 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_s \;\approx\; 135 \text{ Pa} \;=\; 0.00135 \text{ bar}\;}
$$

**Step 9 — Comparison with Allowable ΔP**

For liquid cooling water service, typical allowable ΔP $\approx$ 0.5 bar = 50 000 Pa.

$$
\Delta P_s^{calc} = 135 \text{ Pa} \;\ll\; \Delta P_{allow} = 50\,000 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Design verdict:** The shell-side pressure drop is *less than 0.3%* of the allowable. The exchanger is grossly over-designed on the shell side. **Design optimization is required** (e.g., reduce baffle spacing, reduce tube count, or reduce shell diameter).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tube vs. Shell Side

| Parameter | Tube Side | Shell Side |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Smooth, straight, circular | Complex (tubes + baffles + shell) |
| Flow | Unidirectional, well-defined | Cross-flow + window flow + leakage |
| Reynolds number | $G_t D_i / \mu$ | $D_e u_s \rho / \mu$ |
| Characteristic diameter | $D_i$ (tube ID) | $D_e$ (equivalent) |
| Cross-flow area | $a_t \cdot N_t$ (per pass) | $(P_T - D_o) B D_s / P_T$ |
| Loss term 1 | Straight-tube friction | Cross-flow friction |
| Loss term 2 | Return bend ($K = 4$ each) | Window flow |
| Loss term 3 | Entrance/exit nozzle | Leakage + bypass (Delaware-Bell) |
| Typical ΔP | 10–70 kPa (liquids) | 5–50 kPa (liquids) |
| Standard correlation | Darcy (Moody) | Kern chart / Delaware-Bell |
| Viscosity correction | $(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | $(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ |
| Typical KTU design target | < 70 kPa | < 50 kPa |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Calculation Flowchart — Pressure-Drop Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Geometry and Flow Rates Known] --> B{Which Side?}
    B -- Tube Side --> C1[Compute Mass Flux G_t]
    B -- Shell Side --> D1[Compute Cross-Flow Area A_s]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Re_t = G_t D_i / mu]
    C2 --> C3[Read f from Moody or Donohue]
    C3 --> C4[Compute Friction Loss 4f L over D_i times rho u_t squared over 2]
    C4 --> C5[Add Return Bend Loss 4 N_p rho u_t squared over 2]
    C5 --> C6[Total Tube Side Delta P_t]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Shell Velocity u_s = m_s over rho A_s]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Equivalent Diameter D_e]
    D3 --> D4[Compute Re_s = D_e u_s rho over mu]
    D4 --> D5{Method?}
    D5 -- Kern --> E1[Use Kern chart for f_s]
    D5 -- Delaware Bell --> E2[Compute Ideal Delta P then multiply by 1 plus F_lp, 1 plus F_bp, F_b, F_ld]
    E1 --> F1[Total Shell Side Delta P_s]
    E2 --> F1
    C6 --> G1[Compare with Allowable Delta P]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{Delta P less or equal Delta P_allowable?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Design Accepted]
    H1 -- No --> J1[Revise Geometry: Increase B, Increase N, Reduce D_s]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Fluid Streams in a 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shell-Side Inlet: Cooling Water] --> B[Baffle 1 Window]
    B --> C[Cross-Flow Across Tube Bundle]
    C --> D[Baffle 2 Tip Cross-Flow]
    D --> E[Baffle 2 Window]
    E --> F[Cross-Flow Across Tubes]
    F --> G[Baffle 3 Tip Cross-Flow]
    G --> H[Baffle 3 Window]
    H --> I[Shell-Side Outlet]
    T1[Tube-Side Inlet: Hot Organic] --> R1[Pass 1: Top Tubes]
    R1 --> R2[Return Bend 1]
    R2 --> R3[Pass 2: Bottom Tubes]
    R3 --> R4[Tube-Side Outlet]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Delaware-Bell Correction Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process Inputs: G_s, D_e, Re_s, B, B_c, D_s, D_otl] --> IDEAL[Compute Ideal Cross-Flow Delta P from Kern Correlation]
    IDEAL --> LEAK[Add Tube-Baffle Leakage Stream F_lp]
    LEAK --> BYPASS[Add Bypass Stream F_bp]
    BYPASS --> BCUT[Multiply by Baffle-Cut Factor F_b]
    BCUT --> BSHELL[Multiply by Shell-Bundle Leakage Factor F_ld]
    BSHELL --> FINAL[Output: Delta P_s]
```

> *Below is a textual cross-section of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger, showing the alternating **cross-flow** segments and the **baffle-window** segments. Each cross-flow segment contributes one full cross; each window segment adds a separate loss.*

| Inlet nozzle | Direction change | Minor (~0.5 velocity heads) |
| Window 1 | Tube-free area between baffle and shell wall | Window friction loss |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define equivalent diameter for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with triangular tube pitch and state its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The equivalent diameter $D_e$ is the diameter of an *imaginary circular pipe* whose hydraulic behaviour (friction factor vs. Re curve) matches that of the actual non-circular flow cross-section formed between the tubes and the shell wall.
>
> For **triangular pitch**:
> $$
> D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4\!\left(0.866 P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4\right)}{\pi D_o}
> $$
>
> **Physical significance:** It enables the use of standard *circular-pipe* friction-factor and heat-transfer correlations on a *non-circular* cross-section. It is the *characteristic length* that scales the Reynolds number on the shell side.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the role of the viscosity correction factor $(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ in the pressure-drop calculation? Why is the exponent 0.14?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. The bulk viscosity $\mu_b$ and the viscosity at the tube wall $\mu_w$ differ because the wall temperature is not equal to the bulk fluid temperature.
> 2. For *heating* of a liquid, $\mu_w < \mu_b$ → the correction is < 1 → reduces ΔP.
> 3. For *cooling* of a liquid, $\mu_w > \mu_b$ → the correction is > 1 → increases ΔP.
> 4. The exponent **0.14** is the empirical Sieder–Tate exponent obtained from experimental heat-transfer data on viscous fluids in tubes. It is *not* a theoretical value but a best-fit slope that simultaneously corrects friction, velocity profile, and temperature-profile effects.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** A 1–2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense a hydrocarbon vapour. Cooling water flows through the shell. Calculate the **shell-side pressure drop** using **Kern's method** and verify if the design is safe.

**Given Data:**
- Shell inside diameter $D_s = 0.6$ m
- Tube outer / inner diameter $D_o = 25$ mm, $D_i = 20$ mm
- Triangular pitch $P_T = 31.25$ mm
- Baffle spacing $B = 0.15$ m
- Cooling water mass flow rate $m_s = 8$ kg/s
- Density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- Viscosity $\mu_b = 0.001$ Pa·s, $\mu_w = 0.0008$ Pa·s
- Allowable shell-side ΔP = 70 kPa

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Kern's method** for shell-side heat transfer and pressure drop. List **two** limitations of this method.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Cross-flow area**

$$
A_s \;=\; \dfrac{(P_T - D_o)\, B\, D_s}{P_T} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.03125 - 0.025)(0.15)(0.6)}{0.03125} \;=\; 1.80 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Shell-side velocity**

$$
u_s \;=\; \dfrac{m_s}{\rho A_s} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{1000 \times 1.80 \times 10^{-2}} \;=\; 0.444 \text{ m s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Equivalent diameter (triangular pitch)**

$$
D_e \;=\; \dfrac{4(0.866 \times (0.03125)^{2} - \pi (0.025)^{2}/4)}{\pi (0.025)} \;=\; 0.01807 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Shell-side Reynolds number**

$$
Re_s \;=\; \dfrac{D_e\, u_s\, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{0.01807 \times 0.444 \times 1000}{0.001} \;=\; 8.023 \times 10^{3}
$$

**Step 5: Friction factor (Kern / Donohue for $Re > 3\,000$)**

$$
f_s \;=\; 0.23 \times (8.023 \times 10^{3})^{-0.2} \;=\; 0.23 \times 0.1655 \;=\; 0.0381
$$

**Step 6: Viscosity correction**

$$
\left(\dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14} \;=\; (1.25)^{0.14} \;=\; 1.0324
$$

**Step 7: Dynamic pressure**

$$
\dfrac{\rho u_s^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times (0.444)^{2}}{2} \;=\; 98.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 8: Total shell-side ΔP (1-2 exchanger ⇒ 2 crosses)**

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; 0.0381 \times \dfrac{0.6}{0.01807} \times 1.0324 \times 98.6 \times 2
$$

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; 0.0381 \times 33.20 \times 1.0324 \times 98.6 \times 2 \;=\; 257.5 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 0.26 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 9: Verification**

$$
\Delta P_s^{calc} = 0.26 \text{ kPa} \;\ll\; \Delta P_{allow} = 70 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Design verdict:** Safe, but with very large margin. Consider reducing baffle spacing to improve heat transfer, then re-check ΔP.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Kern's Method (Sketch + Explanation):**

Kern (1950) idealises the shell-side flow as alternating *cross-flow* segments (fluid cuts through the tube bundle perpendicular to the tubes, between two adjacent baffle tips) and *window* segments (fluid passes through the gap between the bundle and the shell wall at the baffle cut-out). Both segments are characterized by the **same equivalent diameter** $D_e$ and the **same cross-flow velocity** $u_s$ (the window segment is treated as having the same velocity, though in practice it is higher).

The pressure drop is the **sum of cross-flow losses over $(N+1)$ crosses** plus window losses (Kern lumps them together through the use of the total number of crosses):

$$
\Delta P_s = f_s\, (D_s/D_e)\, (\rho u_s^{2}/2)\, (N+1)
$$

**Two limitations:**
1. **No leakage correction:** Kern assumes *all* fluid crosses the bundle the *same* number of times. In real exchangers, some fluid leaks through baffle-tube gaps (inter-stream leakage), bypassing the bundle and lowering both the heat transfer and ΔP.
2. **No bypass correction:** The shell-baffle gap allows some fluid to *bypass* the bundle, lowering effective heat transfer and ΔP. Kern over-predicts both.
3. *(Bonus limitation)* Window flow is treated with the same friction factor as cross-flow, which is inaccurate because the window geometry is open (no tubes).

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive, starting from the Fanning equation, the **complete expression for tube-side pressure drop** in a multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchanger. State clearly the meaning of every term.

**(b)** A cold fluid enters a 2-pass heat exchanger at 0.5 kg/s, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.002$ Pa·s. The tubes have $D_i = 0.020$ m, $D_o = 0.025$ m, $L = 2.5$ m, 50 tubes per pass. Calculate the **tube-side pressure drop** (use the friction-factor formula $f = 0.0014 + 0.125 Re_t^{-0.32}$). State whether the design is acceptable if the allowable ΔP is 50 kPa.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

(For a clean write-up, present the derivation in the order given in **Section 3.1** above, starting from $\mathrm{d}P = 2 f_{Fanning} \rho u_t^{2}\,\mathrm{d}L/D_i$, integrating over the effective tube length, converting Fanning to Darcy, applying the Sieder–Tate correction, and finally adding the return-bend loss $4 N_p(\rho u_t^2/2)$.)

> **The full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes; reproduce it in the answer booklet, including the boxed final equation for full marks.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Mass velocity**

Cross-sectional area of one tube $a_t = \pi D_i^{2}/4 = \pi (0.020)^{2}/4 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ m².

Total area per pass = $50 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} = 1.571 \times 10^{-2}$ m².

$$
G_t \;=\; \dfrac{m}{a_t N_t^{per\,pass}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{1.571 \times 10^{-2}} \;=\; 31.83 \text{ kg m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Velocity**

$$
u_t \;=\; \dfrac{G_t}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{31.83}{1100} \;=\; 0.02894 \text{ m s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Reynolds number**

$$
Re_t \;=\; \dfrac{G_t D_i}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{31.83 \times 0.020}{0.002} \;=\; 318.3
$$

**Step 4: Friction factor (use the given formula)**

$$
f \;=\; 0.0014 + 0.125 \times (318.3)^{-0.32}
$$

Compute $(318.3)^{-0.32}$:

$$
\ln(318.3) = 5.763 \;\Rightarrow\; -0.32 \times 5.763 = -1.844 \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-1.844} = 0.1584
$$

$$
f \;=\; 0.0014 + 0.125 \times 0.1584 \;=\; 0.0014 + 0.0198 \;=\; 0.0212
$$

**Step 5: Friction loss** (assume $\mu/\mu_w \approx 1$ for simplicity; if not given, this is the correct assumption)

Tube length per pass $L = 2.5$ m.

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; 4 f \!\left(\dfrac{L}{D_i}\right)\!\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2}
$$

Dynamic pressure:

$$
\dfrac{\rho u_t^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1100 \times (0.02894)^{2}}{2} \;=\; 0.4608 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; 4 \times 0.0212 \times \dfrac{2.5}{0.020} \times 0.4608
$$

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; 4 \times 0.0212 \times 125 \times 0.4608 \;=\; 4.884 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 6: Return-bend loss** ($N_p = 2$ passes)

$$
\Delta P_{bend} \;=\; 4 \times 2 \times 0.4608 \;=\; 3.686 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 7: Total tube-side ΔP**

$$
\Delta P_t \;=\; 4.884 + 3.686 \;=\; 8.57 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 0.0086 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 8: Comparison**

$$
\Delta P_t^{calc} = 0.0086 \text{ kPa} \;\ll\; \Delta P_{allow} = 50 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Design verdict:** The tube-side pressure drop is negligible — about 0.017% of allowable. **Design is acceptable** but, like the shell side in part (a), the exchanger is grossly oversized. Practical recommendation: increase the number of tubes per pass *or* reduce the tube length.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Note):**
> 1. **Forgetting the return-bend term** $4 N_p(\rho u_t^{2}/2)$ on the tube side. About 20–40% of the tube-side ΔP comes from U-bends in 2-pass exchangers — examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Using $D_o$ (outside diameter) instead of $D_i$ (inside diameter)** on the tube side. The fluid flows *inside* the tube, so $D_i$ is the correct characteristic length.
> 3. **Cross-flow area formula:** A frequent mistake is to forget the $D_s/P_T$ factor in $A_s = (P_T - D_o) B D_s / P_T$, leading to an area off by a factor of 10.
> 4. **Equivalent diameter formula:** The 0.866 factor in $D_e$ is specific to **triangular pitch**. For **square pitch**, the formula is $D_e = 4(P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4)/(\pi D_o)$ (no 0.866). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Kern's $f_s$ vs. tube-side $f$:** Kern's $f_s$ for the shell side is **5–10× larger** than the smooth-pipe Darcy $f$ at the same Re because the tube bundle is *not* a smooth pipe. Students often plug in the wrong $f$.
> 6. **No conclusion on safety:** A KTU 14-mark answer that ends with a number but no "ΔP_calc < ΔP_allow ⇒ design safe" statement loses 1–2 marks for "incomplete analysis".
> 7. **Unit confusion:** ΔP comes out in Pa; the answer must be converted to kPa or bar before comparing with the allowable (which is usually given in kPa/bar).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Tube-side ΔP** has two parts: (i) straight-tube friction $4f(L/D_i)(\rho u_t^{2}/2)$ with viscosity correction, and (ii) return-bend loss $4 N_p (\rho u_t^{2}/2)$.
- [x] **Tube-side Re** uses $D_i$ (not $D_o$).
- [x] **Friction factor** for tubes: use Moody's chart (Darcy) or Donohue ($f = 0.0014 + 0.125 Re^{-0.32}$, smooth tubes).
- [x] **Viscosity correction** $(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ is the **Sieder–Tate** factor. Always apply unless explicitly told $\mu_w = \mu_b$.
- [x] **Shell-side cross-flow area:** $A_s = (P_T - D_o) B D_s / P_T$.
- [x] **Equivalent diameter — triangular pitch:** $D_e = 4(0.866 P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4)/(\pi D_o)$.
- [x] **Equivalent diameter — square pitch:** $D_e = 4(P_T^{2} - \pi D_o^{2}/4)/(\pi D_o)$.
- [x] **Number of crosses** in an $N$-pass shell = $N + 1$.
- [x] **Kern's ΔP formula:** $\Delta P_s = f_s (D_s/D_e)(\rho u_s^{2}/2)(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}(N+1)$.
- [x] **Delaware-Bell** = Kern × correction factors $F_{lp}, F_{bp}, F_b, F_{ld}$ (more accurate, used in HTRI / TEMA).
- [x] **Allowable ΔP** typical values: liquids 0.5–1.0 bar, gases 0.01–0.1 bar, condensing 0.1–0.3 bar.
- [x] **Always compare** $\Delta P_{calc}$ with $\Delta P_{allow}$ and state the verdict.
- [x] **KTU cross-flow velocity limit** to avoid tube vibration: $u_s \le 1$ m/s for liquids, $\le 3$ m/s for gases (empirical).
- [x] **Viscosity correction exponent is 0.14** — same on tube and shell sides (Sieder–Tate).
- [x] **Nozzle entrance/exit losses** are usually *negligible* compared to friction and bends.
- [x] **Total exchanger ΔP** = max(ΔP_tube, ΔP_shell) only when the streams are independent. In a real exchanger, the *tube-side* ΔP drives one fluid and the *shell-side* ΔP drives the other — both must satisfy the allowable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Design of Shell and Tube Condensers for Single Component Isothermal Condensation of Saturated Vapours in the Shell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Design of Shell and Tube Condensers for Single Component Isothermal Condensation of Saturated Vapours in the Shell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Shell and Tube Condenser** is a heat transfer device in which a saturated vapour is condensed on the **shell side** by transferring its latent heat of condensation to a coolant (typically water) flowing through the tubes. The vapour is generally distributed in the shell space, condenses on the outer surface of the tubes, and the liquid condensate is collected at the bottom of the shell.

For a **single-component isothermal condensation** of a saturated vapour in the shell:
- The vapour pressure is constant (isobaric process).
- The condensing temperature $T_{sv}$ remains **constant** throughout the condensation process (isothermal phase change).
- The only thermal resistance to heat transfer is the condensate film on the outer tube surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** For a pure saturated vapour undergoing condensation at constant pressure, the temperature difference profile on the shell side is flat, so the **LMTD correction factor $F_T = 1$**. The simple LMTD is used directly without the complex cross-flow correction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are sitting in a hot, steamy bathroom after a shower. The steam (vapour) hits the cool glass mirror and turns into water droplets — the mirror "sweats". The steam loses its **latent heat** to the cold mirror surface. This is exactly what happens in a condenser:

- **The Mirror (Cold Surface):** The tubes carrying cooling water.
- **The Steam (Vapour):** The hot saturated vapour in the shell.
- **The Sweat (Condensate):** The liquid formed when vapour gives up its latent heat.

A **shell and tube condenser** is just a *giant industrial mirror* — but instead of one flat surface, there are thousands of tubes, multiplying the cooling surface area and condensing massive quantities of vapour efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in Design:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
> - These properties are evaluated at the **mean film temperature**: $T_f = T_{sv} - 0.75 \Delta T$

## 1.3 Why "Isothermal" Condensation Matters in Design

| Type of Process | Temperature Profile | LMTD Correction |
|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Isothermal condensation (pure vapour) | Flat line at $T_{sv}$ | **$F_T = 1.0$** (no correction needed) |
| Non-isothermal condensation (vapour mixture) | Sloped line | $F_T$ must be calculated |

The isothermal assumption simplifies the design significantly because:
1. The LMTD is calculated as a simple log-mean temperature difference.
2. There is no need for complex $\Delta T$ correction factors from charts.
3. The only thermal resistance worth careful calculation is the **condensate film** on the outside of the tubes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic (Why and How)

The design of a shell-and-tube condenser follows a structured procedure:

**Step 1 — Define the Heat Duty ($Q$):**
$$Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda_{fg}$$
where $\dot{m}_v$ is the vapour mass flow rate and $\lambda_{fg}$ is the latent heat of condensation.

**Step 2 — Set the Energy Balance on Coolant Side:**
$$Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$
This determines the coolant flow rate.

**Step 3 — Calculate LMTD:**
$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$
where $\Delta T_1 = T_{sv} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{sv} - T_{c,out}$. **No correction factor required**.

**Step 4 — Calculate Shell-Side Condensation Coefficient ($h_o$):**
Apply Nusselt's film-condensation correlation.

**Step 5 — Calculate Tube-Side Coolant Coefficient ($h_i$):**
Use standard convective correlations (Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate, etc.).

**Step 6 — Calculate Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U_o$):**
$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do} + \frac{x_w \cdot D_o}{k_w \cdot D_m} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + R_{di}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate Heat Transfer Area:**
$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$

**Step 8 — Verify Pressure Drops:**
Both shell and tube side pressure drops must be within allowable limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** The whole condenser design revolves around $h_o$ — the outside condensation coefficient. Nusselt's 1916 analysis is the cornerstone of all condensation design. The correlations differ for horizontal vs vertical tube orientation because gravity-driven condensate flow behaves differently in each case.

## 2.2 Nusselt's Theory — The Heart of Condensation Design

### 2.2.1 Filmwise vs Dropwise Condensation

- **Filmwise condensation:** Condensate wets the surface and forms a continuous film. The film is the dominant resistance. Nusselt's analysis applies here.
- **Dropwise condensation:** Condensate forms discrete droplets that fall off. Heat transfer coefficients are 5–10× higher, but this is rare in practice and unreliable for design.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU designs **always assume filmwise condensation** for conservative engineering estimates.

### 2.2.2 Nusselt's Correlation for Horizontal Tubes (Shell-Side Condensation)

For $N$ tubes in a vertical tier (typical of horizontal shell-and-tube condensers):

$$h_o = 0.725 \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg} \cdot k_l^3}{N \cdot \mu_l \cdot D_o \cdot \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

This is the **most commonly used equation** for shell-and-tube condensers in KTU problems.

### 2.2.3 Nusselt's Correlation for Vertical Tubes

For a single vertical tube of length $L$:

$$h_o = 0.943 \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg} \cdot k_l^3}{\mu_l \cdot L \cdot \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

### 2.2.4 Reynolds Number of the Condensate Film

Used to check whether the film flow is **laminar** (Nusselt's theory requires laminar flow):
$$Re_{film} = \frac{4 \cdot \dot{m}_{cond}}{\pi \cdot D_o \cdot \mu_l \cdot N_t}$$

For $Re_{film} < 1800$ → laminar (Nusselt valid).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Heat Duty | $Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda_{fg}$ | W | Total latent heat to be removed |
| 2 | Coolant Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_c = Q / (C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c)$ | kg/s | Energy balance |
| 3 | LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | K | **No $F_T$ correction for isothermal** |
| 4 | LMTD (when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1$ | K | Special case (counter-current) |
| 5 | $h_o$ — Horizontal tubes | $h_o = 0.725 \left[\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3 / (N \mu_l D_o \Delta T)\right]^{1/4}$ | $W/m^2K$ | **Most used in KTU** |
| 6 | $h_o$ — Vertical tube | $h_o = 0.943 \left[\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3 / (\mu_l L \Delta T)\right]^{1/4}$ | $W/m^2K$ | For vertical condensers |
| 7 | Overall $U_o$ | $1/U_o = 1/h_o + R_{do} + (x_w D_o)/(k_w D_m) + (D_o)/(D_i h_i) + R_{di}$ | $m^2K/W$ | Based on outside area |
| 8 | Heat Transfer Area | $A_o = Q / (U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$ | $m^2$ | Required outside area |
| 9 | Number of Tubes | $N_t = A_o / (\pi D_o L)$ | — | Total tubes required |
| 10 | Shell ID (Kern) | $D_s = D_{baffle\_pitch} \cdot (N_t / 0.785)^{1/2}$ | m | Approximate shell diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on Property Evaluation:** All condensate properties $\rho_l, k_l, \mu_l, \lambda_{fg}$ must be evaluated at the **mean condensate film temperature**: $T_f = T_{sv} - 0.75 \Delta T$, where $\Delta T = T_{sv} - T_{w,o}$ (tube outside wall temperature).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shell-and-tube condensers are used in nearly every chemical/petrochemical/refinery plant:
- **Refineries:** Condensation of overhead vapours from distillation columns.
- **Power plants:** Condensation of low-pressure exhaust steam from turbines.
- **Refrigeration:** Liquefaction of refrigerants like ammonia, propane, or R-134a.
- **Air separation plants:** Condensation of oxygen, nitrogen, or argon vapours.

The design ensures efficient heat recovery, energy savings, and reliable plant operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Nusselt's Derivation — Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

This derivation forms the theoretical foundation of the horizontal-tube correlation. The 0.725 constant in the horizontal correlation comes from a similar but more complex integration on a cylindrical surface.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions

1. The condensate flows as a **laminar** film under gravity.
2. The vapour is **stationary** and exerts no shear on the film.
3. The tube wall temperature $T_w$ is **constant**.
4. Physical properties of the condensate are **constant** and evaluated at the mean film temperature.
5. The temperature profile is **linear** across the film (conduction-only heat transfer).
6. Heat transfer across the film is by **conduction only**; the interface is at $T_{sv}$.

### 3.1.2 Momentum Analysis (Velocity Profile)

Consider a vertical plate at temperature $T_w < T_{sv}$. The condensate film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows downward under gravity.

The x-momentum equation for steady, fully-developed laminar film flow (with negligible inertia and constant properties) reduces to:

$$\mu \frac{d^2 v}{dy^2} = -\rho_l \cdot g$$

The negative sign indicates that gravity is in the +x direction (downward).

Integrating once:
$$\mu \frac{dv}{dy} = -\rho_l g \cdot y + C_1$$

**Boundary Condition 1:** At the liquid-vapour interface ($y = \delta$), the shear stress is zero because the vapour is stationary.

$$\left.\frac{dv}{dy}\right|_{y=\delta} = 0 \implies C_1 = \rho_l g \delta$$

So:
$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu}(\delta - y)$$

Integrating again:
$$v = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) + C_2$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** No-slip at the wall ($y = 0$), $v = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$.

$$\boxed{v(y) = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)}$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile**, with maximum velocity at $y = \delta$:
$$v_{max} = \frac{\rho_l g \delta^2}{2\mu}$$

### 3.1.3 Mass Flow Rate of Condensate

The mass flow rate per unit width at position $x$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta} v \, dy = \rho_l \cdot \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu} \int_0^{\delta}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$\int_0^{\delta}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy = \left[\frac{\delta y^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{6}\right]_0^{\delta} = \frac{\delta^3}{2} - \frac{\delta^3}{6} = \frac{\delta^3}{3}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\dot{m} = \frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \delta^3}{3\mu}}$$

### 3.1.4 Energy Analysis (Temperature Profile)

With the assumption of conduction-only heat transfer and constant wall temperature, the energy equation reduces to:

$$k_l \frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} = 0 \implies \frac{dT}{dy} = \text{constant}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $T(0) = T_w$ (at wall)
- $T(\delta) = T_{sv}$ (at vapour-liquid interface)

So the temperature profile is linear:
$$T(y) = T_w + (T_{sv} - T_w)\frac{y}{\delta}$$

The heat flux at the wall:
$$q_w = -k_l \left.\frac{dT}{dy}\right|_{y=0} = -k_l \cdot \frac{T_{sv} - T_w}{\delta} \cdot (-1) = \frac{k_l (T_{sv} - T_w)}{\delta}$$

Or:
$$q_w = \frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T}{\delta}$$

### 3.1.5 Coupling Momentum and Energy (Film Thickness Equation)

In a small strip of width $dx$, the heat transferred through the wall must equal the latent heat released by the condensing vapour:

$$q_w \cdot dx = \lambda_{fg} \cdot d\dot{m}$$

Substituting the expressions:
$$\frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T}{\delta} dx = \lambda_{fg} \cdot d\left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3}{3\mu}\right)$$

$$\frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T}{\delta} dx = \frac{\lambda_{fg} \cdot \rho_l^2 g}{3\mu} \cdot 3\delta^2 \, d\delta$$

$$\frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T}{\delta} dx = \frac{\lambda_{fg} \cdot \rho_l^2 g}{\mu} \cdot \delta^2 \, d\delta$$

Rearranging:
$$\delta^3 \, d\delta = \frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T \cdot \mu}{\lambda_{fg} \cdot \rho_l^2 g} \, dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $\delta = 0$) to $x$:
$$\frac{\delta^4}{4} = \frac{k_l \cdot \Delta T \cdot \mu}{\lambda_{fg} \cdot \rho_l^2 g} \cdot x$$

$$\boxed{\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu \cdot k_l \cdot \Delta T \cdot x}{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

### 3.1.6 Local Heat Transfer Coefficient

The local heat transfer coefficient is:
$$h_x = \frac{q_w}{\Delta T} = \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)}$$

Substituting the expression for $\delta(x)$:
$$h_x = \frac{k_l}{\left[\frac{4 \mu k_l \Delta T x}{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}} = \left[\frac{k_l^4 \cdot \rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg}}{4 \mu k_l \Delta T x}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\boxed{h_x = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg} \cdot k_l^3}{4 \mu \Delta T x}\right]^{1/4}}$$

### 3.1.7 Average Heat Transfer Coefficient over a Plate of Height $L$

$$h_{avg} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \int_0^L x^{-1/4} \, dx$$

The integral:
$$\int_0^L x^{-1/4} \, dx = \left[\frac{x^{3/4}}{3/4}\right]_0^L = \frac{4 L^{3/4}}{3}$$

Therefore:
$$h_{avg} = \frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \frac{4 L^{3/4}}{3}$$

$$h_{avg} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{L^{1/4}} \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$h_{avg} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{4 \mu \Delta T \cdot L}\right]^{1/4} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{4^{1/4}} \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu \Delta T L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Since $4^{1/4} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$ and $\frac{4/3}{1.4142} \approx 0.9428$:

$$\boxed{h_{avg} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg} \cdot k_l^3}{\mu_l \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}\right]^{1/4}}$$

This is the **Nusselt equation for condensation on a vertical surface**.

## 3.2 Adaptation to Horizontal Tube and Bank of Tubes

For a single horizontal tube, the condensate drips off the bottom and falls onto the tube below. Nusselt integrated the film flow on the upper half of a horizontal cylinder and obtained:

$$h_{D,1} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

For a vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes, the condensate from each tube adds to that on the next tube below. The effective film thickness increases, so the heat transfer coefficient decreases as $N^{-1/4}$:

$$\boxed{h_{o,N} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{N \mu_l D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A horizontal shell-and-tube condenser condenses **saturated steam at 1 atm** ($\lambda_{fg} = 2257 \, kJ/kg$, $T_{sv} = 100°C$). The tube outside diameter is $D_o = 25 \, mm$ and each vertical tier contains $N = 4$ tubes. The tube outside wall temperature is $T_{w,o} = 90°C$. Estimate the condensation heat transfer coefficient.

**Given:**
- $\rho_l = 960 \, kg/m^3$
- $\mu_l = 2.82 \times 10^{-4} \, Pa \cdot s$
- $k_l = 0.677 \, W/m \cdot K$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = T_{sv} - T_{w,o} = 100 - 90 = 10 \, K$$

$$h_o = 0.725 \left[\frac{(960)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times (0.677)^3}{4 \times 2.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 \times 10}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator inside bracket:
$(960)^2 = 921600$
$\times 9.81 = 9.04 \times 10^6$
$\times 2257 \times 10^3 = 2.04 \times 10^{13}$
$\times (0.677)^3 = 2.04 \times 10^{13} \times 0.3104 = 6.33 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator inside bracket:
$4 \times 2.82 \times 10^{-4} = 1.128 \times 10^{-3}$
$\times 0.025 = 2.82 \times 10^{-5}$
$\times 10 = 2.82 \times 10^{-4}$

Ratio:
$\frac{6.33 \times 10^{12}}{2.82 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.245 \times 10^{16}$

$$\left(2.245 \times 10^{16}\right)^{1/4} = (2.245)^{1/4} \times 10^4 = 1.224 \times 10^4$$

$$h_o = 0.725 \times 1.224 \times 10^4 = 8874 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

$$\boxed{h_o \approx 8874 \, W/m^2 \cdot K \approx 8.87 \, kW/m^2K}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Condenser Design

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete thermal design procedure for a shell-and-tube condenser with isothermal condensation in the shell:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Define Process Inputs] --> B[Step2 Calculate Heat Duty Q]
    B --> C[Step3 Energy Balance on Coolant]
    C --> D[Step4 Compute LMTD Isothermal FT equals 1]
    D --> E[Step5 Assume Initial U Overall]
    E --> F[Step6 Compute Trial Area A]
    F --> G[Step7 Select Tube Geometry Do Di L]
    G --> H[Step8 Determine Tubes per Tier N]
    H --> I[Step9 Evaluate Film Properties at Tf]
    I --> J[Step10 Compute Shell Side ho Nusselt]
    J --> K[Step11 Compute Tube Side hi Sieder Tate]
    K --> L[Step12 Calculate Revised Uo]
    L --> M{Step13 Is Uo within 10 percent of assumed U}
    M -->|No| E
    M -->|Yes| N[Step14 Final Area and Number of Tubes]
    N --> O[Step15 Check Shell Side Pressure Drop]
    O --> P{Step16 Is Delta Ps within Allowable Limit}
    P -->|No| Q[Step17 Adjust Baffle Spacing or Pitch]
    Q --> H
    P -->|Yes| R[Step18 Final Mechanical Design]
```

## 4.2 Physical Configuration Diagram (Block Schematic)

A horizontal shell-and-tube condenser with vapour on the shell side and cooling water inside the tubes has this functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vapour Inlet Top of Shell] --> B[Shell Side Distribution Space]
    B --> C[Tube Bundle Horizontal Tubes]
    C --> D[Condensate Collection Zone]
    D --> E[Condensate Outlet Bottom]
    F[Cooling Water Inlet] --> G[Channel Head Inlet]
    G --> H[Tube Side Flow Path]
    H --> I[Channel Head Outlet]
    I --> J[Cooling Water Outlet]
    C -. Latent Heat Transfer .-> H
    K[Baffles Shell Side] -.-> C
```

## 4.3 Functional Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SHELL_ASSEMBLY[Shell Assembly]
        S1[Shell with Vapour Space]
        S2[Distribution Baffles]
        S3[Condensate Collector]
        S4[Support Plates]
    end
    subgraph TUBE_BUNDLE[Tube Bundle]
        T1[Horizontal Tubes carrying Cooling Water]
        T2[Tube Sheet Connections]
        T3[Tube Layout Triangular or Square Pitch]
    end
    subgraph CHANNELS[Channel Heads]
        C1[Inlet Channel for Coolant]
        C2[Outlet Channel for Coolant]
        C3[Pass Partitions]
    end
    S1 --- T1
    T1 --- C1
    T1 --- C2
    S3 --- S1
    T2 --- T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above represent **functional architecture** rather than physical engineering drawings. In KTU examinations, such flowcharts demonstrating the logical design flow carry full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: Differentiate between filmwise and dropwise condensation. Why is filmwise condensation the conservative design basis for shell-and-tube condensers?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Filmwise Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Surface coverage | Continuous liquid film | Discrete droplets |
| Surface condition | Wetted (clean) | Non-wetted (treated/promoted) |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Lower (5–10 kW/m²K) | Higher (50–100 kW/m²K) |
| Thermal resistance | High (film-dominated) | Low (no film) |
| Reliability in design | Predictable & reproducible | Unreliable in practice |

**Why filmwise is the design basis:** In real industrial condensers, the tube surfaces are always wetted by condensate over time. The high $h$ values predicted for dropwise condensation are not sustainable in long-term operation. Hence, KTU designs **always use the Nusselt filmwise model** for safe, conservative estimates.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q: State Nusselt's equation for condensation on a single horizontal tube and on a vertical tier of N horizontal tubes. Explain the role of $N$ in the equation.**

**Model Answer:**

For a **single horizontal tube**:
$$h_o = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

For a **vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes**:
$$h_o = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3}{N \mu_l D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Role of $N$:** As the condensate drips from one tube to the next in a vertical stack, the volume of liquid accumulating on each successive tube increases. The condensate film becomes progressively **thicker**, which increases the thermal resistance. As a result, the heat transfer coefficient decreases with $N$ according to $h_o \propto N^{-1/4}$. This is why vertical tiers in condensers are kept small (typically 2–4 tubes).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the Nusselt equation for the heat transfer coefficient during film condensation on a **vertical plate** of height $L$. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal shell-and-tube condenser is to be designed to condense **10,000 kg/h of saturated steam at 0.2 MPa** ($T_{sv} = 120.2°C$, $\lambda_{fg} = 2202 \, kJ/kg$). Cooling water enters the tubes at $30°C$ and leaves at $45°C$. The tubes have $D_o = 25 \, mm$, $D_i = 22 \, mm$, and are arranged on triangular pitch. Each vertical tier has $N = 3$ tubes. The assumed overall coefficient is $U_o = 1800 \, W/m^2K$. Calculate: **(i)** the LMTD, **(ii)** the heat transfer area, and **(iii)** the number of tubes required (assuming 3 m long tubes). **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step Nusselt derivation. The key steps are:

1. **Momentum equation** for laminar film: $\mu d^2v/dy^2 = -\rho_l g$
2. **Velocity profile**: $v(y) = (\rho_l g / \mu)(\delta y - y^2/2)$
3. **Mass flow rate per unit width**: $\dot{m} = \rho_l^2 g \delta^3 / (3\mu)$
4. **Heat flux at wall**: $q_w = k_l \Delta T / \delta$
5. **Coupling**: $\delta(x) = [4\mu k_l \Delta T x / (\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg})]^{1/4}$
6. **Average $h$**: $h_{avg} = 0.943 [\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3 / (\mu_l L \Delta T)]^{1/4}$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Heat Duty:**
$$Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda_{fg} = \frac{10{,}000}{3600} \times 2202 \times 10^3 = 6.117 \times 10^6 \, W$$

**Step 2 — LMTD:**
$$\Delta T_1 = T_{sv} - T_{c,in} = 120.2 - 30 = 90.2 \, K$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{sv} - T_{c,out} = 120.2 - 45 = 75.2 \, K$$
$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{90.2 - 75.2}{\ln(90.2/75.2)} = \frac{15}{0.1818} = 82.5 \, K$$

**Step 3 — Heat Transfer Area:**
$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{6.117 \times 10^6}{1800 \times 82.5} = 41.2 \, m^2$$

**Step 4 — Number of Tubes (using 3 m length):**
$$A_o = N_t \cdot \pi D_o L$$
$$N_t = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o L} = \frac{41.2}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 3} = 175 \, \text{tubes}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question

**(a)** A horizontal shell-and-tube condenser condenses saturated $n$-pentane vapour at $T_{sv} = 50°C$. The tube outside wall temperature is $T_{w,o} = 42°C$. The tube outside diameter is $D_o = 20 \, mm$, and each vertical tier has $N = 4$ tubes. Using the data below, calculate the **outside condensation heat transfer coefficient**.

Data for $n$-pentane condensate at mean film temperature $T_f = 50 - 0.75(8) = 44°C$:
- $\rho_l = 615 \, kg/m^3$
- $\mu_l = 1.95 \times 10^{-4} \, Pa \cdot s$
- $k_l = 0.118 \, W/m \cdot K$
- $\lambda_{fg} = 351 \, kJ/kg$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A condenser has the following design data: $h_o = 8500 \, W/m^2K$, $h_i = 4500 \, W/m^2K$, tube outside diameter $D_o = 25 \, mm$, tube inside diameter $D_i = 22 \, mm$, tube wall thermal conductivity $k_w = 16 \, W/m \cdot K$, tube wall thickness $x_w = 1.5 \, mm$, shell-side fouling factor $R_{do} = 0.0002 \, m^2K/W$, and tube-side fouling factor $R_{di} = 0.0003 \, m^2K/W$. Calculate the **overall heat transfer coefficient based on outside area**. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Condensation Coefficient

**Step 1 — Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T = T_{sv} - T_{w,o} = 50 - 42 = 8 \, K$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nusselt's Equation for Horizontal Tubes:**
$$h_o = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \cdot g \cdot \lambda_{fg} \cdot k_l^3}{N \cdot \mu_l \cdot D_o \cdot \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Numerator inside the bracket:**
- $\rho_l^2 = 615^2 = 3.78 \times 10^5$
- $g = 9.81$
- $\lambda_{fg} = 351 \times 10^3$
- $k_l^3 = 0.118^3 = 1.643 \times 10^{-3}$

Product: $3.78 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 351 \times 10^3 \times 1.643 \times 10^{-3}$
$= 3.78 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 576.4$
$= 3.78 \times 10^5 \times 5655.5$
$= 2.138 \times 10^9$

**Denominator inside the bracket:**
- $N = 4$
- $\mu_l = 1.95 \times 10^{-4}$
- $D_o = 0.020$
- $\Delta T = 8$

Product: $4 \times 1.95 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.020 \times 8 = 1.248 \times 10^{-4}$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{2.138 \times 10^9}{1.248 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.713 \times 10^{13}$$

**Fourth root:**
$$(1.713 \times 10^{13})^{1/4} = (1.713)^{1/4} \times 10^{3.25} = 1.144 \times 1778.3 = 2034.6$$

**Final result:**
$$h_o = 0.725 \times 2034.6 = 1475 \, W/m^2K$$

$$\boxed{h_o \approx 1475 \, W/m^2 \cdot K \approx 1.48 \, kW/m^2K}$$

#### Part (b) — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

**Step 1 — Mean Diameter:**
$$D_m = \frac{D_o + D_i}{2} = \frac{25 + 22}{2} = 23.5 \, mm = 0.0235 \, m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Overall Coefficient Equation:**
$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do} + \frac{x_w \cdot D_o}{k_w \cdot D_m} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + R_{di}$$

Compute each term:
- $\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{8500} = 1.176 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2K/W$
- $R_{do} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2K/W$
- $\frac{x_w \cdot D_o}{k_w \cdot D_m} = \frac{0.0015 \times 0.025}{16 \times 0.0235} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-5}}{0.376} = 9.97 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2K/W$
- $\frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} = \frac{0.025}{0.022 \times 4500} = \frac{0.025}{99} = 2.525 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2K/W$
- $R_{di} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2K/W$

**Step 3 — Sum:**
$$\frac{1}{U_o} = (1.176 + 2.0 + 0.997 + 2.525 + 3.0) \times 10^{-4} = 9.698 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2K/W$$

**Step 4 — Compute $U_o$:**
$$U_o = \frac{1}{9.698 \times 10^{-4}} = 1031 \, W/m^2K$$

$$\boxed{U_o \approx 1031 \, W/m^2 \cdot K \approx 1.03 \, kW/m^2K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $N^{1/4}$ term in $h_o$:** Students often omit $N$ in the denominator. This causes the condenser to be **under-designed** (too few tubes) by a factor of $N^{1/4}$ — losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong LMTD direction:** $\Delta T_1$ must be at the **same end** as $\Delta T_2$ — both at the inlet OR both at the outlet. Mixing them gives negative LMTD.
> 3. **Wrong property evaluation temperature:** Properties should be at $T_f = T_{sv} - 0.75 \Delta T$, **not** at $T_{sv}$ or $T_{w,o}$.
> 4. **Mixing up $U_i$ and $U_o$:** The problem statement must specify which is required. Most condenser problems use $U_o$ (since vapour is on outside).
> 5. **Skipping the (4/3)/(4)^(1/4) simplification** in derivation — examiners expect the final 0.943 constant, not 4/3 × something.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Shell-and-Tube Condensers**

- [x] **Isothermal condensation** of pure saturated vapour → constant $T_{sv}$ on shell side.
- [x] **LMTD correction factor $F_T = 1$** — no cross-flow correction needed.
- [x] **Nusselt's correlation for horizontal tubes:** $h_o = 0.725 [\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3 / (N \mu_l D_o \Delta T)]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Nusselt's correlation for vertical tubes:** $h_o = 0.943 [\rho_l^2 g \lambda_{fg} k_l^3 / (\mu_l L \Delta T)]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Constants to memorise:** $0.725$ (horizontal), $0.943$ (vertical).
- [x] **Property evaluation temperature:** $T_f = T_{sv} - 0.75 \Delta T$ (mean film temperature).
- [x] **Overall $U_o$ equation:** Includes inside $h_i$, outside $h_o$, tube wall resistance, and **both** fouling factors.
- [x] **Area formula:** $A_o = Q / (U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$.
