- [x] **Number of tubes:** $N_t = A_o / (\pi D_o L)$.
- [x] **Key assumption:** Laminar condensate film ($Re_{film} < 1800$).
- [x] **Vertical tier $N$** is the number of tubes stacked vertically — typically 2 to 4 in industrial designs.
- [x] **The $(1/4)$ exponent** in Nusselt's equation comes from the integration $x^{-1/4}$ across the film.
- [x] **Condensate properties matter** — they strongly depend on $T_f$; check steam tables carefully.
- [x] **Fouling factors** are critical for long-term operation — never omit them in design.
- [x] **Coolant side** uses forced-convection correlations (Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate) — different from Nusselt.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Heat Transfer Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Heat Transfer Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Estimation of Heat Transfer Area** is the fundamental engineering calculation performed during the preliminary design phase of a heat exchanger to determine the surface area required to transfer a specified thermal duty between two (or more) process streams, while honouring the thermodynamic driving force, the overall resistance to heat flow, and the geometric configuration of the unit. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this step belongs to the *Rating Problem* (also called the *Performance Problem*) of Process Equipment Design.

The governing relationship is rooted in the classical Fourier conduction equation integrated across a composite wall carrying convective heat flux on both sides:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U_o \, F_T \, \Delta T_m}$$

where every symbol on the right-hand side is either a process-derived value, a thermophysical property, or a correction factor obtained from the standard TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) charts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The estimation procedure is taught as a **process flow of five sub-steps** — (i) thermal duty calculation, (ii) bulk temperature determination, (iii) LMTD computation, (iv) correction factor (Ft) selection, and (v) plug-in into the area equation using an assumed/calculated overall heat transfer coefficient.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tap Water" Analogy

Imagine you are boiling **rice** in a large open vessel and you want to *cool it down quickly* using tap water flowing through a thin copper pipe immersed inside. The rice cools, the tap water heats up, and heat has to pass through the **pipe wall** separating them.

- The amount of heat you need to remove = how much rice × how much cooler you want it × its specific heat → **Q**.
- The pipe wall, plus the film of stationary rice-water on each side, resists the heat flow → **1/U**.
- The temperature gap between hot rice and cold tap water shrinks as you move along the pipe; the *effective* average gap is the **LMTD**.
- A longer pipe means more surface → **A**.

So **"Estimation of Heat Transfer Area" is simply answering: *how long (or how big) does the pipe need to be so that the rice cools to the target temperature in the allowed time?*** The mathematics behind this question is what we are about to master.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Metric Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Heat duty, **Q** | **W** or **kW** | 1 kW = 1 kJ·s⁻¹ |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient, **U** | **W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** | Industrial range: 10 – 1500 |
| Area, **A** | **m²** | Always use SI |
| Temperature difference, **ΔT** | **K** or **°C** | Numerically identical |
| Specific heat, **Cp** | **kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹** | |
| Mass flow rate, **m** | **kg·s⁻¹** | Convert from kg·hr⁻¹ by ÷ 3600 |
| Thermal conductivity, **k** | **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹** | |
| LMTD correction factor, **Ft** | dimensionless | 0 ≤ Ft ≤ 1 |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant, **σ** | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** | Used in radiation only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use °F or BTU/hr in KTU exams?** KTU explicitly follows SI units; failure to convert kg·hr⁻¹ into kg·s⁻¹ is one of the highest-frequency mark-losing mistakes reported by examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Heat Transfer Logic

Heat transfer from a *hot fluid* to a *cold fluid* across a heat exchanger wall proceeds by three coupled mechanisms in series:

1. **Convection** from the bulk hot fluid to the outer surface of the wall.
2. **Conduction** through the solid wall (plus any deposits or tubes).
3. **Convection** from the inner surface to the bulk cold fluid.

The *resistances* in this series network are added algebraically (analogous to electrical resistors) and the inverse of the total resistance gives the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**.

#### Step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Thermal Duty (Q):** Compute the heat to be transferred using stream enthalpies or sensible-heat change: $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \Delta T_c$.
- **Step 2 — LMTD ($\Delta T_m$):** Compute the counter-current log-mean temperature difference; this is the *true* average thermodynamic driving force for a true-counterflow exchanger.
- **Step 3 — Correction Factor (Ft):** Real exchangers are *not* pure counter-current. They are multi-pass, cross-flow, or shell-and-tube with baffles, so a correction factor less than 1 (read from TEMA charts) multiplies the LMTD.
- **Step 4 — U-value:** Either assumed from tabulated experience (for preliminary design) or built up from individual film coefficients plus fouling and wall resistances.
- **Step 5 — Area (A):** Plug all of the above into the design equation and solve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Step 5 alone fetches ~7 of 14 marks in a Module-1 Part-B question. Skipping Step 1–4 logic and directly writing "A = Q/(U·LMTD)" without showing the heat balance is a guaranteed 4-mark penalty.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Must-Memorise)

> [!NOTE]
> Pipes (`\vert`) are used inside table cells instead of vertical bars to preserve markdown formatting.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}\, C_p\, \Delta T$ | Sensible-heat thermal duty | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_m^{cf} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$ | Counter-flow LMTD | K |
| 3 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} ; \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | End-temperature differences | K |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{do} + R_{di} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{f}$ | Composite resistance = overall U | m²·K·W⁻¹ |
| 5 | $R_f$ | Fouling factor (extra resistance) | m²·K·W⁻¹ |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o\, F_T\, \Delta T_m}$ | **Master Area Equation** | m² |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$ | LMTD-correction parameter S | – |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}$ | LMTD-correction parameter R | – |
| 9 | $F_T = f(S,R,N_{pass})$ | Read from TEMA chart, function of S, R, pass arrangement | – |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho \, Q_v}{3600}$ | Volumetric → mass flow conversion | kg·s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $N_t = \dfrac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L}$ | Number of tubes in shell-and-tube | – |

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Process Plant Sizing:** A 1% error in U-value estimation leads to a 3–5% overshoot in installed heat-transfer area, costing millions of rupees in capital expenditure for large refineries.
- **Safety & Energy Auditing:** Underestimation causes exchangers that cannot meet duty → plant trips.
- **Retrofit & Debottlenecking:** Estimating A *before* disassembly confirms whether the unit can handle new process conditions.
- **Green Energy:** Compact plate-fin exchangers rely on accurate A-estimation to claim thermal effectiveness > 0.85.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a true counter-current exchanger where the hot fluid cools from $T_{h,in}$ to $T_{h,out}$ and the cold fluid heats from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$. At any differential element $dA$, the local heat flux equals the change in enthalpy of either stream:

$$dQ = U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

Rearranging the first equality to isolate $dA$:

$$dA = \frac{-\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{U} \cdot \frac{dT_h}{\Delta T}$$

But $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ at the same position. Differentiating $\Delta T$ along the exchanger:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

From the energy balances, $dT_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} dT_h$. Substituting:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h \left( 1 - \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right) = dT_h \left( \frac{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} - \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

Using the global energy balance $\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ and integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$):

$$\int_{0}^{A} dA = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} - \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} \cdot \frac{1}{U} \int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T}$$

Evaluating the integral $\int \dfrac{dx}{x} = \ln x$:

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} - \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} \cdot \frac{1}{U} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)$$

Recognising that the prefactor equals $\dfrac{Q}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{U \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}$ and substituting the heat duty:

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Comparing with $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ identifies:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **counter-flow LMTD**; for shell-and-tube units with shell-side passes ≠ tube-side passes, multiply by Ft.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern)

**Problem (KTU 2019-pattern):**
A shell-and-tube heat exchanger (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) cools **10,000 kg/hr** of an oil stream from **150 °C to 80 °C** using cooling water entering at **30 °C** and leaving at **50 °C**. Take $C_{p,oil} = 2.1$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ and the overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o = 500$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. Calculate the required heat transfer area. Use the TEMA Ft chart with S = 3.5, R = 0.286 → Ft ≈ 0.86.

#### Solution — Sub-Part (a): Thermal Duty & LMTD

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = \frac{10{,}000 \; \text{kg/hr}}{3600 \; \text{s/hr}} = 2.778 \; \text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute thermal duty on the oil side:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_{oil} \, C_{p,oil} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) \\
  &= 2.778 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 80) \\
  &= 2.778 \times 2.1 \times 70 \\
  &= 408.33 \; \text{kW} = 408{,}330 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute end temperature differences for counter-current case:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 50 = 100 \; °\text{C}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50 \; °\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute counter-flow LMTD:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_m &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} \\
           &= \frac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} \\
           &= \frac{50}{0.6931} = 72.13 \; °\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Solution — Sub-Part (b): Area Calculation

**Step 5 — Apply correction factor Ft:**

$$\Delta T_{m,\text{actual}} = F_t \cdot \Delta T_m = 0.86 \times 72.13 = 62.03 \; °\text{C}$$

**Step 6 — Plug into master area equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U_o \, F_t \, \Delta T_m} \\
  &= \frac{408{,}330}{500 \times 0.86 \times 72.13} \\
  &= \frac{408{,}330}{31{,}015.9} \\
  &= 13.17 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: A ≈ 13.2 m².** For preliminary design, round up to ~14 m² to provide a 5–10% design margin (this is KTU's expected engineering practice).

### 3.3 Alternative U-Value Build-Up Worked Example

**Given:** Tube outer dia = 25 mm, wall thickness = 2.5 mm, $k_{steel} = 16$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, inner convective coefficient $h_i = 1500$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, outer coefficient $h_o = 1200$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, fouling $R_{do} = 0.0003$, $R_{di} = 0.0004$ m²·K·W⁻¹.

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do} + \frac{x}{k} + R_{di} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{1200} + 0.0003 + \frac{0.0025}{16} + 0.0004\!\left(\frac{25}{20}\right) + \frac{1}{1500}\!\left(\frac{25}{20}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = 0.000833 + 0.000300 + 0.000156 + 0.000500 + 0.000833 = 0.00262$$

$$U_o = 382 \; \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}$$

Notice the **large contribution of fouling (~24%)** — a major KTU take-away: ignoring fouling in U-build-up is one of the most common industry mistakes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Design Flow for Heat Transfer Area Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROCESS DATA COLLECTION\nInlet-Outlet Temperatures\nMass Flows and Cp"] --> B["STEP 1\nCompute Heat Duty Q"]
    B --> C["STEP 2\nCompute Counter-Flow LMTD"]
    C --> D["STEP 3\nCompute S and R Parameters"]
    D --> E["STEP 4\nRead Ft from TEMA Chart"]
    E --> F["STEP 5\nDecide U-value Method"]
    F --> F1["Option A\nAssume U from Literature"]
    F --> F2["Option B\nBuild Up U from h_i h_o R_f"]
    F1 --> G["STEP 6\nCompute Area A = Q by U Ft LMTD"]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["STEP 7\nSelect Tube Diameter\nand Length"]
    H --> I["STEP 8\nCalculate Number of Tubes\nN = A by pi d L"]
    I --> J["STEP 9\nApply 10% Design Margin\nand Finalize"]
    J --> K["FINAL AREA\nA_design in m2"]
```

### 4.2 Resistance-Network Analogy (Composite Wall)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["1 by ho\nOuter Convection"] --> R2["Rdo\nOuter Fouling"]
    R2 --> R3["x by kw\nWall Conduction"]
    R3 --> R4["Rdi\nInner Fouling"]
    R4 --> R5["1 by hi\nInner Convection"]
    R5 --> S["Total Resistance 1 by Uo"]
    R1 -.joins.-> S
```

### 4.3 Schematic — 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH["HOT INLET\n150 C"] --> T1["TUBE PASS 1\nDrops to 115 C"]
    T1 --> T2["TUBE PASS 2\nDrops to 80 C"]
    T2 --> EXIT["HOT OUTLET\n80 C"]
    CS["COLD INLET\n30 C"] --> CMID["SHELL SIDE\nHeats to 50 C"]
    CMID --> CEXIT["COLD OUTLET\n50 C"]
    T1 -. heat crosses wall .- CMID
    T2 -. heat crosses wall .- CMID
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams here are **logical block-flow** representations (per the safe-compilation protocol); they replace physical drawings such as TEMA-J shell layouts which are difficult to render in Mermaid but convey the same design logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**

**Q1. Define *Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient* (U) and state its industrial typical range for liquid-liquid shell-and-tube exchangers.**

**Model Answer:**
The overall heat transfer coefficient U is the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance between two fluid streams exchanging heat across a composite wall (including convective films on both sides, the wall itself, and any fouling deposits). It represents the heat flux per unit area per unit temperature driving force:

$$Q = U_o \, A \, \Delta T_m$$

Industrial range: **100 – 1500 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** for liquid-liquid shell-and-tube units.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1b]**

**Q2. What is meant by the *Log Mean Temperature Difference* (LMTD)? Why is the arithmetic mean inadequate?**

**Model Answer:**
The LMTD is the *effective* constant temperature driving force that would give the same heat transfer as the actual variable driving force in a counter-flow exchanger. It is mathematically:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

The arithmetic mean is inadequate because the temperature difference along the exchanger varies *exponentially* (due to the first-order nature of convective heat transfer), so a logarithmic mean correctly averages this profile. The arithmetic mean can give errors > 25% when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 > 2$.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each is a 14-mark sub-part (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a counter-current heat exchanger.

**(b)** A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense **8,000 kg/hr** of saturated steam at **120 °C** using cooling water entering at **25 °C** and leaving at **45 °C**. The latent heat of condensation is **2200 kJ/kg**, and the overall heat transfer coefficient based on outer area is **1500 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**. Determine the heat transfer area. Assume Ft = 0.9.

#### Model Solution (a):

The derivation parallels **Section 3.1** above. Starting from $dQ = U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} dT_h = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} dT_c$, isolating $dA$ and integrating over the exchanger length using the global energy balance:

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} - \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} \cdot \frac{1}{U} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$

Recognising the prefactor equals $Q/[U(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)]$:

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate conversion:**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{8{,}000}{3600} = 2.222 \; \text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal duty (latent heat basis):**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times \lambda = 2.222 \times 2200 = 4{,}888.9 \; \text{kW} = 4{,}888{,}889 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — End temperature differences (condensation: $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 120$ °C):**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 45 = 75 \; °\text{C}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 25 = 95 \; °\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Counter-flow LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{75 - 95}{\ln(75/95)} = \frac{-20}{-0.2357} = 84.85 \; °\text{C}$$

(Negative sign in numerator and denominator cancels; LMTD is always positive.)

**Step 5 — Apply Ft and compute area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U_o \, F_t \, \Delta T_m} = \frac{4{,}888{,}889}{1500 \times 0.9 \times 84.85} = \frac{4{,}888{,}889}{114{,}548} = 42.68 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Final Answer: A ≈ 42.7 m²** (round up to ~47 m² with design margin).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the LMTD correction factor (Ft)? Under what conditions does it approach unity, and why is it always less than 1 for a 1-2 exchanger?

**(b)** Estimate the overall heat transfer coefficient for a shell-and-tube exchanger handling a gaseous process stream (h_o = 200 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) on the shell side and cooling water (h_i = 5000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) on the tube side. Tube: 1-inch OD (25.4 mm), wall thickness 2.77 mm, k = 50 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹. Fouling factors: R_di = 0.0004 m²·K·W⁻¹, R_do = 0.0008 m²·K·W⁻¹.

#### Model Solution (a):

The LMTD correction factor $F_t$ accounts for the deviation of real shell-and-tube / multi-pass exchangers from true counter-current flow. It is read from TEMA charts as a function of the dimensionless parameters:

$$S = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}, \qquad R = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}$$

$F_t \to 1$ when (i) the heat-capacity rates of both streams are equal (R = 1), or (ii) the unit is a true single-pass counter-current exchanger. For a 1-2 shell-and-tube unit, the tube-side fluid reverses direction between the two passes, creating a non-counter-current temperature cross region that *always* depresses the effective driving force below the counter-flow LMTD; hence $F_t < 1$ in general.

#### Model Solution (b):

Outer radius $r_o = 25.4/2 = 12.7$ mm; inner radius $r_i = 12.7 - 2.77 = 9.93$ mm.

Composite resistance (based on outer area):

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}\cdot\frac{2\pi r_o L}{1} + R_{di}\,\frac{r_o}{r_i} + \frac{1}{h_i}\,\frac{r_o}{r_i}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{200} + 0.0008 + \frac{\ln(12.7/9.93)}{50} + 0.0004 \times \frac{12.7}{9.93} + \frac{1}{5000} \times \frac{12.7}{9.93}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = 0.005 + 0.0008 + 0.00494 + 0.000511 + 0.000256 = 0.01151 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K·W}^{-1}$$

$$U_o = \frac{1}{0.01151} = 86.9 \; \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer: U_o ≈ 87 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.** Notice the *controlling resistance* is the shell-side gas film (~43%); this dictates that to increase U we must either increase shell-side turbulence or use finned tubes.

### 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to convert kg/hr → kg/s** before computing Q. Losses: ~2 marks per question.
> - **Using LMTD without Ft** in multi-pass exchangers. Losses: ~2 marks.
> - **Confusing sign conventions** for ΔT₁ and ΔT₂; the LMTD is *always positive* — if your numerator goes negative, the denominator also goes negative and the ratio remains positive. Loss if mishandled: ~1 mark.
> - **Ignoring fouling factor** in U-build-up problems. Loss: ~2 marks.
> - **Wrong direction of area correction** — students sometimes divide by Ft instead of multiplying, getting a 10–15% error. KTU examiners treat this as a *conceptual error* and deduct full marks for the calculation step.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Area Equation (Master):** $A = Q / (U_o F_t \Delta T_m)$ — remember to use **all five** quantities.
- **Q Calculation:** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ for sensible heat, or $Q = \dot{m} \lambda$ for latent heat (condensers/boilers).
- **LMTD:** $\Delta T_m = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ — counter-flow value first; correct with Ft afterward.
- **Correction Factor Ft:** function of S, R, and number of passes; read from TEMA chart for shell-and-tube.
- **U-value:** either assumed from experience tables (preliminary design) or built up as $1/U = \sum (\text{resistances})$.
- **Fouling is non-negligible:** Typical industrial fouling factors 0.0001 – 0.001 m²·K·W⁻¹ — always include them in detailed design.
- **Unit discipline:** SI throughout (W, m², K, kg·s⁻¹); convert from kg/hr.
- **Design Margin:** Add 5–10% to calculated area before procurement.
- **Use the Temperature Cross Check:** If $T_{h,out} < T_{c,out}$ (stream temperatures cross), Ft may drop below 0.75 → consider changing the pass arrangement or using multiple shells.
- **Master the worked numerical** of Section 3.2 — it appears in >70% of past KTU Module-1 Part-B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Bundle Size and Shell Diameter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Bundle Size and Shell Diameter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the design of a **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** as per **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** standards, the **bundle** refers to the entire assembly of tubes, tube sheets, baffles, tie rods, and sealing strips that fits inside the shell. The **bundle diameter ($D_b$)** is defined as the **diameter of the smallest circle that circumscribes the outermost row of tubes** in the tube layout pattern, measured at the tube sheet plane.

The **shell diameter ($D_s$)** is the **inner diameter of the cylindrical shell** that encloses the tube bundle plus a necessary **bundle-to-shell clearance** to allow fabrication tolerances, assembly, and thermal expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The estimation of bundle size and shell diameter is a **geometric synthesis step** that links the heat transfer requirement (number of tubes $N_t$) to a mechanically realizable pressure vessel dimension, ensuring the exchanger satisfies both thermal and mechanical design codes (IS 2825 / ASME Section VIII).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drinking Straw Bundle"

Imagine taking 200 drinking straws of equal diameter and gluing them together in a tight honeycomb pattern. The bundle is a hexagonal-shaped cluster of straws. If you now want to put this bundle inside a transparent plastic cylindrical tube (the shell), the **bundle size** is the distance across the widest part of the honeycomb cluster, and the **shell diameter** is the inside diameter of the plastic tube, which must be slightly larger than the bundle so the cluster can slide in with a small gap (clearance). 

**Mapping to engineering:**
- **Straws** $\rightarrow$ Heat transfer tubes
- **Honeycomb arrangement** $\rightarrow$ Tube layout (triangular/square pitch)
- **Honeycomb size** $\rightarrow$ Bundle diameter $D_b$
- **Plastic tube** $\rightarrow$ Shell (pressure vessel)
- **Gap between honeycomb and plastic** $\rightarrow$ Bundle-to-shell clearance

### 1.3 Key Geometric Variables

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Tube outer diameter | $d_o$ | **19 mm, 25 mm, 38 mm** (TEMA standard) |
| Tube pitch (centre-to-centre) | $P_T$ | $1.25\,d_o$ to $1.5\,d_o$ |
| Pitch ratio | $PR$ | **1.25** (minimum, TEMA) to 1.5 |
| Number of tubes | $N_t$ | 50 – 5000 (typical) |
| Number of tube passes | $N_p$ | 2, 4, 6, 8 |
| Number of shell passes | $N_s$ | 1, 2, 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The pitch ratio $PR = P_T/d_o$ must be **≥ 1.25** to allow tube-to-tube-sheet expansion rolling, and to provide cleaning access. Values below 1.25 cause **vibration and fabrication failure** — a common viva question at KTU.

### 1.4 Standard Tube Layout Patterns

The arrangement of tubes inside the shell is governed by the chosen layout pattern, which directly affects $D_b$ and the heat transfer coefficient.

- **Triangular pitch ($30°/60°$):** Most compact; highest heat transfer area per shell volume; difficult to clean mechanically.
- **Triangular pitch ($90°$):** Used when the shell-side flow must be in cross-flow direction; less common.
- **Square pitch ($90°$):** Easier mechanical cleaning; suitable for fouling fluids.
- **Rotated Square ($45°$):** Compromise between cleaning and heat transfer density.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Heat Duty to Shell Diameter

The estimation follows a **five-step causal chain**:

1. **Heat duty $Q$** $\rightarrow$ from mass flow and enthalpy balance
2. **Log Mean Temperature Difference ($LMTD$)** $\rightarrow$ corrected by factor $F_T$
3. **Total heat transfer area $A$** $\rightarrow$ via $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T_m)$
4. **Number of tubes $N_t$** $\rightarrow$ via $A = N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$
5. **Bundle diameter $D_b$** $\rightarrow$ via Kern's correlation (geometry-dependent)
6. **Shell diameter $D_s$** $\rightarrow$ via $D_s = D_b + \text{clearance}$

### 2.2 Step 1 — Number of Tubes

The total external tube surface area must equal the area needed for heat transfer:

$$A = N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the effective tube length per pass. Solving for $N_t$:

$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The overall coefficient $U$ is **iteratively refined** because the assumed $U$ and the actual $U$ (after solving tube-side and shell-side $h$ values) must converge. KTU problems often fix $U$ as a given.

### 2.3 Step 2 — Tube Pitch and Pitch Ratio

$$P_T = PR \cdot d_o$$

The minimum pitch ratio of **1.25** is set by TEMA to:
- Prevent tube-to-tube vibration
- Allow tube rolling into the tube sheet
- Provide inter-tube gap for cleaning

### 2.4 Step 3 — Bundle Diameter (Kern's Geometric Correlation)

The most accurate empirical correlations were developed by **Donald Q. Kern** and remain the **industry standard** for preliminary STHE sizing. They were derived by counting tubes in successive rows of a hexagonal/square lattice and fitting the relationship:

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{n_1}}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitch ratio | $PR = P_T / d_o$ | dimensionless | $1.25 \le PR \le 1.5$ |
| 2 | Tube pitch | $P_T = PR \cdot d_o$ | m | Centre-to-centre |
| 3 | Number of tubes | $N_t = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_m}$ | — | $A = N_t \pi d_o L$ |
| 4 | $D_b$ — Triangular $30°/60°$ | $D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_t}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207}$ | m | **Most common** |
| 5 | $D_b$ — Triangular $90°$ | $D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_t}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207}$ | m | Same as 30°/60° |
| 6 | $D_b$ — Square $90°$ | $D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_t}{0.319}\right)^{1/2.643}$ | m | Easier cleaning |
| 7 | $D_b$ — Rotated Square $45°$ | $D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_t}{0.215}\right)^{1/2.207}$ | m | Compromise layout |
| 8 | Geometric $D_b$ (triangular) | $D_b = 1.155 \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t}$ | m | Hex close-packing approx. |
| 9 | Geometric $D_b$ (square) | $D_b = 1.128 \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t}$ | m | Square packing approx. |
| 10 | Shell-bundle clearance | $b = 0.625 \cdot \sqrt{D_b \, (\text{in inches})}$ | inches | **TEMA correlation** |
| 11 | Shell diameter | $D_s = D_b + b$ | m | Round to std. pipe size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two formula groups?** The geometric (Group 8–9) formulas assume **perfect hexagonal/square close packing of the entire bundle** in one continuous circle. The Kern's correlations (Group 4–7) account for the **tube layout in multiple passes** (e.g., 1-2 has tubes split across two passes with a central imperforate lane), so they are **preferred for KTU exams and industrial design**.

### 2.6 Step 4 — Shell-to-Bundle Clearance and $D_s$

The clearance $b$ is added to allow for:
- Fabrication tolerance (shell rolling, tube sheet machining)
- Assembly access for tube bundle insertion
- Differential thermal expansion
- Bundle-to-shell bypassing leakage

The **TEMA empirical correlation** (in inches, for imperial):

$$b \, (\text{inch}) = 0.625 \cdot \sqrt{D_b \, (\text{inch})}$$

For SI units, convert: $1\,\text{inch} = 0.0254\,\text{m}$. The shell diameter is then:

$$D_s = D_b + b$$

Finally, $D_s$ is **rounded up** to the next standard pipe size: **200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1100, 1200 mm** etc.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost optimization:** Each step-up in standard pipe size adds ~15–25% to shell cost, so accurate $D_b$ estimation directly controls capital cost.
- **Vibration prevention:** A loose bundle (excess clearance) causes shell-side flow-induced vibration of the outermost tubes, leading to fatigue failure.
- **Cross-referencing with fabrication:** The estimated $D_s$ is sent to the mechanical designer for pressure vessel thickness calculation per ASME Section VIII.
- **Retrofit design:** When an existing shell is available, the reverse calculation gives the **maximum allowable $N_t$** for a fixed $D_s$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Tubes

The total heat duty transferred through the tube wall is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

The area $A$ is the **outer surface area of all tubes** (since $U$ is usually referenced to $d_o$):

$$A = N_t \cdot (\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L)$$

where $L$ is the effective length per pass. For a 2-pass exchanger with total length $L_{total}$:

$$L = \frac{L_{total}}{N_p}$$

Substituting $A$ into the heat duty equation:

$$Q = U \cdot N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_m$$

Solving for $N_t$:

$$\boxed{N_t = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_m}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bundle Diameter — Hexagonal Close-Packing Model

**Geometric setup:** Place $N_t$ tubes of outer diameter $d_o$ in a triangular pitch lattice. Each tube occupies an effective hexagonal cell of side $P_T/\sqrt{3}$ (centre-to-centre distance in the perpendicular direction).

The **total projected area** of all tube cells on the tube sheet is:

$$A_{cells} = N_t \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \cdot P_T^2$$

This projected area equals the area of a regular hexagon that **circumscribes** the bundle. The hexagon has a vertex-to-vertex distance equal to $D_b$, so its area is:

$$A_{hex} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{D_b}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \cdot D_b^2$$

Equating $A_{cells} = A_{hex}$:

$$N_t \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P_T^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \cdot D_b^2$$

Cancel $\sqrt{3}$ from both sides:

$$N_t \cdot \frac{P_T^2}{2} = \frac{3}{8} \cdot D_b^2$$

Solve for $D_b$:

$$D_b^2 = \frac{4 \cdot N_t \cdot P_T^2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{D_b = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t} = 1.1547 \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t}}$$

For square pitch, the projected area per tube is $P_T^2$, and the bundle circle is $\pi D_b^2 / 4$:

$$N_t \cdot P_T^2 = \frac{\pi D_b^2}{4} \implies D_b = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t} = 1.1284 \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t}$$

### 3.3 Kern's Empirical Correlation (Origin)

For a **1-2 STHE** (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes), the tubes are split into two groups by a central vertical partition. Kern tabulated the actual number of tubes per row for layouts up to $N_t \approx 2000$ and fitted a power-law. The result, for a triangular $30°/60°$ layout, is:

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207}$$

The constants $K_1 = 0.249$ and $n_1 = 2.207$ are **dimensionless best-fit parameters** that absorb the geometric details of tube counting in the multiple-pass arrangement.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to cool **$50{,}000\,\text{kg/h}$** of oil (Cp = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) from $100°C$ to $60°C$ using cooling water entering at $30°C$ and leaving at $50°C$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **$U = 450\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$**. Tubes are **$d_o = 25\,\text{mm}$**, $2\,\text{mm}$ wall thickness, length **$L = 3\,\text{m}$**, arranged on triangular pitch with $PR = 1.25$. Estimate: (a) number of tubes, (b) bundle diameter, (c) shell diameter.

#### Part (a) — Number of Tubes

**Step 1:** Heat duty from oil-side energy balance:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = \frac{50{,}000}{3600} \cdot 2.5 \cdot (100 - 60)$$

$$Q = 13.889 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 40 = 1388.89\,\text{kW} = 1.389 \times 10^6\,\text{W}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD).

Hot-end approach: $\Delta T_1 = 100 - 50 = 50°C$
Cold-end approach: $\Delta T_2 = 60 - 30 = 30°C$

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{50 - 30}{\ln(50/30)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{20}{\ln(1.6667)} = \frac{20}{0.5108} = 39.15°C$$

**Step 3:** Apply correction factor $F_T$ for 1-2 exchanger:

Using the LMTD correction chart for $P = 0.40$ and $R = 0.50$, $F_T \approx 0.92$.

$$\Delta T_m = F_T \cdot LMTD = 0.92 \cdot 39.15 = 36.02°C$$

**Step 4:** Required heat transfer area:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{1.389 \times 10^6}{450 \cdot 36.02}$$

$$A = \frac{1.389 \times 10^6}{16{,}209} = 85.69\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Number of tubes per pass. With $N_p = 2$:

$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} = \frac{85.69}{\pi \cdot 0.025 \cdot 3}$$

$$N_t = \frac{85.69}{0.2356} = 363.7 \approx 364\,\text{tubes}$$

#### Part (b) — Bundle Diameter

For triangular pitch $30°/60°$, apply Kern's correlation:

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207}$$

$$D_b = 0.025 \cdot \left(\frac{364}{0.249}\right)^{0.4531}$$

$$D_b = 0.025 \cdot (1461.8)^{0.4531}$$

Calculate $(1461.8)^{0.4531}$:

$$\log_{10}(1461.8) = 3.1649$$
$$3.1649 \cdot 0.4531 = 1.4340$$
$$10^{1.4340} = 27.16$$

$$D_b = 0.025 \cdot 27.16 = 0.679\,\text{m} = 679\,\text{mm}$$

**Cross-check with geometric formula:**

$$D_b \approx 1.155 \cdot P_T \cdot \sqrt{N_t} = 1.155 \cdot (1.25 \cdot 0.025) \cdot \sqrt{364}$$

$$D_b = 1.155 \cdot 0.03125 \cdot 19.08 = 0.689\,\text{m}$$

The two methods agree within 1.5%, validating the result.

#### Part (c) — Shell Diameter

**Step 1:** Convert $D_b$ to inches:

$$D_b = 0.679\,\text{m} = \frac{679}{25.4} = 26.73\,\text{inches}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the bundle-to-shell clearance:

$$b = 0.625 \cdot \sqrt{26.73} = 0.625 \cdot 5.170 = 3.231\,\text{inches}$$

Convert back to mm: $b = 3.231 \cdot 25.4 = 82.07\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 3:** Calculate shell diameter:

$$D_s = D_b + b = 679 + 82.07 = 761.1\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4:** Round up to the next standard pipe size:

$$\boxed{D_s = 800\,\text{mm (standard pipe size)}}$$

### 3.5 Summary of Numerical Results

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Heat duty $Q$ | **1388.9 kW** |
| $\Delta T_m$ (corrected) | **36.02 °C** |
| Heat transfer area $A$ | **85.69 m²** |
| Number of tubes $N_t$ | **364** |
| Bundle diameter $D_b$ | **679 mm** |
| Shell diameter $D_s$ (raw) | **761 mm** |
| **Final $D_s$ (standard)** | **800 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Design Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following flowchart maps the complete logical chain from the thermal specification to the final mechanical shell diameter:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Thermal Specification] --> B[Calculate Q from Energy Balance]
    B --> C[Determine LMTD from Inlet Outlet Temperatures]
    C --> D[Apply FT Correction Factor]
    D --> E[Compute Corrected DeltaTm]
    E --> F[Assume U from Experience or Correlation]
    F --> G[Calculate Required Area A]
    G --> H[Select Tube Geometry: d_o, L, N_p]
    H --> I[Compute Number of Tubes N_t]
    I --> J{Choose Tube Layout Pattern}
    J -->|Triangular 30/60 deg| K1[Db = do * Nt over 0.249 to power 1 over 2.207]
    J -->|Square 90 deg| K2[Db = do * Nt over 0.319 to power 1 over 2.643]
    J -->|Rotated Square 45 deg| K3[Db = do * Nt over 0.215 to power 1 over 2.207]
    K1 --> L[Bundle Diameter Db in mm]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Convert Db to inches]
    M --> N[Compute Clearance b = 0.625 times sqrt Db inches]
    N --> O[Calculate Ds = Db + b]
    O --> P[Round Up to Standard Pipe Size]
    P --> Q[Final Shell Diameter Output]
    Q --> R[Send to Pressure Vessel Design per ASME Sec VIII]
```

### 4.2 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the physical components and the location of the bundle within the shell:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL_ASSEMBLY[Shell Side Assembly]
        S1[Inlet Nozzle: Hot Fluid] --> S2[Shell Cylinder of Diameter Ds]
        S2 --> S3[Bundle Region: Diameter Db with clearance b]
        S3 --> S4[Outlet Nozzle: Hot Fluid]
        S2 --> S5[Baffles: Segmental Type for Flow Guidance]
        S5 --> S3
    end

    subgraph TUBE_BUNDLE[Tube Bundle Assembly]
        T1[Tube Sheet: Front] --> T2[Tubes: Nt tubes of OD do]
        T2 --> T3[Pass Divider Plate for 1-2 Configuration]
        T3 --> T4[Tubes: Return Pass Nt over 2 tubes]
        T4 --> T5[Tube Sheet: Rear]
    end

    T1 --> S3
    T5 --> S3
    S2 --> CS1[Clearance b between bundle and shell inner wall]
    CS1 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Tube Layout Pattern Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tube Layout Selection Logic] --> B{Tube-Side or Shell-Side Fouling?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Square Pitch 90 deg]
    B -->|No| D[Triangular Pitch 30/60 deg]
    C --> E[Note: Db constant K1 = 0.319, n1 = 2.643]
    D --> F[Note: Db constant K1 = 0.249, n1 = 2.207]
    E --> G[Advantage: Mechanical Cleaning Possible]
    F --> H[Advantage: Higher Heat Transfer Density]
    G --> I[Select Highest Ds for given Nt]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define tube pitch and pitch ratio. What is the significance of the minimum pitch ratio of 1.25 in shell-and-tube heat exchanger design?*

**Model Answer:**
Tube pitch ($P_T$) is the centre-to-centre distance between two adjacent tubes in the bundle. Pitch ratio ($PR = P_T / d_o$) is the ratio of pitch to tube outer diameter. The minimum $PR = 1.25$ is fixed by TEMA standards to:
- Permit tube expansion rolling into the tube sheet
- Provide mechanical cleaning access between tubes
- Avoid tube-to-tube vibration from shell-side cross flow
- Allow welding and fabrication tolerances
A pitch ratio below 1.25 leads to structural failure of the tube joints and operational vibration damage.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Compare triangular and square pitch tube layouts. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Triangular Pitch | Square Pitch |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer density** | Higher (more tubes per shell) | Lower (~10% fewer tubes) |
| **Mechanical cleaning** | Difficult (lane only 0.25 $d_o$ between rows) | Easy (0.866 $d_o$ lane) |
| **Pressure drop** | Higher | Lower |
| **Fouling service** | Not suitable | **Preferred** |
| **Best use** | Clean service, gas cooling | Viscous, fouling, dirty fluids |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the bundle diameter of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with triangular pitch layout, using the close-packing geometric model. State clearly the assumptions made. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumption:* All $N_t$ tubes are arranged in a perfect hexagonal close-packed lattice with uniform pitch $P_T$.

*Step 1:* Each tube occupies a hexagonal cell of area:

$$A_{cell} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P_T^2$$

*Step 2:* Total area of all cells:

$$A_{total} = N_t \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P_T^2$$

*Step 3:* The bundle is bounded by a regular hexagon of vertex-to-vertex distance $D_b$. Area of circumscribed hexagon:

$$A_{hex} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \cdot D_b^2$$

*Step 4:* Equate and solve:

$$N_t \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P_T^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \cdot D_b^2$$

$$D_b^2 = \frac{4 N_t P_T^2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{D_b = \frac{2 P_T \sqrt{N_t}}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.155 \, P_T \sqrt{N_t}}$$

*Assumptions restated:*
- No tube-pass partitions (single pass idealisation)
- Perfect hexagonal packing
- Bundle perfectly circular in projection

---

**(b)** A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger handles 30,000 kg/h of a process fluid (Cp = 3.2 kJ/kg·K) cooling from 120°C to 50°C using cooling water entering at 25°C and leaving at 45°C. Given: U = 600 W/m²K, $d_o = 19\,\text{mm}$, tube length = 4 m, $PR = 1.25$, triangular pitch. Calculate (i) the number of tubes, (ii) the bundle diameter, and (iii) the shell diameter. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Heat duty:*

$$Q = \frac{30{,}000}{3600} \cdot 3.2 \cdot (120 - 50) = 8.333 \cdot 3.2 \cdot 70 = 1866.7\,\text{kW}$$

*Step 2 — LMTD:*

$$\Delta T_1 = 120 - 45 = 95°C, \quad \Delta T_2 = 50 - 25 = 25°C$$

$$LMTD = \frac{95 - 25}{\ln(95/25)} = \frac{70}{\ln(3.8)} = \frac{70}{1.335} = 52.43°C$$

For 1-2 exchanger with $P = 0.467$, $R = 0.714$, $F_T \approx 0.91$:

$$\Delta T_m = 0.91 \cdot 52.43 = 47.71°C$$

*Step 3 — Area:*

$$A = \frac{1.867 \times 10^6}{600 \cdot 47.71} = 65.20\,\text{m}^2$$

*Step 4 — Number of tubes:*

$$N_t = \frac{65.20}{\pi \cdot 0.019 \cdot 4} = \frac{65.20}{0.2388} = 273.0 \approx 274\,\text{tubes}$$

*Step 5 — Bundle diameter (triangular 30°/60°):*

$$D_b = 0.019 \cdot \left(\frac{274}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207} = 0.019 \cdot (1100.4)^{0.4531}$$

$$\log_{10}(1100.4) = 3.0415, \quad 3.0415 \cdot 0.4531 = 1.3780, \quad 10^{1.378} = 23.88$$

$$D_b = 0.019 \cdot 23.88 = 0.454\,\text{m} = 454\,\text{mm}$$

*Step 6 — Shell diameter:*

Convert to inches: $D_b = 454/25.4 = 17.87\,\text{in}$

$$b = 0.625 \cdot \sqrt{17.87} = 0.625 \cdot 4.227 = 2.642\,\text{in} = 67.1\,\text{mm}$$

$$D_s = 454 + 67 = 521\,\text{mm}$$

Round up to standard pipe size: $D_s = \mathbf{550\,\text{mm}}$.

**Final Answer:** $N_t = 274$ tubes, $D_b = 454\,\text{mm}$, $D_s = 550\,\text{mm}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat tube layout sketches, the four standard tube layout patterns used in shell-and-tube heat exchangers. Compare their relative bundle diameters for the same $N_t$. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

The four standard TEMA layout patterns are described as follows:

**1. Triangular Pitch $30°/60°$:**
- Tubes arranged in equilateral triangles; flow direction parallel to a row.
- **Most compact** arrangement; highest $N_t$ for a given $D_b$.
- **Disadvantage:** Lane between rows is narrow (0.25 $d_o$), so mechanical cleaning is impossible — only chemical cleaning is feasible.
- **Use:** Clean services (e.g., gas–gas, oil–water in lube coolers).

**2. Triangular Pitch $90°$:**
- Similar to $30°/60°$ but flow is perpendicular to a row.
- Bundle diameter constant: $K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 2.207$ (same as $30°/60°$).
- Rarely used in practice; not preferred over $30°/60°$.

**3. Square Pitch $90°$:**
- Tubes in a square grid; flow perpendicular to rows.
- **Advantage:** Inter-tube lane = $(P_T - d_o) = 0.25\,d_o$ for $PR = 1.25$, much wider than triangular.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower $N_t$ for same $D_b$ (~10–15% fewer tubes than triangular).
- **Use:** Fouling liquids requiring mechanical cleaning (e.g., crude oil, river water).

**4. Rotated Square Pitch $45°$:**
- Square pattern rotated by $45°$ — gives a staggered effect with a wider cleaning lane.
- **Compromise** between compactness (triangular) and cleanability (square).
- Bundle constant: $K_1 = 0.215$, $n_1 = 2.207$.

**Relative Bundle Diameter Comparison** (for same $N_t$, $d_o$):
For $N_t = 500$, $d_o = 25\,\text{mm}$:

| Layout | $D_b$ (mm) | Relative Size |
|---|---|---|
| Triangular 30°/60° | **680** | Smallest (most compact) |
| Rotated Square 45° | **725** | Intermediate |
| Square 90° | **780** | Largest |

*Conclusion:* The triangular pitch yields the **smallest bundle**, hence the cheapest shell. Square pitch yields the **largest bundle** but allows mechanical cleaning.

---

**(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is required to handle a duty of 800 kW. The LMTD is 45°C and the assumed overall coefficient is 500 W/m²K. Tubes of 25 mm OD and 4 m length are to be used in a 1-2 configuration. Calculate: (i) the number of tubes, (ii) the bundle diameter for square pitch layout with $PR = 1.25$, and (iii) the shell diameter. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Heat transfer area:*

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{800{,}000}{500 \cdot 45} = 35.56\,\text{m}^2$$

*Step 2 — Number of tubes:*

$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} = \frac{35.56}{\pi \cdot 0.025 \cdot 4} = \frac{35.56}{0.3142} = 113.2 \approx 114\,\text{tubes}$$

*Step 3 — Bundle diameter (square pitch 90°):*

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{0.319}\right)^{1/2.643} = 0.025 \cdot \left(\frac{114}{0.319}\right)^{0.3784}$$

$$\frac{114}{0.319} = 357.4$$

$$\log_{10}(357.4) = 2.5533, \quad 2.5533 \cdot 0.3784 = 0.9662, \quad 10^{0.9662} = 9.252$$

$$D_b = 0.025 \cdot 9.252 = 0.2313\,\text{m} = 231.3\,\text{mm}$$

*Step 4 — Shell diameter:*

Convert to inches: $D_b = 231.3 / 25.4 = 9.106\,\text{in}$

$$b = 0.625 \cdot \sqrt{9.106} = 0.625 \cdot 3.018 = 1.886\,\text{in} = 47.9\,\text{mm}$$

$$D_s = 231.3 + 47.9 = 279.2\,\text{mm}$$

Round up to standard pipe size: $D_s = \mathbf{300\,\text{mm}}$.

**Final Answer:** $N_t = 114$ tubes, $D_b = 231.3\,\text{mm}$, $D_s = 300\,\text{mm}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mark-loss point (–2 marks):** Forgetting to apply the **LMTD correction factor $F_T$** in part-(a) of any heat exchanger sizing problem. Always check if it's a 1-2, 1-4, or 2-4 exchanger and look up $F_T$ from the chart.
> 2. **Second most common (–1 mark):** Using the wrong Kern's constant. The constants $K_1, n_1$ are **layout-specific**. Triangular uses $0.249 / 2.207$, square uses $0.319 / 2.643$. Memorise this table.
> 3. **Third most common (–1 mark):** Failing to **round $D_s$ up** to the next standard pipe size. $D_s = 521\,\text{mm}$ → must be $550\,\text{mm}$, not $500\,\text{mm}$.
> 4. **Unit errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing inches and millimetres in the clearance formula. Convert $D_b$ to inches, compute $b$ in inches, then convert back — or use the SI-equivalent $D_s = D_b(\text{mm}) + 3.15\sqrt{D_b(\text{mm})}$.
> 5. **Pitch ratio error (–2 marks):** Writing $PR = 1.5$ for a "minimum" design. The minimum is **1.25**; values above 1.25 are design choices for specific service, not a minimum.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bundle size = $D_b$** (circumscribing circle of the tube array), **Shell size = $D_s = D_b + b$**.
- [x] **Tube pitch $P_T = PR \cdot d_o$**, with $PR_{min} = 1.25$ per TEMA.
- [x] **Number of tubes $N_t = Q / (U \pi d_o L \Delta T_m)$** — always the first calculation step.
- [x] **Kern's bundle diameter correlation** is **layout-dependent** — memorise the four $(K_1, n_1)$ pairs.
- [x] **Geometric formulas** (1.155 $P_T \sqrt{N_t}$ for triangular, 1.128 $P_T \sqrt{N_t}$ for square) are **quick cross-checks** but not substitutes for Kern's.
- [x] **TEMA clearance** in imperial: $b (\text{in}) = 0.625 \sqrt{D_b (\text{in})}$. SI equivalent: $b (\text{mm}) \approx 3.15 \sqrt{D_b (\text{mm})}$.
- [x] **Always round $D_s$ up** to the next standard pipe size (200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 700, 800 ...).
- [x] **Triangular pitch** = compact + high heat duty; **Square pitch** = cleanable + fouling service.
- [x] **LMTD correction factor $F_T$** must be applied for multi-pass exchangers (typically 0.85–0.95).
- [x] **Final $D_s$ is sent to the mechanical designer** for ASME Section VIII pressure vessel thickness calculation — your design output drives the next module.
- [x] **Common units trap:** Pitch ratio is dimensionless but tubes are in mm; always keep units consistent in the clearance step.
- [x] **KTU 2024 scheme weightage:** This topic typically carries **14 marks in Part B** (Module 1 focus area) and **3 marks in Part A** for tube layout definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of In-Shell Condensation Coefficients Using Dukler Charts/Nusselt Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of In-Shell Condensation Coefficients Using Dukler Charts/Nusselt Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**In-shell condensation** refers to the process where a process vapor condenses on the **outside (shell side)** of horizontal tubes arranged in a bundle inside a shell-and-heat-exchanger (commonly a **kettle reboiler**, **total condenser**, or **partial condenser**). The condensate film flows downward under gravity from one tier of tubes to the next, while the bulk vapor flows horizontally across the tube bundle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> In-shell condensation is the heat transfer phenomenon where a saturated or superheated vapor undergoes a phase change to liquid on the outer surface of a horizontal tube bundle. The controlling resistance is the liquid condensate film, and the mean film coefficient is estimated by correlations derived from the **Nusselt theory of film condensation (1916)** with subsequent **Dukler modifications (1961)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment block during a heavy rainstorm**. Raindrops (vapor) condense on the window ledges (tube surface) and the water sheet (condensate film) drips down from one ledge to the next.

- The **flow rate of water** on each ledge represents the **condensate loading (Γ)**.
- The **angle of the sheets** and how fast they fall depends on the **viscosity, density, and temperature** of water.
- The **wind (vapor velocity)** blowing across the building tears the sheets, accelerating drainage — this is the **vapor shear effect**.
- The **building height (number of tube tiers, N)** dictates how much water accumulates on the lowest floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (Bolded for KTU Board Examinations):**
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity)
> - **N = number of tubes in the vertical tier** (typically the bottom row count)
> - **D_o = outside tube diameter** in **m**
> - All thermal properties evaluated at the **mean film temperature T_f = (T_sat + T_w)/2**

## 1.3 When is In-Shell Condensation Encountered?

- **Kettle Reboilers** – bottoms product vaporization; vapor condenses back on tubes.
- **Total Condensers** – entire vapor stream converted to liquid.
- **Partial Condensers** – selective condensation in multi-component systems.
- **Air-cooled Condensers** (finned tubes) – compact in-shell condensation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics

The Nusselt model rests on the following rigorous **assumptions**:

1. **Laminar, wavy, falling film** with **negligible inertial forces** in the condensate.
2. The **vapor exerts no shear** on the condensate surface (pure gravity-driven flow).
3. **Constant wall temperature** (T_w is uniform).
4. **Heat transfer across the film is purely by conduction** (no convection inside the film).
5. **Negligible sensible cooling** of condensate below saturation — but later modified.
6. **Vapor density << liquid density** (ρ_v ≪ ρ_l) — buoyancy effect simplified.
7. The **physical properties** (ρ_l, μ_l, k_l) are **constant** in the original Nusselt derivation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why are these assumptions needed?**
> They reduce the complex **Navier–Stokes + Energy equation** system to a tractable **parabolic velocity profile** analogous to flow between two plates — allowing analytical solution.

## 2.2 Stepwise Logical Flow of Nusselt's Theory

1. The condensate film is treated as a **gravity-driven falling film** along the underside of a tube.
2. The momentum equation is integrated to yield a **parabolic velocity profile** `u(y) = (ρ_l g / μ_l) (δy − y²/2)`.
3. The **mass flow rate** per unit width is integrated: `Γ = ρ_l g δ³ / (3 μ_l)`.
4. The **energy equation** assuming conduction through stagnant film: `q = k_l (T_sat − T_w)/δ`.
5. Combining these gives a **local** and then an **average** heat transfer coefficient.

## 2.3 The Dukler Modification — Why It Was Needed

Dukler (1961) identified **two major inadequacies** in the pure Nusselt model for **in-tube (and in-shell) condensation**:

- The original Nusselt equation **under-predicts** the heat transfer coefficient when vapor velocity is high.
- **Property variation with temperature** significantly affects the predicted h.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dukler's Approach:**
> Dukler proposed to **multiply the Nusselt h** by **two correction factors** (f₁ and f₂) obtained from his well-known **Dukler Charts**:
> - **Chart I (f₁):** Accounts for the effect of **vapor shear** and **inertia**, depending on the condensate Reynolds number (Re).
> - **Chart II (f₂):** Accounts for the **variation of physical properties** with temperature across the film (k, ρ, μ ratios).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all formulas** the student is expected to reproduce in a KTU 2024 ESE for condensation coefficient problems.

| **#** | **Parameter / Formula** | **Expression** | **Units** |
|:-----:|:------------------------|:---------------|:---------:|
| 1 | Modified latent heat | $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68\, c_{p,l}\, \Delta T$ | J/kg |
| 2 | Condensate load (per tube) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{W_c}{N\, \pi\, D_o}$ | kg/(m·s) |
| 3 | Total mass flow of condensate | $W_c = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}'}$ | kg/s |
| 4 | Reynolds number of condensate | $Re_\Gamma = \dfrac{4\, \Gamma}{\mu_l}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Nusselt h (in-shell) | $h_N = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho_l\,(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}'\,k_l^3}{N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 6 | Dukler h | $h_D = h_N \times f_1 \times f_2$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 7 | Mean film temperature | $T_f = \dfrac{T_{sat} + T_w}{2}$ | K |
| 8 | Property evaluation rule | $k_l,\, \mu_l,\, \rho_l$ @ T_f | — |
| 9 | Number of tubes in vertical tier | $N = N_{tubes,\,tier}$ | — |
| 10 | Driving force | $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_w$ | K or °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall for KTU Board:**
> In the Nusselt equation, the term $N$ is **the number of tubes in a single vertical stack (tier)**, **NOT** the **total number of tubes in the bundle**. A common mistake by students is to substitute total tube count.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of kettle reboilers** in distillation columns — determines required tube bundle area.
- **Sizing condensers** in refrigeration cycles (e.g., ammonia, propane, ethylene plants).
- **Optimization of reflux condensers** — accurate h reduces excessive tube counts.
- **Process Safety** — under-prediction of h leads to undersized exchangers and dangerous runaway in exothermic condensers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nusselt In-Shell Condensation Equation

Consider a horizontal tube of outside diameter $D_o$ at the **N-th (bottom) tier** in a vertical stack of $N$ tubes. The condensate flows by gravity from the topmost tube to the bottom.

### Step 1 — Geometry & Coordinate

Let the **effective length of the falling film on the N-th tube** be $L$. The **vertical projection** of this length on a vertical tube equivalent is:

$$
L = N \cdot D_o
$$

This is the **Nusselt substitution** that converts a multi-tier horizontal-tube problem into an equivalent single vertical-tube problem of length $L = N D_o$.

### Step 2 — Momentum Balance (Gravity-Driven Film)

A force balance on a film element of thickness $\delta(x)$ at distance $x$ from the leading edge yields a **parabolic velocity profile**:

$$
u(y) = \frac{\rho_l \, g}{\mu_l}\left(\delta \, y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)
$$

where $y$ is measured from the tube wall inward to the free surface.

### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate per Unit Width (Γ)

The condensate mass flow per unit wetted perimeter (width) is obtained by integrating $u(y)$ across the film:

$$
\Gamma = \int_0^\delta \rho_l \, u(y)\, dy = \frac{\rho_l^2 \, g \, \delta^3}{3 \, \mu_l}
$$

Solving for the film thickness $\delta$:

$$
\delta = \left(\frac{3\, \mu_l\, \Gamma}{\rho_l^2 \, g}\right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 4 — Energy Balance (Conduction Across the Film)

Heat is transferred across the film by pure conduction (Nusselt assumption). At any location $x$:

$$
q_x = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta} = \frac{k_l \Delta T}{\delta}
$$

### Step 5 — Relating Γ to Local Heat Flux

Using the relation for the local heat flux and the total enthalpy absorbed:

$$
q_x \, dA = h_{fg} \, dW_c \quad\Rightarrow\quad q_x = h_{fg} \, \frac{d\Gamma}{dx}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_{fg} \, \frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{k_l \Delta T}{\delta}
$$

### Step 6 — Substituting δ from Step 3

$$
h_{fg} \, \frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = k_l \Delta T \left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g}{3\, \mu_l\, \Gamma}\right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 7 — Separation of Variables and Integration

Rearranging and integrating from $x = 0$ (where $\Gamma = 0$) to $x = L$:

$$
\int_0^{\Gamma_L} \Gamma^{1/3} d\Gamma = \frac{k_l \Delta T}{h_{fg}} \left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g}{3\, \mu_l}\right)^{1/3} \int_0^L dx
$$

The left integral gives $\frac{3}{4}\Gamma_L^{4/3}$, and the right gives $L$. Hence:

$$
\frac{3}{4}\Gamma_L^{4/3} = \frac{k_l \Delta T}{h_{fg}} \left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g}{3\, \mu_l}\right)^{1/3} L
$$

Solving for $\Gamma_L$:

$$
\Gamma_L = \left[\frac{4\, k_l \Delta T}{3\, h_{fg}} \left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g}{3\, \mu_l}\right)^{1/3} L\right]^{3/4}
$$

### Step 8 — Mean Heat Transfer Coefficient

The mean heat flux is $q = h_{fg} \Gamma_L / L$, while the definition gives $q = h_N \Delta T$. Equating:

$$
h_N \Delta T = \frac{h_{fg} \Gamma_L}{L}
$$

Substituting the expression for $\Gamma_L$ and simplifying:

$$
h_N = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{3\, \mu_l\, L\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \times 0.943
$$

### Step 9 — Converting to In-Shell Configuration (Multi-Tier)

Replacing $L = N D_o$ and combining the buoyancy factor $(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\rho_l$ for vapor displacement, the **final Nusselt equation for in-shell condensation** is:

$$
\boxed{\, h_N = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\, h_{fg}'\, k_l^3}{N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \,}
$$

where the modified latent heat $h_{fg}'$ accounts for sensible cooling of the condensate.

---

## 3.2 Dukler's Correction — Analytical Derivation

### Step 1 — Physical Property Correction Factor f₂

Dukler evaluated the influence of property variation by **analytically integrating** the momentum and energy equations using a **reference temperature** approach. The result, captured in his Chart II, depends on the ratio:

$$
F = \frac{\mu_w}{\mu_f}\Bigg/\left(\frac{\rho_w}{\rho_f}\right)^{1/2}
$$

where subscript $w$ is at the wall and $f$ at the film. For most engineering cases $f_2 \approx 0.95$ to $1.10$.

### Step 2 — Vapor Shear Correction Factor f₁

Dukler showed that the **interfacial vapor shear stress** $\tau_i$ reduces the effective film thickness by inducing a flow component at the interface. The correction factor $f_1$ is a function of the **condensate Reynolds number** $Re_\Gamma$:

$$
f_1 = f_1(Re_\Gamma) \quad \text{(read from Dukler Chart I)}
$$

### Step 3 — Combined Dukler Correlation

$$
\boxed{\, h_D = h_N \times f_1(Re_\Gamma) \times f_2(\text{properties}) \,}
$$

### Step 4 — Typical Numerical Behaviour

| **Re_Γ** | **f₁ (Dukler Chart I)** | **Effect** |
|:--------:|:----------------------:|:-----------|
| 30 | ~0.99 | Negligible — pure Nusselt valid |
| 300 | ~1.30 | Mild vapor shear enhancement |
| 1500 | ~1.85 | Significant — wavy/turbulent film |
| > 1800 | > 2.0 | Fully turbulent — Nusselt invalid |
