# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the assumptions of Nusselt's theory for film condensation on a horizontal tube bundle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nusselt theory of film condensation rests on the following assumptions:

1. **Laminar flow** of condensate film with **negligible inertial forces** (1 mark).
2. The condensate film is governed by **conduction heat transfer only** — no convection within the film (1 mark).
3. The vapor exerts **negligible shear** on the condensate free surface, and physical properties $k_l, \rho_l, \mu_l$ are **constant**; the wall is at **uniform temperature** (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the modified latent heat $h_{fg}'$ in the Nusselt equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The modified latent heat $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_{p,l}\,\Delta T$ accounts for the **sensible cooling of the condensate below its saturation temperature** as it falls down the tube stack (2 marks). This sub-cooling contributes additional heat transfer which the pure latent heat term $h_{fg}$ would neglect, leading to more accurate and slightly higher h estimates (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern:**
> The student is expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both choices are given below for the sake of comprehensive preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

Saturated steam at **1.013 bar (T_sat = 100 °C)** is condensed on the outside of a horizontal tube bundle used in a shell-and-tube condenser. The cooling water inside the tubes maintains the outer wall temperature at **T_w = 90 °C**. The bundle has tubes of outside diameter **D_o = 25 mm** arranged in a triangular pitch with **4 tubes in the vertical stack (N = 4)**. The total condensation rate is **W_c = 0.04 kg/s**.

#### (a) Calculate the outside film heat transfer coefficient using the **Nusselt correlation**. Take the modified latent heat as $h_{fg}' = 2.30 \times 10^6$ J/kg. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Given:**

$$
T_{sat} = 100\,°C,\quad T_w = 90\,°C,\quad D_o = 0.025\,m,\quad N = 4
$$

$$
\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_w = 100 - 90 = 10\,K
$$

**Step 1 — Identify physical properties of liquid water at the mean film temperature:**

$$
T_f = \frac{T_{sat} + T_w}{2} = \frac{100 + 90}{2} = 95\,°C
$$

At $T_f = 95\,°C$:

$$
\rho_l \approx 961.9\,kg/m^3,\quad \mu_l \approx 2.96 \times 10^{-4}\,Pa{\cdot}s,\quad k_l \approx 0.682\,W/(m{\cdot}K)
$$

$$
\rho_v \approx 0.6\,kg/m^3 \quad (\text{negligible compared to } \rho_l)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nusselt equation:**

$$
h_N = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}'\,k_l^3}{N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator factor $\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}'\,k_l^3$:**

$$
\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v) \approx 961.9 \times 961.3 \approx 9.247 \times 10^5\,(kg/m^3)^2
$$

$$
g \cdot h_{fg}' = 9.81 \times 2.30 \times 10^6 = 2.256 \times 10^7
$$

$$
k_l^3 = (0.682)^3 = 0.3172
$$

Multiplying these:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 9.247 \times 10^5 \times 2.256 \times 10^7 \times 0.3172
$$

$$
= 9.247 \times 10^5 \times 7.156 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\approx 6.618 \times 10^{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator factor $N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T$:**

$$
N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T = 4 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 \times 10
$$

$$
= 4 \times 7.4 \times 10^{-5} = 2.96 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the bracket:**

$$
\frac{6.618 \times 10^{12}}{2.96 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.236 \times 10^{16}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the fourth root:**

$$
h_N = 0.725 \times (2.236 \times 10^{16})^{0.25} = 0.725 \times 3.86 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\boxed{\, h_N \approx 2.80 \times 10^4\,W/(m^2{\cdot}K) \,}
$$

**Verification:** This value (≈ 28,000 W/m²·K) is in the typical range for steam in-shell condensation (10,000 – 50,000 W/m²·K). ✓

---

#### (b) Using the **Dukler correction method**, estimate the refined heat transfer coefficient. The condensate Reynolds number is $Re_\Gamma = 300$, and from the Dukler charts, $f_1 = 1.30$ and $f_2 = 0.98$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Given from part (a):** $h_N = 2.80 \times 10^4\,W/(m^2{\cdot}K)$.

**Dukler correction factors:**

$$
f_1 = 1.30 \quad\text{(vapor-shear correction from Chart I at } Re_\Gamma = 300\text{)}
$$

$$
f_2 = 0.98 \quad\text{(property variation correction from Chart II)}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Dukler equation:**

$$
h_D = h_N \times f_1 \times f_2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
h_D = 2.80 \times 10^4 \times 1.30 \times 0.98
$$

**Step 3 — Sequential multiplication:**

$$
h_D = 2.80 \times 10^4 \times 1.274
$$

$$
\boxed{\, h_D \approx 3.57 \times 10^4\,W/(m^2{\cdot}K) \,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

- The Dukler-corrected h is **~27% higher** than the Nusselt h.
- This enhancement is due to the **vapor-shear effect** ($f_1 > 1$) at moderate condensate loading.
- The slight property correction ($f_2 < 1$) **marginally reduces** the coefficient because wall viscosity is lower than bulk.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

A refinery condenser handles **n-hexane vapor** condensing at **T_sat = 70 °C** on the outside of a horizontal tube bundle. The bundle configuration: **D_o = 19 mm (3/4″ BWG 16)**, **N = 6** tubes in a vertical tier, wall temperature **T_w = 60 °C**, condensation rate per tube **W_c = 0.012 kg/s per tube**.

#### (a) Estimate the in-shell condensation coefficient using the **Nusselt correlation**. Properties of n-hexane at mean film temperature T_f = 65 °C: ρ_l = 625 kg/m³, μ_l = 3.2 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s, k_l = 0.115 W/(m·K), h_fg at 70 °C = 365 kJ/kg, c_p,l = 2400 J/(kg·K). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta T$ and $h_{fg}'$:**

$$
\Delta T = 70 - 60 = 10\,K
$$

$$
h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_{p,l}\,\Delta T = 365{,}000 + 0.68 \times 2400 \times 10
$$

$$
= 365{,}000 + 16{,}320 = 381{,}320\,J/kg
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nusselt equation (neglecting $\rho_v$):**

$$
h_N = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2\, g\, h_{fg}'\, k_l^3}{N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
\rho_l^2 = (625)^2 = 3.906 \times 10^5
$$

$$
g\, h_{fg}' = 9.81 \times 381{,}320 = 3.741 \times 10^6
$$

$$
k_l^3 = (0.115)^3 = 1.521 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.906 \times 10^5 \times 3.741 \times 10^6 \times 1.521 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\approx 2.222 \times 10^9
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
N\, \mu_l\, D_o\, \Delta T = 6 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.019 \times 10
$$

$$
= 6 \times 6.08 \times 10^{-5} = 3.648 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the bracket and take the fourth root:**

$$
\frac{2.222 \times 10^9}{3.648 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.091 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
(6.091 \times 10^{12})^{0.25} = 1.572 \times 10^3
$$

$$
h_N = 0.725 \times 1.572 \times 10^3 \approx 1{,}140\,W/(m^2{\cdot}K)
$$

$$
\boxed{\, h_N \approx 1.14 \times 10^3\,W/(m^2{\cdot}K) \,}
$$

This is consistent with the typical order-of-magnitude for **organic vapor condensation** (500 – 2000 W/m²·K), which is much lower than steam due to lower k_l and h_fg'.

---

#### (b) Comment on the effect of **tube tier count (N)**, **vapor velocity**, and **non-condensable gases** on the Nusselt estimate. Suggest a design modification to improve the coefficient. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Conceptual):**

**(i) Effect of N (number of tubes in vertical tier):**

The Nusselt h scales as $h_N \propto N^{-1/4}$. Doubling N reduces h by only ~16%, so the effect is **mild but adverse** — taller stacks drain thicker films, lowering efficiency (2 marks).

**(ii) Effect of vapor velocity:**

- At **low velocities**, vapor shear is negligible → Nusselt valid.
- At **high velocities**, vapor drag thins the condensate film, **enhancing** h — captured by Dukler's $f_1$ (1 mark).
- However, **excessive** velocity causes **shear-driven flooding** (in vertical condensers) — a critical safety limit.

**(iii) Effect of non-condensable gases (NCGs):**

- Even **1–2% NCGs** can reduce h by **50% or more** because they accumulate at the liquid-vapor interface and **block mass transfer** of the condensable vapor.
- Industrial practice: **vent ports** and **inert gas purging** at condenser outlet (2 marks).

**(iv) Design Modifications to Improve h:**

1. **Vertical orientation of tubes** — counter-current drainage (h increases by factor 1.3 vs horizontal).
2. **Enhanced surface tubes** (e.g., **Groked, Turbo-B, or finned tubes**) increase effective area 2–4×.
3. **Lower tube pitch** — reduces condensate re-entrainment between tubes.
4. **Tapered bundle design** — fewer tubes at top (low condensate) and more at bottom (high condensate) to balance loadings (2 marks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Avoid These Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Using the total number of tubes in the bundle instead of the number in a single vertical tier (N).** This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board papers. Always use N = number of tubes stacked vertically.
> 2. **Forgetting the modified latent heat $h_{fg}'$.** The 0.68·c_p·ΔT term accounts for sensible cooling and is **mandatory** in the standard Nusselt form taught at KTU.
> 3. **Property evaluation at the wrong temperature.** All liquid properties must be at $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$, NOT at T_sat or T_w.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — μ_l must be in Pa·s, k_l in W/(m·K), D_o in metres, and g in m/s². Mixing cm/s or mm leads to order-of-magnitude errors.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the Dukler correction in problems that explicitly ask for it.** A 3–4 mark deduction is typical.
> 6. **Confusing in-tube vs in-shell condensation** — the in-shell form has a 0.725 constant; the in-tube form has a 0.555 constant (different geometry).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Nusselt equation** is the **foundation** for in-shell condensation coefficient estimation; it is derived from **gravity-driven laminar film theory**.
- [x] The key constant for in-shell condensation is **0.725**; the constant **0.555** is for in-tube condensation.
- [x] The **modified latent heat** $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_{p,l}\,\Delta T$ accounts for **sensible sub-cooling** of the condensate.
- [x] **N** in the Nusselt equation = **number of tubes in a single vertical tier** (not total bundle count).
- [x] **Liquid properties** ($k_l, \mu_l, \rho_l, c_{p,l}$) are evaluated at the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$.
- [x] **Dukler correction** multiplies Nusselt h by **two factors** — $f_1$ (vapor shear, from Chart I) and $f_2$ (property variation, from Chart II).
- [x] The Dukler correction is most significant for **moderate-to-high condensate Reynolds numbers** (Re_Γ > 200).
- [x] For typical **steam in-shell condensation**, h ≈ **5,000 – 50,000 W/(m²·K)**; for **organic vapors**, h ≈ **500 – 2,000 W/(m²·K)**.
- [x] **Non-condensable gases** are the most damaging factor in industrial condensers — they can reduce h by 50%+.
- [x] **Enhanced tubes** (Groked, finned) are preferred when h is the bottleneck for organic vapors.
- [x] **Design rule of thumb:** if $Re_\Gamma < 30$, the pure Nusselt equation is adequate; if $Re_\Gamma > 200$, apply Dukler corrections.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** Always verify the **Nusselt assumptions** (laminar, constant properties, no vapor shear) before applying the bare equation — Dukler is the more robust method for real condensers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the rate of heat transfer through a composite or multi-layered system (typically a heat exchanger wall separating two fluids) to the **driving temperature difference** (log-mean temperature difference, LMTD) and the total available heat transfer area. Mathematically, the rate of heat transfer $Q$ is expressed as: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where: - $Q$ = rate of heat transfer (**W** or **kW**) - $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K** or **kcal/hr·m²·°C**) - $A$ = heat transfer area (**m²**) - $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (**K** or **°C**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient represents the *combined effect* of all individual modes of heat transfer (convection on the hot side, conduction through the wall, and convection on the cold side) and the additional resistances introduced by **fouling** on the surfaces. It is the inverse of the **total thermal resistance** per unit area. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are sitting inside a closed room on a cold winter night with a glass window. The heat from inside the room tries to escape to the outside cold environment. The heat must pass through several "barriers": 1. **First Barrier:** A thin film of still air clinging to the inner glass surface (this is the *inside convective film*). 2. **Second Barrier:** The glass pane itself (this is *conduction through the wall*). 3. **Third Barrier:** A layer of dust, grime, or moisture that has accumulated on the outer glass surface (this is the *fouling layer*). 4. **Fourth Barrier:** A thin film of still air on the outside (this is the *outside convective film*). Each barrier resists the heat flow. The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is essentially a single number that *summarizes how easily heat can pass through this entire series of barriers combined*. A high U means heat flows easily (good conductor, thin walls, clean surfaces), while a low U means the system is heavily insulated against heat flow. > [!NOTE] > **Why is U important in Chemical Engineering Design?** > In heat exchanger design, the value of U directly determines the **required heat transfer area (A)**. If U is underestimated, the heat exchanger will be undersized and fail to meet process requirements. If U is overestimated, the equipment becomes oversized, leading to **excessive capital costs**. ## 1.3 The Concept of Thermal Resistances Just as electrical current flow is impeded by electrical resistance (Ohm's Law: $V = IR$), heat flow is impeded by **thermal resistance** ($R$). For heat transfer through a composite wall, the thermal resistances occur in **series** and therefore **add up**. The fundamental relationship is: $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{U_{overall}} = R_{total} = \sum R_i$$ Each resistance corresponds to a distinct physical phenomenon occurring at each layer of the heat flow path. > [!TIP] > **Key Design Insight:** The **controlling resistance** (the largest individual $R_i$) dominates the design. For example, in a clean gas-to-gas heat exchanger, the convective films on both sides dominate, while in a clean condensing steam system, the steam-side resistance is negligible. Engineers often focus their design efforts on minimizing the *largest* resistance. ## 1.4 Typical Magnitude of U Values > [!IMPORTANT] > Students must commit these **typical ranges of U** to memory for KTU examinations. The order of magnitude alone can help you quickly validate a numerical answer. | Service / Application | Typical $U$ Value (W/m²·K) | |---|---| | Gases to Gases (low pressure) | **10 – 50** | | Gases to Liquids | **15 – 300** | | Liquids to Liquids (viscous) | **50 – 500** | | Liquids to Liquids (low viscosity, water) | **500 – 2000** | | Condensing steam to water (clean) | **1500 – 5000** | | Condensing steam to organic liquids | **500 – 1500** | | Condensing refrigerant (inside tubes) to water | **500 – 1500** | | Boiling water to condensing steam | **1500 – 5000** | | Boiler furnace wall to boiling water | **50 – 200** (incl. radiation) | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Heat Flow Path in a Typical Heat Exchanger Consider a typical shell-and-tube heat exchanger where a hot fluid flows inside the tubes and a cold fluid flows in the shell. The heat $Q$ must pass sequentially through the following **resistances in series**: 1. **Convective resistance** of the hot fluid inside the tube. 2. **Fouling resistance** on the inside surface of the tube. 3. **Conductive resistance** of the metal tube wall. 4. **Fouling resistance** on the outside surface of the tube. 5. **Convective resistance** of the cold fluid in the shell. ## 2.2 The Series Resistance Network Using the electrical analogy (where $\Delta T \leftrightarrow V$, $Q \leftrightarrow I$, and $R \leftrightarrow R$), we can write for steady-state heat flow: $$Q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}}$$ And the overall heat transfer coefficient is defined based on the total resistance referred to a chosen area: $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total} \cdot A}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below contains the **most critical formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Master these derivations and the conditions under which each applies. | # | Configuration | Resistance Term | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Inside convective film | $R_i$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_i \cdot A_i}$ | K/W | | 2 | Outside convective film | $R_o$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_o \cdot A_o}$ | K/W | | 3 | Plane (flat) wall conduction | $R_{wall}$ | $\dfrac{x}{k \cdot A}$ | K/W | | 4 | Cylindrical wall conduction | $R_{wall}$ | $\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ | K/W | | 5 | Inside fouling | $R_{fi}$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_{fi} \cdot A_i}$ | K/W | | 6 | Outside fouling | $R_{fo}$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_{fo} \cdot A_o}$ | K/W | ### 2.3.1 Case A: Composite Plane Wall (Flat Surface) For a flat composite wall separating two fluids (e.g., a furnace wall, a storage tank wall), the overall heat transfer coefficient is referenced to a **common area $A$** (since all areas are equal in a flat wall): $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{fi} + R_{fo}}$$ where: - $h_i$ = inside convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**) - $h_o$ = outside convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**) - $x_1, x_2$ = thickness of each wall layer (**m**) - $k_1, k_2$ = thermal conductivity of each wall layer (**W/m·K**) - $R_{fi}, R_{fo}$ = fouling factors (**m²·K/W**) ### 2.3.2 Case B: Cylindrical Wall (Heat Exchanger Tube) This is the **most important case for KTU examinations** because chemical engineers predominantly design tubular heat exchangers. In a cylindrical geometry, the inner and outer surface areas are different: $A_i = 2\pi r_i L$ and $A_o = 2\pi r_o L$. The **total thermal resistance** for a cylindrical tube is: $$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o} + R_{fi} + R_{fo}$$ The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the **outside area $A_o$** is: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{A_o}{h_i A_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{fi}\left(\frac{A_o}{A_i}\right) + R_{fo}}$$ Similarly, based on the **inside area $A_i$**: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_i} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \frac{A_i}{h_o A_o} + R_{fi} + R_{fo}\left(\frac{A_i}{A_o}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion between $U_o$ and $U_i$:** Since $U \cdot A = \text{constant}$ (i.e., $Q = U_i A_i \Delta T = U_o A_o \Delta T$), we have: > $$U_i A_i = U_o A_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{U_i}{U_o} = \frac{r_o}{r_i} = \frac{D_o}{D_i}$$ ### 2.3.3 Inclusion of Fouling Factors **Fouling** is the accumulation of unwanted deposits (scale, rust, biological growth, coke, polymerization products) on heat transfer surfaces. Fouling acts as an *additional thermal resistance* that grows over time. To account for this in design, a **fouling factor** (also called a **dirt factor** or **fouling resistance**) $R_f$ is added to the denominator. > [!TIP] > **Standard SI Unit:** Fouling factor has units of **m²·K/W**. Typical values range from **$0.0001$ to $0.001$ m²·K/W** for most services. Steam-side and cooling water-side fouling are the most common scenarios tested in KTU problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Heat Exchanger Sizing:** Once $U$ is estimated, the design equation $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$ directly gives the required area. 2. **Performance Evaluation:** During operation, $U$ can be back-calculated from measured data: $U = Q / (A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$ to monitor the *health* of the exchanger. A decreasing $U$ with time indicates fouling buildup. 3. **Economic Trade-off Study:** A higher $U$ requires less area (lower equipment cost) but may require higher pumping power (higher operating cost). The optimum $U$ balances capital vs. operating costs. 4. **Material Selection:** Knowing $U$ helps justify exotic materials (e.g., using Inconel tubes in highly corrosive services) by quantifying performance improvements. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall U for a Composite Plane Wall ### Setup Consider a flat composite wall with three layers (a metal wall sandwiched between two fouling layers) separating a hot fluid (left) from a cold fluid (right). Let the surface temperatures be $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5$ from left to right. The convective film equation gives: $$Q = h_i A (T_{hot} - T_1)$$ Fourier's law for conduction through each layer: $$Q = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{x_1}, \quad Q = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{x_2}$$ Fouling film: $$Q = \frac{A (T_3 - T_4)}{R_{f1}}$$ Outside convection: $$Q = h_o A (T_4 - T_{cold})$$ ### Derivation Rearranging each equation to express the temperature drop across each layer: $$T_{hot} - T_1 = \frac{Q}{h_i A}$$ $$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q x_1}{k_1 A}$$ $$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q x_2}{k_2 A}$$ $$T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q R_{f1}}{A}$$ $$T_4 - T_{cold} = \frac{Q}{h_o A}$$ **Summing all equations** (telescoping sum — all intermediate temperatures cancel): $$ \begin{aligned} (T_{hot} - T_1) + (T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) + (T_4 - T_{cold}) &= \frac{Q}{h_i A} + \frac{Q x_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{Q x_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{Q R_{f1}}{A} + \frac{Q}{h_o A} \\ T_{hot} - T_{cold} &= \frac{Q}{A} \left( \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + R_{f1} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Defining the overall heat transfer coefficient** by comparison with $Q = U A (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + R_{f1} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall U for a Cylindrical Tube Wall ### Setup Consider a cylindrical tube of length $L$, inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, thermal conductivity $k$. Hot fluid inside (film coefficient $h_i$) and cold fluid outside (film coefficient $h_o$). Fouling factors $R_{fi}$ (inside) and $R_{fo}$ (outside). **Step 1 — Convective film resistance (inside):** $$Q = h_i A_i (T_{hot} - T_{si}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_1 = \frac{Q}{h_i A_i} = \frac{Q}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)}$$ **Step 2 — Fouling resistance (inside):** $$Q = \frac{A_i \Delta T_2}{R_{fi}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_2 = \frac{Q R_{fi}}{A_i} = \frac{Q R_{fi}}{2\pi r_i L}$$ **Step 3 — Conductive resistance (cylindrical wall):** Using Fourier's law for radial conduction in a cylinder: $$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_{si} - T_{so})}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_3 = \frac{Q \ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$ **Step 4 — Fouling resistance (outside):** $$Q = \frac{A_o \Delta T_4}{R_{fo}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_4 = \frac{Q R_{fo}}{A_o} = \frac{Q R_{fo}}{2\pi r_o L}$$ **Step 5 — Convective film resistance (outside):** $$Q = h_o A_o (T_{so} - T_{cold}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_5 = \frac{Q}{h_o A_o} = \frac{Q}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}$$ **Summing the temperature drops:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{hot} - T_{cold} &= \frac{Q}{2\pi L} \left[ \frac{1}{h_i r_i} + \frac{R_{fi}}{r_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \frac{R_{fo}}{r_o} + \frac{1}{h_o r_o} \right] \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Defining U based on the outside area $A_o = 2\pi r_o L$:** Since $Q = U_o A_o (T_{hot} - T_{cold}) = U_o (2\pi r_o L) (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$: $$ \begin{aligned} U_o (2\pi r_o L) (T_{hot} - T_{cold}) &= \frac{(T_{hot} - T_{cold})}{\frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{h_i r_i} + \frac{R_{fi}}{r_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \frac{R_{fo}}{r_o} + \frac{1}{h_o r_o}\right]} \end{aligned} $$ Inverting and simplifying: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{r_o}{h_i r_i} + \frac{R_{fi} \cdot r_o}{r_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + R_{fo} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ ## 3.3 Numerical Solved Example (KTU Typical Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example 1:** A steam condenser has a steel tube ($k = 45$ W/m·K) of inner diameter 25 mm and wall thickness 2.5 mm. Steam condenses on the outside ($h_o = 8000$ W/m²·K) and cooling water flows inside ($h_i = 5000$ W/m²·K). The inside and outside fouling factors are $R_{fi} = 0.0002$ and $R_{fo} = 0.0001$ m²·K/W. Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outside area. **Given Data:** - $r_i = 12.5$ mm = 0.0125 m - Wall thickness = 2.5 mm, so $r_o = 15.0$ mm = 0.0150 m - $k = 45$ W/m·K - $h_i = 5000$ W/m²·K - $h_o = 8000$ W/m²·K - $R_{fi} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W - $R_{fo} = 0.0001$ m²·K/W **Step 1 — Calculate inside convective resistance term:** $$\frac{r_o}{h_i r_i} = \frac{0.0150}{5000 \times 0.0125} = \frac{0.0150}{62.5} = 0.000240 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate inside fouling resistance term:** $$\frac{R_{fi} \cdot r_o}{r_i} = \frac{0.0002 \times 0.0150}{0.0125} = \frac{0.0000030}{0.0125} = 0.000240 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate wall conduction resistance term:** $$\frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} = \frac{0.0150 \times \ln(0.0150/0.0125)}{45} = \frac{0.0150 \times \ln(1.2)}{45} = \frac{0.0150 \times 0.18232}{45} = \frac{0.0027348}{45} = 0.0000608 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate outside fouling resistance:** $$R_{fo} = 0.000100 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate outside convective resistance:** $$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{8000} = 0.000125 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 6 — Sum all resistances:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U_o} &= 0.000240 + 0.000240 + 0.0000608 + 0.000100 + 0.000125 \\ &= 0.0007658 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Calculate $U_o$:** $$U_o = \frac{1}{0.0007658} = 1305.8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 8 — Sanity check:** This value (~1300 W/m²·K) is within the expected range for condensing steam-to-water service (1500–5000 for clean conditions, but with fouling it drops). ✓ ## 3.4 Numerical Solved Example 2: Plane Wall > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example 2:** A furnace wall consists of a 100 mm firebrick layer ($k = 1.4$ W/m·K), a 50 mm insulating brick layer ($k = 0.35$ W/m·K), and a 6 mm steel plate ($k = 45$ W/m·K). The inside and outside convective film coefficients are $h_i = 100$ W/m²·K and $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K. The fouling factor on the hot side is $R_f = 0.0005$ m²·K/W. Calculate $U$. **Given:** All values in SI units. **Step 1 — Inside convection:** $$\frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01000 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Fouling:** $$R_f = 0.0005 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Firebrick conduction:** $$\frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.100}{1.4} = 0.07143 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 4 — Insulating brick conduction:** $$\frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.050}{0.35} = 0.14286 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 5 — Steel plate conduction:** $$\frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.006}{45} = 0.000133 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 6 — Outside convection:** $$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10000 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 7 — Total resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U} &= 0.01000 + 0.0005 + 0.07143 + 0.14286 + 0.000133 + 0.10000 \\ &= 0.32492 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Calculate U:** $$U = \frac{1}{0.32492} = 3.078 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** Notice that the **insulating brick layer (0.1429)** and the **outside air film (0.1000)** together contribute **75%** of the total resistance. This shows why insulation is the dominant design parameter for furnace walls — the rest of the wall is essentially negligible by comparison. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heat Flow Path with Resistances The following block diagram illustrates the **series resistance network** through a heat exchanger tube wall, with each block representing a distinct physical phenomenon and thermal resistance. ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["HOT FLUID

The voltage-equivalent is the **temperature drop** across each resistor, and the current-equivalent is the **heat transfer rate $Q$** (which is the same through every resistance in series). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: Conceptual Short-Answer **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient" for a heat exchanger. List the individual thermal resistances that combine to give the total resistance. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference across the heat exchanger. It is the reciprocal of the sum of all individual thermal resistances in the heat flow path. For a typical tubular heat exchanger with fouling, the individual resistances are: 1. Inside convective film resistance: $1/(h_i A_i)$ 2. Inside fouling resistance: $R_{fi}/A_i$ 3. Wall conductive resistance: $\ln(r_o/r_i)/(2\pi k L)$ 4. Outside fouling resistance: $R_{fo}/A_o$ 5. Outside convective film resistance: $1/(h_o A_o)$ Mathematically: $\dfrac{1}{U A} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{R_{fi}}{A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{R_{fo}}{A_o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ --- ### Question A2: Conceptual Short-Answer **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is a "fouling factor"? Why is it included in heat exchanger design even when the exchanger is brand new? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **fouling factor ($R_f$)** is an additional thermal resistance (units: m²·K/W) introduced into the design to account for the **expected accumulation of deposits** (scale, corrosion products, biological growth, polymers, coke) on heat transfer surfaces during the operational life of the equipment. It is included in the design of a new exchanger because: 1. **Over time**, even a clean exchanger will accumulate deposits on the surfaces, increasing thermal resistance. 2. Including $R_f$ in the design calculation **ensures the exchanger can meet duty requirements at the end of an operating cycle** (typically 6–12 months between cleanings), not just on day one. 3. Ignoring fouling would result in a **grossly undersized** heat exchanger that fails to perform after a short service period. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question Choice A: Cylindrical Wall with Multiple Resistances **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** A counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense saturated steam at 2 bar (outside the tubes) using cooling water flowing inside the tubes. **Given Data:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Tube inner diameter ($D_i$) | 20 mm | | Tube outer diameter ($D_o$) | 25 mm | | Tube thermal conductivity ($k$) | 50 W/m·K (carbon steel) | | Steam-side condensing coefficient ($h_o$) | 7000 W/m²·K | | Water-side convective coefficient ($h_i$) | 4500 W/m²·K | | Steam-side fouling factor ($R_{fo}$) | 0.0001 m²·K/W | | Water-side fouling factor ($R_{fi}$) | 0.0002 m²·K/W | | Steam condensation rate | 1.5 kg/s | | Latent heat of steam at 2 bar | 2200 kJ/kg | #### Part (a) — Calculate the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient based on the outside area $U_o$ and inside area $U_i$. **(7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert diameters to radii. - $r_i = 10$ mm = 0.010 m - $r_o = 12.5$ mm = 0.0125 m **Step 2:** Compute $\ln(r_o/r_i)$. $$\ln(r_o/r_i) = \ln(12.5/10) = \ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$ **Step 3:** Use the formula for $U_o$ based on outside area. $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{r_o}{h_i r_i} + \frac{R_{fi} r_o}{r_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + R_{fo} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ **Term 1 — Inside convective film:** $$\frac{r_o}{h_i r_i} = \frac{0.0125}{4500 \times 0.010} = \frac{0.0125}{45} = 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 2 — Inside fouling:** $$\frac{R_{fi} r_o}{r_i} = \frac{0.0002 \times 0.0125}{0.010} = 2.500 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 3 — Wall conduction:** $$\frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} = \frac{0.0125 \times 0.2231}{50} = 5.578 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 4 — Outside fouling:** $$R_{fo} = 1.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 4:** Outside convective film (need this after Term 5). $$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{7000} = 1.429 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 5:** Summing all five terms. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U_o} &= 2.778 \times 10^{-4} + 2.500 \times 10^{-4} + 5.578 \times 10^{-5} + 1.000 \times 10^{-4} + 1.429 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 8.265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Calculate $U_o$. $$U_o = \frac{1}{8.265 \times 10^{-4}} = 1209.9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \approx 1210 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 7:** Calculate $U_i$ using the area conversion rule. $$U_i = U_o \times \frac{r_o}{r_i} = 1210 \times \frac{12.5}{10} = 1512.5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Final Answer (Part a):** $U_o \approx 1210$ W/m²·K, $U_i \approx 1512.5$ W/m²·K #### Part (b) — If the heat duty is $Q = 3.3$ MW and the LMTD is 30 °C, calculate the required heat transfer area based on the outside diameter. **(7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Verify heat duty from steam data. $$Q = \dot{m} \times \lambda = 1.5 \text{ kg/s} \times 2200 \text{ kJ/kg} = 3300 \text{ kW} = 3.3 \text{ MW} \checkmark$$ **Step 2:** Use the basic heat transfer equation to find area. $$Q = U_o A_o \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $A_o$. $$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^6}{1210 \times 30} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^6}{36300} = 90.91 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4:** Verify with the inside area basis. $$A_i = \frac{Q}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^6}{1512.5 \times 30} = 72.73 \text{ m}^2$$ Check: $U_i A_i = 1512.5 \times 72.73 = 110,000$ and $U_o A_o = 1210 \times 90.91 = 110,000$ ✓ **Step 5:** Determine the number of tubes required if each tube is 5 m long. $$N = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o L} = \frac{90.91}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 5} = \frac{90.91}{0.3927} = 231.5 \approx 232 \text{ tubes}$$ **Final Answer (Part b):** $A_o = 90.91$ m², requiring approximately **232 tubes** of 5 m length. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert diameters to radii** before using the cylindrical conduction formula — this is the most common error and costs 1 mark immediately. > 2. **Mixing up $U_o$ and $U_i$**: If the problem asks for area based on the *outside*, use $U_o$ throughout. Never mix. > 3. **Units inconsistency**: Always convert mm to m **before** substituting. A diameter in mm will give a $U$ value that is 1000× too large. > 4. **Forgetting the natural log term**: Students often write $r_o - r_i$ instead of $\ln(r_o/r_i)$. This is incorrect for cylindrical geometry and is worth **2 marks** lost. --- ### Question Choice B: Plane Wall with LMTD Method **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** A chemical reactor vessel wall is constructed of three layers from inside to outside: - 8 mm of stainless steel ($k_1 = 16$ W/m·K) - 100 mm of insulating material ($k_2 = 0.08$ W/m·K) - 4 mm of carbon steel cladding ($k_3 = 45$ W/m·K) The inner surface is in contact with a process fluid at 250 °C with $h_i = 1200$ W/m²·K. The outer surface is exposed to ambient air at 30 °C with $h_o = 15$ W/m²·K. The fouling factor on the process side is $R_{fi} = 0.0003$ m²·K/W. #### Part (a) — Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ and identify the dominant resistance. **(7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** List all thermal resistance terms in order. **Term 1 — Inside convection:** $$\frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 2 — Inside fouling:** $$R_{fi} = 3.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 3 — Stainless steel conduction:** $$\frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.008}{16} = 5.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 4 — Insulation conduction:** $$\frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.100}{0.08} = 1.2500 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 5 — Carbon steel conduction:** $$\frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.004}{45} = 8.889 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Term 6 — Outside convection:** $$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{15} = 6.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 2:** Sum the resistances. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U} &= 8.333 \times 10^{-4} + 3.000 \times 10^{-4} + 5.000 \times 10^{-4} + 1.2500 + 8.889 \times 10^{-5} + 6.667 \times 10^{-2} \\ \frac{1}{U} &= 1.3217 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate $U$. $$U = \frac{1}{1.3217} = 0.7566 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 4:** Identify the dominant resistance. **Term Analysis — Percentage contribution:** - Insulation: $\frac{1.2500}{1.3217} \times 100 = 94.57\%$ - Outside air film: $\frac{0.06667}{1.3217} \times 100 = 5.04\%$ - All others combined: $\approx 0.4\%$ **The dominant resistance is the insulating layer (94.6% of total).** **Final Answer (Part a):** $U = 0.757$ W/m²·K, and the insulating layer controls the design. #### Part (b) — Calculate the outer surface temperature $T_{so}$ of the vessel cladding, and the rate of heat loss per m² of vessel area. **(7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the heat flux (rate per unit area) using the overall driving force. $$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.7566 \times (250 - 30) = 0.7566 \times 220 = 166.45 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the outer surface temperature $T_{so}$. The heat flux through the outside air film gives: $$q = h_o (T_{so} - T_{ambient})$$ Solving for $T_{so}$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{so} &= T_{ambient} + \frac{q}{h_o} \\ &= 30 + \frac{166.45}{15} \\ &= 30 + 11.10 \\ &= 41.10 \text{ °C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Verify by calculating the temperature drop across each layer. - Drop across inside film: $\Delta T_1 = q/h_i = 166.45/1200 = 0.139$ °C - Drop across inside fouling: $\Delta T_2 = q \cdot R_{fi} = 166.45 \times 0.0003 = 0.050$ °C - Drop across stainless: $\Delta T_3 = q \cdot (x_1/k_1) = 166.45 \times 0.0005 = 0.083$ °C - Drop across insulation: $\Delta T_4 = q \cdot (x_2/k_2) = 166.45 \times 1.25 = 208.06$ °C - Drop across carbon steel: $\Delta T_5 = q \cdot (x_3/k_3) = 166.45 \times 0.0000889 = 0.015$ °C - Drop across outside film: $\Delta T_6 = q/h_o = 166.45/15 = 11.10$ °C **Total drop = 219.45 °C ≈ 220 °C** ✓ (matches $\Delta T = 250 - 30$) **Step 4:** The rate of heat loss per m² of vessel area: $$\boxed{q = 166.45 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **Final Answer (Part b):** $T_{so} = 41.1$ °C and heat loss = 166.45 W/m² > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Plane Wall Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the natural log issue:** For plane walls, the formula is $x/k$, **NOT** $\ln(r_o/r_i)/k$. Reserve the log for cylindrical geometry only. > 2. **Incorrect unit conversion:** Wall thicknesses are often given in mm. Convert to meters **before** any calculation. A common mistake is using mm in the formula, giving a $U$ value 1000× too large. > 3. **Identifying the wrong dominant resistance:** Always calculate the percentage contribution of each term. The largest term by a significant margin is the dominant one. Failing to identify this is worth **1 mark** lost in descriptive questions. > 4. **Not verifying the temperature sum:** After calculating $T_{so}$, sum the temperature drops across each layer. The sum should equal the overall $\Delta T$. This is a powerful self-check. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations:** ### Core Conceptual Points - The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** combines all individual heat transfer modes and fouling resistances in series. - $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ — the **fundamental design equation**. - Units: **W/m²·K** (SI) or **kcal/hr·m²·°C** (CGS). - **Higher U** ⇒ easier heat flow ⇒ **less area** required for a given duty. - **Fouling factor** is a design margin — always include it for industrial equipment. ### Geometric Formula Mastery - **Plane wall:** $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,\text{total}}$ - **Cylindrical wall (outside basis):** $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{r_o}{h_i r_i} + \sum \dfrac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \text{fouling terms}$ - **Area conversion:** $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$ ⇒ $\dfrac{U_i}{U_o} = \dfrac{D_o}{D_i}$ ### Key Numerical Safeguards 1. **Always convert mm to m** before calculation. 2. Use the **natural log** $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ for cylindrical conduction — never use $r_o - r_i$. 3. **Typical U values:** gas-gas (~10–50), water-water (~500–2000), condensing steam-water (~1500–5000). Use these to **sanity-check** your numerical answer. 4. The **largest resistance controls** the design. Identifying it is critical for both design optimization and exam marks. ### Common Exam Traps - Mixing the area reference ($U_i$ vs $U_o$) with the wrong area — must be **consistent**. - Forgetting to include fouling in the design calculation. - Confusing $h$ (convective coefficient) with $k$ (conductivity). - Using the wrong geometry formula (plane vs cylindrical) for the given problem. ### One-Sentence Summary > **"U is the inverse of the sum of all individual thermal resistances (convection, conduction, fouling) in series, and a higher U means more efficient heat transfer requiring less surface area."**

topics.title: Estimation of Heat Transfer Area in Single Effect and Multiple Effect (Double/Triple Effect Forward Feed Only) Evaporators with Negligible Boiling Point Rise
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Heat Transfer Area in Single Effect and Multiple Effect (Double/Triple Effect Forward Feed Only) Evaporators with Negligible Boiling Point Rise

# 🔥 Evaporator Heat Transfer Area Estimation: A Comprehensive KTU Guide

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

An **evaporator** is a heat transfer device used in chemical process industries to concentrate a solution by removing volatile solvents (predominantly water) through boiling. The **Heat Transfer Area (A)** of an evaporator is the effective surface area available for heat exchange between the heating medium (usually steam) and the liquid to be concentrated, governed by the fundamental heat transfer rate equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where the symbols carry their standard SI units and meanings used across all KTU board examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus**: The estimation procedure explicitly assumes **negligible Boiling Point Rise (BPR)**. This is a critical simplifying assumption taught in PBCHT504, which allows the total available temperature difference to be **equally distributed** across all effects in a multiple-effect evaporator.

### 1.2 The Two Principal Evaporator Configurations

| Configuration | Feed Flow Direction | Pressure/Vacuum Profile | Industrial Application |
|---------------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Forward Feed** | Feed & steam flow in the **same direction** (1→2→3) | Highest pressure in 1st effect, lowest in last | Dilute, non-viscous, non-heat-sensitive feeds (e.g., sugar juice, milk) |
| **Backward Feed** | Feed flows **opposite** to steam | Steam enters the last effect | Viscous, heat-sensitive solutions |

> [!NOTE]
> The current KTU Module 2 syllabus **restricts study to Forward Feed only** for Double and Triple Effect systems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Relay Race" 🏃‍♂️

Imagine three buckets lined up in a row representing three evaporator effects. Water is being boiled off in each bucket:

- **Bucket 1 (1st Effect)**: Fresh dilute feed enters. Steam heats it, water vapor escapes (concentrates the solution).
- **Bucket 2 (2nd Effect)**: Concentrated solution from Bucket 1 enters, and the **vapor from Bucket 1** acts as the heating medium. Lower pressure here allows boiling at a lower temperature.
- **Bucket 3 (3rd Effect)**: More concentrated solution from Bucket 2 enters, heated by **vapor from Bucket 2**.

> [!TIP]
> **The Magic**: Each "bucket" reuses the steam (vapor) from the previous one, dramatically improving **steam economy** — you evaporate far more water per kg of live steam consumed. With **negligible BPR**, the temperature drop in each bucket is identical, like three equally spaced steps on a staircase.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water (λ)** ≈ **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C, 101.325 kPa
- **Specific heat of dilute aqueous solutions (Cp)** ≈ **4.187 kJ/(kg·K)** (assumed equal to water when BPR is negligible and solution is dilute)
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**:
  - Single effect: **U ≈ 1500–3000 W/(m²·K)**
  - Multiple effect (each effect): **U ≈ 1000–2500 W/(m²·K)** (decreases due to lower ΔT and viscosity rise)
- **Steam Economy (E)**: kg of water evaporated per kg of live steam supplied
  - Single effect: E ≈ **0.8–1.0**
  - Double effect: E ≈ **1.6–2.0**
  - Triple effect: E ≈ **2.4–3.0**
- **Capacity (C)**: kg of water evaporated per hour (independent of number of effects, fixed by total ΔT)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Evaporator Heat Transfer Estimation

The estimation procedure follows a rigorous four-step logical flow universally accepted in KTU board valuation:

- **Step 1 — Material Balance (Mass)**: Quantify the total water to be evaporated based on feed rate, initial concentration, and desired final concentration.
- **Step 2 — Energy Balance (Heat)**: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to determine the live steam requirement using latent and sensible heat considerations.
- **Step 3 — Temperature Distribution**: Partition the total available ΔT (steam temp to condenser/vapor space temp) across all effects — equally when BPR is negligible.
- **Step 4 — Area Calculation**: Apply **Q = U·A·ΔT** for each effect, with the total Q being equal to total water evaporated × latent heat (under negligible BPR).

### 2.2 The Governing Assumptions for Negligible BPR Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the Boiling Point Rise is **assumed zero** (as mandated by the current KTU Module 2 sub-topic title), the following rigorous assumptions apply:

- The boiling point of the solution in each effect equals the saturation temperature of the vapor space (i.e., **T_solution = T_vapor** in every effect).
- The latent heat of vaporization is **constant** across all effects (equal to that of pure water at the corresponding pressure).
- The specific heat of the dilute solution is **constant** and equal to that of water.
- The total available temperature difference is **equally divided** among N effects:
  $$\Delta T_{\text{each effect}} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{N}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $F = L + V$ | Total mass balance | kg/h | All evaporators |
| 2 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Solute balance (non-volatile) | kg solute/h | Concentration change |
| 3 | $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Total water to be evaporated | kg/h | Capacity calculation |
| 4 | $U_S \cdot \lambda_S = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Single effect energy balance | W = J/s | Single effect only |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{\text{total}} = T_S - T_{\text{condenser}}$ | Total driving force | K or °C | All configurations |
| 6 | $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{N}$ | Equal ΔT per effect (negligible BPR) | K or °C | N-effect forward feed |
| 7 | $A_i = \dfrac{V_i \cdot \lambda_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Area of i-th effect | m² | Each effect |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{V \cdot \lambda}{U \cdot \Delta T}$ | Single effect area (V = U_S = V₁) | m² | Single effect |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{U_S}$ | Steam economy | dimensionless | Performance metric |
| 10 | $E_N \approx N \cdot E_1$ | Theoretical economy scaling | dimensionless | Ideal case (lossless) |
| 11 | $C = V_{\text{total}}$ | Evaporator capacity | kg/h | Independent of N (ideal) |
| 12 | $A_{\text{equal}} = \dfrac{V_1 \lambda_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} = \dfrac{V_2 \lambda_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2}$ | Equal-area design constraint | m² | Equal area design |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry**: Multi-effect evaporators concentrate cane juice from ~15% to ~70% solids.
- **Dairy Industry**: Milk concentration from 12% to 52% using triple/triple effect forward feed.
- **Paper & Pulp**: Black liquor concentration in Kraft process.
- **Desalination**: Large-scale multi-effect distillation (MED) plants.
- **Caustic Soda**: Evaporation of weak liquor in chlor-alkali plants.

> [!NOTE]
> In all these industries, the **heat transfer area** directly determines capital cost, while **steam economy** determines operating cost. The KTU 2024 design problem typically asks students to balance these two economic factors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Effect Evaporator Mass and Energy Balance

**Setup**: A single effect evaporator receives **F** kg/h of feed at concentration **x_F** (mass fraction) and concentration **x_L**. Live steam **U_S** kg/h at temperature **T_S** is supplied to the steam chest, condensing at temperature **T_S** with latent heat **λ_S**.

**Step 1 — Total Mass Balance**:
The total mass entering must equal the total mass leaving. Feed (F) enters; thick liquor (L) and vapor (V) leave.

$$F = L + V$$

**Step 2 — Solute (Non-volatile) Mass Balance**:
Solute is conserved — it does not leave with vapor.

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

**Step 3 — Combine Both to Find V**:
From mass balance, $L = F - V$. Substituting into solute balance:

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$

$$F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L$$

$$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F = F(x_L - x_F)$$

$$\boxed{V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental capacity equation** for all evaporator designs.

**Step 4 — Energy Balance (Steady State)**:
Heat in = Heat out (no accumulation). Heat enters as sensible heat of feed + latent heat of steam. Heat leaves as sensible heat of thick liquor + latent heat of vapor + sensible heat of vapor.

For the simplified KTU case (feed at boiling point, negligible heat losses, equal specific heats):

$$U_S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V$$

Because when the feed enters at its boiling point, all the heat supplied by the condensing steam goes into vaporizing water (no sensible heating required). Therefore:

$$\boxed{U_S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_V}{\lambda_S}}$$

Since λ_S ≈ λ_V for water at typical operating conditions:

$$U_S \approx V$$

**Step 5 — Area Calculation**:
The rate of heat transfer from condensing steam to boiling solution:

$$Q = U_S \cdot \lambda_S = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for **A**:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{U_S \cdot \lambda_S}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_V}{U \cdot \Delta T}}$$

where $\Delta T = T_S - T_{\text{solution boiling}}$ (negligible BPR ⇒ $T_{\text{solution}} = T_{\text{vapor}}$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Double Effect Forward Feed Evaporator (Negligible BPR)

**Setup**: Feed **F** at **x_F** enters Effect 1. Thick liquor **L₂** at **x_L₂** leaves Effect 2. Vapor **V₁** from Effect 1 serves as heating medium in Effect 2.

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance**:
For the entire two-effect system:

$$F = L_2 + (V_1 + V_2)$$

Total water evaporated: $V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2$.

**Step 2 — Overall Solute Balance**:

$$F \cdot x_F = L_2 \cdot x_{L_2}$$

**Step 3 — Individual Effect Mass Balances**:
- **Effect 1**: $F = L_1 + V_1$, and $F \cdot x_F = L_1 \cdot x_{L_1}$
- **Effect 2**: $L_1 = L_2 + V_2$, and $L_1 \cdot x_{L_1} = L_2 \cdot x_{L_2}$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance for Effect 1 (Steam Side)**:
Steam supplied: $U_S$. Heat given up by steam = heat used in Effect 1.

$$U_S \cdot \lambda_{S_1} = V_1 \cdot \lambda_{V_1}$$

**Step 5 — Energy Balance for Effect 2 (Vapor from Effect 1 as Heater)**:
The vapor from Effect 1 (V₁ kg/h) condenses in the steam chest of Effect 2:

$$V_1 \cdot \lambda_{V_1} = V_2 \cdot \lambda_{V_2}$$

**Step 6 — Temperature Distribution (Negligible BPR)**:
Total available temperature difference:

$$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = T_{S_1} - T_{V_2}$$

where $T_{S_1}$ is the steam temperature in Effect 1, and $T_{V_2}$ is the vapor temperature in Effect 2 (which equals the solution boiling point in Effect 2 since BPR = 0).

**Step 7 — Equal ΔT per Effect** (the critical KTU assumption):

$$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Heat Transfer Area of Each Effect**:
Using $Q_i = U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T_i$, and $Q_i = V_i \cdot \lambda_i$:

$$\boxed{A_1 = \frac{V_1 \cdot \lambda_1}{U_1 \cdot \Delta T_1} \quad ; \quad A_2 = \frac{V_2 \cdot \lambda_2}{U_2 \cdot \Delta T_2}}$$

**Step 9 — Equal Area Design** (commonly demanded in KTU problems):
For equal areas $A_1 = A_2 = A$:

$$A = \frac{V_1 \lambda_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} = \frac{V_2 \lambda_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2}$$

This is the key constraint for designing an "equal area" multiple effect evaporator.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Triple Effect Forward Feed Evaporator (Negligible BPR)

**Setup**: Feed **F** at **x_F** enters Effect 1. Vapor from each effect heats the next. Thick liquor **L₃** at **x_{L₃}** exits Effect 3.

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance**:

$$F = L_3 + (V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

**Step 2 — Overall Solute Balance**:

$$F \cdot x_F = L_3 \cdot x_{L_3}$$

Total water evaporated:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_{L_3}}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Energy Balance Across Cascade**:
In each effect, all heat input goes into vaporization (feed at boiling point, negligible BPR, no sensible heat):

$$Q_i = V_i \cdot \lambda_i$$

Heat cascade:

$$U_S \cdot \lambda_S = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2 = V_3 \cdot \lambda_3$$

If latent heats are approximately equal (which holds for moderate temperature ranges with negligible BPR):

$$U_S \approx V_1 \approx V_2 \approx V_3$$

This means **each effect evaporates approximately the same amount of water**, and steam economy approaches the ideal value of **3.0** for a triple effect.

**Step 4 — Temperature Distribution**:

$$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T_3 = \frac{T_{S_1} - T_{V_3}}{3} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Area of Each Effect**:

$$\boxed{A_i = \frac{V_i \cdot \lambda_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i} = \frac{V_i \cdot \lambda}{U_i \cdot (\Delta T_{\text{total}}/3)}}$$

**Step 6 — Steam Economy**:

$$\boxed{E_3 = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{U_S} \approx 3}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Number of Effects on Performance

| Parameter | Single Effect (N=1) | Double Effect (N=2) | Triple Effect (N=3) | KTU Implication |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Steam Economy (E) | ≈ 1.0 | ≈ 2.0 | ≈ 3.0 | Higher E ⇒ lower steam cost |
| Capacity (C = V_total) | Fixed by ΔT | Fixed by ΔT | Fixed by ΔT | Independent of N |
| ΔT per effect | ΔT_total | ΔT_total/2 | ΔT_total/3 | Lower ΔT ⇒ larger A |
| Area (A_total) | Baseline | ≈ 2× Single | ≈ 3× Single | Higher A ⇒ higher capital cost |
| U per effect | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | Viscosity effects reduce U |
| Optimum N (economic) | N = 1 | Often chosen | N = 3–4 | Beyond 4, marginal benefit diminishes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Effect Evaporator — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedIN["FEED F kg per h"] --> effect1{SINGLE EFFECT EVAPORATOR}
    steamIN["LIVE STEAM U_S kg per h"] -->|condenses lambda_S| effect1
    effect1 -->|V kg per h vapor| vaporOUT["VAPOR V kg per h"]
    effect1 -->|L kg per h| thickLiq["THICK LIQUOR L kg per h at x_L"]
    effect1 -.->|"Q = U_S x lambda_S = U x A x delta T"| heatEq["HEAT TRANSFER EQUATION"]

    style feedIN fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style steamIN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style effect1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style vaporOUT fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style thickLiq fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style heatEq fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Double Effect Forward Feed Evaporator — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedF["FEED F at x_F"] --> eff1{EFFECT 1}
    steamS["LIVE STEAM U_S"] -->|lambda_S| eff1
    eff1 -->|"V_1 vapor (heating medium)"| eff2{EFFECT 2}
    eff1 -->|"L_1 thick liquor to next effect"| eff2
    eff2 -->|"V_2 vapor to condenser"| cond["CONDENSER at T_V2"]
    eff2 -->|"L_2 final thick liquor"| finalL["L_2 at x_L2"]

    eff1 -.->|"A_1 = V_1 lambda_1 over U_1 delta T_1"| area1["AREA A_1"]
    eff2 -.->|"A_2 = V_2 lambda_2 over U_2 delta T_2"| area2["AREA A_2"]

    style feedF fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style steamS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style eff1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style eff2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style cond fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style finalL fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style area1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style area2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Triple Effect Forward Feed — Temperature Cascade Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["STEAM T_S (Highest Temp)"] --> T1["EFFECT 1: T_1 = T_S minus delta T by 3"]
    T1 --> T2["EFFECT 2: T_2 = T_1 minus delta T by 3"]
    T2 --> T3["EFFECT 3: T_3 = T_2 minus delta T by 3 (Lowest Temp)"]
    T3 --> Tcond["CONDENSER: T_V3 = T_3 (negligible BPR)"]

    T1 -.->|"delta T_1 = delta T by 3"| dT1["Equal Temperature Drop"]
    T2 -.->|"delta T_2 = delta T by 3"| dT2["Equal Temperature Drop"]
    T3 -.->|"delta T_3 = delta T by 3"| dT3["Equal Temperature Drop"]

    style T0 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Tcond fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style dT1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style dT2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style dT3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

### 4.4 Heat Transfer Area Calculation Flow — Algorithm Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["START: GIVEN F, x_F, x_L, T_S, T_cond, U_i"]) --> step1["STEP 1: COMPUTE V_total from F, x_F, x_L"]
    step1 --> step2["STEP 2: COMPUTE delta T_total = T_S minus T_cond"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3: COMPUTE delta T_i = delta T_total divided by N"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4: ASSUME EQUAL EVAPORATION per effect V_i = V_total divided by N"]
    step4 --> step5["STEP 5: COMPUTE Q_i = V_i x lambda_i"]
    step5 --> step6["STEP 6: COMPUTE A_i = Q_i divided by U_i x delta T_i"]
    step6 --> step7["STEP 7: COMPARE AREAS - EQUAL AREA DESIGN"]
    step7 --> decision{"AREAS EQUAL?"}
    decision -->|"NO"| adjust["ADJUST V_i DISTRIBUTION AND ITERATE"]
    decision -->|"YES"| final["FINAL: A_1 = A_2 = A_3 = A"]
    adjust --> step5
    final --> end1(["END: HEAT TRANSFER AREA OBTAINED"])

    style start fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
