# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Distinguish between the constructional features of a Short Tube Calandria Evaporator and a Long Tube Vertical Evaporator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Short Tube Calandria | Long Tube Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| **Tube orientation** | Horizontal | Vertical |
| **Tube length** | 1.0 – 2.0 m | 4.0 – 10.0 m |
| **Tube diameter** | 50 – 75 mm | 25 – 50 mm |
| **Circulation** | Natural via central downtake | Climbing film (rising) or falling film |
| **Vapor separation** | In upper large chamber | In top-mounted cyclone separator |
| **Typical U value** | 1500 – 2500 W/m²·K | 2000 – 3500 W/m²·K |
| **Capacity** | 500 – 30,000 kg/hr | 30,000 – 500,000 kg/hr |

*(Award 3 marks for 4–6 clear distinguishing points with relevant industrial context.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Why is forced circulation preferred for viscous and scaling liquids? Justify with two constructional features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Forced circulation evaporators are preferred for viscous and scaling liquids because:

1. **External centrifugal circulation pump** maintains a high tube-side velocity of **1.5 to 3.5 m/s**, which gives turbulent flow and a high heat transfer coefficient (5000–8000 W/m²·K), preventing laminar boundary layer buildup.

2. **Suppression of boiling inside tubes** — by maintaining tube pressure above saturation pressure, the liquid remains single-phase (no bubble formation), which eliminates localized superheating and reduces scaling/fouling rate on tube walls.

3. **External heat exchanger design** allows tube bundle to be isolated for mechanical cleaning without dismantling the entire evaporator.

*(Award 1 mark per valid justification, max 3.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

A single-effect **Short Tube Calandria Evaporator** is required to concentrate a feed of **10,000 kg/hr** of a weak liquor from **8% to 30% solids (by mass)**. Saturated steam at **2.0 bar (absolute)** is supplied. The boiling point elevation of the liquor is **6 K**. The evaporator operates under vacuum such that the saturation temperature of water is **80°C**. Assume overall heat transfer coefficient **$U = 1700$ W/m²·K** and tube dimensions: **$d_o = 60$ mm, $L = 1.25$ m**.

**Calculate:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The hourly steam consumption, hourly water evaporated, and steam economy.

**(b) [7 marks]** The required number of tubes and the shell (calandria) diameter assuming triangular pitch with pitch-to-diameter ratio $p_t/d_o = 1.5$.

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Given Data**

- Feed: $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.08$
- Product: $x_L = 0.30$
- Steam: $P_s = 2.0$ bar abs → From steam tables: $T_s = 120.21°C$, $\lambda_s = 2201.6$ kJ/kg
- Operating saturation: $T_{sat} = 80°C$ → $\lambda_w = 2308$ kJ/kg (approx)
- $\Delta T_{BPE} = 6$ K → $T_{liq} = 80 + 6 = 86°C$
- $U = 1700$ W/m²·K, $d_o = 0.060$ m, $L = 1.25$ m
- Pitch ratio: $p_t/d_o = 1.5$ → $p_t = 0.090$ m

---

#### **Part (a) — Mass and Energy Balance**

**Concentrate flow rate** (solids balance):
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} \\
L &= 10000 \times \frac{0.08}{0.30} \\
L &= 2666.67 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Water evaporated:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= F - L \\
W &= 10000 - 2666.67 \\
W &= 7333.33 \text{ kg/hr} \\
W &= 2.037 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective temperature difference:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_s - T_{liq} \\
\Delta T &= 120.21 - 86 \\
\Delta T &= 34.21 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Heat duty:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= W \cdot \lambda_w \\
Q &= 2.037 \times 2308 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
Q &= 4701.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam consumption:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s &= \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{4701.4}{2201.6} \\
m_s &= 2.135 \text{ kg/s} \\
m_s &= 7687 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam economy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{W}{m_s} = \frac{7333.33}{7687} \\
E &= 0.954
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic:** Solids balance → mass evaporated → latent heat → steam mass → economy.

---

#### **Part (b) — Tube Count and Shell Diameter**

**Heat transfer area:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} \\
A &= \frac{4701.4 \times 10^3}{1700 \times 34.21} \\
A &= 80.84 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of tubes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} \\
N &= \frac{80.84}{\pi \times 0.060 \times 1.25} \\
N &= \frac{80.84}{0.2356} \\
N &= 343.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round up → $N = 344$ tubes** (use next even number for symmetric layout).

**Shell diameter** (using triangular pitch count formula):
For triangular pitch with $p_t/d_o = 1.5$, the tube-count constant:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= 0.249, \quad n_1 = 2.207 \quad \text{(from Kern's tables for tri. pitch, 1 tube pass)} \\
D_s &= d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1} \\
D_s &= 0.060 \times \left(\frac{344}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207} \\
D_s &= 0.060 \times (1381.5)^{0.4531} \\
D_s &= 0.060 \times 26.41 \\
D_s &= 1.585 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round to standard → $D_s = 1.6$ m** shell diameter.

> **Final Summary:**
> - **Hourly steam consumption = 7687 kg/hr**
> - **Hourly water evaporated = 7333 kg/hr**
> - **Steam economy = 0.954**
> - **Number of tubes = 344**
> - **Shell diameter ≈ 1.6 m**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A **Forced Circulation Evaporator** is to be designed to concentrate **15,000 kg/hr** of a viscous organic solution from **12% to 45% solids**. Available steam is at **3 bar (absolute)**. The solution has the following properties:
- Density = 1050 kg/m³
- Viscosity = $3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Thermal conductivity = 0.42 W/m·K
- Specific heat = 2.8 kJ/kg·K

The feed enters at **35°C**, and the solution boils at **110°C** at operating pressure. Tube dimensions: $d_o = 40$ mm, $d_i = 35$ mm, $L = 4$ m. Required tube velocity = 2.0 m/s.

**Calculate:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The total heat duty, steam consumption, and steam economy.

**(b) [7 marks]** The number of tubes, mass flow rate per tube, and tube-side heat transfer coefficient using Dittus–Boelter correlation. State whether the assumed $U$ value (taken as 2500 W/m²·K) needs iteration.

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Given Data**

- $F = 15{,}000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.12$, $x_L = 0.45$
- Steam: $P_s = 3$ bar abs → $T_s = 133.5°C$, $\lambda_s = 2163.5$ kJ/kg
- $T_F = 35°C$, $T_{liq} = 110°C$, $\lambda_w$ at 110°C ≈ 2230 kJ/kg
- $\rho_l = 1050$ kg/m³, $\mu_l = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $k_l = 0.42$ W/m·K, $c_p = 2800$ J/kg·K
- $d_o = 0.040$ m, $d_i = 0.035$ m, $L = 4$ m, $u = 2.0$ m/s
- $U = 2500$ W/m²·K

---

#### **Part (a) — Mass, Energy, and Economy**

**Concentrate mass flow rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} = 15000 \times \frac{0.12}{0.45} \\
L &= 4000 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Water evaporated:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= F - L = 15000 - 4000 = 11000 \text{ kg/hr} \\
W &= 3.0556 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Heat duty (sensible + latent):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_F c_p (T_{liq} - T_F) + W \cdot \lambda_w \\
\dot{m}_F &= \frac{15000}{3600} = 4.1667 \text{ kg/s} \\
Q_{sensible} &= 4.1667 \times 2.8 \times (110 - 35) \\
Q_{sensible} &= 4.1667 \times 2.8 \times 75 = 875 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{latent} &= 3.0556 \times 2230 = 6814 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{total} &= 875 + 6814 = 7689 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam consumption:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s &= \frac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_s} = \frac{7689}{2163.5} \\
m_s &= 3.554 \text{ kg/s} \\
m_s &= 12794 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam economy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{W}{m_s} = \frac{11000}{12794} \\
E &= 0.860
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Economy < 1 because part of the steam's latent heat is "wasted" in preheating the cold feed — a typical KTU observation.

---

#### **Part (b) — Tubes, Flow, and Heat Transfer Coefficient**

**Effective ΔT:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_s - T_{liq} = 133.5 - 110 = 23.5 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Heat transfer area:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{7689 \times 10^3}{2500 \times 23.5} \\
A &= 130.87 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of tubes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} = \frac{130.87}{\pi \times 0.040 \times 4} \\
N &= \frac{130.87}{0.5027} \\
N &= 260.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round up → $N = 261$ tubes.**

**Mass flow rate per tube:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{tube} &= \rho_l \cdot u \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2 \\
\dot{m}_{tube} &= 1050 \times 2.0 \times \frac{\pi}{4} (0.035)^2 \\
\dot{m}_{tube} &= 1050 \times 2.0 \times 9.621 \times 10^{-4} \\
\dot{m}_{tube} &= 2.020 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total circulation rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{circ} &= N \times \dot{m}_{tube} = 261 \times 2.020 \\
m_{circ} &= 527.2 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reynolds number:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho u d_i}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 2.0 \times 0.035}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\
Re &= \frac{73.5}{0.003} = 24500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Prandtl number:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Pr &= \frac{c_p \mu}{k_l} = \frac{2800 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.42} = 20.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dittus–Boelter (heating, exponent 0.4):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Nu &= 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4} \\
Re^{0.8} &= (24500)^{0.8} = 4547 \\
Pr^{0.4} &= (20)^{0.4} = 3.314 \\
Nu &= 0.023 \times 4547 \times 3.314 \\
Nu &= 346.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tube-side coefficient:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_i &= \frac{Nu \cdot k_l}{d_i} = \frac{346.5 \times 0.42}{0.035} \\
h_i &= 4158 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration check:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + \frac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_d \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $h_o = 8000$ (steam condensing), $k_w = 50$ W/m·K (steel), $R_d = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{8000} + \frac{0.040}{0.035 \times 4158} + \frac{0.040 \times \ln(1.143)}{2 \times 50} + 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \\
\frac{1}{U} &= 0.000125 + 0.000275 + 0.0000534 + 0.00017 \\
\frac{1}{U} &= 0.0006234 \\
U_{new} &= 1604 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Since $U_{new} = 1604 < U_{assumed} = 2500$, **iteration is required**. The corrected area would be larger.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** in vacuum evaporators → wrong ΔT → wrong area. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using saturation temperature of water** instead of the liquor boiling temperature. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Steam economy > 1** in single-effect → implies second-law violation; should be ≤ 0.95 in realistic design. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing downtake (liquid return) with steam chest condensate line** in calandria schematic. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert kg/hr to kg/s** in $Q$ calculation → 3600× error in $A$. (–3 marks)
> 6. **Not iterating $U$** in forced circulation design — a 30–40% error in area goes uncorrected. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Wrong tube-count constants** ($K_1, n_1$) — Kern's tables differ for square vs triangular pitch. (–1 mark)
> 8. **Neglecting sensible heat of feed** in forced circulation design with cold feed → underestimates Q. (–1 mark)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this 60 seconds before entering the exam hall.**

### **A. Calandria (Short Tube) — Key Points**
- Horizontal tubes, 1–2 m long, 50–75 mm OD.
- **Natural circulation** via **central downtake** — driven by density difference.
- Robust, low cost, easy to clean — preferred for **crystallizing/ scaling** sugar juices.
- $U \approx 1500$–$2500$ W/m²·K.
- Downtake area typically ≥ 75–100% of total tube cross-section.

### **B. Long Tube Vertical — Key Points**
- Tubes 4–10 m long, 25–50 mm OD.
- **Climbing film** (rising) — feed at bottom; **Falling film** — feed at top.
- Climbing film: high $U$ (2000–3500); Falling film: low residence time → heat-sensitive liquids.
- Cannot handle viscous (>5 cP) or scaling liquids reliably.

### **C. Forced Circulation — Key Points**
- External pump; **boiling suppressed** in tubes → single-phase sensible heating.
- Tube velocity: **1.5–3.5 m/s** (golden rule).
- $h_i$ by **Dittus–Boelter**: $0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4} (k/d_i)$.
- Always iterate $U$ using $\dfrac{1}{U}$ resistance model.
- Used for **caustic, black liquor, viscous organics, scaling salts**.

### **D. Universal Design Equations (Always Applicable)**
1. $Q = U A \Delta T$
2. $Q = m_s \lambda_s = W \lambda_w$
3. $W = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$
4. $E = W/m_s$ (≤ 1 for single effect)
5. $N = A / (\pi d_o L)$

### **E. Selection Criteria Table**

| Property | Calandria | Long Tube | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low viscosity (< 5 cP) | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| High viscosity (> 5 cP) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ |
| Scaling tendency | ✓ (easy to clean) | ✗ | ✓✓ |
| Heat-sensitive | ✗ | ✓ (falling film) | ✓ |
| Large capacity | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | High |

### **F. Frequently Asked Numerical Touchpoints**
- BPE correction → +ΔT available
- ΔT calculation: $T_s - (T_{sat} + \text{BPE})$
- Shell diameter via Kern's formula (triangular pitch).
- Tube-side $h_i$ via Dittus–Boelter in forced circulation.

### **G. Common Pitfalls to Avoid (Exam Time)**
- Don't confuse **kg/hr** with **kg/s** in Q.
- Don't use $T_{sat}$ instead of $T_{liq}$ for $\Delta T$.
- Don't report $E > 1$.
- Don't skip $U$ iteration in forced circulation.
- Don't draw the downtake on the wrong side of the tube sheet.

---

### **KTU 2024 Module 2 — One-Line Takeaway**
> **Calandria = natural, robust, scaling-friendly; Long Tube = high capacity, sensitive to viscosity; Forced Circulation = pump-driven, ideal for viscous and fouling services — design revolves around $Q = UA\Delta T$ with $\Delta T$ corrected for BPE and the appropriate correlation for $U$.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Design of Process Equipment: Design of Thin Walled Pressure Vessels as per IS 2825 - Shells Subject to Internal and External Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Design of Process Equipment: Design of Thin Walled Pressure Vessels as per IS 2825 - Shells Subject to Internal and External Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 2825 Perspective)

A **pressure vessel** is a closed container, cylindrical or spherical (or any other geometry), designed to hold gases or liquids at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. The **Indian Standard IS 2825** (Unfired Pressure Vessels) governs the design, fabrication, testing, and inspection of such vessels used in the chemical, petrochemical, fertilizer, and allied process industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel Assumption (IS 2825 / ASME Equivalent)**
> A pressure vessel is classified as **thin-walled** when the ratio of the inner diameter to the wall thickness satisfies:
>
> $$\frac{D_i}{t} \geq 20 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{t}{D_i} \leq 0.05$$
>
> Under this assumption, the stress distribution across the wall thickness is assumed to be **uniform (membrane theory)**, and bending stresses through the thickness are neglected.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why a Pressure Vessel Looks the Way It Looks"

Imagine blowing up a long balloon (a cylinder) versus a round soccer ball (a sphere) to the same internal air pressure:

- The **soccer ball (sphere)** is much harder to burst because the curvature is uniform in **all directions**, and stress is shared across two principal meridians.
- The **long balloon (cylinder)** bulges out in the middle and is weaker along its **length** than around its **girth** — this is why an LPG cylinder fails by splitting along a longitudinal seam and not a circumferential one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In a thin cylindrical shell, the **hoop (circumferential) stress is exactly twice the longitudinal (axial) stress**. This is why pipe designers always place the welded longitudinal joint first to be inspected — it carries twice the load of a circumferential weld.

## 1.3 Classification of Pressure Vessels by Geometry

| Geometry | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| **Cylindrical** with hemispherical / torispherical / ellipsoidal / flat heads | Reactors, distillation columns, storage tanks |
| **Spherical** | LPG bullets, Horton spheres, very high-pressure reactors |
| **Conical** | Hopper bottoms, transitions |
| **Tubes** | Heat exchanger tubes, boiler tubes |

## 1.4 IS 2825 — Scope of Application

IS 2825 is the Indian counterpart of ASME BPVC Section VIII (Division 1). It prescribes:

- **Material specifications** (carbon, alloy, stainless steels)
- **Design stresses** and **joint efficiencies**
- **Shell thickness calculations** for internal and external pressure
- **Head design** (formed, forged, flat)
- **Nozzle reinforcement** rules
- **Corrosion allowance** and **weld details**

> [!TIP]
> The **standard metric units** for design pressure (MPa or N/mm²), diameter (mm), and thickness (mm) must be used uniformly throughout the design calculations — KTU examiners will penalize mixed units harshly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Membrane Stress Theory — Cylindrical Shell

When a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $D_i$ and wall thickness $t$ is subjected to internal gauge pressure $P$, two principal stresses develop in the shell wall.

### 2.1.1 Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — $\sigma_h$

The hoop stress acts in the circumferential direction and is computed by considering equilibrium of a half-cylinder of unit length, with the pressure acting on the projected diameter:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2t}$$

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — $\sigma_l$

The longitudinal stress acts along the axis of the cylinder and is computed by considering the equilibrium of the end cap force acting on the cross-sectional area of the wall:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4t}$$

### 2.1.3 The 2:1 Stress Ratio

From the two equations above, the ratio is **invariant** with respect to $P$ and $t$:

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{PD_i/2t}{PD_i/4t} = 2$$

This 2:1 ratio is the most important design takeaway in the entire module.

## 2.2 Membrane Stress Theory — Spherical Shell

For a thin spherical shell, the stress is identical in every meridian (an *isotropic* membrane state). Equilibrium of a hemisphere gives:

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A sphere is **2× more efficient** than a cylinder on a per-area basis. For a given pressure and diameter, a sphere requires only **half the wall thickness** of a cylinder. This is why large-volume gas storage is always spherical whenever economically feasible.

## 2.3 Design Stresses and Joint Efficiencies (IS 2825)

The **required thickness** is derived by equating the primary membrane stress to the **maximum allowable design stress**:

$$f = \frac{\text{Specified minimum tensile strength at design temperature}}{n} \times \text{Joint Efficiency } (\eta)$$

where $n$ is the **safety factor** (typically $n = 4.0$ for carbon and low-alloy steels, $n = 3.5$ for austenitic stainless steels per IS 2825).

| Weld Joint Type | Joint Efficiency $\eta$ |
|---|---|
| Spot-examined (radiographed 100% spot) | 1.00 |
| Welded, fully radiographed (Type 1) | 0.95 |
| Welded, spot radiographed (Type 2) | 0.85 |
| Welded, no radiography (Type 3) | 0.70 |

## 2.4 Corrosion Allowance ($C$)

An extra thickness is added to account for material loss over the design life (typically 10–20 years). IS 2825 recommends a **minimum corrosion allowance of 1.5 mm**, or greater if process conditions warrant.

$$t_{\text{required}} = \frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta - P} \quad \text{(cylindrical, internal pressure, hoop-controlled)}$$

$$t_{\text{total to be procured}} = t_{\text{required}} + C$$

## 2.5 External Pressure Design — The Buckling Phenomenon

A shell under **external pressure** does not fail by stress rupture; it fails by **elastic instability (buckling)**. The shell becomes geometrically unstable and collapses inward, even though the wall stress is still well below the yield strength.

The governing parameter is the **critical (buckling) external pressure**, $P_{cr}$, which depends on:
- Diameter $D_o$
- Thickness $t$
- Material Young's modulus $E$
- Material yield strength $f_y$
- Shell length / unsupported length $L$ (between stiffening rings)

IS 2825 prescribes the **graphical method (Fig. 5 of IS 2825)** for cylindrical shells:

1. Calculate parameter $A = 0.0915 \, L / D_o$
2. Calculate parameter $B = \dfrac{f_y}{2} \cdot \dfrac{D_o}{L_e \cdot t}$ (using effective length $L_e$)
3. Read off the **factor $C$** from the IS 2825 Fig. 5 chart corresponding to $(A, B)$
4. Compute **allowable external pressure**:
$$P_a = \frac{4B}{3(D_o/t)} \quad \text{(governed by } B \text{ from chart)}$$

The required thickness is back-calculated by iteration until the calculated $P_a$ exceeds the design external pressure with a safety factor of 1.5 (or 3.0 per IS 2825 collapse basis).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storage tanks** for LPG (spherical or mounded cylindrical) — high pressure, requires IS 2825 rigor
- **Distillation columns** — internal pressure, often tall, requiring vacuum or external pressure considerations during cool-down
- **Condensers & evaporators** — frequently designed for external pressure during start-up when internal vacuum exists
- **Steam drums** — combined internal pressure + external pipe reactions

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Module-2 numerical.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hoop stress (cylinder, internal) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D_i}{2t}$ | MPa | Primary stress in cylinder |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress (cylinder, internal) | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ | MPa | Half of hoop stress |
| 3 | Membrane stress (sphere, internal) | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ | MPa | Isotropic stress |
| 4 | Required thickness (cylinder, hoop) | $t = \dfrac{P D_i}{2 f \eta - P}$ | mm | Set $\sigma_h = f$ |
| 5 | Required thickness (sphere) | $t = \dfrac{P D_i}{4 f \eta - P}$ | mm | |
| 6 | Required thickness (cylinder, longitudinal) | $t = \dfrac{P D_i}{4 f \eta - P}$ | mm | Set $\sigma_l = f$ |
| 7 | Design stress | $f = \dfrac{S_{ut}\,@\,T_d}{n}$ | MPa | $n$ = 4 for CS, 3.5 for SS |
| 8 | Final procured thickness | $t_p = t + C$ | mm | $C \geq 1.5$ mm |
| 9 | Pressure ratio check (validity) | $\dfrac{P D_i}{2 f \eta} \leq 0.385$ | — | IS 2825 limit |
| 10 | Chart parameter $A$ | $A = 0.0915 \, L / D_o$ | — | External pressure |
| 11 | Chart parameter $B$ | $B = \dfrac{f_y}{2} \cdot \dfrac{D_o}{L_e \cdot t}$ | MPa | External pressure |
| 12 | Allowable external pressure | $P_a = \dfrac{4B}{3(D_o/t)}$ | MPa | From IS 2825 Fig. 5 |
| 13 | Critical buckling (cl-cl, long cyl) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\dfrac{t}{D_o}\right)^3$ | MPa | Euler-type formula |
| 14 | Hoop-to-longitudinal stress ratio | $\sigma_h / \sigma_l = 2$ | — | Geometric invariant |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Free Body**

Take a cylindrical shell of inner diameter $D_i$, thickness $t$, and consider a small length $L$ cut into two halves along a longitudinal plane.

**Step 2 — Forces Acting on the Free Body**

- The internal pressure $P$ pushes the two halves apart over the projected area $D_i \cdot L$.
- The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ resists separation across the two wall cross-sections, each of area $t \cdot L$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation**

Setting total resisting force equal to total pushing force:

$$\sigma_h \cdot (2 t L) = P \cdot (D_i \cdot L)$$

**Step 4 — Simplification**

Divide both sides by $2L$:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P D_i}{2 t}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P D_i}{2 t}}$$

> This is the **primary (governing) stress** in a cylindrical shell under internal pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell

**Step 1 — Free Body Selection**

Cut the shell with a plane **perpendicular** to the axis. The free body is the entire end cap plus the half-shell behind it.

**Step 2 — Forces Acting**

- The internal pressure $P$ acts on the projected end-cap area $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2$.
- The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts on the wall ring cross-section $\pi D_i t$ (approx. for thin shells).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation**

$$\sigma_l \cdot (\pi D_i t) = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Simplification**

Cancel $\pi D_i$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{P D_i}{4 t}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{P D_i}{4 t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** difference between hoop and longitudinal stress arises purely from **geometry** — the hoop must resist the load over a single diameter, while the longitudinal must resist it over the entire end-cap area, which (for the same wall area) gives a different stress value.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Stress in a Spherical Shell

**Step 1 — Free Body Selection**

Cut a sphere into two equal hemispheres along a great circle.

**Step 2 — Force Balance**

The pressure $P$ acts on the projected circular area $\pi D_i^2 / 4$. The membrane stress $\sigma_s$ acts tangentially on the circular cut $\pi D_i t$ (in two perpendicular meridians — the total resisting area is $2 \pi D_i t$).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation**

$$2 \sigma_s (\pi D_i t) = P \cdot \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Solve**

Cancel $\pi D_i$:

$$2 \sigma_s t = \frac{P D_i}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = \frac{P D_i}{4 t}}$$

> This is identical to the longitudinal stress in a cylinder, but **doubles the strength** because the stress is sustained in two orthogonal meridians simultaneously.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Thickness as per IS 2825 (Internal Pressure, Cylindrical Shell)

**Step 1 — Set the Membrane Stress Equal to the Allowable Design Stress**

By the **membrane + maximum stress design criterion** of IS 2825:

$$\sigma_h = f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{P D_i}{2 t} = f$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Joint Efficiency $\eta$**

To account for welded joint weakness, the allowable stress is reduced by $\eta$:

$$\frac{P D_i}{2 t} = f \eta$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$**

$$t = \frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract Pressure from Denominator (Rigorous IS 2825 Form)**

For accuracy when $P$ is not negligible compared to $f \eta$:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta - P}}$$

**Step 5 — Add Corrosion Allowance**

$$t_{\text{procurement}} = t + C, \quad C \geq 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Add Mill Tolerance** (negative)

Plate is ordered as a standard commercial plate, so add a mill tolerance (typically 0.0 to +0.3 mm — but **minus tolerance is not permitted**, so no subtraction).

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Critical Buckling Pressure for Long Cylinders (Clapeyron / Linear Theory)

For a long cylinder ($L \gg D$) clamped at both ends, the elastic buckling pressure is given by:

**Step 1 — Assumed Buckled Shape**

A long cylinder buckles into a series of diamond-shaped lobes (Lamé buckling mode). The critical mode corresponds to $n = 2$ lobes in the circumferential direction.

**Step 2 — Classical Buckling Formula (Bryan / Donnell)**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3$$

For steel, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa and $\nu = 0.3$, so:

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{0.91} \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 4.40 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison to Internal Pressure Capacity**

For $D_o / t = 100$ and $f = 140$ MPa:

- Hoop rupture pressure: $P = 2 f t / D_o = 2 \times 140 / 100 = 2.8$ MPa
- Buckling pressure: $P_{cr} = 4.40 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.44$ MPa

> The shell **buckles long before** it ruptures! This is why external pressure vessels are sized by stiffness, not strength.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Cylindrical Shell Under Internal Pressure

**Given:**
- Internal diameter $D_i = 1500$ mm
- Design pressure $P = 2.5$ MPa (gauge)
- Design temperature $T_d = 200$ °C
- Material: SA 516 Gr. 70 carbon steel, $S_{ut}$ at 200 °C $= 170$ MPa
- Safety factor $n = 4.0$
- Joint efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ (spot-radiographed)
- Corrosion allowance $C = 2.0$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute Design Stress**

$$f = \frac{170}{4.0} = 42.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required Thickness**

$$t = \frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta - P} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 42.5 \times 0.85 - 2.5} = \frac{3750}{72.25 - 2.5} = \frac{3750}{69.75}$$

$$t = 53.76 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Add Corrosion Allowance**

$$t_p = 53.76 + 2.0 = 55.76 \text{ mm} \approx 56 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Select Standard Plate**

Order next commercial size: **60 mm plate** (standard stock).

**Step 5 — Verify Maximum Pressure Ratio**

$$\frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 42.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{3750}{72.25} = 0.519$$

> [!WARNING]
> The value 0.519 **exceeds the IS 2825 limit of 0.385**. This means the shell cannot be designed by formula alone — it falls under the **Design by Analysis (Rule 6)** category, requiring a stress analysis, fatigue analysis, or use of heavier stiffening. The student must call this out explicitly in the answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IS 2825 Design Procedure for Internal Pressure

The schematic below maps the step-by-step functional architecture of a design workflow for a thin-walled cylindrical shell subjected to internal pressure, following IS 2825 provisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[START: Process Inputs] --> inputA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define a thin-walled pressure vessel as per IS 2825. What is the design stress and how is it computed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pressure vessel is classified as **thin-walled** when the ratio of internal diameter to wall thickness satisfies $\dfrac{D_i}{t} \geq 20$. Under this condition, membrane theory applies and stress through the thickness is uniform.

The **design stress** $f$ is computed as the ratio of the **specified minimum tensile strength at design temperature** $S_{ut}\,@\,T_d$ to a **factor of safety** $n$:

$$f = \frac{S_{ut}\,@\,T_d}{n}$$

Per IS 2825, $n = 4.0$ for carbon and low-alloy steels, and $n = 3.5$ for austenitic stainless steels. The design stress is then further reduced by the **joint efficiency** $\eta$ to obtain the effective allowable stress in welded regions.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel subjected to internal pressure. Why is the circumferential joint less critical than the longitudinal joint?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hoop Stress $\sigma_h$ | Longitudinal Stress $\sigma_l$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnitude** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D_i}{2t}$ | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ |
| **Direction** | Circumferential (around the girth) | Axial (along the length) |
| **Magnitude ratio** | $2\times$ longitudinal | Half of hoop |
| **Governing** | **Yes** — controls shell thickness | No — generally smaller |

The **circumferential weld** carries only the longitudinal stress ($\sigma_l$), while the **longitudinal weld** carries the much larger hoop stress ($\sigma_h$). Therefore, the longitudinal joint is the more critical one and must be fully radiographed to achieve the highest joint efficiency (0.95–1.0), whereas circumferential joints may be spot-examined.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Pressure Design**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **2000 mm** and is designed to operate at an internal pressure of **3.0 MPa** (gauge) and a temperature of **250 °C**. The material is **SA 387 Gr. 11** (chrome-moly steel) with a specified minimum tensile strength at 250 °C of **210 MPa**. The longitudinal joint is **fully radiographed (Type 1)**. Take a safety factor of 4.0 and a corrosion allowance of **1.5 mm**. **Determine the required shell thickness** as per IS 2825. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical pressure vessel is to be designed for the **same operating conditions** (D, P, T, material, joint type). **Calculate the required thickness** for the spherical shell and comment on the comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Cylindrical Shell**

**Step 1 — Design stress:**

$$f = \frac{210}{4.0} = 52.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Joint efficiency** (Type 1, fully radiographed):

$$\eta = 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Required thickness** (IS 2825 formula for cylinder under internal pressure):

$$t = \frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta - P} = \frac{3.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 52.5 \times 0.95 - 3.0}$$

$$t = \frac{6000}{99.75 - 3.0} = \frac{6000}{96.75}$$

$$\boxed{t = 62.01 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_p = 62.01 + 1.5 = 63.51 \text{ mm} \approx 64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Select next standard commercial plate** (e.g., 65 mm or 70 mm).

**Step 6 — Pressure ratio check (IS 2825 limit):**

$$\frac{P D_i}{2 f \eta} = \frac{3.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 52.5 \times 0.95} = \frac{6000}{99.75} = 0.6015$$

> **Ratio > 0.385** → The shell exceeds IS 2825 formula-design limits; **Design by Analysis (Rule 6)** must additionally be performed, or the joint efficiency must be raised to 1.0 (full spot-examination) and/or material must be upgraded.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Spherical Shell**

**Step 1 — Use spherical shell formula:**

$$t = \frac{P D_i}{4 f \eta - P} = \frac{3.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 52.5 \times 0.95 - 3.0}$$

$$t = \frac{6000}{199.5 - 3.0} = \frac{6000}{196.5}$$

$$\boxed{t = 30.53 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_p = 30.53 + 1.5 = 32.03 \text{ mm} \approx 32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison comment (carries 2 of the 7 marks):**

The sphere requires **32 mm vs. 64 mm** for the cylinder — a **2:1 thickness advantage**, confirming that a sphere uses material twice as efficiently. The reason: hoop stress in a cylinder is 2× the membrane stress in a sphere, and required thickness scales linearly with stress. For very large storage volumes (e.g., LPG bullets), the spherical geometry is preferred for material economy despite the higher fabrication complexity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — External Pressure Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A vertical cylindrical vacuum column of internal diameter **1500 mm** and unsupported (effective) length **5000 mm** must withstand an external pressure of **0.10 MPa** (gauge vacuum). The shell material is **SA 240 Gr. 304** stainless steel, with yield strength at design temperature **$f_y = 175$ MPa** and Young's modulus **$E = 1.95 \times 10^5$ MPa**. Take Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$ and assume clamped-clamped end conditions. **Estimate the required shell thickness** using the classical buckling formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the design code (IS 2825) demands a **safety factor of 3.0** on the critical buckling pressure and you must add a **1.5 mm corrosion allowance**, what is the **final plate thickness to be ordered**? Justify whether a stiffening ring is required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Classical Buckling Thickness**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $D_o \approx D_i + 2t \approx D_i$ for thin wall (initially assume $D_o = 1500$ mm)
- $L / D_o = 5000 / 1500 = 3.33$ — **long cylinder** classification
- $E = 1.95 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 2 — Apply classical (Bryan) buckling formula for long cylinders:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \times 1.95 \times 10^5}{1 - 0.09} \left(\frac{t}{1500}\right)^3 = \frac{3.90 \times 10^5}{0.91} \left(\frac{t}{1500}\right)^3$$

$$P_{cr} = 4.286 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{t}{1500}\right)^3$$

**Step 3 — Set the allowable buckling pressure equal to design external pressure** (factor of safety applied separately in part b):

$$P_{cr} = P_{ext} = 0.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$0.10 = 4.286 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{t}{1500}\right)^3$$

$$\left(\frac{t}{1500}\right)^3 = \frac{0.10}{4.286 \times 10^5} = 2.333 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{t}{1500} = (2.333 \times 10^{-7})^{1/3} = 6.16 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t = 6.16 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 = 9.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Initial conclusion:** About **9–10 mm** is the minimum thickness for elastic stability.

> However, classical theory alone is **not** sufficient for IS 2825 compliance. The graphical method (Fig. 5) should also be used as a check. In KTU examinations, mentioning this limitation carries marks.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Safety Factor, Corrosion Allowance, and Stiffener Decision**

**Step 1 — Apply safety factor of 3.0:**

$$P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{3.0}$$

For the trial thickness $t = 9.24$ mm, $P_{cr} = 0.10$ MPa already (since we set them equal in part a). So with the SF:

$$P_{cr, \text{required}} = 3.0 \times 0.10 = 0.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Re-solve for new thickness $t_{new}$:**

$$0.30 = 4.286 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{t_{new}}{1500}\right)^3$$

$$\left(\frac{t_{new}}{1500}\right)^3 = \frac{0.30}{4.286 \times 10^5} = 7.00 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{t_{new}}{1500} = (7.00 \times 10^{-7})^{1/3} = 8.88 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t_{new} = 13.31 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{\text{required, total}} = 13.31 + 1.5 = 14.81 \text{ mm} \approx 15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Order commercial plate size** (e.g., **16 mm** standard plate).

**Step 5 — Stiffener ring decision (carries 3 of the 7 marks):**

A stiffening ring is required if the unsupported length $L = 5000$ mm produces a $D_o / t$ ratio that would otherwise require an impractically thick shell. Per IS 2825:

$$\frac{L}{D_o} = \frac{5000}{1500} = 3.33$$

This is moderately long, and 15 mm plate is acceptable without a stiffener for $P_{ext} = 0.10$ MPa. However, for **taller vessels or higher vacuums**, a stiffening ring should be added at mid-span to reduce $L_e$ to ~2500 mm, halving the required thickness and material cost. **Decision: No stiffener needed for the given case**, but the design must be verified via the IS 2825 Fig. 5 chart.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places where KTU students lose marks on Pressure Vessel Module-2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — mixing $D_i$ in m with $P$ in MPa causes a **factor-of-1000 error** that is a guaranteed zero. Always work in **mm, MPa, N/mm²** consistently.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the pressure term in the denominator** — Writing $t = PD/(2f\eta)$ instead of $t = PD/(2f\eta - P)$ is a common but serious error. The rigorous IS 2825 form must be used.
>
> 3. **Not adding corrosion allowance** — $t_{procurement} = t_{calculated} + C$. Omitting $C$ costs a full 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the 0.385 pressure-ratio check** — If $\dfrac{PD}{2f\eta} > 0.385$, the formula-design method is invalid; the student must explicitly state this and recommend "Design by Analysis." Many students silently ignore this.
>
> 5. **Confusing external and internal pressure formulas** — External pressure uses the **chart-based $P_a$** (or classical buckling), not the membrane rupture formula. Using $t = PD/(2f\eta - P)$ for external pressure is a fatal conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels)**

- **Thin-walled criterion:** $D_i/t \geq 20$; membrane theory applies.
- **Hoop stress** in a cylinder: $\sigma_h = PD/(2t)$ — the **primary** stress.
- **Longitudinal stress** in a cylinder: $\sigma_l = PD/(4t)$ — exactly **half** of hoop.
- **Hoop-to-longitudinal ratio** is always **2:1** — independent of $P$, $D$, or $t$.
- **Spherical stress:** $\sigma_s = PD/(4t)$ — sphere uses **half** the material of a cylinder.
- **Design stress:** $f = S_{ut}\,@\,T_d \,/\, n$ where $n = 4$ (CS) or $3.5$ (SS).
- **Joint efficiency $\eta$:** 1.00 / 0.95 / 0.85 / 0.70 based on NDT method.
- **Required thickness (cylinder, internal):** $t = PD_i / (2f\eta - P)$ + **corrosion allowance**.
- **Pressure ratio limit:** $PD_i/(2f\eta) \leq 0.385$ for formula-based design.
- **External pressure failure** is **buckling**, not rupture — shell is stiffness-controlled.
- **Critical buckling pressure (long cyl):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2}(t/D_o)^3$.
- **IS 2825 external pressure:** use Fig. 5 chart with parameters $A$ and $B$.
- **Stiffener rings** reduce effective length and permit thinner shells under external pressure.
- **Safety factors:** SF = 3.0 on critical buckling for external pressure.
- **Units consistency:** always mm + MPa + N/mm² throughout.

> **Final Note:** The thin-walled pressure vessel design module is the most heavily weighted topic in PBCHT504 Module 2. Master the four core formulas (hoop, longitudinal, sphere, thickness), the joint-efficiency concept, and the external-pressure buckling logic. KTU questions typically test **both computational accuracy and code-compliance judgment** — you must always check the 0.385 ratio and add the corrosion allowance.

---

topics.content:

> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flat Ends, Ends Concave to Pressure, and Convex to Pressure: Domed Ends and Conical Ends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Pressure Vessel End Closures

In the design of pressure vessels, the **closure (or "end")** is the structural component used to seal the open end(s) of a cylindrical or spherical shell. The selection of the appropriate end geometry is one of the most critical decisions in pressure vessel design, as it directly influences:

- **Material consumption** and weight
- **Stress distribution** under internal/external pressure
- **Manufacturing cost** and complexity
- **Mechanical stability** (buckling, fatigue, rupture)
- **Code compliance** (ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A pressure vessel end is a curved or flat structural element, integral or welded to the shell, that closes the open extremity of a vessel and contains the process fluid pressure while transferring load to the cylindrical shell.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Pressure Vessel Ends

The **six principal categories** of vessel closures (per KTU Module 2 syllabus) are:

| Type | Geometry | Behavior Under Internal Pressure |
|------|----------|----------------------------------|
| **Flat Ends** | Planar disc | High bending stress |
| **Domed Ends (Convex)** | Curved outward (hemispherical, ellipsoidal, torispherical) | Membrane (tensile) stress |
| **Conical Ends (Convex)** | Tapered cone | Membrane (tensile) stress |
| **Concave Ends** | Curved inward | Membrane (compressive) stress |
| **Torispherical** | Spherical crown + toroidal knuckle | Mixed membrane + bending |
| **Hemispherical** | Half sphere | Lowest stress (most efficient) |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: The "Inflated Balloon vs. The Cardboard Box Lid"

Imagine you have an open cardboard box (the cylindrical shell) and you need to close it.

- **Flat End (Cardboard Box Lid)**: If you press on a flat cardboard lid, it bends dramatically with very little force. The flat plate has to *resist bending* to contain the pressure. **Stress is HIGH** because the flat geometry has no natural curvature to develop membrane (in-plane) tension.

- **Domed End (Half a Soccer Ball)**: A soccer ball, when kicked, distributes the impact force across its entire curved surface through pure *membrane tension* (like a stretched rubber balloon). The curvature converts pressure into in-plane tensile stress, which is far more efficient. **Stress is LOW** — the more curved the dome, the lower the stress.

- **Conical End (Traffic Cone)**: A traffic cone has an intermediate sloped shape. It develops mostly membrane stress along its surface, similar to a dome, but the slope introduces a *component of force* that pushes outward at the junction with the cylinder. The half-angle α governs this effect.

- **Concave End (Inverted Bowl)**: An inverted bowl, if pressed down from above, would experience *compression*. Concave ends are used where external pressure or vacuum is dominant. They behave opposite to convex ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** Curvature is a *friend* under internal pressure (membrane tension is efficient). Flat geometry is an *enemy* (bending stress is expensive in material). Hence: **Hemispherical > Ellipsoidal > Torispherical > Conical > Flat**, in terms of stress efficiency.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Internal Design Pressure (P):** in **MPa** or **kg/cm²** (per IS 2825 / ASME BPVC).
- **Inside Diameter (D):** in **mm**.
- **Allowable Stress (S):** in **MPa**, derived from ultimate tensile strength / factor of safety.
- **Joint Efficiency (E):** dimensionless, 0.7–1.0 depending on weld inspection.
- **Corrosion Allowance (c):** added to calculated thickness, typically **1.5–3 mm**.
- **Standard Test Pressure Factor:** **1.3 × P** (hydrostatic) or **1.43 × P** (pneumatic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 2, the student is expected to derive the thickness equation for **each** of the five end types (flat, domed-hemispherical, domed-ellipsoidal, torispherical, and conical) using the membrane theory of shells and plate theory.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flat Ends (Flat Heads / Cover Plates)

Flat ends are the **simplest and cheapest** closures. They are circular plates (or rectangular) bolted or welded to the shell flange.

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Basis — Plate Bending Theory

A flat circular plate clamped at its edge and subjected to uniform pressure behaves as a **flexural (bending) member**, not a membrane. The governing equation is derived from **Kirchhoff's plate theory**:

$$\frac{d^4 w}{dr^4} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{d^3 w}{dr^3} - \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r^3}\frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{q}{D_p}$$

Where the **flexural rigidity** of the plate is:

$$D_p = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $t$ = plate thickness
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $q$ = uniformly distributed pressure

### 2.1.2 Maximum Bending Stress (Edge-Clamped Plate)

For a circular flat plate **fully clamped (fixed)** at the periphery, the **maximum bending stress occurs at the outer edge**:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot R^2}{4 \cdot t^2} \quad \text{(clamped)}$$

For a **simply supported** (loose) plate:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot R^2}{8 \cdot t^2} \cdot \frac{(3 + \nu)}{(1 + \nu)} \quad \text{(simply supported)}$$

For steel with $\nu = 0.3$, the simply supported case gives $\sigma_{max} \approx \frac{3.81 P R^2}{8 t^2}$.

### 2.1.3 ASME Code Design Equation (Unified Form)

The **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1, UG-34** provides a unified formula for all flat heads:

$$t = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot P}{S \cdot E}}$$

Where the **factor C** depends on the edge attachment condition:

| Edge Condition | C Value |
|----------------|---------|
| Circular plate, simply supported (loose) | **0.30** |
| Circular plate, fully fixed (integral with shell) | **0.50** |
| Circular plate with **fillet weld** (corner reinforcement) | **0.25** |
| Circular plate, corner **bending** (not recommended for high P) | 0.33 |
| Square plate, simply supported | 0.20 |
| Square plate, fully fixed | 0.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why factor C?** The C factor is essentially the ratio $\frac{\sigma_{max}}{3P(R/t)^2}$ rearranged to express required thickness. Lower C means less thickness required because the edge attachment provides greater rotational restraint.

### 2.1.4 Practical Design Steps

1. Identify edge condition → select **C**
2. Compute minimum thickness **t = d·√(CP/SE)**
3. Add **corrosion allowance (c)** → t_design = t + c
4. Verify **bolting/flange** strength if removable
5. Check **dishing** under hydrotest (max deflection $\delta_{max} \approx 0.7 R^4 P / E t^3$)

---

## 2.2 Domed Ends — Convex to Pressure (Most Common in Industry)

Domed ends have a **curvature convex toward the inside** of the vessel, so internal pressure produces **membrane (in-plane tensile) stress**, which is far more efficient than bending.

### 2.2.1 Hemispherical Head

The most stress-efficient shape — half of a perfect sphere.

**Membrane Stress Equilibrium (Derivation in §3):**

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t}$$

**ASME BPVC Required Thickness:**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P}$$

Where **R = inside crown radius = D/2**.

### 2.2.2 Ellipsoidal Head (2:1 Semi-Ellipsoidal)

The most widely used domed head in industry. The **2:1** designation means the major axis is **twice** the minor axis (depth = D/4).

For a general ellipsoid with semi-axes **a** (major, in-plane) and **b** (minor, depth):

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot a^2}{2 \cdot t \cdot b} \quad \text{(at the crown, where curvature is maximum)}$$

**ASME BPVC Required Thickness (2:1 ratio):**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P}$$

Notice this is **identical in form to the hemispherical** equation — by KTU convention, the 2:1 ellipsoidal is treated with this simplified formula.

### 2.2.3 Torispherical Head (Korbbogen)

Composed of a **spherical crown** (radius $R_c$) and a **toroidal knuckle** (radius $r$).

**ASME BPVC Required Thickness:**

$$t = \frac{0.885 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{S \cdot E - 0.1 \cdot P}$$

For the standard **2:1 torispherical** (Korbbogen):

$$R_c = D, \quad r = 0.06 \cdot D \quad \text{(or alternatively } r = 0.1D\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The factor **0.885** comes from a stress concentration factor of **~1.13** at the crown-knuckle transition. The crown stress is $\sigma = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 t}$, but the **knuckle (torus) region** has a stress concentration that elevates the local stress, hence the 1/(2 × 0.885) ≈ 1/1.77 correction.

---

## 2.3 Conical Ends (Convex to Pressure)

Conical ends are used as **transitions** between vessels of different diameters, or as **hoppers/bins** for solid flow.

### 2.3.1 Membrane Stress on a Conical Shell

For a cone of half-angle **α**, the principal membrane stresses are:

$$\sigma_{meridional} = \frac{P \cdot d}{2 \cdot t \cdot \cos \alpha}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot d}{t \cdot \cos \alpha}$$

Where **d** is the **local** inside diameter at the section of interest.

**ASME BPVC Required Thickness:**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \cos \alpha \cdot (S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P)}$$

Where D is the **largest** inside diameter of the cone (at the shell junction).

> [!WARNING]
> **The 0.6P Term:** This denominator term accounts for the **secondary bending stress** at the cone-cylinder junction. Unlike cylindrical shells, cones experience both membrane stress AND discontinuity stress at the transition. The 0.6 factor (instead of 0.2 used for cylinders) reflects this higher stress intensity.

### 2.3.2 Reinforcement at the Cone-Cylinder Junction

A **reinforcement pad (or ring)** is often required because the cone-cylinder intersection has:

- Geometric discontinuity (slope change)
- **Discontinuity shear force** $V = \frac{P \cdot D \cdot \tan \alpha}{2}$
- **Discontinuity bending moment** $M = \frac{P \cdot D \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot t_c / 2}}{2}$

The reinforcement area (ASME) must satisfy:

$$A_r = \frac{0.0006 \cdot D^2 \cdot P \cdot Y \cdot \cos \alpha}{\tan \alpha} \quad \text{(simplified form)}$$

---

## 2.4 Ends Concave to Pressure

When the curvature is **concave toward the pressure side** (e.g., an inverted bowl with internal pressure), the membrane stresses are **compressive**. These are used in:

- **Vacuum vessels** (external pressure dominant)
- **Storage tanks** with internal vacuum relief
- **Specialty reactors** with reverse flow

**Governing Failure Mode: Buckling**, not yielding.

**Critical Buckling Pressure (for a spherical shell under external pressure):**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^2}{\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^2)}}$$

**ASME BPVC — Rules for External Pressure:**

The thickness is governed by an **elastic stability chart** (chart in ASME BPVC):

$$P_a = \frac{4 \cdot E}{(3 \cdot (D_o/t))} \quad \text{(spherical shell, external pressure)}$$

Where $D_o$ is the **outside diameter** and $t$ is thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** Problems on concave ends often ask to determine the **maximum allowable external pressure** given thickness, or to find **required thickness** to resist a given external pressure using the elastic stability concept.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **End Type** | **Required Thickness Equation (ASME BPVC)** | **Stress Equation** | **Failure Mode** |
|--------------|----------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Flat (loose) | $t = d\sqrt{\dfrac{0.30 \cdot P}{S \cdot E}}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P R^2}{8 t^2} \cdot \dfrac{(3+\nu)}{(1+\nu)}$ | Bending |
| Flat (fixed) | $t = d\sqrt{\dfrac{0.50 \cdot P}{S \cdot E}}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}$ | Bending |
| Flat (fillet) | $t = d\sqrt{\dfrac{0.25 \cdot P}{S \cdot E}}$ | Bending with reduced lever arm | Bending |
| Hemispherical | $t = \dfrac{P R}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{P R}{2 t}$ | Membrane (tensile) |
| 2:1 Ellipsoidal | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | Membrane (tensile) |
| Torispherical (Korbbogen) | $t = \dfrac{0.885 P R_c}{S E - 0.1 P}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{P R_c}{2 t}$ at crown | Membrane + SCF |
| Conical | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 \cos \alpha (S E - 0.6 P)}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{P D}{2 t \cos \alpha}$ | Membrane + discontinuity |
| Concave (External P) | Use ASME chart: $P_a = \dfrac{4 E}{3(D_o/t)}$ | Compressive | Buckling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas in this exact form** — KTU exam questions typically present the numerical values and expect you to substitute directly. The **0.2P, 0.6P, and 0.1P** constants are **the most commonly missed** marks in university exams.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Preferred End Type** | **Reason** |
|----------------|------------------------|------------|
| LPG bullets, propane tanks | **Hemispherical** | Maximum stress efficiency, lowest weight |
| Reactor vessels, distillation columns | **2:1 Ellipsoidal** | Standard, good stress efficiency, easy to manufacture |
| Storage tanks (low pressure) | **Torispherical** | Cheaper than ellipsoidal, slight efficiency loss |
| Cyclone separators, hoppers | **Conical** | Allows solid/slurry flow by gravity |
| Vacuum receivers | **Concave / flat** | No inward pressure; flat OK if small diameter |
| Heat exchangers (channel) | **Channel cover (flat with gasket)** | Removable for tube cleaning |
| Pharmaceutical reactors | **Flat (bolted)** | Easy inspection, clean-in-place access |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Membrane Stress in a Hemispherical Head

### Step 1 — Free-Body Setup

Consider a hemisphere of inner radius $R$ and wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $P$. Cut the hemisphere into two equal halves with a vertical plane through the equator.

**Conversion logic:** The internal pressure pushes outward on the inside surface; the cut exposes the membrane stress that resists this pressure.

### Step 2 — Force Balance

The pressure force acting on the cut hemisphere (projected onto the vertical plane) is:

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

This force is resisted by the **meridional (longitudinal) membrane stress** acting around the cut circle of circumference $2\pi R$ through thickness $t$:

$$F_{stress} = \sigma \cdot (2 \pi R) \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation

For static equilibrium, the resisting force must equal the applied pressure force:

$$\sigma \cdot (2 \pi R) \cdot t = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Membrane Stress

Divide both sides by $2 \pi R t$:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot \pi R^2}{2 \pi R t} = \frac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t}$$

This is the **hoop stress in a hemispherical head**:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{P R}{2 t}}$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Required Thickness Equation

Set the calculated stress equal to the allowable stress $S$ (with joint efficiency $E$), and solve for $t$:

$$\frac{P R}{2 t} \leq S \cdot E \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \geq \frac{P R}{2 S E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rigour Step:** In the **more accurate** ASME formulation, the denominator uses $2 S E - 0.2 P$ (not just $2 S E$). This accounts for the fact that the calculated stress $PR/(2t)$ is *not* the only stress — the difference comes from the **hoop stress** $\sigma_h = P R / t$ which is twice the meridional. Using Tresca or von Mises yield criterion, the safety factor equation becomes:

$$t = \frac{P R}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

This is the **governing design thickness** in the KTU syllabus and ASME BPVC.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Membrane Stress in a 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head

### Step 1 — Geometry of the 2:1 Ellipsoid

The 2:1 ellipsoidal head has:
- **Major axis** (in-plane semi-axis): $a = D/2$ (in the plane of the head)
- **Minor axis** (depth semi-axis): $b = D/4$ (perpendicular to the head)

The defining ellipsoid equation:

$$\frac{x^2 + y^2}{a^2} + \frac{z^2}{b^2} = 1$$

### Step 2 — Principal Radii of Curvature at the Crown

For an ellipsoid of revolution, the two principal radii of curvature at the **crown (pole, deepest point)** are:

$$R_1 = R_2 = \frac{a^2}{b} \quad \text{(both principal radii equal at the pole)}$$

Substituting $a = D/2$ and $b = D/4$:

$$R_1 = \frac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = \frac{D^2/4}{D/4} = D$$

So at the crown, the radius of curvature equals **D**, the inside diameter. This is the same as a hemisphere of diameter D — that's why a 2:1 ellipsoid behaves like a "deep" hemisphere at the crown.

### Step 3 — Stress at the Crown (Membrane Equilibrium)

Using the general shell membrane equation (from Flugge or Timoshenko):

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot a^2}{2 t \cdot b} = \frac{P \cdot (D/2)^2}{2 t \cdot (D/4)} = \frac{P \cdot D^2 / 4}{2 t \cdot D/4} = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

This is **identical in form to the hemisphere** with $R = D/2$ — a coincidence of the 2:1 geometry!

### Step 4 — Required Thickness

Setting $\sigma = S E$ and solving:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 2:1 ellipsoid and hemisphere have the **same thickness equation**, but the ellipsoid requires **2× less depth** to be fabricated. That's why it's the industrial default.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torispherical (Korbbogen) Head Stress

### Step 1 — Geometry Definition

A torispherical head consists of:
- A **central spherical cap** (crown) of radius $R_c$ (typically $R_c = D$ for standard heads)
- A **toroidal knuckle** of radius $r$ (typically $r = 0.06 D$ to $0.1 D$)
- A **straight flange** (skirt) that joins the cylindrical shell

### Step 2 — Stress in the Spherical Crown

At the very top of the crown, the membrane stress is **hemispherical** in character:

$$\sigma_{crown} = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 t}$$

### Step 3 — Stress Concentration at the Knuckle

The knuckle is a section of a **torus** (donut-shape). In the toroidal region, the principal radii of curvature have **opposite signs**:

- One radius is the **torus radius** $r$ (in-plane curvature, small)
- The other is the **meridional radius** along the torus centerline (large, convex)

The membrane stress in a torus is:

$$\sigma_{torus} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 t} \cdot \frac{R_{c,local}}{R_{c,local} - r}$$

where $R_{c,local}$ is the local meridional radius along the torus centerline. The factor $\frac{R_{c,local}}{R_{c,local} - r}$ is a **stress concentration factor** that can be ~1.10 to 1.20 at the knuckle.

### Step 4 — Combined Governing Stress

The maximum stress is at the **crown-knuckle junction**. Combining membrane stress with the SCF:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 t} \cdot SCF$$

For a standard 2:1 torispherical, the ASME code uses the empirical factor $SCF \approx 1/0.885 \approx 1.13$, leading to:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{1.77 \cdot t} = \frac{0.885 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{t}$$

### Step 5 — Required Thickness

Setting $\sigma_{max} = S E - 0.1 P$:

$$t = \frac{0.885 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{S E - 0.1 P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **0.1P** in the denominator (instead of 0.2P) reflects the lower secondary stress contribution. ASME 0.1P is empirical, calibrated against the 1.13 SCF.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Conical Head (Membrane + Junction Discontinuity)

### Step 1 — Membrane Stress on a Cone

Consider a thin cone with half-angle $\alpha$ (measured from the axis), wall thickness $t$, at a location where the local inside diameter is $d$.

**Free-body:** Cut the cone perpendicular to the axis at the location of interest. The pressure force on the projected area:

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The resisting force in the **meridional (slant) direction** acts along the slant circumference, but its vertical component must equal the pressure force. The slant circumference at the cut is $\pi d / \cos \alpha$, and the meridional stress acts along the slant.

**Vertical component of meridional force:**

$$F_{meridional, vertical} = \sigma_m \cdot (\pi d) \cdot t \cdot \cos \alpha$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $\cos \alpha$ arises because the meridional stress is directed along the slant, and only the axial (vertical) component of the slant circumference contributes to the axial force balance.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium

$$\sigma_m \cdot \pi d \cdot t \cdot \cos \alpha = P \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Solve for meridional stress:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{P \cdot d}{4 t \cos \alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait!** Earlier (§2.3) I wrote $\sigma_m = \frac{P d}{2 t \cos \alpha}$. Let me recheck the free body.

**Corrected free body:** The slant circumference at a horizontal cut is **$\pi d / \cos \alpha$** (the cut is at diameter $d$, but measured along the slant it's longer by $1/\cos \alpha$). The meridional force is:

$$F_{meridional} = \sigma_m \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d}{\cos \alpha}\right) \cdot t$$

Its **axial (vertical) component**:

$$F_{meridional, vertical} = \sigma_m \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d}{\cos \alpha}\right) \cdot t \cdot \cos \alpha = \sigma_m \cdot \pi d \cdot t$$

Equating to pressure:

$$\sigma_m \cdot \pi d \cdot t = P \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \Rightarrow \sigma_m = \frac{P d}{4 t}$$

> **Conversion logic confirmed:** The meridional stress in a cone is **identical to a cylinder** of the same local diameter — it does NOT depend on the cone angle! This is a beautiful result from membrane theory.

### Step 3 — Hoop (Circumferential) Stress in a Cone

For a cone, applying the membrane hoop equilibrium:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot d}{2 t \cos \alpha}$$

> **Why the $1/\cos\alpha$?** The hoop stress acts perpendicular to the slant in the circumferential direction. The pressure force on a small element projects to a larger area on the slant, hence the geometric amplification.

### Step 4 — Combined Stress (for design)

The maximum principal stress is the hoop stress (since $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_m$ in a cone). The required thickness is:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 \cos \alpha (S E - 0.6 P)}$$

The **0.6P** term in the denominator (much higher than the 0.2P of a cylinder) accounts for the **junction discontinuity stresses** at the cone-cylinder interface.

### Step 5 — Junction Discontinuity Stress (Conceptual)

At the cone-cylinder transition, the shell slope changes abruptly. This creates:

- **Shear force** $V = \dfrac{P D \tan\alpha}{2}$ (per unit circumference)
- **Bending moment** $M = V \cdot \sqrt{R_c t}$ (where $R_c$ is the cylinder radius)

These are **secondary stresses** (self-limiting, can relieve by local yielding) but must be considered in the design. ASME compensates with the **0.6P** term.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Flat Plate (Bending Theory)

### Step 1 — Differential Equation of a Circular Plate

Kirchhoff's plate theory gives, for axisymmetric loading on a circular plate:

$$D_p \left( \frac{d^2}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr} \right)\left( \frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dw}{dr} \right) = q$$

where $D_p = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is flexural rigidity and $q$ is uniform pressure.

### Step 2 — Solution for a Clamped Plate

For a uniformly loaded circular plate **clamped at $r = R$**, the deflection is:

$$w(r) = \frac{q}{64 D_p}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)^2$$

Maximum deflection at center ($r = 0$):

$$w_{max} = \frac{q R^4}{64 D_p} = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$

### Step 3 — Bending Stress

The radial bending moment per unit length is:

$$M_r = -D_p \left( \frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{dw}{dr} \right)$$

At the clamped edge ($r = R$), this evaluates to:

$$M_r(R) = -\frac{q R^2}{8} = -\frac{P R^2}{8}$$

The maximum bending stress (at the outer fiber) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 M_r}{t^2} = \frac{6 P R^2}{8 t^2} = \frac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}$$

This matches the formula in §2.1.2. ✓

### Step 4 — Unified ASME Form

Define $C$ such that $\sigma = \frac{3 P R^2}{C_{geom} t^2}$ and $t = d\sqrt{\frac{C \cdot P}{S E}}$. For a clamped plate, comparing gives:

$$C = \frac{3 \cdot (3/4)}{4} = 0.5 \quad \text{(for fixed)}$$

For simply supported with $\nu = 0.3$:

$$C = 0.30 \text{ (rounded)}$$

This is the **origin of the factor C** in the ASME formula — it is a geometric constant encoding the boundary conditions.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Flat (C=0.5)** | **Hemisphere** | **2:1 Ellipsoidal** | **Torispherical** | **Conical (α=30°)** |
|---------------|------------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Stress at P=1 MPa, D=2 m, t=10 mm | **375 MPa** | 50 MPa | 50 MPa | 88.5 MPa | 57.7 MPa |
| Relative thickness (for S=140 MPa) | 200 mm | 7.2 mm | 7.2 mm | 8.3 mm | 8.7 mm |
| Material efficiency | **Worst** | Best | Good | Good | Moderate |
| Manufacturing cost | Lowest | Highest (deep draw) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate (rolled) |
| Used for | Low P, large D | Very high P | General purpose | Standard tanks | Hoppers, transitions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the table:** At the same conditions, a flat end has **7.5× higher stress** than a hemispherical head of the same thickness. This is *why* flat ends are reserved for low-pressure, small-diameter, or removable-cover applications.
