# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Distinguish between flat ends and domed ends in pressure vessel design. Mention two situations where flat ends are preferred over domed ends."**

**Model Answer (8 lines, ~3 marks):**

| Feature | Flat End | Domed End |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Stress type** | Bending (flexural) | Membrane (tensile) |
| **Stress magnitude** | High (∝ R²/t²) | Low (∝ R/t) |
| **Thickness required** | Very large | Moderate |
| **Manufacturing** | Simple (plate) | Complex (pressing/rolling) |
| **Weight** | Heavy | Light |
| **Cost** | Material high, fabrication low | Material low, fabrication high |

**Flat ends are preferred when:**
1. **Frequent opening** is needed (e.g., heat exchanger channel covers, reactor manways) — flat cover with bolts allows easy removal.
2. **Low pressure + large diameter** (e.g., storage tanks) — domed ends become very deep; flat ends avoid headroom issues.
3. **Vacuum service** with stiffening rings.
4. **Cost-sensitive** applications where fabrication simplicity outweighs material cost.

**Marking:** 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for situations, 1 mark for diagram/relevance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"Why is a 2:1 semi-ellipsoidal head preferred over a hemispherical head in industrial practice, even though both have the same ASME thickness formula?"**

**Model Answer:**

Although the **ASME design thickness equation** is identical for both — $t = \frac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ — the 2:1 ellipsoidal is preferred because:

1. **Fabrication depth is halved:** A 2:1 ellipsoidal head has depth = **D/4**, while a hemisphere has depth = **D/2**. A 2000 mm diameter hemisphere would be 1000 mm deep, requiring deep-drawing presses; the 2:1 ellipsoidal is only 500 mm deep.

2. **Lower tooling cost:** Ellipsoidal heads can be **cold-formed or hot-formed** in standard presses; hemispheres often require segmental welding.

3. **Standardization:** The 2:1 ratio is the **ASME standard reference shape** for ellipsoidal heads (UG-32(d)).

4. **Easier transportation** due to lower profile.

The slight stress concentration at the knuckle/crown transition is offset by the manufacturing advantage.

**Marking:** 1 mark for depth comparison, 1 mark for fabrication logic, 1 mark for ASME code reference.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **A pressure vessel of inside diameter 1.5 m is to operate at a design pressure of 2.5 MPa. The allowable stress of the material is 130 MPa, joint efficiency is 0.85, and corrosion allowance is 2 mm. Calculate the required thickness for:**
> **(a) A 2:1 semi-ellipsoidal head (7 marks)**
> **(b) A torispherical (Korbbogen) head with $R_c = D$ and $r = 0.1 D$ (7 marks)**
> **Compare the two and comment on the design choice.**

---

#### **Part (a) — 2:1 Semi-Ellipsoidal Head (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm
- $P = 2.5$ MPa
- $S = 130$ MPa
- $E = 0.85$
- $c = 2$ mm (corrosion allowance)

**Formula (ASME):**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$t = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 130 \times 0.85 - 0.2 \times 2.5}$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$2 \times 130 \times 0.85 = 221 \text{ MPa}$$

$$0.2 \times 2.5 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 221 - 0.5 = 220.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$2.5 \times 1500 = 3750 \text{ MPa·mm}$$

**Calculate t:**

$$t = \frac{3750}{220.5} = 17.0068 \text{ mm}$$

**Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{design} = 17.01 + 2 = 19.01 \text{ mm} \approx 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Round up to nearest standard plate thickness: $t_{design} = 20$ mm.**

**Mark allocation:**
- Formula: 1 mark
- Substitution: 2 marks
- Denominator: 1.5 marks
- Final t: 1.5 marks
- Corrosion: 1 mark

---

#### **Part (b) — Torispherical (Korbbogen) Head (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $R_c = D = 1500$ mm
- $r = 0.1 D = 150$ mm
- Same $P, S, E, c$

**Formula (ASME):**

$$t = \frac{0.885 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{S \cdot E - 0.1 \cdot P}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$t = \frac{0.885 \times 2.5 \times 1500}{130 \times 0.85 - 0.1 \times 2.5}$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$130 \times 0.85 = 110.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$0.1 \times 2.5 = 0.25 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 110.5 - 0.25 = 110.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$0.885 \times 2.5 \times 1500 = 0.885 \times 3750 = 3318.75 \text{ MPa·mm}$$

**Calculate t:**

$$t = \frac{3318.75}{110.25} = 30.103 \text{ mm}$$

**Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{design} = 30.10 + 2 = 32.10 \text{ mm} \approx 32 \text{ mm} \text{ (or 34 mm next standard)}$$

**Mark allocation:**
- Formula: 1 mark
- Substitution: 2 marks
- Denominator: 1.5 marks
- Final t: 1.5 marks
- Corrosion: 1 mark

---

#### **Comparison and Design Comment:**

| Criterion | 2:1 Ellipsoidal | Torispherical |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Required t (mm) | 20 | 32 |
| Material saving | **Saves ~38% material** | Uses more material |
| Manufacturing | Deep draw, more steps | Slightly simpler crown |
| Standard availability | Universal | Universal |
| **Preferred choice** | ✅ **Yes, for moderate-high P** | Used for low-pressure tanks |

**Conclusion:** For the given design pressure of 2.5 MPa, the **2:1 ellipsoidal head is preferred** because it requires substantially less thickness, lower weight, and lower material cost. The torispherical head is economical only for low-pressure or large-diameter storage tanks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]**

> **A cylindrical pressure vessel of inside diameter 2.0 m is closed at one end by a flat circular cover plate. The design pressure is 1.0 MPa and the allowable stress is 140 MPa, joint efficiency = 0.85, corrosion allowance = 1.5 mm. Determine:**
> **(a) The required thickness of the flat head if it is fully clamped (fixed) at the periphery (7 marks)**
> **(b) The required thickness if the same plate is welded with a fillet weld (corner reinforcement), and the percentage saving in material compared to (a) (7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Flat Head, Fully Fixed (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $d = D = 2.0$ m $= 2000$ mm
- $P = 1.0$ MPa
- $S = 140$ MPa
- $E = 0.85$
- $c = 1.5$ mm

**Formula (ASME — fully fixed):**

$$t = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot P}{S \cdot E}} \quad \text{with } C = 0.50$$

**Substitute values:**

$$t = 2000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.50 \times 1.0}{140 \times 0.85}}$$

**Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{0.50 \times 1.0}{140 \times 0.85} = \frac{0.5}{119} = 4.2017 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Take square root:**

$$\sqrt{4.2017 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.06482 \text{ mm}^{-1} \text{ (dimensionless factor, but with units)}$$

**Calculate t:**

$$t = 2000 \times 0.06482 = 129.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{design} = 129.65 + 1.5 = 131.15 \text{ mm} \approx 132 \text{ mm}$$

**Mark allocation:**
- C value identification: 1 mark
- Formula: 1 mark
- Substitution: 1.5 marks
- Square root + result: 2 marks
- Corrosion: 1.5 marks

---

#### **Part (b) — Flat Head with Fillet Weld, Material Saving (7 marks)**

**Formula (ASME — fillet welded):**

$$t = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot P}{S \cdot E}} \quad \text{with } C = 0.25$$

**Substitute values:**

$$t = 2000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.25 \times 1.0}{140 \times 0.85}}$$

**Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{0.25 \times 1.0}{140 \times 0.85} = \frac{0.25}{119} = 2.1008 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Take square root:**

$$\sqrt{2.1008 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04583$$

**Calculate t:**

$$t = 2000 \times 0.04583 = 91.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{design} = 91.66 + 1.5 = 93.16 \text{ mm} \approx 94 \text{ mm}$$

**Material saving calculation:**

Since material volume is proportional to thickness (for the same plate area), the percentage saving is:

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{t_a - t_b}{t_a} \times 100 = \frac{131.15 - 93.16}{131.15} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{37.99}{131.15} \times 100 = 28.97\% \approx 29\%$$

**Conclusion:** A fillet-welded flat head saves **~29% material** compared to a fully fixed (rigidly clamped) head because the corner reinforcement effectively reduces the bending moment lever arm.

**Mark allocation:**
- C value (0.25) identification: 1 mark
- Formula: 1 mark
- Substitution + sqrt: 2 marks
- t value: 1 mark
- % saving calculation: 1.5 marks
- Comment: 0.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant in the denominator (0.2P, 0.1P, 0.6P)** — this is the **#1 mark-loss cause**. Always write the FULL ASME formula on the answer script.
> 2. **Using D where R is required, or vice versa** — Hemispherical uses **R = D/2**; ellipsoidal/torispherical use **D** directly. Mixing these loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** — Always add **c** to the calculated thickness; many students omit this and lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Not rounding UP to nearest standard plate thickness** — A thickness of 17.0 mm must be rounded to 18 or 20 mm (next standard), not 17.
> 5. **Forgetting joint efficiency E in the formula** — Some students use S only. The correct form is $S \cdot E$.
> 6. **For torispherical heads, using the wrong formula** — Some students apply the cylindrical shell formula $t = PR/(SE - 0.6P)$, which is INCORRECT. The torispherical formula has the **0.885 factor** and **0.1P** in the denominator.
> 7. **For cones, forgetting the $\cos \alpha$ in the denominator** — A common error leads to under-thickness.
> 8. **Mixing up D and $D_o$ (outside diameter)** for external pressure calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Memorize These Points**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- ✅ **Flat ends** → bending stress, formula: $t = d\sqrt{CP/SE}$, with C = 0.30, 0.50, 0.25 depending on edge condition.
- ✅ **Domed ends** → membrane (tensile) stress; hemispherical is most efficient, 2:1 ellipsoidal is industrial standard.
- ✅ **Conical ends** → membrane + junction discontinuity; **0.6P** term is critical.
- ✅ **Concave ends** → compressive membrane + buckling risk; use ASME external pressure chart.
- ✅ **Curvature ⇒ stress efficiency.** More curved = less material for the same pressure.

### 🎯 Formula Memory Aids
- ✅ **Hemispherical:** $t = \dfrac{PR}{2SE - 0.2P}$, with $R = D/2$
- ✅ **2:1 Ellipsoidal:** $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ — **same as hemisphere!**
- ✅ **Torispherical:** $t = \dfrac{0.885 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{SE - 0.1P}$ — **has 0.885 factor**
- ✅ **Conical:** $t = \dfrac{PD}{2\cos\alpha(SE - 0.6P)}$ — **has cosα and 0.6P**
- ✅ **Flat (ASME):** $t = d\sqrt{\dfrac{CP}{SE}}$, C = 0.30, 0.50, 0.25

### 🎯 Code Constants — The "Magic Numbers"
- ✅ **0.2P** — denominator correction for cylindrical and hemispherical shells.
- ✅ **0.1P** — denominator correction for torispherical heads (lower because SCF is already in 0.885).
- ✅ **0.6P** — denominator correction for conical shells (high, due to junction discontinuity).
- ✅ **0.885** — SCF correction for torispherical crown (= 1/1.13).
- ✅ **E = 0.85 or 0.7** — typical joint efficiencies for spot- or partially inspected welds.
- ✅ **1.3 × P** — hydrostatic test pressure multiplier.

### 🎯 Design Hierarchy
- ✅ **Stress efficiency order** (best → worst): Hemisphere > 2:1 Ellipsoid > Torispherical > Conical (small α) > Conical (large α) > Flat (fillet) > Flat (fixed) > Flat (loose).
- ✅ **Cost efficiency order** (cheapest → most expensive fabrication): Flat → Torispherical → Conical → 2:1 Ellipsoid → Hemisphere.

### 🎯 Failure Modes
- ✅ **Domed/Convex:** Failure by **yielding/rupture** at high stress location.
- ✅ **Flat:** Failure by **bending cracking** at the clamped edge.
- ✅ **Concave:** Failure by **elastic buckling/instability**.
- ✅ **Conical:** Failure by **junction fatigue** if not reinforced.

### 🎯 Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Using $P$ without the **0.2P correction** in the denominator.
- ❌ Forgetting to add **corrosion allowance**.
- ❌ Confusing $D$ vs $R$ in formulas.
- ❌ Forgetting **joint efficiency E** in the denominator.
- ❌ Not rounding up to the nearest **standard plate thickness**.
- ❌ Mixing flat-head C-values (0.30 vs 0.50 vs 0.25).
- ❌ Using the cylindrical shell formula for a torispherical head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎓 Final KTU Examination Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always write down the formula first** (1 mark just for that), then list the given data with units, then substitute and compute. Examiners reward method as much as the final answer. A correctly-formulated answer with a minor arithmetic error often gets 80% marks; a perfectly-numbered answer with the wrong formula gets 0.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestions on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Suggestions on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBCHT504 – Chemical Engineering Design-I** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, *Suggestions on Project Topics* refers to the curated set of guidelines, scope definitions, and thematic boundaries within which a B.Tech Chemical Engineering student must identify, scope, and execute a **capstone chemical process / equipment design project**. According to KTU stipulations, the project must demonstrate the integration of core competencies: **mass and energy balances, thermodynamics, transport phenomena, reaction engineering, mechanical design of process equipment, process safety, instrumentation, and economic evaluation (capital + operating cost estimation).**

The official KTU terminology treats this as a **"Design Project Problem (DPP)"** — an open-ended, semi-constrained industrial problem statement that the student must transform into a complete engineered deliverable comprising: a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**, a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**, **equipment datasheets**, **material & energy balances**, **unit sizing calculations**, **cost estimates**, and **safety/environmental compliance statements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The project topic is **not** a research thesis. It is a *design synthesis* exercise — the student must arrive at a feasible, safe, and economically viable engineered solution using established design heuristics, codes (ASME, TEMA, IS, IS 2825, IS 4503), and standard chemical engineering software (ASPEN PLUS / HYSYS / DWSIM).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect of a Chemical Plant" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Readers**

Imagine you are an **architect**, but instead of designing a building with bricks and concrete, you are designing a **"factory"** that converts raw materials (like crude oil, sugarcane, or natural gas) into useful products (like petrol, sugar, or ammonia).

A **suggestion on project topics** is essentially the **list of "buildable plots"** the university gives you — plots that are realistic in size, lie within your engineering budget, follow local building codes (safety/environmental regulations), and can be completed within the **single semester timeline**.

Just as an architect's plot must have:
- A clear **boundary** (inlet raw materials → outlet products),
- A clear **blueprint** (process flow diagram),
- Defined **rooms and dimensions** (equipment sizing),
- Defined **plumbing and wiring** (piping & instrumentation),
- A defined **budget and profit margin** (cost estimation),

… a chemical engineering design project must fulfill the exact same structural requirements. The "Suggestions on Project Topics" module tells you which "plots" (i.e., real industrial problems) are large enough to be meaningful, yet small enough to be solved by a single student in one semester.

---

## 1.3 Standard Scope Boundaries per KTU Guidelines

The **2024 Scheme syllabus** mandates that the design project should fall under one (or a logical combination) of the following categories:

| S.No | Design Domain | Typical Sub-Topic Examples |
|:----:|:--------------|:----------------------------|
| 1 | **Process Design** | Acetic acid from methanol (carbonylation), Biodiesel from waste cooking oil |
| 2 | **Equipment Design** | Shell & tube heat exchanger, Sieve-tray distillation column |
| 3 | **Plant Utility Design** | Cooling tower, Boiler, DM water plant, Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) |
| 4 | **Reactor Design** | PFR for phthalic anhydride, CSTR for soap saponification |
| 5 | **Separation System Design** | Multi-component distillation, Absorption column, Extractor |
| 6 | **Storage & Handling** | Atmospheric storage tank, Horton sphere, Fire protection system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> - **PFD (Process Flow Diagram):** Shows major equipment, stream connections, and mass/energy balance data.
> - **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram):** Shows piping, valves, instruments, control loops, and safety interlocks.
> - **Equipment Datasheet:** A standardized engineering document specifying design pressure, design temperature, MOC, capacity, and mechanical details.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Logic Stack" Behind Selecting a Project Topic

The selection of a chemical engineering design project under KTU is governed by a **five-tier logic filter** that every student must apply:

### **Tier 1 — Industrial Relevance Filter**
- The problem must originate from a **real industrial process** (chemical, petrochemical, fertilizer, food, pharma, or wastewater sector).
- **Why?** KTU explicitly seeks design solutions that demonstrate **employability and industry readiness**, satisfying the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** mandate of the NEP 2020 framework.

### **Tier 2 — Data Availability Filter**
- The student **must be able to obtain** reliable thermodynamic, kinetic, and physical property data from literature, handbooks (Perry's, McCabe–Smith–Harriott, Coulson & Richardson's), or open databases (NIST WebBook, DIPPR).
- **Why?** A design project without property data is unworkable; this filter forces feasibility.

### **Tier 3 — Scope Realization Filter (the "One-Semester Test")**
- The problem must be **bounded** such that a single student can:
  - Define the feed/product streams,
  - Develop mass & energy balances,
  - Size at least **one major equipment** in mechanical detail,
  - Produce a preliminary cost estimate (CAPEX + OPEX).
- **Why?** KTU's assessment rubric rewards depth in **one unit operation** rather than shallow breadth across many.

### **Tier 4 — Safety, Health & Environment (SHE) Compliance Filter**
- The chosen process must permit a **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** study, identification of MSDS-based hazards, and a documented **environmental impact statement** (effluent treatment plan, emissions).
- **Why?** This satisfies the **CO5: Safety & Sustainability** outcome in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### **Tier 5 — Economic Viability Filter**
- The student must be able to compute at least a **preliminary pay-back period** or **Return on Investment (ROI)** using:
  - **Lang Factor method** (CAPEX estimation),
  - **Six-Tenths Rule** (scaling for capacity),
  - **Purchase cost → Installed cost → Total capital investment** chain.
- **Why?** Engineering design without economics is incomplete — satisfies **CO4: Economic Analysis** in the syllabus.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations for Project Work)

> [!NOTE]
> These equations are repeatedly tested in KTU ESE and in viva-voce of the design project. Master them.

| Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|:--------------|:--------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| **Six-Tenths Rule (Cost Scaling)** | $C_{B} = C_{A} \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}}\right)^{0.6}$ | $C_{A}$ = known cost, $C_{B}$ = new cost, $Q$ = capacity | Scaling equipment cost for different capacities |
| **Lang Factor (CAPEX from Purchased Equipment Cost)** | $C_{TM} = F_{L} \cdot \sum C_{P}$ | $F_{L}$ = Lang factor (≈ **4.28** for solid, **3.10** for fluid), $C_{P}$ = purchased equipment cost | Quick capital cost estimation |
| **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** | $TCI = FCI + WCI$ | FCI = Fixed Capital Investment, WCI = Working Capital Investment | Cost sheet assembly |
| **Cost Index Inflation Adjustment** | $C_{2} = C_{1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\right)$ | $I_{1}, I_{2}$ = cost indices (CEPCI, Marshall & Swift) | Time-value adjustment of old cost data |
| **Pay-Back Period** | $PBP = \dfrac{FCI}{\text{Annual Net Profit}}$ | Years | Economic viability check |
| **Annualized Cost (Capital Recovery)** | $CR = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $P$ = present capital, $i$ = interest rate, $n$ = project life (yrs) | Equivalent annual cost comparison |
| **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (OHTC)** | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_{i}} + \dfrac{x_{w}}{k_{w}} + \dfrac{1}{h_{o}}$ | W/m²·K | Heat exchanger sizing |
| **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)** | $H_{column} = HETP \cdot N_{t}$ | $N_{t}$ = number of theoretical stages | Distillation/absorption column height |
| **Reaction Rate (Power Law)** | $-r_{A} = k \cdot C_{A}^{n}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $n$ = order | Reactor volume calculation |
| **Space Time / Space Velocity** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_{0}}, \quad SV = \dfrac{v_{0}}{V}$ | $V$ = reactor volume, $v_{0}$ = volumetric feed rate | CSTR/PFR design |
| **Sauter Mean Diameter (Spray/Spray dryer)** | $d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_{i} d_{i}^{3}}{\sum n_{i} d_{i}^{2}}$ | m | Droplet size distribution |
| **Manning Equation (Open channel flow)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | $n$ = roughness, $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = slope | ETP / drainage design |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulae collectively empower the student to:
1. **Justify equipment selection** (e.g., why a shell-and-tube HX over a plate HX),
2. **Communicate with vendors** (using standardized datasheet language),
3. **Defend the design in KTU viva** with quantitative reasoning,
4. **Build a portfolio** suitable for **placement interviews** in EPC firms (L&T, Tata Projects, Uhde India), **PSUs** (BPCL, IOCL, ONGC), and **process industries** (Reliance, SRF, UPL).

---

## 2.4 The "Topic Classification Heuristic" — Picking Your Project

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree for Topic Selection:**
> 1. *Do you have access to plant data?* → **YES** → Choose a plant-specific problem (e.g., debottlenecking a column).
> 2. *Do you prefer a greenfield design?* → **YES** → Choose a textbook-defined process (e.g., formaldehyde from methanol, ethanol from molasses).
> 3. *Do you want low-data-load?* → **YES** → Choose an **equipment-centric** project (single-unit deep dive).
> 4. *Do you want a sustainability angle?* → **YES** → Choose an ETP, CO₂ capture, or waste-to-energy topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Six-Tenths Rule — Complete Derivation

The Six-Tenths Rule is one of the most heavily tested **cost-scaling concepts** in KTU examinations. The derivation shown below connects the empirical exponent **0.6** to the **economies of scale** principle.

### **Starting Premise**

A piece of equipment whose capacity is scaled by factor $S = Q_{B}/Q_{A}$ will have its cost scaled by a power of $S$ raised to an empirical exponent $x$. We seek the value of $x$.

$$C_{B} = C_{A} \cdot S^{x}$$

### **Step 1 — Define "Capacity" Geometrically**

For most chemical equipment (vessels, tanks, towers), capacity is **volumetric** and scales with the cube of a characteristic linear dimension $L$:

$$Q \propto L^{3}$$

Therefore, the geometric scaling of length is:

$$L_{B} = L_{A} \cdot S^{1/3}$$

### **Step 2 — Cost Scales with Surface Area (Material-Based Equipment)**

For a **pressure vessel, shell-and-tube HX, or storage tank**, the cost is dominated by the **material of construction (MOC)** (steel, stainless steel, alloy), which is proportional to surface area, $\propto L^{2}$:

$$\text{Cost} \propto L^{2}$$

### **Step 3 — Combine Steps 1 and 2**

Substituting $L_{B}$ into the cost-scaling expression:

$$\frac{C_{B}}{C_{A}} = \left(\frac{L_{B}}{L_{A}}\right)^{2} = \left(S^{1/3}\right)^{2} = S^{2/3}$$

### **Step 4 — Introduce the Empirical Adjustment**

Empirically, real equipment costs scale somewhere between **linear ($S^{1.0}$)** and **surface-area ($S^{2/3} \approx 0.667$)** behavior because of added complexity (instrumentation, internals, support structures). The empirically observed average exponent across chemical equipment is **0.6**.

$$\boxed{\,C_{B} = C_{A} \cdot \left(\frac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}}\right)^{0.6}\,}$$

### **Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example**

**Problem:** A 1000 m³ ammonia storage tank costs ₹50 lakhs (installed). Estimate the cost of a 5000 m³ tank using the Six-Tenths Rule.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $C_{A}$ = ₹50,00,000,
- $Q_{A}$ = 1000 m³,
- $Q_{B}$ = 5000 m³.

Compute the capacity ratio $S$:

$$S = \frac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}} = \frac{5000}{1000} = 5.0$$

Apply the Six-Tenths Rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{B} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \cdot (5.0)^{0.6} \\
      &= 50{,}00{,}000 \cdot 2.6265 \\
      &\approx 1{,}31{,}32{,}500 \\
      &\approx \text{₹ 1.31 Crore}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A 5× capacity increase does NOT give a 5× cost increase — only ~2.6×. This **economy of scale** is precisely why the Six-Tenths exponent is **less than 1**.

---

## 3.2 The Lang Factor — Complete Cost Sheet Derivation

### **Step 1 — Total Module Cost Definition**

$$C_{TM} = F_{L} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{P,i}$$

where $F_{L}$ is the **Lang factor** (constant for a given process type) and $C_{P,i}$ is the **purchased cost of the i-th major equipment**.

### **Step 2 — Typical $F_{L}$ Values (Memorize These)**

| Process Type | Lang Factor $F_{L}$ |
|:-------------|:--------------------|
| Solid processing | **4.28** |
| Solid–Fluid processing | **4.77** |
| Fluid processing | **3.10** |

### **Step 3 — Conversion to Fixed Capital Investment**

$$FCI = 1.12 \cdot C_{TM}$$

The factor **1.12** accounts for **contractor's fee, contingency, and auxiliary facilities**.

### **Step 4 — Worked Example**

**Problem:** A fluid-processing plant has 4 major equipment items with purchased costs ₹20 L, ₹35 L, ₹15 L, ₹50 L. Compute the FCI.

**Solution:**

Step (a): Sum of purchased costs:

$$\sum C_{P} = 20 + 35 + 15 + 50 = \text{₹ 120 Lakhs}$$

Step (b): Apply Lang Factor for fluid processing ($F_{L} = 3.10$):

$$C_{TM} = 3.10 \times 120 = \text{₹ 372 Lakhs}$$

Step (c): Convert to FCI:

$$FCI = 1.12 \times 372 = \text{₹ 416.64 Lakhs}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Project Topic Classification

| Project Theme | Data Intensity | Software Skill | Industry Domain | KTU CO Mapped | Recommended Scale |
|:--------------|:---------------|:---------------|:----------------|:--------------|:------------------|
| **Methanol to Formaldehyde** | Medium | Aspen Plus | Bulk chemicals | CO1, CO2, CO4 | Process + Reactor |
| **Biodiesel from Waste Oil** | Medium | Aspen / Excel | Renewable | CO1, CO4, CO5 | Process + HX |
| **Distillation Column Design** | Low | Excel / Mathcad | Petroleum | CO2, CO3 | Single equipment |
| **ETP for Distillery Waste** | High | ASPEN / manual | Environmental | CO5, CO4 | Multi-unit |
| **Shell-and-Tube HX Design** | Low | HTRI / Excel | Thermal | CO2, CO3 | Single equipment |
| **CSTR for Saponification** | Low | Polymath | Specialty | CO1, CO2 | Reactor + kinetics |
| **Phenol from Cumene** | High | Aspen Plus | Petrochemical | CO1, CO2, CO4 | Full plant |
| **Atmospheric Storage Tank** | Medium | PV Elite / manual | Storage | CO3, CO5 | Mechanical design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mapping:** Every project must satisfy **CO1 (Process Knowledge)**, **CO2 (Design Skills)**, **CO3 (Modern Tool Usage)**, **CO4 (Engineering Economics)**, and **CO5 (Environment & Sustainability)**. Choose a topic that hits **all five**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Project Topic Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Interest Area] --> B[Select Industrial Domain]
    B --> C{Data Available?}
    C -- YES --> D[Literature / Plant Survey]
    C -- NO --> E[Use Standard Textbook Process]
    D --> F[Define Feed and Product Specs]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Perform Mass and Energy Balance]
    G --> H[Select Major Equipment]
    H --> I{Multiple Units?}
    I -- YES --> J[Develop PFD then P and ID]
    I -- NO --> K[Single Equipment Deep Dive]
    J --> L[Mechanical Design per IS or ASME]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Cost Estimation via Lang Factor]
    M --> N[Safety and HAZOP Study]
    N --> O[Sustainability and ETP Plan]
    O --> P[Compile Design Report]
    P --> Q[KTU Submission Ready]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Design Project Deliverable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Raw Material Specs]
        A2[Product Specs]
        A3[Site and Utility Data]
    end
    subgraph ProcessLayer
        B1[Mass and Energy Balance]
        B2[PFD Generation]
        B3[P and ID Generation]
    end
    subgraph DesignLayer
        C1[Reactor Sizing]

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six broad categories of chemical engineering design projects suitable for a B.Tech capstone.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Process Plant Design** — e.g., design of an acetic acid or methanol plant.
2. **Equipment Design** — e.g., detailed mechanical design of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger.
3. **Reactor Design** — e.g., design of a PFR for phthalic anhydride production.
4. **Separation System Design** — e.g., multi-component distillation column design.
5. **Plant Utility Design** — e.g., cooling tower, boiler, DM water plant.
6. **Environmental/Safety Design** — e.g., ETP for distillery spent wash, HAZOP study of an LPG bullet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if at least 6 distinct categories are listed with one example each.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Lang Factor values for solid, solid–fluid, and fluid processes. Why are they different?**

**Model Answer:**

| Process Type | Lang Factor |
|:--|:--|
| Solid processing | **4.28** |
| Solid–fluid processing | **4.77** |
| Fluid processing | **3.10** |

The factors differ because **fluid-handling plants** require more expensive equipment (pumps, compressors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, instrumentation) which inflates the *purchased-equipment cost* relative to the *installed cost*, lowering the Lang factor. Conversely, **solid-handling plants** have cheaper equipment (conveyors, silos, crushers) but expensive civil/structural and dust-collection work, raising the Lang factor.

---

## ✏️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with justification the **five-tier filter** that should be applied while selecting a chemical engineering design project topic under KTU 2024 Scheme.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A distillation column for a benzene–toluene separation has a purchased cost of ₹25 lakhs. Estimate the cost of a similar column with **2.5 times** the capacity using (i) Six-Tenths Rule, and (ii) a cost-index adjusted version if the index rose from 450 to 540 over 4 years.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **(a) Five-Tier Filter Explanation [7 Marks]**

1. **Industrial Relevance Filter [1.5 marks]** — Topic must originate from a real industrial process; ensures alignment with NEP 2020 OBE mandate of industry-readiness.

2. **Data Availability Filter [1.5 marks]** — Reliable thermodynamic, kinetic, and physical property data must be accessible (Perry's, DIPPR, NIST); without it the design cannot proceed.

3. **Scope Realization Filter [1.5 marks]** — Topic must be **bounded** such that one student in one semester can deliver: mass/energy balance, sizing of at least one major equipment, cost estimate, and PFD/P&ID.

4. **SHE Compliance Filter [1.5 marks]** — Project must permit a HAZOP study and an environmental compliance statement, satisfying **CO5** of KTU 2024 syllabus.

5. **Economic Viability Filter [1 mark]** — At minimum a pay-back period or ROI must be computable using Lang Factor + Six-Tenths Rule; satisfies **CO4**.

#### **(b) Cost Estimation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $C_{A}$ = ₹25,00,000
- $Q_{B}/Q_{A}$ = 2.5
- $I_{1}$ = 450, $I_{2}$ = 540

**Part (i) — Six-Tenths Rule [3.5 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{B} &= C_{A} \cdot \left(\frac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}}\right)^{0.6} \\
      &= 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot (2.5)^{0.6} \\
      &= 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.7317 \\
      &\approx \text{₹ 43,29,250}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Cost-Index Adjusted Version [3.5 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{B,\text{adjusted}} &= C_{B} \cdot \left(\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\right) \\
                      &= 43{,}29{,}250 \cdot \left(\frac{540}{450}\right) \\
                      &= 43{,}29{,}250 \cdot 1.20 \\
                      &\approx \text{₹ 51,95,100}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **forget to apply the cost-index adjustment** after the Six-Tenths Rule, or they **incorrectly multiply the exponent**. Marks are commonly lost on the sequence: **(a) capacity-scale first, then (b) index-scale the result.** Do NOT reverse the order unless explicitly told the index-adjusted *base* cost is given.

---

### 📌 **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate Set]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **scope and deliverables** expected from a KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Chemical Engineering Design Project, with reference to the **OBE course outcomes (CO1–CO5)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fluid-processing plant has purchased equipment costs totalling ₹80 lakhs. Using the Lang Factor method, compute the **Total Module Cost (C_TM)** and the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**. Comment on whether such an estimate is sufficient for **board-level sanction**.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **(a) Scope and Deliverables [7 Marks]**

The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates the following deliverables, mapped to course outcomes:

| S.No | Deliverable | Mapped CO |
|:----:|:------------|:---------:|
| 1 | Problem definition and literature survey | **CO1** |
| 2 | Mass and Energy Balance with block diagram | **CO1, CO2** |
| 3 | Process Flow Diagram (PFD) | **CO2** |
| 4 | Detailed P&ID of critical unit | **CO2** |
| 5 | Mechanical design of at least 1 major equipment (per IS/ASME) | **CO2, CO3** |
| 6 | Cost estimation (CAPEX + OPEX) using Lang/Six-Tenths | **CO4** |
| 7 | HAZOP, MSDS, ETP, environmental compliance note | **CO5** |
| 8 | Final consolidated design report + viva | **CO1–CO5** |

#### **(b) Lang Factor Computation [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $\sum C_{P} = $ ₹80,00,000; process type = **fluid processing** → $F_{L} = 3.10$

**Step 1 — Total Module Cost [3 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= F_{L} \cdot \sum C_{P} \\
       &= 3.10 \times 80{,}00{,}000 \\
       &= \text{₹ 2,48,00,000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Fixed Capital Investment [3 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
FCI &= 1.12 \cdot C_{TM} \\
    &= 1.12 \times 2{,}48{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= \text{₹ 2,77,76,000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on Sufficiency for Board Sanction [1 mark]:**

This is a **preliminary/budget estimate** (±30–50% accuracy). For actual board sanction, the project requires a **detailed engineering estimate (±10%)** with itemized line-items, vendor quotations, civil/structural drawings, electrical single-line diagrams, and a **definitive HAZOP and risk assessment**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):** A common error is using the **solid-processing Lang factor (4.28)** for what is clearly stated as a fluid plant. Another frequent mistake is forgetting the **1.12 multiplier** when converting $C_{TM}$ to $FCI$. Always re-read the process type and the conversion chain.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can tick all these boxes:**

- [x] A KTU design project is a **Design Project Problem (DPP)**, not a research thesis.
- [x] Five mandatory filters: **Industrial Relevance, Data Availability, Scope Realization, SHE Compliance, Economic Viability.**
- [x] Every project must hit **all five Course Outcomes (CO1–CO5)** under OBE.
- [x] Standard deliverables: **PFD, P&ID, datasheet, M&E balance, sizing, cost sheet, HAZOP, ETP.**
- [x] **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_{B} = C_{A} \cdot (Q_{B}/Q_{A})^{0.6}$ — apply **capacity scale first**, then **cost-index adjustment**.
- [x] **Lang Factors to memorize:** Solid = **4.28**, Solid–Fluid = **4.77**, Fluid = **3.10**.
- [x] $FCI = 1.12 \times C_{TM}$ — do not forget the **1.12 contractor/contingency factor**.
- [x] Pay-back period = $FCI / \text{Annual Net Profit}$ — quick economic screening tool.
- [x] For PFR: $V = v_{0} \cdot \int_{X_{in}}^{X_{out}} \dfrac{dX}{-r_{A}}$; for CSTR: $V = v_{0} \cdot \dfrac{X_{out} - X_{in}}{-r_{A,\text{out}}}$.
- [x] Heat exchanger equation: $1/U = 1/h_{i} + x_{w}/k_{w} + 1/h_{o}$.
- [x] Column height: $H = HETP \times N_{t}$ or $H = (\text{NTP}/\text{Efficiency}) \times \text{tray spacing}$.
- [x] Always cite **codes** (IS 2825 for unfired pressure vessels, TEMA for HX, ASME Sec VIII).
- [x] Always include a **safety valve sizing calculation** for any pressure-containing equipment.
- [x] Viva-voce trick: examiners often ask *"Why did you choose this process?"* — answer via the **five-tier filter** for instant marks.

---

### 🎯 Final One-Liner to Remember

> *"A KTU design project is not about **inventing** chemistry — it is about **engineering** an existing chemistry into a **safe, sized, and costed** industrial reality."*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identify a Tubular Exchanger in a Plant and Design the Exchanger: Selection of Exchanger Type, Material of Construction, Physical Properties, Manual Sizing (First Design), Computational Optimization, and Design Data Sheet Preparation as per Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Tubular Heat Exchanger?

A **Tubular Heat Exchanger** is a class of indirect-contact heat transfer equipment in which one or more fluids are confined inside cylindrical tubes, and heat is transferred through the tube walls between the tube-side fluid and a shell-side fluid (or between multiple tube-side streams). The most common industrial realization is the **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)**, which serves as the workhorse of the chemical, petrochemical, refinery, power, and process industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim academic standard):**
> A tubular heat exchanger is a pressure vessel comprising a bundle of tubes enclosed within a cylindrical shell, designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more process fluids separated by a solid metallic (or non-metallic) tube wall, with the flow arrangement, baffle geometry, and nozzle configuration standardized by the **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Water Heater"

Imagine a large apartment building in Kerala during the monsoon. The building has a central **hot water boiler (shell)** through which **cold water from each flat (tubes)** is circulated. Each flat's water gets heated as it passes through individual pipes immersed in the common hot zone. The flats (tube-side streams) never directly mix with the central hot water (shell-side stream) — they are separated by the pipe wall.

Similarly, in a shell-and-tube exchanger:
- The **shell** acts like the central hot zone.
- The **tubes** act like the individual apartment pipes.
- The **tube wall** acts like the pipe material that conducts heat.
- The **baffles** inside the shell act like fins that redirect the hot water to flow across (perpendicular to) the pipes for maximum contact.

This analogy makes it intuitive why we have **tube-side** and **shell-side** fluids and why baffles are essential for efficient heat transfer.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in Design

The following universally accepted standards govern tubular exchanger design:

- **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** — defines mechanical standards, Nomenclature, and selection classes (R, C, B).
- **ASME Section VIII Div. 1** — governs pressure vessel design, fabrication, and testing.
- **ASME B31.3** — governs process piping and nozzle design.
- **API 660** — for refinery and petrochemical service (high-pressure applications).
- **IS 4503 / IS 2825** — Indian Standards for unfired pressure vessels.
- **HEI (Heat Exchange Institute)** — for condensation and reboiler standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic so important in KTU 2024?**
> Module 2 bridges Module 1 (heat transfer fundamentals) with the design data sheet preparation required in plant practice. A student must demonstrate ability to: (a) identify the exchanger in a real plant, (b) classify it as per TEMA, (c) select materials, (d) estimate physical properties, (e) perform a hand-calculation sizing, and (f) iterate computationally until the design converges.

---

### 1.4 Identifying a Tubular Exchanger in a Plant

When a chemical engineer walks through a process plant, the following visual cues identify a tubular heat exchanger:

| Visual Cue | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Large horizontal/vertical cylindrical vessel with two channel heads | Shell-and-tube STHE |
| Inlet and outlet nozzles on shell, plus two on channel head | Two-fluid exchanger (one shell-pass, one tube-pass) |
| Multiple smaller nozzles on shell sides | Two-shell-pass or multi-pass design |
| Bonnet-type removable channel cover | Floating head or U-tube design |
| Inverted U-bundle visible on pull-out | U-tube heat exchanger |
| Tall vertical vessel with vapour inlet at top | Kettle reboiler (TEMA K) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Plant Identification Trick:**
> - Horizontal cylindrical + bolted channel head + rectangular baffle cuts ⇒ **1-2 STHE (Fixed Tubesheet)**.
> - Same shape but with a floating head at the rear ⇒ **AES or AET (Floating Head)**.
> - Inverted-U tubes visible at the rear ⇒ **BEM or BEU (U-Tube)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 TEMA Classification of Shell-and-Tube Exchangers

The **TEMA designation** uses a three-letter code:

**Format:** `(Front End)(Shell)(Rear End)`

### 2.1.1 Front-End Head Types

| TEMA Letter | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Channel integral with tubesheet (bonnet) | Clean fluids, moderate pressure |
| **B** | Bonnet (removable cover) integral with shell | Allows bundle removal from shell side |
| **C** | Channel integral with tubesheet and removable cover | Used with fixed tubesheets |

### 2.1.2 Shell Types

| TEMA Letter | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **E** | One-pass shell | Simplest, used for low-pressure drop |
| **F** | Two-pass shell with longitudinal baffle | Higher heat transfer |
| **G** | Split-flow | Lower pressure drop |
| **H** | Double split-flow | Used in reboilers |
| **J** | Divided-flow | High heat recovery |
| **K** | Kettle reboiler | Vapour disengagement |
| **X** | Cross-flow | Condensers |

### 2.1.3 Rear-End Head Types

| TEMA Letter | Description |
|---|---|
| **L** | Fixed tubesheet (like front head A) |
| **M** | Fixed tubesheet (like front head B) |
| **N** | Fixed tubesheet (like front head C) |
| **P** | Outside-packed floating head |
| **S** | Floating head with backing device |
| **T** | Pull-through floating head |
| **U** | U-tube bundle |
| **W** | Packed channel with floating tubesheet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most Asked Configuration:** **AES, AEL, BEM, BEU, AET** — these account for >80% of industrial installations.

## 2.2 Selection of Exchanger Type — Decision Logic

The selection of exchanger type follows this **engineering decision tree**:

```
Step 1: Is thermal expansion difference between fluids significant?
   ├─ YES → Floating head (AES/AET) or U-tube (BEU)
   └─ NO  → Fixed tubesheet (AEL/CEL) — cheaper

Step 2: Is the shell-side fluid fouling or dirty?
   ├─ YES → U-tube (cleanable from inside only)
   └─ NO  → Floating head preferred

Step 3: What is the operating pressure?
   ├─ < 30 bar → Fixed tubesheet
   └─ > 30 bar → Floating head or U-tube

Step 4: Is phase change involved?
   ├─ Condensation → E-shell with horizontal layout
   └─ Vaporisation → Kettle (K) or vertical thermosiphon

Step 5: Number of tube passes for duty?
   ├─ ΔT correction factor FT < 0.75 → Consider 2-shell-pass
   └─ FT ≥ 0.75 → Single shell-pass acceptable
```

## 2.3 Material of Construction (MOC) Selection

The MOC must resist **corrosion, erosion, thermal stress, and pressure**.

### 2.3.1 Selection Matrix

| Service Fluid / Condition | Recommended MOC |
|---|---|
| Steam, water, air | **Carbon Steel (CS, SA-516 Gr.70)** |
| Mild corrosive organics | **SS 304 / SS 304L** |
| Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄) | **SS 316 / SS 316L / Hastelloy C-276** |
| Caustic (NaOH) | **SS 304L / Nickel** |
| Refrigerants (NH₃, propane) | **Carbon Steel** (but high-purity) |
| Cryogenic (LNG, N₂) | **Aluminium / SS 304L** |
| Sea water, brine | **Titanium / Cupronickel (90/10, 70/30)** |
| High-temp (T > 500 °C) | **SS 321, SS 347, Inconel 600** |
| Sulphur-containing crude | **SS 321 (Ti-stabilized)** |
| Polymerization service | **Glass-lined / Teflon-lined** |

### 2.3.2 Key MOC Properties

| MOC | Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | Max Service T (°C) | Cost Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Steel | **54** | 425 | 1.0 |
| SS 304 | **16.3** | 870 | 2.5 |
| SS 316 | **16.3** | 870 | 3.2 |
| Cupronickel 70/30 | **29** | 425 | 4.0 |
| Titanium | **17** | 600 | 12.0 |
| Hastelloy C | **10** | 1100 | 25.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb:** Place the **more corrosive, dirtier, or higher-pressure fluid on the tube side**. This makes tube replacement cheaper and protects the more expensive shell.

## 2.4 Physical Properties Required for Design

Before any sizing calculation, the following physical properties must be compiled at **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{bulk,in} + T_{bulk,out})/2$ for each stream:

| Property | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | $\mu$ | Pa·s |
| Specific heat | $C_p$ | J/(kg·K) |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | W/(m·K) |
| Fouling factor | $R_f$ | m²·K/W |
| Latent heat (if phase change) | $\lambda$ | J/kg |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | N/m |
| Vapour pressure | $P_v$ | Pa |
| Boiling/condensation point | $T_b / T_c$ | K or °C |
| Corrosivity index | — | — |

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula sheet** for KTU 2024 board exams. Memorize all entries with units.

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty | $Q = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | W | Sensible heat transfer |
| 2 | Heat duty (latent) | $Q = \dot{m} \, \lambda$ | W | Phase change |
| 3 | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K | Counter/cocurrent flow |
| 4 | Heat transfer rate (overall) | $Q = U \, A \, F_T \, \text{LMTD}$ | W | After correction factor |
| 5 | Required area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \, F_T \, \text{LMTD}}$ | m² | Core sizing eq. |
| 6 | Clean overall coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | m²·K/W | Conduction + convection |
| 7 | Dirty (design) coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | m²·K/W | With fouling |
| 8 | Tube-side hi (Dittus-Boelter) | $h_i = 0.023 \, Re_i^{0.8} \, Pr_i^{0.4} \, \dfrac{k_i}{D_i}$ | W/(m²·K) | Turbulent flow, heating |
| 9 | Tube-side hi (cooling) | $h_i = 0.023 \, Re_i^{0.8} \, Pr_i^{0.3} \, \dfrac{k_i}{D_i}$ | W/(m²·K) | Turbulent flow, cooling |
| 10 | Shell-side ho (Kern) | $h_o = 0.36 \, Re_s^{0.55} \, Pr_s^{0.33} \, \dfrac{k_s}{D_e}$ | W/(m²·K) | Kern method |
| 11 | Equivalent shell diameter | $D_e = 4 \left(\dfrac{P_t^2 - \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_o^2}{\pi d_o}\right)$ | m | Kern method |
| 12 | Reynolds number (tube) | $Re_i = \dfrac{G_i \, D_i}{\mu_i} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D_i \mu_i}$ | — | Tube-side |
| 13 | Reynolds number (shell) | $Re_s = \dfrac{D_e \, G_s}{\mu_s}$ | — | Shell-side |
| 14 | Prandtl number | $Pr = \dfrac{C_p \, \mu}{k}$ | — | All fluids |
| 15 | Tube-side pressure drop | $\Delta P_t = 4f \dfrac{L N_p}{D_i}\dfrac{G_i^2}{2\rho_i}\left(\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{-0.14}$ | Pa | Kern |
| 16 | Shell-side pressure drop | $\Delta P_s = f \dfrac{G_s^2 \, D_s \, (N+1)}{2\rho_s \, D_e}$ | Pa | Kern |
| 17 | Friction factor (turbulent) | $f = 0.0791 \, Re^{-0.25}$ (smooth) | — | Blasius |
| 18 | Tubes per shell | $N_t = 0.785 \dfrac{D_b^2}{P_t^2} \cdot C_{TP}$ | — | Bundle layout |
| 19 | Bundle diameter | $D_b = d_o \left(\dfrac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1}$ | m | Empirical |
| 20 | MTD correction factor | $F_T = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1})}{2-P(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1})}\right]}$ | — | Multi-pass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $R = (T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1)$, $P = (t_2 - t_1)/(T_1 - t_1)$, $T$ = hot stream temperature, $t$ = cold stream temperature, $F_T$ = correction factor from TEMA charts.

## 2.6 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

Tubular exchangers account for **~70% of all heat transfer equipment** in oil refineries and chemical plants because:
1. They handle **high pressures and temperatures**.
2. They accommodate **large surface areas** (up to 5000 m² per unit).
3. They permit **mechanical cleaning** of both sides.
4. They are **manufacturable from a wide range of alloys**.
5. Their performance is **predictable** using empirical correlations (Kern, Bell-Delaware).

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires you to demonstrate competence in **manual sizing first** (which builds engineering intuition) and then **computational optimization** (which reflects industrial practice using HTRI, Aspen EDR, or COMSOL).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a differential element of length $\mathrm{d}x$ along the exchanger. Let the hot fluid (tube-side) enter at temperature $T_1$ and leave at $T_2$. The cold fluid (shell-side) enters at $t_1$ and leaves at $t_2$.

The differential heat transferred across the area $\mathrm{d}A$ is:

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = U \, \mathrm{d}A \, (T - t)
$$

For the hot fluid (cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$):

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = -\dot{m}_h \, C_{p,h} \, \mathrm{d}T
$$

For the cold fluid (heating from $t_1$ to $t_2$):

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, \mathrm{d}t
$$

Therefore, the temperature difference change $\mathrm{d}(T - t) = \mathrm{d}T - \mathrm{d}t$. Substituting the energy balances:

$$
\mathrm{d}(T - t) = \mathrm{d}T - \mathrm{d}t = -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}
$$

$$
\mathrm{d}(T - t) = -\mathrm{d}Q \left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}\right) = -\mathrm{d}Q \left(\dfrac{1}{W_h} + \dfrac{1}{W_c}\right)
$$

Where $W_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$ and $W_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$ are the heat capacity rates. Combining with $\mathrm{d}Q = U \, \mathrm{d}A \, (T - t)$:

$$
\mathrm{d}(T - t) = -U \, \mathrm{d}A \, (T - t) \left(\dfrac{1}{W_h} + \dfrac{1}{W_c}\right)
$$

Separating variables and integrating from inlet (subscript $i$) to outlet (subscript $o$):

$$
\int_{T_i - t_i}^{T_o - t_o} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}(T - t)}{T - t} = -U \left(\dfrac{1}{W_h} + \dfrac{1}{W_c}\right) \int_0^A \mathrm{d}A
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_o - t_o}{T_i - t_i}\right) = -U A \left(\dfrac{1}{W_h} + \dfrac{1}{W_c}\right)
$$

Also from total energy balance $Q = W_h (T_i - T_o) = W_c (t_o - t_i)$. Adding and rearranging gives the LMTD:

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

Where $\Delta T_1 = T_i - t_o$ (hot inlet vs cold outlet) and $\Delta T_2 = T_o - t_i$ (hot outlet vs cold inlet).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** LMTD is valid only for **counter-current flow** of constant-$U$ single-pass exchangers. For multi-pass shells, a correction factor $F_T$ must be applied.

## 3.2 Derivation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U_d$

For a composite cylindrical wall with two fluid films and a tube wall:

Heat flux through each resistance must be the same in steady state:

$$
q = h_i (T_{bulk,i} - T_{w,i}) = \dfrac{k_w}{x_w}(T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) = h_o (T_{w,o} - T_{bulk,o})
$$

Total temperature drop:

$$
T_{bulk,i} - T_{bulk,o} = q\left(\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}\right)
$$

By definition $U = q / (T_{bulk,i} - T_{bulk,o})$, therefore:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

When fouling resistances $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ are added:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o}
$$

For thin tubes $x_w = (d_o - d_i)/2$ and we often write:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{d_o}{d_i \, h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{d_o \, \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

(Equation in terms of **outside area basis**.)

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Manual Sizing (First Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a complete KTU 2024 ESE-style 14-mark problem worked out step-by-step.**

### Problem Statement

A chemical plant requires a shell-and-tube heat exchanger to cool **5000 kg/h of an organic liquid** from **150 °C to 90 °C** using cooling water available at **30 °C** that may be heated to **50 °C**. Design the exchanger using Kern's method.

**Given (assume initial values from literature):**

- Hot fluid (organic): $C_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.15$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $R_{f,i} = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W.
- Cold fluid (water): $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $\mu = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.62$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 995$ kg/m³, $R_{f,o} = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W.
- Tubes: $d_o = 25$ mm, $d_i = 20$ mm, $k_w = 50$ W/(m·K) (carbon steel), $L = 3$ m, square pitch $P_t = 32$ mm.
- Allowable pressure drops: $\Delta P_{tube} \le 50$ kPa, $\Delta P_{shell} \le 70$ kPa.

### SOLUTION (Full 14-Mark Working)

#### Step 1 — Calculate Heat Duty

$$
\dot{m}_h = 5000 / 3600 = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h \, C_{p,h} \, \Delta T_h = 1.389 \times 2.5 \times 10^3 \times (150 - 90)
$$

$$
Q = 1.389 \times 2500 \times 60 = 208{,}350 \text{ W} \approx 208.35 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate Cooling Water Flow Rate

$$
\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{4180 \times (50 - 30)} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{83{,}600} = 2.492 \text{ kg/s} \approx 8972 \text{ kg/h}
$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate LMTD

Assuming 1 shell-pass, 2 tube-passes (most common configuration):

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 50 = 100 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{100 - 60}{\ln(100/60)} = \dfrac{40}{0.5108} = 78.30 \text{ °C}
$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate LMTD Correction Factor $F_T$

$$
R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{150 - 90}{50 - 30} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3.0
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{50 - 30}{150 - 30} = \dfrac{20}{120} = 0.167
$$

For 1 shell-pass, 2 tube-passes (TEMA E, 2N shell layout): From TEMA chart $F_T \approx 0.94$.

Since $F_T > 0.75$, the configuration is acceptable.

#### Step 5 — Estimate Overall Coefficient $U_d$

Assume initially $U_d = 500$ W/(m²·K) (from typical values for organics cooled by water).

#### Step 6 — Calculate Required Heat Transfer Area

$$
A_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{Q}{U_d \, F_T \, \text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{500 \times 0.94 \times 78.30}
$$

$$
A_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{36{,}795} = 5.66 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 7 — Determine Number of Tubes

Per tube (3 m length, 25 mm OD):

$$
A_1 = \pi \, d_o \, L = \pi \times 0.025 \times 3 = 0.2356 \text{ m}^2/\text{tube}
$$

$$
N_t = \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{A_1 \times \text{(overdesign)}} = \dfrac{5.66}{0.2356 \times 0.85} = 28.26 \approx 30 \text{ tubes}
$$

(Apply ~15% overdesign factor for fouling/safety.)

#### Step 8 — Calculate Tube-Side Velocity & Re

Choose 2 tube passes, so flow per pass is $\dot{m}_h / N_p = 1.389 / 2 = 0.6945$ kg/s through all 15 tubes per pass:

Flow per tube:

$$
\dot{m}_{tube} = \dfrac{1.389}{30} = 0.0463 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Tube cross-section:

$$
A_i = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_i^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Velocity:

$$
u_i = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{tube}}{\rho_i \, A_i} = \dfrac{0.0463}{850 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1733 \text{ m/s}
$$

Mass flux:

$$
G_i = \rho_i \, u_i = 850 \times 0.1733 = 147.3 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

Reynolds number:

$$
Re_i = \dfrac{G_i \, d_i}{\mu_i} = \dfrac{147.3 \times 0.020}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2946 \quad (\text{Transitional})
$$

To improve, use 4 tube passes, $N_p = 4$, total tubes = 32:

$$
\dot{m}_{tube} = 1.389 / 32 = 0.0434 \text{ kg/s}, \quad u_i = 0.1624 \text{ m/s}, \quad Re_i = 2759
$$

Better to use **fewer, longer tubes**. Try $L = 4.8$ m, $N_p = 2$, $N_t = 16$, $d_o = 25$ mm:

$$
\dot{m}_{tube} = 1.389/16 = 0.0868 \text{ kg/s}, \quad u_i = 0.325 \text{ m/s}, \quad Re_i = 5523 \quad (\text{Turbulent — Acceptable})
$$

#### Step 9 — Calculate Tube-Side $h_i$ (Dittus-Boelter, cooling)

$$
Pr_i = \dfrac{C_{p,i} \, \mu_i}{k_i} = \dfrac{2500 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 16.67
$$

(Heating of water in tubes but we are cooling the tube-side organic; if tube is being cooled, exponent on Pr is 0.3.)

$$
h_i = 0.023 \, Re_i^{0.8} \, Pr_i^{0.3} \dfrac{k_i}{d_i} = 0.023 \times (5523)^{0.8} \times (16.67)^{0.3} \times \dfrac{0.15}{0.020}
$$

$$
(5523)^{0.8} = 770.0, \quad (16.67)^{0.3} = 2.36
$$

$$
h_i = 0.023 \times 770.0 \times 2.36 \times 7.5 = 313.4 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
$$

#### Step 10 — Calculate Shell-Side $h_o$ (Kern Method)

Bundle layout: square pitch $P_t = 32$ mm, $d_o = 25$ mm, so cross-flow area:

$$
A_s = \dfrac{P_t \, D_s \, (1 - d_o / P_t)}{P_t} \quad \text{(baffle window area)}
$$

Assume baffle spacing $B = 0.5 \times D_s$ and shell diameter $D_s \approx 0.20$ m.

Cross-flow area between tubes:

$$
A_c = (P_t - d_o) \, D_s \, (B / P_t) = (0.032 - 0.025) \times 0.20 \times (0.10/0.032) = 0.004375 \text{ m}^2
$$

Shell-side mass flux:

$$
G_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{2.492}{0.004375} = 569.6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

Equivalent diameter (square pitch):

$$
D_e = \dfrac{4(P_t^2 - \pi d_o^2/4)}{\pi d_o} = \dfrac{4(0.032^2 - \pi \times 0.025^2/4)}{\pi \times 0.025}
$$

$$
D_e = \dfrac{4(1.024 \times 10^{-3} - 4.909 \times 10^{-4})}{0.0785} = \dfrac{4 \times 5.331 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0785} = 0.0272 \text{ m}
$$

Reynolds number (shell):

$$
Re_s = \dfrac{D_e \, G_s}{\mu_s} = \dfrac{0.0272 \times 569.6}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 19{,}366 \quad (\text{Turbulent})
$$

Prandtl number (water at 40 °C):

$$
Pr_s = \dfrac{4180 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.62} = 5.39
$$

Kern equation:

$$
h_o = 0.36 \, Re_s^{0.55} \, Pr_s^{0.33} \dfrac{k_s}{D_e} = 0.36 \times (19366)^{0.55} \times (5.39)^{0.33} \times \dfrac{0.62}{0.0272}
$$

$$
(19366)^{0.55} = 153.1, \quad (5.39)^{0.33} = 1.75
$$

$$
h_o = 0.36 \times 153.1 \times 1.75 \times 22.79 = 2199 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
$$

#### Step 11 — Recalculate $U_d$

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{d_o}{d_i \, h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.020 \times 313.4} + 1.7 \times 10^{-4} + \dfrac{0.025 \times \ln(1.25)}{2 \times 50} + 1.7 \times 10^{-4} + \dfrac{1}{2199}
$$

$$
= 3.99 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-4} + 5.58 \times 10^{-5} + 1.7 \times 10^{-4} + 4.55 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
= 4.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_d = 213.2 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
$$

#### Step 12 — Iterate

New area required:

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{213.2 \times 0.94 \times 78.30} = \dfrac{208{,}350}{15{,}695} = 13.27 \text{ m}^2
$$

Required tubes ($L = 4.8$ m, $d_o = 25$ mm):

$$
N_t = \dfrac{13.27}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 4.8 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{13.27}{0.320} = 41.5 \Rightarrow 42 \text{ tubes}
$$

Try $N_t = 42$, $N_p = 2$, $D_s = 0.25$ m. Re-compute $G_s$ and $G_i$. Iterate until convergence.

#### Step 13 — Pressure Drop Check

Tube-side:

$$
\Delta P_t = 4f \dfrac{L N_p}{d_i}\dfrac{G_i^2}{2 \rho_i} \left(\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{-0.14}
$$

With $f = 0.0791 \times Re^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times (5523)^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times 0.0660 = 5.22 \times 10^{-3}$.

$$
\Delta P_t = 4 \times 5.22 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{4.8 \times 2}{0.020} \times \dfrac{147.3^2}{2 \times 850} \times 1.0 = 4 \times 5.22 \times 10^{-3} \times 480 \times 12.77 = 128 \text{ Pa} \ll 50 \text{ kPa}
$$

Shell-side (Kern):

$$
\Delta P_s = f \dfrac{G_s^2 \, D_s \, (N+1)}{2 \rho_s \, D_e}
$$

With $N = (L/B) - 1 = (4.8/0.125) - 1 = 37.4 \approx 38$:

$$
f = 0.0791 \times (19366)^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times 0.0458 = 3.62 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta P_s = 3.62 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{569.6^2 \times 0.25 \times 39}{2 \times 995 \times 0.0272}
$$

$$
= 3.62 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{569.6^2 \times 0.25 \times 39}{54.13}
$$

$$
\Delta P_s = 3.62 \times 10^{-3} \times 58{,}378 = 211 \text{ Pa} \ll 70 \text{ kPa}
$$

#### Step 14 — Final Conclusion

| Parameter | Final Value |
|---|---|
| Heat duty $Q$ | 208.35 kW |
| LMTD | 78.30 °C |
| $F_T$ | 0.94 |
| $U_d$ | 213.2 W/(m²·K) |
| Total area | 13.27 m² |
| Tubes | 42 nos, $d_o$ 25 mm, $L$ 4.8 m |
| Tube passes | 2 |
| TEMA type | AEL or BEU |
| MOC | Carbon Steel (tube) + CS shell |
| $\Delta P$ (tube/shell) | OK |

> [!TIP]
> **Manual sizing complete.** Next, hand over to HTRI or Aspen EDR for **computational optimization** (refining baffle cut %, bundle entrance/exit losses, vibration analysis).

## 3.4 Computational Optimization Strategy

Once the manual design is complete, computational tools refine the design by:

1. **Iterating on bundle layout** — triangular pitch gives 14% more tubes per shell area than square pitch (but harder to clean).
2. **Baffle cut optimization** — typical 20–25% of shell ID; reducing cut increases $h_o$ but raises $\Delta P_s$.
3. **Inlet/outlet nozzle placement** — Impingement plate protection reduces tube vibration.
4. **Cross-flow vs. window flow** — Bell-Delaware method replaces Kern for accuracy.
5. **Vibration analysis** — TEMA R, TEMA RGP, and ASME code checks for tube failure.

A standard **AES (TEMA)** shell-and-tube exchanger has the following physical arrangement (described because Mermaid cannot physically draw it):

       +-------------------------+         +-------------------------+

       |  +---+ +---+ +---+      |         |                         |
       |  | T | | T | | T |      |  SHELL  |   T T T T T T T T T    |
       |  +---+ +---+ +---+      |  (E)    |   T T T T T T T T T    |
       |  Tubesheet (fixed)      |         |   Tubesheet (floating) |
       +-------------------------+         +-------------------------+
                  |                                      |
                  v                                      ^
              INLET (Cold)                         OUTLET (Cold)
       Tubeside: A → B (1 pass, 2 tube-passes per shell)
       Shellside: F (2-pass) or E (1-pass)
       Baffles: horizontal segmental, spaced 0.5 to 1.0 shell IDs apart
```
