# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "overall heat transfer coefficient" for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. How is it affected by fouling?**

**Model Answer:**

The overall heat transfer coefficient $U_d$ is defined as the **rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference** between the bulk hot and cold fluids. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

Each term represents a resistance to heat transfer — inside convective film, inside fouling, tube wall conduction, outside fouling, and outside convective film.

**Effect of Fouling:** Fouling deposits (scale, coke, biological growth) act as additional thermal resistances on either side of the tube wall. Since resistances are additive (series), the addition of $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ **decreases $U_d$**, thereby requiring **more surface area** for the same heat duty. This is why periodic cleaning and overdesign margins are essential.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is LMTD correction factor $F_T$? When does its value become less than 0.75, and what action is suggested?**

**Model Answer:**

The LMTD correction factor $F_T$ accounts for the deviation from **pure counter-current flow** in multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers. For a 1-shell-pass N-tube-pass exchanger, $F_T < 1$ because true counter-current flow does not exist.

$F_T$ depends on two dimensionless groups:
$$
R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}, \quad P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}
$$

**When $F_T < 0.75$:** The temperature cross is severe; the configuration is economically inefficient.

**Suggested Action:** Change the design to a **2-shell-pass arrangement** (TEMA F shell) using two heat exchangers in series or an internal longitudinal baffle, which brings $F_T$ back into a usable range (typically 0.75–1.0).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **TEMA classification system** for shell-and-tube heat exchangers. List any **four TEMA types** and identify one application for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chemical plant needs to condense **8000 kg/h of saturated toluene vapour at 110 °C** using cooling water available at **25 °C** that may be heated to **45 °C**. Estimate the **required heat transfer area** assuming an overall coefficient of $U_d = 600$ W/(m²·K). Latent heat of vaporisation of toluene = **363 kJ/kg**.

#### SOLUTION — (a) TEMA Classification

**Sketch:**

```
   +---+   +---+   +---+   +---+         (Front Head, e.g. 'A')
   | C |   | H |   | A |   | N |
   | H |---| N |---| N |---| N |------- (Shell, e.g. 'E')  +--------+
   | A |   | L |   | E |   | L |        |   Tubes         | Floating|
   | N |   |   |   | L |   |   |   ====>|   (3-letter TEMA|  Head   |
   | N |   |   |   |   |   |   |        |   code: AEL,    | (S, T, U)|
   | E |   |   |   |   |   |   |        |   AES, BEU etc.)|         |
   | L |   |   |   |   |   |   |        |                 |         |
   +---+   +---+   +---+   +---+        +-----------------+---------+
   Front   Shell   Rear                       Shell with baffles
   Head    Type    Head
```

**TEMA uses a 3-letter code:** Front End + Shell + Rear End.

**Four TEMA types and applications:**

| TEMA Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AEL** | Channel + one-pass shell + fixed tubesheet | Clean water/oil coolers, low pressure |
| **AES** | Channel + one-pass shell + floating head | Hot oil coolers, refineries |
| **BEM** | Bonnet + one-pass shell + fixed tubesheet | High-pressure gas cooling |
| **BEU** | Bonnet + one-pass shell + U-tube bundle | Thermal expansion services, chemical plants |

#### SOLUTION — (b) Condenser Sizing Calculation

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 8000/3600 = 2.222$ kg/s
- $\lambda = 363{,}000$ J/kg
- $T_{c,in} = 25$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 45$ °C
- $U_d = 600$ W/(m²·K)
- $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 110$ °C (isothermal condensation)

**Step 1: Heat Duty**

$$
Q = \dot{m} \lambda = 2.222 \times 363{,}000 = 806{,}667 \text{ W} \approx 806.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: LMTD**

Since $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 110$ °C:

$$
\Delta T_1 = 110 - 45 = 65 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = 110 - 25 = 85 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{85 - 65}{\ln(85/65)} = \dfrac{20}{\ln(1.3077)} = \dfrac{20}{0.2683} = 74.55 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3: Correction Factor**

For pure condensation, $F_T = 1.0$ (since condensation is at constant temperature).

**Step 4: Required Area**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U_d \, F_T \, \text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{806{,}667}{600 \times 1.0 \times 74.55}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{806{,}667}{44{,}730} = 18.03 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Final Answer**

**Required heat transfer area = 18.03 m²**

For 25 mm OD, 4.8 m long tubes: $N_t = 18.03 / (\pi \times 0.025 \times 4.8) = 47.8 \Rightarrow 48$ tubes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **selection criteria for material of construction** for shell-and-tube heat exchangers. Provide a decision matrix for at least **five services**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger heats **1.2 kg/s of water from 30 °C to 80 °C** using an oil entering at **150 °C** and leaving at **100 °C**. The inner tube has $d_i = 25$ mm, $d_o = 32$ mm, $k_w = 50$ W/(m·K), and length $L = 10$ m. The oil-side coefficient $h_o = 1200$ W/(m²·K) and water-side $h_i = 4500$ W/(m²·K). Calculate the **overall coefficient $U$** and the **actual outlet temperature of water**.

#### SOLUTION — (a) Material Selection

Material selection in heat exchanger design must balance **corrosion resistance, thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, fabricability, and cost**.

**Selection Criteria:**

1. **Corrosion Resistance** — Material must withstand both fluids and trace contaminants (O₂, H₂S, Cl⁻).
2. **Operating Temperature and Pressure** — Affects creep strength and code compliance.
3. **Thermal Conductivity** — Higher $k_w$ reduces wall resistance but is less critical than $h_i$ and $h_o$.
4. **Fabricability** — Welding, bending, rolling suitability.
5. **Cost and Availability** — Initial cost vs. lifecycle cost.
6. **Galvanic Compatibility** — Avoid dissimilar metal corrosion.
7. **Cleanability** — Smooth surfaces (e.g. polished SS) reduce fouling adhesion.

**Decision Matrix for Five Services:**

| Service Fluid | Recommended MOC | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Steam, boiler feed water | Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr.70 | Cost-effective, adequate corrosion resistance |
| Sea water cooling | Titanium / 70-30 Cu-Ni | Resists chloride pitting |
| Sulphuric acid (conc.) | Cast iron / Hastelloy C | Concentrated H₂SO₄ is non-oxidising |
| Caustic soda (NaOH) | Nickel / SS 304L | Resists caustic embrittlement |
| Cryogenic LNG | Aluminium / SS 304L | Retains toughness at −162 °C |

#### SOLUTION — (b) Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger

**Step 1: Calculate Overall Coefficient**

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{0.032}{0.025 \times 4500} + \dfrac{0.032 \ln(0.032/0.025)}{2 \times 50} + \dfrac{1}{1200}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{0.032}{112.5} + \dfrac{0.032 \times 0.2469}{100} + 8.333 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
= 2.844 \times 10^{-4} + 7.90 \times 10^{-5} + 8.333 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
= 1.197 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_o = 835.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
$$

**Step 2: Verify by Energy Balance**

Heat capacity rate of water: $W_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = 1.2 \times 4180 = 5016$ W/K

Heat capacity rate of oil: from assumed $C_{p,o} = 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K), calculate oil flow.

Heat given by oil = Heat received by water (steady state, counter-current):

Assume $C_{p,o} = 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\dot{m}_o = 1.0$ kg/s (typical).

$W_o = 1.0 \times 2200 = 2200$ W/K

Since $W_o < W_c$, oil is the **minimum fluid**. Oil-side temperature change = $150 - 100 = 50$ °C.

Maximum possible heat transfer: $Q_{\max} = W_{\min} \Delta T_{\max} = 2200 \times (150 - 30) = 264{,}000$ W

NTU approach or direct LMTD method:

**Step 3: LMTD**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - T_{c,out}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ °C}
$$

We need to find $T_{c,out}$. From energy balance:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - 30) = 5016 (T_{c,out} - 30)
$$

Also: $Q = \dot{m}_o C_{p,o} (150 - 100) = 1.0 \times 2200 \times 50 = 110{,}000$ W (given from oil side)

So:

$$
T_{c,out} = 30 + \dfrac{110{,}000}{5016} = 30 + 21.93 = 51.93 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 4: Recalculate LMTD with $T_{c,out} = 51.93$ °C**

$$
\Delta T_1 = 150 - 51.93 = 98.07 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{98.07 - 70}{\ln(98.07/70)} = \dfrac{28.07}{0.3369} = 83.32 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5: Verify with $Q = U_o A \Delta T_{LMTD}$**

$$
A = \pi d_o L = \pi \times 0.032 \times 10 = 1.005 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{\text{calculated}} = 835.5 \times 1.005 \times 83.32 = 69{,}970 \text{ W} \approx 70 \text{ kW}
$$

This is **less than 110 kW**, meaning the actual outlet temperature of water will be **less than 51.93 °C** because the assumed oil flow rate / properties need to be solved iteratively.

**Iteration approach:** Iterate on $T_{c,out}$ until $Q_{\text{calc}} = Q_{\text{required}}$:

Let $Q = 70{,}000$ W:

$$
T_{c,out} = 30 + \dfrac{70{,}000}{5016} = 43.95 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_1 = 150 - 43.95 = 106.05 \text{ °C}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 70 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{36.05}{0.4157} = 86.72 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
Q = 835.5 \times 1.005 \times 86.72 = 72{,}820 \text{ W} \approx 72.8 \text{ kW}
$$

Continue iterating until $Q$ stabilises at ~73 kW. **Final answer: $T_{c,out} \approx 44.5$ °C.**

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{U_o = 835.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}, \quad T_{c,out} \approx 44.5 \text{ °C}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $G$ units:** Many students use $G$ in kg/(hr·m²) but the Kern equation requires **kg/(s·m²)**. Always convert flow rates to kg/s before computing mass flux.
> 
> 2. **Incorrect Pr exponent:** For Dittus-Boelter, exponent is **0.4 for heating** and **0.3 for cooling** of tube-side fluid. Mixing these up gives ~15% error.
> 
> 3. **Using $d_o$ vs $d_i$ inconsistently:** Decide on area basis first. If using outside area, all $h$ values must be on outside basis (use $d_o$ in Re and correction factor).
> 
> 4. **Forgetting $F_T$:** A very common error is to skip the LMTD correction factor for multi-pass shells, leading to 20–40% undersizing.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Reynolds criterion:** Tube-side flow should be **turbulent (Re > 10,000)**. If transitional, the Dittus-Boelter equation is not valid; use Sieder-Tate instead.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring viscosity correction:** $(\mu/\mu_w)^{-0.14}$ is mandatory in the pressure drop formula; neglecting it gives non-conservative designs.
> 
> 7. **Wrong log-mean formula for LMTD:** Ensure $\Delta T_1$ is taken at the **same end** of the exchanger (both hot inlet — cold outlet, or both hot outlet — cold inlet). Reverse the assignment if $Q_{\text{calc}}$ is negative.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

### A. Core Concepts to Memorise
- [ ] **Heat duty** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ for sensible, $Q = \dot{m} \lambda$ for latent
- [ ] **LMTD** formula and the two end-temperature differences
- [ ] **Correction factor $F_T$** from TEMA charts; iterate if $F_T < 0.75$
- [ ] **Overall coefficient** $U_d$ as series of thermal resistances
- [ ] **TEMA 3-letter code** (Front, Shell, Rear)

### B. Important Design Equations
- [ ] **Dittus-Boelter** for tube-side (0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^0.3/0.4)
- [ ] **Kern equation** for shell-side (0.36 Re^0.55 Pr^0.33)
- [ ] **Equivalent diameter** $D_e = 4(P_t^2 - \pi d_o^2/4)/\pi d_o$ for square pitch
- [ ] **Blasius friction factor** $f = 0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$
- [ ] **Tube-side pressure drop** with viscosity correction
- [ ] **Shell-side pressure drop** with baffle count $N+1$

### C. Decision Rules
- [ ] Place **dirty/corrosive fluid on tube side**
- [ ] **Fixed tubesheet** for clean, similar-T fluids
- [ ] **Floating head / U-tube** for high ΔT or fouling
- [ ] Apply **15–25% area overdesign** for fouling margin
- [ ] Ensure **tube-side $Re > 10{,}000$** for turbulent flow
- [ ] Stay within **allowable $\Delta P$** (50–70 kPa typical)

### D. Standards to Quote in Examinations
- [ ] **TEMA** for nomenclature and mechanical standards
- [ ] **ASME Section VIII Div. 1** for pressure vessels
- [ ] **ASME B31.3** for process piping
- [ ] **IS 4503 / IS 2825** for Indian context
- [ ] **HTRI / Aspen EDR** for computational refinement

### E. Common Errors to Avoid
- [ ] Wrong end assignment for $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$ in LMTD
- [ ] Forgetting $F_T$ in multi-pass designs
- [ ] Confusing heating vs cooling Pr exponent
- [ ] Mixing area bases (inside vs outside)
- [ ] Not iterating when $U_{\text{calc}}$ differs from $U_{\text{assumed}}$ by >10%
- [ ] Omitting units in final numerical answer

### F. Software Tools for Computational Optimisation
- [ ] **HTRI Xchanger Suite** — Industry standard
- [ ] **Aspen EDR (Exchanger Design & Rating)** — Integrated with Aspen Plus
- [ ] **COMSOL Multiphysics** — CFD-based detailed analysis
- [ ] **PVElite / Codeware** — Mechanical design per ASME
- [ ] **HTFS (Heat Transfer & Fluid Flow Service)** — Research-grade

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identify a Process that Uses Different Types of Evaporators, Choose a Suitable Evaporator and Design It: Selection of Evaporator Type, Material of Construction, Physical Properties, Manual Sizing (First Design), Computational Optimization, and Design Data Sheet Preparation as per Standards
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identify a Process that Uses Different Types of Evaporators, Choose a Suitable Evaporator and Design It: Selection of Evaporator Type, Material of Construction, Physical Properties, Manual Sizing (First Design), Computational Optimization, and Design Data Sheet Preparation as per Standards

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Evaporator** is a heat transfer device designed to concentrate a solution consisting of a non-volatile solute and a volatile solvent (most commonly water) by removing the solvent through vaporization, utilizing a heat source such as steam, hot oil, or electrical heating.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** *Evaporation is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (typically water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute to obtain a concentrated solution (thick liquor). The vapor leaving the solution is termed the **Secondary Vapor** (or *vapor space product*), while the concentrated liquid leaving the evaporator is called the **Thick Liquor** (or *final product*).*

**Design of an Evaporator** is the systematic engineering process that integrates:
1. Process identification and operating conditions.
2. Selection of appropriate evaporator type.
3. Selection of **Material of Construction (MOC)**.
4. Collection of physical and thermal **Physical Properties**.
5. Manual sizing (first-pass design) using heat and mass balance.
6. Computational optimization (refinement of area, economy, BPE).
7. Preparation of a **Design Data Sheet (DDS)** as per industry standards.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Tea Kettle" Analogy:**
> Imagine boiling a thin salt-water solution in a kettle on a stove. The steam escapes, leaving behind saltier water. This is **single-effect evaporation** — one kettle, one heat source, one batch.
>
> Now, if you capture the steam from the first kettle and feed it into a second kettle (containing slightly saltier water), you have **multiple-effect evaporation** — recycling energy. This is the heart of industrial evaporation.
>
> **Evaporator Design** is essentially the engineering version of answering: *How big a kettle do I need? What should it be made of? How many kettles should I chain together? And how much steam will it consume?*

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants/metrics are standardly used and are **bolded** for emphasis:

- **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** — in **°C** or **K**
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** — in **W/m²·K**
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization (λ)** — in **kJ/kg**
- **Steam Economy (E)** — dimensionless (kg vapor / kg steam)
- **Capacity (C)** — in **kg water evaporated/hour**
- **Heat Transfer Area (A)** — in **m²**

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A typical **14-mark KTU question** on this module asks the student to *identify a process*, *select the evaporator*, *justify the MOC*, and *perform manual sizing* — all in a single structured problem. Memorizing the *decision flow chart* (mermaid diagram in Section 4) is therefore a high-yield activity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Evaporators (Structured Logic)

Evaporators are classified along **two primary axes**:

#### A. By Mode of Operation
- **Batch Evaporator** — feed loaded, concentrated, discharged (low capital, used for small capacity / viscous products).
- **Continuous Evaporator** — steady feed, steady discharge (used in large industrial plants).

#### B. By Circulation/Heating Geometry
- **Natural Circulation (Vertical / Horizontal Tube)**
  - *Short-Tube Vertical Evaporator* (calandria type)
  - *Long-Tube Vertical Evaporator* (LTV, with or without vapor binding)
- **Forced Circulation Evaporator** (pump-driven)
- **Falling Film Evaporator** (FFE)
- **Rising Film Evaporator** (RFE)
- **Agitated Thin Film Evaporator** (ATFE / Wiped Film)
- **Plate Evaporator**
- **Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR)** and **Thermal Vapor Recompression (TVR)**

---

### 2.2 Selection of Evaporator Type — The "Why" Behind the Choice

| Process Criterion | Recommended Evaporator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat-sensitive materials** (e.g., fruit juices, antibiotics) | Falling Film / Forced Circulation | Short residence time, low ΔT |
| **Viscous / fouling liquids** (e.g., black liquor, sugar) | Forced Circulation / Agitated Thin Film | High tube velocity, mechanical agitation |
| **Crystallizing solutions** (e.g., NaCl, Na₂SO₄) | Forced Circulation with slurry recirculation | Avoids scaling, handles suspended solids |
| **Large capacity, dilute solutions** (e.g., paper mill black liquor) | Multiple-Effect Long Tube Vertical | High steam economy, large throughput |
| **Foaming liquids** | Forced Circulation | Foam control through high velocity |
| **Corrosive service** (e.g., phosphoric acid) | Falling Film with graphite / PTFE-lined MOC | Compatible materials, easy cleaning |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logic Box:** Selection is governed by **six cardinal rules**:
> 1. **Viscosity** of the feed and thick liquor
> 2. **Thermal sensitivity** of the solute
> 3. **Tendency to foul / scale / crystallize**
> 4. **Foaming behavior**
> 5. **Capital vs. operating cost trade-off**
> 6. **Required steam economy and capacity**

---

### 2.3 Material of Construction (MOC) — Selection Logic

The MOC must simultaneously resist:
- **General corrosion** (uniform attack)
- **Pitting and crevice corrosion**
- **Stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**
- **Erosion-corrosion** (especially in forced circulation units)

| Liquid Service | Recommended MOC |
|---|---|
| Neutral salt solutions (NaCl, Na₂SO₄) | Carbon Steel (CS) — ASTM A516 / SA-516 |
| Caustic solutions (NaOH) | **Stainless Steel 304 / 316** (Ni content avoids caustic SCC) |
| Phosphoric / Sulfuric acid | **Stainless Steel 316L**, Hastelloy, rubber-lined CS |
| Fruit juices / dairy | **SS 304** (sanitary grade, polished to 0.8 μm Ra) |
| Highly corrosive (HCl) | Glass-lined steel, PTFE-lined, graphite |
| Black liquor (paper) | **SS 304L / 316L** with stress-relief |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** must be considered when MOC is selected at the *maximum design temperature*, not just the saturation temperature of steam. MOC rating tables (e.g., ASME Section II) require **temperature derating** for higher BPE.

---

### 2.4 Physical Properties Required for Design

| Property | Symbol | Typical Source |
|---|---|---|
| Feed concentration | $x_F$ (% by weight) | Process data |
| Final concentration | $x_L$ (% by weight) | Process requirement |
| Boiling Point Elevation | **BPE** | Dühring chart / OLI simulation |
| Density (feed & thick liquor) | $\rho_F, \rho_L$ | Perry's Handbook |
| Viscosity (feed & thick liquor) | $\mu_F, \mu_L$ | Perry's Handbook |
| Specific heat | $C_{pF}, C_{pL}$ | Perry's / NIST REFPROP |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | Perry's |
| Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda$ | Steam tables / Antoine eq. |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient (initial estimate) | $U$ | Typical values: 1500–3500 W/m²·K for water; 500–1500 for viscous |

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize this for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat duty from steam | **kW** |
| 2 | $Q = m_v \cdot \lambda_v$ | Heat duty from vapor side | **kW** |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Standard heat transfer equation | **kW** |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required heat transfer area | **m²** |
| 5 | $m_v = m_F\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Water evaporated | **kg/h** |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{m_v}{m_s}$ | Steam economy (single effect ≈ 0.8–1.0) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{available} = T_s - T_v - BPE - \text{Raoult's loss}$ | Effective ΔT | **°C** |
| 8 | $T_{sat}(x) = T_{sat}(\text{water}) + BPE(x)$ | Boiling temp of solution | **°C** |
| 9 | $\text{Steam Economy (multi-effect)} \approx 0.8 \cdot N$ | For N effects | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{U_i}}{\sum \dfrac{1}{U_i}}$ | ΔT distribution in multi-effect (equal area method) | **°C** |
| 11 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Area per effect | **m²** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Pitfall:** Always keep $Q$, $U$, $A$, and $\Delta T$ in **consistent units**. KTU examiners deduct marks when $Q$ is in **kW** but $A$ is calculated using $\lambda$ in **kJ/kg** without proper time conversion. *Always convert kg/h → kg/s before multiplication.*

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaporator design underpins the **entire concentrated chemicals industry**:
- **Sugar industry** — concentrating cane juice from ~15% to 70% solids (multiple-effect falling film).
- **Paper & pulp** — black liquor concentration from 15% to 70%+ (forced circulation, 5–7 effects with MVR).
- **Dairy** — milk concentration from 12% to 50% TS (falling film, MVR).
- **Desalination** — seawater to fresh water (multi-effect distillation, MED).
- **Cement / chlor-alkali** — caustic concentration.
- **Pharma / biotech** — heat-sensitive API concentration (wiped film evaporators).

The **design data sheet** that emerges from this exercise is the **legal and operational document** used by fabrication shops, third-party inspectors (TPI), and statutory bodies (IBR, ASME).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Duty and Mass Balance for Single-Effect Evaporator

**Statement:** Derive the expressions for water evaporated, steam required, and heat transfer area for a single-effect continuous evaporator.

**Given:**
- Feed rate = $m_F$ (kg/h) at concentration $x_F$ (mass fraction of solute)
- Thick liquor rate = $m_L$ (kg/h) at concentration $x_L$
- Steam temperature (saturated) = $T_s$ (°C)
- Vapor space temperature (at the boiling solution) = $T_v$ (°C)
- Latent heat of steam = $\lambda_s$ (kJ/kg)
- Latent heat of secondary vapor = $\lambda_v$ (kJ/kg)
- Specific heat of feed = $C_{pF}$ (kJ/kg·°C)
- Feed temperature = $T_F$ (°C)
- Overall heat transfer coefficient = $U$ (W/m²·K)
- Boiling point elevation = BPE (°C)

---

#### Step 1 — Total Mass Balance

$$
m_F = m_L + m_v
$$

where $m_v$ is the **rate of water vaporized** (kg/h).

---

#### Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance

Solute is non-volatile, so the salt entering must equal salt leaving.

$$
m_F \cdot x_F = m_L \cdot x_L
$$

Solving for $m_L$:

$$
m_L = m_F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Water Evaporated

Substituting $m_L$ into the total balance:

$$
m_v = m_F - m_F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} = m_F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

This is the **amount of water to be removed**.

---

#### Step 4 — Overall Energy Balance (steady state, no heat loss)

Heat in = Heat out. The steam supplies heat $Q$ to the evaporator. Inside the evaporator:
- The feed is heated from $T_F$ to its boiling point $T_v$.
- A fraction is vaporized.

$$
Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = m_F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_v - T_F) + m_v \cdot \lambda_v
$$

(Assuming thick liquor leaves at $T_v$.)

---

#### Step 5 — Steam Required

$$
m_s = \frac{m_F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_v - T_F) + m_v \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}
$$

---

#### Step 6 — Effective ΔT

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \text{BPE} - \text{raoult's\_loss}
$$

For first design, **raoult's loss** is often neglected.

---

#### Step 7 — Heat Transfer Area

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}
$$

with all units in SI: $Q$ in **W**, $U$ in **W/m²·K**, $\Delta T$ in **K** or **°C**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — ΔT Distribution in Multi-Effect Evaporator (Equal Area Method)

**Statement:** Show that the temperature drop across the $i$-th effect is inversely proportional to its $U_i$.

**Concept:** In a multi-effect evaporator with $N$ effects, the total $\Delta T_{total}$ is distributed such that **each effect has the same area $A$**.

The heat transferred in effect $i$:

$$
Q_i = U_i \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_i
$$

Since each effect evaporates the *same* amount of water (ideal case with equal area), and $\lambda$ is approximately constant:

$$
Q_i = Q = \text{constant} \quad \forall \, i
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_i \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_i = U_j \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_j
$$

This gives:

$$
\Delta T_i = \frac{Q}{U_i \cdot A}
$$

And the sum across all effects:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q}{U_i \cdot A} = \Delta T_{total}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{\Delta T_{total}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{U_i}
$$

Back-substituting to get $\Delta T_i$:

$$
\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{U_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{U_j}}
$$

This is the **standard equal-area ΔT distribution** used in all multi-effect design codes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Economy for Multi-Effect Evaporator

For an $N$-effect evaporator with negligible sensible heat load and equal latent heats:

$$
E = N \cdot E_1
$$

where $E_1$ is the economy of a single effect (typically 0.85–0.95 for natural circulation). In practice:

$$
E \approx 0.85 \cdot N \quad \text{(for } N \leq 4 \text{)}
$$

For $N > 4$, the incremental economy diminishes due to:
- **Boiling point elevation** spreading the available ΔT.
- **Falling ΔT** per effect (the last effect may have $\Delta T < 5\,°C$).
- **Increasing U-drop** due to higher viscosity in later effects.

A **five-effect evaporator** is generally the **economic optimum** for water-based systems; beyond this, **MVR (Mechanical Vapor Recompression)** becomes more cost-effective.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example (Worked)

**Problem (KTU-Style, Module 2):**

A single-effect continuous evaporator concentrates 10,000 kg/h of a NaOH solution from 10% (w/w) to 30% (w/w). Saturated dry steam at 3 bar (abs) is used. Feed enters at 30 °C. The evaporator operates at atmospheric pressure (101.3 kPa). BPE of the 30% solution is **6 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is estimated as **U = 2000 W/m²·K**. Latent heat of steam at 3 bar = **λₛ = 2164 kJ/kg**. Latent heat of secondary vapor at ~100 °C ≈ **λᵥ = 2257 kJ/kg**. Specific heat of dilute feed ≈ **C_pF = 3.8 kJ/kg·°C**. Compute: (a) water evaporated, (b) steam required, (c) heat transfer area.

**Solution:**

**(a) Water Evaporated:**

$$
m_v = m_F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10000 \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.30}\right)
$$

$$
m_v = 10000 \times \left(1 - 0.3333\right) = 10000 \times 0.6667 = 6667 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

**(b) Steam Required:**

Boiling temperature in evaporator:
- $T_{sat}(\text{water at 1 atm}) = 100\,°C$
- $T_v = 100 + \text{BPE} = 100 + 6 = 106\,°C$

Heat to bring feed to boiling + heat to vaporize:

$$
Q_{total} = m_F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_v - T_F) + m_v \cdot \lambda_v
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 10000 \times 3.8 \times (106 - 30) + 6667 \times 2257
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 10000 \times 3.8 \times 76 + 6667 \times 2257
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 2.888 \times 10^6 + 1.505 \times 10^7
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 1.794 \times 10^7 \ \text{kJ/h}
$$

Converting to kW (1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3600 kJ/h):

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{1.794 \times 10^7}{3600} = 4983 \ \text{kW}
$$

Steam required:

$$
m_s = \frac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1.794 \times 10^7}{2164} = 8290 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

**(c) Heat Transfer Area:**

Effective ΔT:

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \text{BPE} = T_s(3\,\text{bar}) - 106
$$

From steam tables: $T_s$ at 3 bar (abs) = **133.5 °C**.

$$
\Delta T = 133.5 - 106 = 27.5\,°C
$$

Area:

$$
A = \frac{Q_{total}}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1.794 \times 10^7 / 3600}{2000 \times 27.5}
$$

$$
A = \frac{4983}{55000} = 0.0906 \ \text{m}^2 \ \text{(kJ/h basis is wrong!)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Pitfall:** Re-doing with $Q$ in **W**:

$$
Q = 4.983 \times 10^6 \ \text{W}
$$

$$
A = \frac{4.983 \times 10^6}{2000 \times 27.5} = \frac{4.983 \times 10^6}{55000} = 90.6 \ \text{m}^2
$$

> This is the **correct area** — a real industrial single-effect evaporator of this capacity would be in the range **80–120 m²**, validating the calculation.

---

### 3.5 Optimization Step — Why Iterate?

The first design (manual sizing) gives a *baseline*. The **computational optimization** loop is:

1. Use the baseline area to compute tube length, tube bundle diameter, and shell diameter.
2. Estimate tube-side and shell-side velocities.
3. Recalculate $U$ from **Dittus-Boelter** (tube-side) and **Kern's method** (shell-side).
4. Iterate the area to convergence ($\vert A_{new} - A_{old} \vert < 1\%$).
5. Re-distribute $\Delta T$ in multi-effect based on the *new* $U_i$'s.
6. Re-calculate steam economy and payback period.
7. Finalize the **DDS** (Design Data Sheet).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Criterion (Standard Practice):** $\vert \Delta A / A \vert \leq 0.01$ in two successive iterations, or $\vert \Delta Q / Q \vert \leq 0.005$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Evaporator Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Process] --> B{Is product heat-sensitive?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Select Falling Film or Wiped Film Evaporator]
    B -- No --> D{Is liquid viscous gt 100 cP or fouling?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Select Forced Circulation Evaporator]
    D -- No --> F{Is solution crystallizing or slurry?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Select Forced Circulation with Slurry Recycle]
    F -- No --> H{Capacity requirement?}
    H -- Small to Medium --> I[Select Short-Tube Vertical Calandria]
    H -- Large --> J[Select Long-Tube Vertical Multiple Effect]
    C --> K[Step 2: Select MOC]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Is liquid corrosive?}
    L -- No --> M[Use Carbon Steel ASTM A516]
    L -- Mildly --> N[Use SS 304 or SS 316]
    L -- Highly --> O[Use Hastelloy / PTFE / Graphite Lined]
    M --> P[Step 3: Collect Physical Properties]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Step 4: Manual Sizing using Q = U A DeltaT]
    Q --> R[Step 5: Computational Optimization Loop]
    R --> S[Step 6: Prepare Design Data Sheet as per TEMA and ASME Standards]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Effect Evaporator System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Feed Tank] --> B1[Feed Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Effect 1: Calandria or Falling Film]
    C1 --> D1[Effect 2]
    D1 --> E1[Effect 3]
    E1 --> F1[Effect 4]
    F1 --> G1[Thick Liquor Discharge Pump]
    C1 --> H1[Secondary Vapor Line]
    H1 --> D1
    D1 --> I1[Secondary Vapor Line]
    I1 --> E1
    E1 --> J1[Secondary Vapor Line]
    J1 --> F1
    F1 --> K1[Final Vapor to Condenser]
    K1 --> L1[Barometric Condenser]
    L1 --> M1[Vacuum System Steam Jet Ejector]
    M1 --> N1[Cooling Water to Wet Well]
    A2[Steam Supply at 3 bar abs] --> C1
    C1 --> O1[Condensate Recovery to Hot Well]
    G1 --> P1[Thick Liquor Product Tank]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Effect 1** receives **live steam** (the *prime mover*).
- Vapor from Effect 1 is the *heating medium* of Effect 2, and so on.
- The **last effect** operates under vacuum (to maximize $\Delta T$).
- The **condenser + ejector** maintain the system vacuum and recover non-condensables.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Computational Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Initial Guess of A and U] --> S2[Calculate Q from Energy Balance]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Tube Side Re and Pr]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Dittus Boelter or Sieder Tate Correlation]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Shell Side h using Kern Method]
    S5 --> S6[Calculate New U overall]
    S6 --> S7[Compute New A from Q and DeltaT]
    S7 --> S8{Check Convergence: absolute value of A new minus A old divided by A old lt 0.01}
    S8 -- No --> S2
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Output Optimized A U and Area]
    S9 --> S10[Generate Design Data Sheet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The convergence check uses the absolute value notation $\vert A_{new} - A_{old} \vert / A_{old} < 0.01$. In the mermaid code above, this is rendered as a plain-text approximation to avoid breaking the parser.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four factors that govern the selection of an evaporator type for a given process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Thermal sensitivity of the solute** — heat-sensitive materials require falling film or wiped film evaporators with short residence time.
2. **Viscosity of feed and thick liquor** — high viscosity demands forced circulation or agitated thin film.
3. **Tendency to foul, scale, or crystallize** — handled by forced circulation with high tube-side velocity.
4. **Foaming behavior of the solution** — foaming liquids require forced circulation to break foams through high velocity.
5. **Required capacity and steam economy** — large capacities favor multiple-effect long-tube vertical evaporators.

*(Any 4 well-explained points = 3 marks; 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and explain its significance in evaporator design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) is the difference between the boiling temperature of a solution and the boiling temperature of pure water at the same pressure.*
$$
\text{BPE} = T_{boil}(\text{solution}) - T_{boil}(\text{water})
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- BPE **reduces the effective ΔT** available for heat transfer, requiring a **larger heat transfer area** for the same duty.
- In **multi-effect evaporators**, BPE accumulates across effects, so the total $\Delta T$ lost = $\sum \text{BPE}_i$.
- BPE is the primary reason why **5 effects** is the practical maximum for water-based systems; beyond 5, the last effect ΔT becomes too small to be useful.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each long question has **internal choice** (either-or). Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B**. Each long question has **two sub-parts**, typically **(a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

A single-effect continuous evaporator is to concentrate **20,000 kg/h** of a sugar solution from **12% (w/w) to 50% (w/w)**. The feed enters at **40 °C** and saturated steam at **2.5 bar (abs)** is used as the heating medium. The evaporator operates at **atmospheric pressure (101.3 kPa)**. The boiling point elevation of the 50% solution is **4 °C**. Take:
- $\lambda_s$ (steam at 2.5 bar) = **2180 kJ/kg**
- $\lambda_v$ (secondary vapor at ~100 °C) = **2257 kJ/kg**
- $C_{pF}$ (dilute feed) = **3.9 kJ/kg·°C**
- $U$ = **1800 W/m²·K**

**(a)** Identify a suitable process industry where this evaporator would be used, justify the selection of a **falling film evaporator (FFE)**, and recommend a **Material of Construction (MOC)** with technical reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **water evaporated, steam consumed, steam economy, and the heat transfer area** required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Process Identification, Selection, and MOC (7 marks):**

**Process Industry (2 marks):**
This evaporator is a **typical sugar mill juice concentrator** (cane juice / beet juice concentration in the sugar industry). The process stream is a sugar solution that is moderately heat-sensitive (sucrose starts hydrolyzing and color formation begins above 120 °C, known as *caramelization / Maillard browning*).

**Selection — Falling Film Evaporator (FFE) (3 marks):**
- **Short residence time** (~5–20 seconds): critical for heat-sensitive sugar syrup to avoid color and taste degradation.
- **High heat transfer coefficients** even at low ΔT, due to thin film hydrodynamics.
- **Handles viscous thick liquor** (50% sugar syrup ≈ 50–200 cP) effectively.
- **Low fouling tendency** because the film is renewed continuously and tube walls are washed by gravity-driven flow.
- **Compact** with vertical orientation — saves floor space in sugar mills.

**MOC Justification (2 marks):**
- **Material: Stainless Steel 304 (SS 304)**, food-grade, with surfaces polished to **0.8 μm Ra** (sanitary finish).
- **Reasoning:** Sugar juice is mildly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5); carbon steel would corrode and contaminate the syrup with iron, causing color formation. SS 304 is approved by **FDA** and **FSSAI** for food contact, has adequate corrosion resistance for sugar service, and is cost-effective compared to SS 316L.

---

**(b) Manual Sizing Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Water Evaporated (1 mark):**

$$
m_v = m_F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 20000 \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.50}\right) = 20000 \times 0.76 = 15200 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Boiling Temperature in Evaporator (0.5 mark):**

$$
T_v = 100\,°C + \text{BPE} = 100 + 4 = 104\,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Steam Temperature (0.5 mark):**

From steam tables: $T_s$ at 2.5 bar abs = **127.4 °C**.

**Step 4 — Heat Duty (1.5 marks):**

$$
Q = m_F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_v - T_F) + m_v \cdot \lambda_v
$$

$$
Q = 20000 \times 3.9 \times (104 - 40) + 15200 \times 2257
$$

$$
Q = 20000 \times 3.9 \times 64 + 15200 \times 2257
$$

$$
Q = 4.992 \times 10^6 + 3.431 \times 10^7
$$

$$
Q = 3.930 \times 10^7 \ \text{kJ/h}
$$

Converting to kW:

$$
Q = \frac{3.930 \times 10^7}{3600} = 10917 \ \text{kW} = 1.092 \times 10^7 \ \text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — Steam Required (1 mark):**

$$
m_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{3.930 \times 10^7}{2180} = 18028 \ \text{kg/h} \approx 18.03 \ \text{TPH}
$$

**Step 6 — Steam Economy (1 mark):**

$$
E = \frac{m_v}{m_s} = \frac{15200}{18028} = 0.843
$$

**Step 7 — Heat Transfer Area (2 marks):**

Effective ΔT:

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_v - \text{BPE} = 127.4 - 104 = 23.4\,°C
$$

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1.092 \times 10^7}{1800 \times 23.4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{1.092 \times 10^7}{42120} = 259.3 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $A \approx 259 \ \text{m}^2$, $m_s \approx 18\,$ TPH, $E \approx 0.84$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A **triple-effect forward-feed evaporator** concentrates a **NaOH solution** from **8% (w/w) to 40% (w/w)**. Feed rate is **30,000 kg/h** at **50 °C**. Saturated steam at **4 bar (abs)** ($T_s = 143.6\,°C$, $\lambda_s = 2133\,$ kJ/kg). The last effect operates at **0.2 bar (abs)** ($T_{sat} = 60.1\,°C$). BPE values for the three effects (8% → 14% → 24% → 40%) are: **1 °C, 3 °C, and 8 °C** respectively. Overall heat transfer coefficients (initial estimates): $U_1 = 2500$, $U_2 = 1800$, $U_3 = 1100\,$ W/m²·K. Take $\lambda \approx 2200\,$ kJ/kg (assumed constant) and $C_{pF} = 3.7\,$ kJ/kg·°C for all streams.

**(a)** Compute the **ΔT distribution** in each effect using the **equal-area method** and verify the **heat duty** and **water evaporated per effect**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **heat transfer area per effect**, the **total area**, and the **steam economy** of the triple-effect system. Also recommend a suitable **MOC** and list the **applicable codes/standards** for the design data sheet. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) ΔT Distribution and Heat Duty (7 marks):**

**Total water evaporated:**

$$
m_v = 30000 \left(1 - \frac{0.08}{0.40}\right) = 30000 \times 0.80 = 24000 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Per effect (forward feed, equal capacity assumption):** Each effect evaporates $m_v/3 = 8000\,$ kg/h.

**Total available ΔT:**

$$
\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_{v,3} - \sum \text{BPE}
$$

where $T_{v,3} = T_{sat}(\text{0.2 bar}) = 60.1\,°C$.

$$
\Delta T_{total} = 143.6 - 60.1 - (1 + 3 + 8) = 143.6 - 60.1 - 12 = 71.5\,°C
$$

**Equal-area ΔT distribution (3 marks):**

The general formula is:

$$
\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{U_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{3} \dfrac{1}{U_j}}
$$

Compute the sum:

$$
\sum \frac{1}{U_j} = \frac{1}{2500} + \frac{1}{1800} + \frac{1}{1100}
$$

$$
= 0.000400 + 0.000556 + 0.000909 = 0.001865 \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

Now compute each $\Delta T_i$:

$$
\Delta T_1 = 71.5 \times \frac{0.000400}{0.001865} = 71.5 \times 0.2145 = 15.34\,°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = 71.5 \times \frac{0.000556}{0.001865} = 71.5 \times 0.2981 = 21.31\,°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_3 = 71.5 \times \frac{0.000909}{0.001865} = 71.5 \times 0.4874 = 34.85\,°C
$$

Sum check: $15.34 + 21.31 + 34.85 = 71.5\,°C$ ✓

**Heat duty per effect (4 marks):**

$$
Q_1 = m_v/3 \times \lambda = 8000 \times 2200 = 1.76 \times 10^7 \ \text{kJ/h} = 4889 \ \text{kW}
$$

For more accuracy, account for sensible heat of the feed entering each effect. **First pass simplification** (assuming preheating to boiling point in the first effect only):

$$
Q_1 = m_F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_{v,1} - T_F) + (m_v/3) \cdot \lambda
$$

where $T_{v,1} = T_{v,3} + \sum \Delta T_i - \text{BPE losses} \approx 60.1 + 71.5 + 12 = 143.6\,°C$ (no — actually $T_{v,1}$ must be computed iteratively).

**Simplified approach** (for KTU 14-mark level, the standard first design):

$$
Q_i = (m_v/3) \cdot \lambda = 8000 \times 2200 = 1.76 \times 10^7 \ \text{kJ/h} = 4889 \ \text{kW} \quad \forall i
$$

(Validity: assumes each effect receives preheated feed — a standard simplifying assumption in first-pass design.)

---

**(b) Area, Economy, MOC, and Standards (7 marks):**

**Heat Transfer Area (3 marks):**

$$
A_1 = \frac{Q_1}{U_1 \cdot \Delta T_1} = \frac{1.76 \times 10^7 / 3600}{2500 \times 15.34} = \frac{4889}{38350} = 0.1275 \ \text{m}^2
$$

Wait — $Q$ in W:

$$
A_1 = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{2500 \times 15.34} = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{38350} = 127.5 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{1800 \times 21.31} = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{38358} = 127.5 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_3 = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{1100 \times 34.85} = \frac{4.889 \times 10^6}{38335} = 127.5 \ \text{m}^2
$$

✓ All three areas are equal (~127.5 m²), confirming the **equal-area method** is satisfied.

**Total Area:**

$$
A_{total} = 3 \times 127.5 = 382.5 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Steam Economy (2 marks):**

Total water evaporated = 24,000 kg/h. Steam to first effect:

$$
m_s = \frac{Q_1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1.76 \times 10^7}{2133} = 8251 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
E = \frac{m_v}{m_s} = \frac{24000}{8251} = 2.91
$$

(Single effect would give $E = 0.85$; triple effect gives $E \approx 2.55-2.95$ — matches expected range.)

**Material of Construction (1 mark):**

For concentrated NaOH (40% NaOH is highly aggressive to CS due to caustic SCC), use **SS 304 (with Ni ≥ 8%)** for the evaporator body and tubes, with stress-relief heat treatment. For the calandria tube sheets, use **SS 316L** for extra corrosion margin. Avoid carbon steel entirely for >30% NaOH at elevated temperatures.

**Applicable Codes/Standards (1 mark):**

- **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) — for shell-and-tube geometry.
- **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII, Div 1** — for unfired pressure vessels.
- **IS 2825** (Indian Standard for unfired pressure vessels).
- **IBR** (Indian Boiler Regulations) — for steam-side design.
- **API 660** — for shell-and-tube heat exchangers in refinery service (if applicable).
- **ASME B31.3** — for piping.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Zones (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes (–2 to –3 marks):** Conflating $Q$ in kW with $\lambda$ in kJ/kg without converting kg/h → kg/s. **Always** write $m \ (\text{kg/s}) = m \ (\text{kg/h}) / 3600$ explicitly.
> 2. **BPE Handling (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to add BPE when computing the *solution boiling temperature* OR subtracting BPE twice (once in $T_v$ and again in $\Delta T$). **Remember:** $\Delta T = T_s - (T_{water, sat} + \text{BPE})$.
> 3. **MOC Justification (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing "use stainless steel" without explaining *which grade* and *why* (corrosion mechanism, pH, chloride content, FDA approval if relevant).
> 4. **Evaporator Type Justification (–2 to –3 marks):** Picking an evaporator type by name without linking to **at least two process properties** (viscosity, heat sensitivity, fouling tendency).
> 5. **Steam Economy Calculation (–1 mark):** Forgetting to specify whether the economy is *per effect* or *for the entire system*.
> 6. **Convergence Check Skipped (–1 mark):** In computational optimization, forgetting to state the convergence criterion ($\vert \Delta A / A \vert < 1\%$).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2, Evaporator Design:**

- [x] **Evaporator types**: Natural circulation, forced circulation, falling film, rising film, agitated thin film, plate, MVR/TVR.
- [x] **Selection rule of thumb**: Heat-sensitive → FFE; Viscous/fouling → Forced Circ.; Crystallizing → Forced Circ. with slurry; Large capacity dilute → LTV multi-effect.
- [x] **BPE** is the *single most important physical property* in evaporator design — it consumes ΔT.
- [x] **Core equation** to memorize: $\boxed{A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)}$
- [x] **Steam economy** of multi-effect: $E \approx 0.85 N$ (for $N \leq 4$); beyond 4, diminishing returns set in.
- [x] **Equal-area ΔT distribution**: $\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} \cdot \dfrac{1/U_i}{\sum 1/U_j}$
- [x] **MOC** for NaOH > 30%: SS 304 minimum (avoid CS — caustic SCC); for sugar/food: SS 304 sanitary; for HCl: glass-lined or PTFE-lined CS.
- [x] **Codes/Standards**: TEMA, ASME Sec VIII Div 1, IS 2825, IBR, API 660 (refinery service).
- [x] **DDS sections**: 8 sections covering general info → process data → mechanical → MOC → thermal → auxiliaries → codes → inspection.
- [x] **Convergence** in computational optimization: $\vert \Delta A / A \vert < 0.01$ or $\vert \Delta Q / Q \vert < 0.005$.
- [x] **Unit conversions**: 1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3600 kJ/h; 1 m²·K/W = 1 (W/m²·K)$^{-1}$.
- [x] **Maximum practical effects** for water-based systems: 4–5 effects (limited by BPE and last-effect ΔT).
- [x] **MVR** becomes economical beyond 5 effects.
- [x] **Forced circulation** always selected for viscous (μ > 100 cP) or crystallizing service — never use natural circulation.

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 🎓**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conduct the Mechanical Design of Tubular Exchangers and Evaporators as per Indian Standards/ASME and Prepare Drawings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT504 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN-I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conduct the Mechanical Design of Tubular Exchangers and Evaporators as per Indian Standards/ASME and Prepare Drawings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mechanical Design of Tubular Exchangers** is the engineering discipline that applies **pressure vessel codes** (IS 4503 / ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII, Division 1) to determine the minimum wall thicknesses, reinforcement requirements, and geometric proportions of shell, heads, tube sheets, nozzles, and flanged joints so that the equipment can safely contain the design pressure and temperature over its assigned service life.

**Mechanical Design of Evaporators** extends the same code framework to vessels that are typically steam-heated, low-to-medium pressure, often equipped with **calandria (short-tube) or long-tube (Kestner/LTV) bundles**, vapour–liquid separators, and condensate removal systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – Module 2)**
> The student must be able to:
> 1. Apply **IS 4503 / ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1** to size shells, heads, and tube sheets.
> 2. Perform **nozzle reinforcement** and flange selection.
> 3. Prepare a **GA (General Arrangement) drawing** and a **fabrication/assembly drawing** with standard symbols.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Intuition

Imagine a domestic pressure cooker working in a chemical plant:

- The **outer pot (shell)** is a thick steel cylinder — it must not burst under internal steam pressure.
- The **lid (head)** is curved, not flat, because a dome spreads stress evenly — exactly like an **ellipsoidal head**.
- The **rubber gasket + clamping lever (flange + bolts)** must seal the joint so steam does not leak.
- The **weight valve (safety relief valve)** opens if pressure exceeds a set value.
- The **perforated plate (tube sheet)** holds the tubes rigidly, distributing point loads to the shell.

A tubular exchanger is simply a **"pressure-cooker-with-tubes-inside"**: the same mechanics of stress and safety apply, but multiplied because of thermal stresses, differential expansion, and vibration from cross-flow.

---

### 1.3 Governing Codes, Standards & Their Role

| Code | Issuing Body | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 4503** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Indian standard for unfired pressure vessels (shell, head, openings) |
| **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1** | American Soc. of Mech. Engineers | International code for unfired pressure vessels (rules for the road) |
| **TEMA** | Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association | Mechanical standards for shell-and-tube heat exchangers (tube-sheet, baffle spacing) |
| **IS 2825** | BIS | Unfired pressure vessels – welding inspection |
| **ASME Sec. IX** | ASME | Welding qualification |
| **PED 2014/68/EU** | EU | European Pressure Equipment Directive (for export) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Margin (Why a Safety Factor?)**
> The **Code allowable stress (S)** = ultimate tensile stress / safety factor (typically **4.0** for carbon steel at room temperature). This guards against material defects, corrosion, and unexpected loadings.

---

### 1.4 Key Design Inputs a Student Must Gather

1. **Design Pressure (P_d)** = `max(MAWP, PSV set pressure) + 0` (no margin needed in IS 4503, but a **10 %** margin is industry practice).
2. **Design Temperature (T_d)** = `max(operating temperature) + 25 °C` (IS 4503) or based on insulation/trace-heating.
3. **Corrosion Allowance (CA)** — typically **1.5 mm to 3 mm** for carbon steel; **0 mm** for stainless in clean service.
4. **Material of Construction (MOC)** — carbon steel SA-516 Gr. 70, SS 304/316, Monel, Inconel.
5. **Diameter, Length, Type (FTS/U-tube/Floating)** — set by the **process design (Module 1)**.

---

### 1.5 What "Prepare Drawings" Means at KTU

The Mechanical Design output is not just numbers — it is a **drawing pack** containing:

- **Process / P&ID** (from Module 1).
- **General Arrangement (GA) drawing** — sectional view, orientation, nozzles.
- **Fabrication drawing** — every weld, nozzle, support, lug.
- **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.
- **Tube sheet drilling pattern**.
- **Nozzle schedule**.

Drawings follow **IS 696** (line conventions), **IS 919** (symbols), and **ASME Y14.5** for GD&T.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam**: *D*esign **S**hell → **H**eads → **T**ube **S**heet → **N**ozzles → **F**langes → **D**rawings → **BOM** = **"Sheets Handle The Nasty Flange Drawings & BOM"**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy of the Design-by-Rule Approach (ASME / IS 4503)

The code-based design assumes:

- The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and ductile** (carbon & low-alloy steels satisfy this).
- The vessel is **essentially cylindrical or spherical** — the most efficient pressure-containing shapes.
- Stresses are **membrane stresses** (averaged across wall thickness); bending stresses are neglected unless explicitly required.
- Welds are **full penetration, post-weld heat-treated** (PWHT) where thick.

The design rule is conceptually:

$$
t_{\text{required}} = \frac{\text{load driving force}}{\text{allowable stress} \times \text{efficiency}} \times R \;+\; \text{corrosion allowance}
$$

where $R$ is the radius.

---

### 2.2 Shell Design (Cylindrical Body under Internal Pressure)

#### 2.2.1 Circumferential (Hoop) Stress — The Governing Case

When a cylinder of radius $R$ and thickness $t$ carries internal pressure $P$, the **hoop stress** is exactly:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot R}{t}
$$

Because the axial stress is only $\sigma_a = PR/2t$, the hoop stress is **twice as severe**, so the code always checks hoop stress first.

#### 2.2.2 IS 4503 / ASME Required Thickness

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \, S \, E - 1.2 \, P} \;+\; c
$$

where:
- $P$ = design pressure (MPa)
- $D_i$ = inside diameter (mm)
- $S$ = allowable stress (MPa) at design temperature
- $E$ = joint efficiency (0.7 for spot-examined, 0.85 for 100 % radiographed, 1.0 for seamless pipe)
- $c$ = corrosion allowance (mm)
- The factor **1.2 P** is the ASME/IS correction for thick-wall theory (not present in thin-wall formula $PR/SE$).

#### 2.2.3 Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP) Check

Once $t$ is chosen, verify:

$$
\text{MAWP} = \frac{2 \, S \, E \, (t - c)}{D_i + 1.2 \, (t - c)}
$$

This MAWP must exceed the design pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $E \le 1$?**
> Welds, even when radiographed 100 %, are not as strong as the parent metal. $E$ quantifies this discount. Seamless shells use $E = 1.0$.

---

### 2.3 Head Design (End Closures)

#### 2.3.1 Ellipsoidal Head (2 : 1) — Code Favourite

A 2 : 1 ellipsoidal head has the depth = ½ × diameter. Required thickness:

$$
t = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 \, S \, E - 0.2 \, P} \;+\; c
$$

The 0.2 P coefficient comes from stress concentration at the knuckle.

#### 2.3.2 Torispherical (Flanged & Dished) Head

$$
t = \frac{0.885 \, P \, R_c}{S \, E - 0.1 \, P} \;+\; c
$$

where $R_c$ = crown radius. $R_c$ is normally equal to the shell OD (for ASME) or $D_i$ (IS).

#### 2.3.3 Hemispherical Head (Ideal but Expensive)

$$
t = \frac{P \, R}{2 \, S \, E - 0.2 \, P} \;+\; c
$$

Only half the ellipsoidal thickness — but harder to fabricate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of efficiency** (thinner for same pressure): **Hemispherical < Ellipsoidal 2:1 < Torispherical < Flat cover**.
> Order of fabrication ease: opposite. The trade-off is why **2:1 ellipsoidal** is the most common in chemical plant practice.

---

### 2.4 Tube Sheet Design (The Heart of an Exchanger)

Three classical cases per **TEMA / ASME**:

#### 2.4.1 Fixed Tubesheet (Most Common)

Tubesheet is welded to the shell, hence it must resist:
1. Tube-side pressure differential,
2. Shell-side pressure differential,
3. Differential thermal expansion $\Delta T$.

A simplified but exam-popular formula (TEMA R = 4.51):

$$
t_{ts} = \frac{F \, G}{3} \sqrt{\frac{P}{\eta \, S}}
$$

More rigorously, the **ASME Sec. VIII Div 1, Appendix AA** provides the full method, balancing axial tube + shell + differential expansion forces.

Where:
- $G$ = tube pitch diameter (mm)
- $F$ = tube-side / shell-side pressure correction
- $\eta$ = tube joint efficiency (~ 0.4 for expanded only, 0.8 for welded)
- $P$ = effective differential pressure
- $S$ = allowable stress

#### 2.4.2 Floating Head
Only the tube-side pressure acts on the tubesheet, so $t_{ts} \approx 0.87 \times$ fixed case. The floating cover is designed like a flat head using ASME flat-cover rules.

#### 2.4.3 U-Tube
No differential expansion stress. The tubesheet behaves like a stationary tubesheet with no bending due to thermal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trick (KTU favourite)**
> For preliminary design, $t_{ts} \approx 0.05 D_s$ for floating head, $0.07 D_s$ for fixed tubesheet — where $D_s$ is the shell ID.

---

### 2.5 Nozzle Reinforcement (UG-37 / 8.5 of IS 4503)

Openings weaken the shell. Code requires that the **metal removed is replaced by reinforcement** (nozzle wall + portion of shell + pad) such that:

$$
A_{\text{reinforcement}} \ge A_{\text{required}}
$$

$$
A_{\text{req}} = d \times t \times F + 2 t \, t_n \, F_2
$$

where $d$ = finished nozzle bore, $t$ = shell required thickness, $F$ = pressure-area factor, $t_n$ = nozzle neck thickness, $F_2$ = 1.0 for shear.

**Rule of thumb for a "quick check"**: the nozzle projection outside the shell face should be at least:

$$
h = \sqrt{d \, t}
$$

or alternatively the nozzle wall should be ≥ $0.5 t$ (so that nozzle alone can replace shell).

---

### 2.6 Flange Design (ASME Sec. VIII Appendix 2 / Y)

A flange joint is checked for:

1. **Gasket seating** (no internal pressure) — bolt stress $S_a = 1.5 \, S_m$ or $S_y$.
2. **Operating condition** (pressure × gasket area / bolts) — bolt stress $S_b = S_m$ typical.

$$
W_m = \frac{\pi}{4} \, G^2 \, P + 2 \pi \, b \, G \, m \, P
$$

where:
- $G$ = gasket load diameter
- $b$ = effective gasket width
- $m$ = gasket factor
- $P$ = design pressure

The required **number of bolts**:

$$
n_b = \frac{W_m}{A_b \times S_b}
$$

where $A_b$ is the nominal bolt area.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic**: *G-m-P-2b-G-m* — **"Gopi, Maths Please, 2B Genuine, Make"** for the bolt-load formula.

---

### 2.7 Evaporator Mechanical Design Highlights

The mechanical design re-uses the rules above plus the following evaporator-specific items:

1. **Calandria** (steam chest) — designed as a low-pressure shell; usually $P_d \le 0.4$ MPa (gauge).
2. **Tube-sheet (calandria plate)** — thick (often 25–50 mm cast iron / carbon steel with stainless lining).
3. **Vapour Body / Separator** — sized for vapour velocity $u = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ (Souders–Brown).
4. **Downtake area** ≥ cross-sectional area of tubes (so liquor can flow down by gravity).
5. **Vapour Outlet** sized for $u_v = 0.1 \times$ sonic velocity (to prevent entrainment).
6. **Long-Tube (Kestner / LTV) Evaporator** — body is a tall pressure vessel with thin-walled tubes (often 25 mm OD, 0.9–1.2 mm wall). Design pressure is the secondary steam pressure on the outside (or inside for falling film).

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE** — In every table cell, the symbol $\vert$ has been replaced by `\vert` so the markdown table never breaks.

| # | Component | Governing Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylindrical shell | $t = \dfrac{P \, D_i}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + c$ | $P$ MPa, $D_i$ mm, $S$ MPa, $E$ – , $c$ mm |
| 2 | MAWP (shell) | $\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{2 S E (t-c)}{D_i + 1.2(t-c)}$ | MPa |
| 3 | 2:1 Ellipsoidal head | $t = \dfrac{P D_i}{2 S E - 0.2 P} + c$ | All same as above |
| 4 | Torispherical head | $t = \dfrac{0.885 \, P \, R_c}{S E - 0.1 P} + c$ | $R_c$ mm |
| 5 | Hemispherical head | $t = \dfrac{P R}{2 S E - 0.2 P} + c$ | $R$ mm |
| 6 | Tubesheet (TEMA) | $t_{ts} = \dfrac{F G}{3}\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\eta S}}$ | mm |
| 7 | Nozzle reinforcement | $A_{\text{req}} = d \, t \, F + 2 t \, t_n \, F_2$ | mm² |
| 8 | Bolt load | $W_m = \dfrac{\pi}{4} G^2 P + 2 \pi b G m P$ | N |
| 9 | Number of bolts | $n_b = \dfrac{W_m}{A_b S_b}$ | – |
| 10 | Hoop stress (verification) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P R}{t-c} \le S E$ | MPa |
| 11 | Souders–Brown vapour velocity | $u = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | m/s |
| 12 | Long-tube wall (thin) | $t = \dfrac{P D_o}{2 S E} + c$ | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **$E$ values to remember**: seamless pipe 1.0 · spot-radiograph 0.7 · 100 % radiograph 0.85 · spot-exam 0.7. These appear almost every year in KTU.

---

### 2.9 Why This Knowledge Matters in Industry

- **Safety**: A boiler or exchanger that bursts is catastrophic. Codes are legally binding.
- **Cost**: A 1 mm extra wall on a 25 m shell adds 12 tonnes of steel ≈ ₹12 lakhs.
- **Inspection**: The drawing pack is the legal document for the Inspector of Boilers / Third-Party Agency.
- **Modularisation**: Proper drawings enable shop-fabrication, transport in pieces, and field assembly.
- **Sustainability**: Right-sized vessels mean less material, less energy, less CO₂.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cylindrical Shell Thickness (ASME Form)

#### 3.1.1 Force Balance on a Longitudinal Half-Cylinder

Consider a thin-walled cylinder of radius $R$, length $L$, and thickness $t$ subjected to internal pressure $P$.

The pressure pushes outward on every element of the wall. The **net force** acting on a longitudinal half-section of length $L$ is the pressure acting on the projected area $2 R L$:

$$
F_{\text{pressure}} = P \times (2 R L)
$$

The wall resists this force through the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the two cut sections of area $2 t L$:

$$
F_{\text{wall}} = \sigma_h \times (2 t L)
$$

For equilibrium, $F_{\text{pressure}} = F_{\text{wall}}$:

$$
P \cdot 2 R L = 2 \sigma_h \, t \, L
$$

Solve for $\sigma_h$:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \, R}{t}
$$

This is the **hoop stress**. Substituting $D_i = 2 R$:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 t}
$$

---

#### 3.1.2 Introducing Allowable Stress, Joint Efficiency, and the Thick-Wall Correction

To convert from stress to required thickness, the code sets $\sigma_h \le S \cdot E$:

$$
\frac{P \, D_i}{2 t} \le S \, E \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \ge \frac{P \, D_i}{2 S E}
$$

This is the **thin-wall** equation. For thick walls ($t/D > 0.05$), the inner fibre is more strained than the outer fibre. The average is taken as $t - c/2$ on the inner side, which gives:

$$
\frac{P \, (D_i + 1.2 (t-c))}{2 (t-c)} \le S \, E
$$

Rearrange for $t$:

$$
t - c = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 S E - 1.2 P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + c
$$

This is the **IS 4503 / ASME required thickness** equation.

---

#### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Statement**: A shell of a shell-and-tube exchanger has inside diameter $D_i = 600$ mm, design pressure $P = 2.0$ MPa, design temperature $T = 200 \, ^\circ\text{C}$, MOC = SA-516 Gr. 70 (carbon steel, $S = 138$ MPa at 200 °C), corrosion allowance $c = 2$ mm, joint efficiency $E = 0.85$. Calculate the required shell thickness and the MAWP.

**Step 1 — Plug into the code equation**:

$$
t = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + c
$$

$$
t = \frac{2.0 \times 600}{2 \times 138 \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times 2.0} + 2
$$

$$
t = \frac{1200}{234.6 - 2.4} + 2 = \frac{1200}{232.2} + 2
$$

$$
t = 5.168 + 2 = 7.17 \text{ mm}
$$

Round up to the **next commercial plate thickness** = **8 mm** (or use the larger of 8 / 10 mm depending on fabrication preference).

**Step 2 — Recompute MAWP** with $t = 8$ mm:

$$
\text{MAWP} = \frac{2 \, S \, E \, (t - c)}{D_i + 1.2 (t - c)} = \frac{2 \times 138 \times 0.85 \times (8 - 2)}{600 + 1.2 \times (8 - 2)}
$$

$$
= \frac{234.6 \times 6}{600 + 7.2} = \frac{1407.6}{607.2} = 2.318 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify** that MAWP (2.32) > $P_d$ (2.0 MPa). ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **The MAWP must be ≥ design pressure.** If not, the vessel is unsafe. The code requires you to round $t$ up, not down.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Ellipsoidal Head Thickness

For a 2 : 1 ellipsoidal head, the maximum stress occurs at the **knuckle** and equals:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 t} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.2 P/S E}
$$

Setting $\sigma_{\max} \le S E$ and solving:

$$
\frac{P D_i}{2 t} \le S E (1 - 0.2 P / S E) = S E - 0.2 P
$$

$$
t \ge \frac{P D_i}{2 S E - 0.2 P}
$$

Adding corrosion allowance:

$$
t = \frac{P D_i}{2 S E - 0.2 P} + c
$$

**Numerical example**: $D_i = 600$ mm, $P = 2.0$ MPa, $S E = 117.3$ MPa, $c = 2$ mm.

$$
t = \frac{2.0 \times 600}{2 \times 117.3 - 0.2 \times 2.0} + 2 = \frac{1200}{234.2} + 2 = 5.124 + 2 = 7.12 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **8 mm** plate.

---

### 3.3 Tube Sheet Thickness — Simplified TEMA R-method

The TEMA R-method gives a **closed-form** preliminary answer:

$$
t_s = F \cdot G \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\eta \cdot S}} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{(per TEMA 9th Ed. Eq. RCB-4.51)}
$$

**Sample input**:
- Tube pitch $G = 25$ mm (triangular)
- $P = 1.6$ MPa (differential)
- $\eta = 0.4$ (expanded)
- $S = 138$ MPa
- $F = 1.0$ (symmetric)

$$
t_s = 1.0 \times 25 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \sqrt{\frac{1.6}{0.4 \times 138}}
$$

$$
t_s = 8.333 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.6}{55.2}} = 8.333 \times \sqrt{0.02899} = 8.333 \times 0.1703 = 1.42 \text{ mm}
$$

This is the *minimum* depth. The **real** $t_s$ is then rounded up to the minimum manufacturing thickness (typically 25 mm for carbon steel, 19 mm for stainless) to provide rigidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why so much thicker than the formula?**
> Tubesheets must remain **flat** under cyclic thermal stresses. The TEMA formula gives the **stress-only** thickness; **deflection** and **corrosion** add 15–25 mm in practice.

---

### 3.4 Nozzle Reinforcement — A Single-Opening Worked Example

> **Problem**: A 250 mm (NB) nozzle (bore $d = 254$ mm) is fitted on a shell having $D_i = 1200$ mm, $t_{\text{shell}} = 10$ mm, $P = 1.0$ MPa, $S = 138$ MPa, $E = 0.85$. Nozzle neck is 14 mm thick ($t_n = 14$ mm), 250 mm projection ($h_1$).

**Step 1 — Required reinforcement area (F = 1 for UG-37)**:

$$
A_{\text{req}} = d \, t_r \, F + 2 \, t_r \, t_n \, F_2
$$

Here $t_r = P R / S E = 1.0 \times 600 / (138 \times 0.85) = 5.12$ mm. But the rule uses the **required shell thickness without corrosion**, often the same.

For simplicity, take $t_r = 5.12$ mm and $F_2 = 1$:

$$
A_{\text{req}} = 254 \times 5.12 \times 1 + 2 \times 5.12 \times 14 \times 1 = 1300.5 + 143.4 = 1443.9 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Available area** from nozzle wall projecting into the shell ($h_1 = $ available height inside the shell, here $h_1 = $ nozzle projection $= 250$ mm, but limited to $4 t_r$ or $4 t_n$ whichever is less):

Available excess nozzle thickness beyond required ($t_n - t_r = 14 - 5.12 = 8.88$ mm) acting over length $2 h_1$ capped at $2 \times 4 \times t_r = 40.96$ mm:

$$
A_1 = 2 \times 40.96 \times 8.88 = 727.4 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Required area not yet covered: $1443.9 - 727.4 = 716.5 \text{ mm}^2$ to be supplied by a **pad**.

**Step 3 — Add a reinforcing pad** of thickness $t_p = 8$ mm, outside diameter = nozzle OD + 2 × 50 mm = ~ 320 mm (typical).

Effective pad width $h_2 = 33$ mm (the smaller of $d$, $R$ or weld sizes).

Pad area $A_2 = 2 \times h_2 \times t_p = 2 \times 33 \times 8 = 528$ mm². Still short → increase pad diameter or thickness.

A 12 mm pad would give $2 \times 33 \times 12 = 792$ mm². Total = 727.4 + 792 = 1519.4 mm² ≥ 1443.9 mm². ✅

> [!TIP]
> **The exam shortcut**: Nozzle wall alone ($t_n$) almost always provides most reinforcement. A small **pad** finishes it. Always check that **pad + nozzle ≥ shell required area**.

---

### 3.5 Evaporator — Long-Tube (Kestner) Mechanical Design Numerical

> **Problem**: Design a long-tube rising-film evaporator body. Process: dilute NaOH, 10 % → 30 %, secondary steam 0.3 MPa (g) on shell side, tube-side vacuum 50 kPa absolute. $D_i$ (vapour body) = 1500 mm, MOC SS-304, $S = 115$ MPa, $E = 1.0$ (seamless), $c = 1$ mm.

**Step 1 — Design pressure**:

- Outside: $P_d = 0.3 + 0.1 \text{ (margin)} + 1 \text{ (atm)} \approx 1.4$ MPa (absolute basis to be safe).
- Inside: vacuum is **external pressure** case → shell under external pressure.
- **Worst case = external pressure on shell side** ⇒ ASME Part UG-28 governs.

**Step 2 — For vacuum, calculate minimum $t$ to prevent buckling**.

Vacuum design pressure (external) = **full vacuum + 0.1 MPa** = 0.1 MPa external.

Use ASME external pressure chart for SS-304 at 100 °C: factor $B = 65$ MPa.

$$
t = \frac{D_o}{2} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{0.000585 P}{B}\right) - 1 \right] \approx 0.0065 D_o
$$

For $D_o = 1506$ mm:

$$
t \approx 9.8 \text{ mm} + c = 10.8 \text{ mm} \quad \rightarrow \text{adopt } 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Tube bundle**: tubes 25 mm OD, 1.2 mm wall, length 7 m. 1.2 mm wall for the body (operating on the inside, vacuum on outside) is fine.

**Step 4 — Vapour body**: it experiences the **secondary steam + atmospheric** when steaming up; MAWP (internal) = 0.3 + 1 = 1.3 MPa.

$$
t_{\text{body}} = \frac{P D_i}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + c = \frac{1.3 \times 1500}{2 \times 115 \times 1 - 1.2 \times 1.3} + 1
$$

$$
= \frac{1950}{228.4} + 1 = 8.54 + 1 = 9.54 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{use } 10 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson**: External-pressure (vacuum) vessels always need **stiffening rings** if the shell is too thin relative to $D/t$. Long, thin shells buckle; rings are spaced every $1.5 D$ to prevent ovalisation.

---

### 3.6 Quick-Reference Analytical Matrix (for Last-Minute Revision)

| Step | Component | What to Calculate | Key Formula | Result Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Establish inputs | $P_d, T_d, c, S, E$ | — | $P_d \ge 1.1 \times$ MAWP |
| 2 | Shell | $t_{\text{shell}}$ | $PD_i / (2SE-1.2P) + c$ | Round up to plate |
| 3 | Verify shell | MAWP | $2SE(t-c) / (D_i+1.2(t-c))$ | $\ge P_d$ |
| 4 | Heads | $t_{\text{head}}$ | Ellipsoidal / Tori. / Hemi. | Use larger of two |
| 5 | Tube sheet | $t_{ts}$ (TEMA) | $FG/3 \sqrt{P/(\eta S)}$ | +min. fab. thickness |
| 6 | Nozzles | $A_{\text{req}} \le A_{\text{avail}}$ | UG-37 | Pad if needed |
| 7 | Flanges | $W_m$, $n_b$ | Bolt-load eq. | $n_b$ integer ≥ 4 |
| 8 | Drawing pack | GA + Fabrication + BOM | — | Issue Rev. 0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mechanical-Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Process Inputs from Module 1] --> A1Duty[Heat Duty Q kW]
    A0 --> A1Press[Design Pressure P_d]
    A0 --> A1Temp[Design Temperature T_d]
    A0 --> A1Vel[Fluid Velocity Limits]
    A0 --> A1MOC[Material Selection S, E, c]
    A0 --> A1Type[Type: FTS / Floating / U-tube]
    A0 --> A1Size[Shell D_i x L]

    A1Duty --> B0Process[Process Sizing - Module 1]
    A1Vel --> B0Process
    A1Type --> B0Process
    A1Size --> B0Process

    B0Process --> B1Tubes[Tube OD, Pitch, Layout]
    B0Process --> B2Baffles[Baffle Cut and Spacing]
    B0Process --> B3Vessel[Vessel D_i x L]

    A1Press --> C0Mech[Mech. Design Module 2]
    A1Temp --> C0Mech
    A1MOC --> C0Mech
    B3Vessel --> C0Mech

    C0Mech --> C1Shell[Shell Thickness t_s]
    C0Mech --> C2Head[Head Thickness t_h]
    C0Mech --> C3TS[Tube Sheet t_ts]
    C0Mech --> C4Nozzle[Nozzle Reinforcement]
    C0Mech --> C5Flange[Flange and Bolts]
    C0Mech --> C6Support[Skirt and Saddle]

    C1Shell --> D0Draw[Drawing Pack]
    C2Head --> D0Draw
    C3TS --> D0Draw
    C4Nozzle --> D0Draw
    C5Flange --> D0Draw
    C6Support --> D0Draw

    B1Tubes --> D0Draw
    B2Baffles --> D0Draw

    D0Draw --> D1GA[GA Drawing]
    D0Draw --> D2Fab[Fabrication Drawing]
    D0Draw --> D3TS[Tube Sheet Drill Pattern]
    D0Draw --> D4BOM[Bill of Materials]

    D1GA --> E0Insp[Inspector / TPI Approval]
    D2Fab --> E0Insp
    D3TS --> E0Insp
    D4BOM --> E0Insp

    E0Insp --> F0Fab[Shop Fabrication]
    F0Fab --> G0Site[Site Erection]
    G0Site --> H0Comm[Commissioning]
```

**Reading the diagram**: The left column is the *Process Design* (Module 1), the centre column is the *Mechanical Design* (Module 2 – this module), and the right column is the *Drawing & Approval* stage. Each arrow represents a deliverable that flows to the next discipline.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Forces Acting on a Fixed-Tube-Sheet Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Shell[Shell Side<br>Pressure P_s] -->|Pushes outward| TS[Tube Sheet]
    Tube[Tube Side<br>Pressure P_t] -->|Pushes inward| TS
    TS -->|Resists| Tubes[Tube Bundle]
    TS -->|Resists| ShellWall[Shell Wall]
    Tubes -->|Bows shell| ShellWall
    Tubes -->|Thermal expand| DeltaT[Differential Expansion dT]
    DeltaT -->|Adds stress| TS
    TS -->|Bending| Gasket[Channel Flange Gasket]
    TS -->|Bending| PassP[Pass Partition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The fixed tubesheet must balance three loads — (i) pressure differential, (ii) axial tube forces, (iii) differential expansion. The ASME Appendix AA equations are essentially a force-moment equilibrium around the tubesheet centre.

---

### 4.3 Schematic of a Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (TEMA E-type, Floating Head)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    In1[Hot Fluid Inlet] --> Channel1[Channel 1]
    Channel1 --> PassDiv[Pass Divider Plate]
    PassDiv --> TubesBank[Tube Bank - Shell Side Cross-flow]
    TubesBank --> PassDiv2[Pass Divider Plate 2]
    PassDiv2 --> Channel2[Channel 2]
    Channel2 --> Out1[Hot Fluid Outlet]

    In2[Cold Fluid Inlet] --> NozzleS[Shell Nozzle]
    NozzleS --> ShellSpace[Shell Space]
    ShellSpace --> NozzleS2[Shell Outlet Nozzle]
    NozzleS2 --> Out2[Cold Fluid Outlet]

    TubesBank --- Baffles[Tubesheets / Floating Head / Baffles]
    Baffles --- Floater[Floating Head Cover]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why a floating head?** The channel + tubes can be pulled out for cleaning. Mechanical consequence: tubesheet is **smaller in diameter** than the shell ID, so the cover must be removable.

---

### 4.4 Evaporator Body — Schematic Cross-Section (Calandria Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Steam[Steam In] --> SteamChest[Steam Chest / Calandria]
    SteamChest -->|Heats via tubes| Tubes[Downtake Tubes]
    Tubes --> Liquid[Vapour-Liquid Space]
    Liquid -->|Vapour rises| VapourBody[Vapour Body Separator]
    VapourBody -->|Vapour out| VapourOut[Secondary Vapour Outlet]
    Liquid -->|Concentrated liquor| Downtake[Central Downtake]
    Downtake --> Tubes
    VapourBody -->|Entrained drops| Demister[Demister Pad]
    Demister --> VapourOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **downtake** is the unsung hero. Without it, the liquor cannot return to the tubes by gravity-driven natural circulation. ASME rules require downtake cross-section ≥ total tube cross-section.

---

### 4.5 ASME / IS Symbol Key for Heat-Exchanger Drawings (Exam Favourite)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Double line, arrowed | Process flow (P&ID) |
| Triangular baffle | Impingement / segmental baffle |
| Solid disc on shell | Tubesheet |
| Z-shape inside | Pass partition plate |
| "V" on top | Vapour outlet |
| Hexagon | Safety relief valve |
| Number-letter e.g. N1 | Nozzle schedule reference |

> [!TIP]
> **In the KTU exam**, a typical 7-mark sub-question asks: *"With the help of a neat sketch, label the parts of a TEMA AEL exchanger."* Memorise the three-letter TEMA code:
> - 1st letter = **Front end** (channel type)
> - 2nd letter = **Shell type**
> - 3rd letter = **Rear end** (head type)
>
> Example: **AEM** = Channel & cover (front) · One-pass shell · Fixed tubesheet bonnet (rear).

---

### 4.6 Nozzle Orientation — Clock-Face Convention (Drawing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Center((Shell Centre)) --> Top[12 o'clock - Vapour Outlet]
    Center --> R2[2 o'clock - Inlet]
    Center --> R4[4 o'clock - Drain]
    Center --> Bot[6 o'clock - Liquid Outlet]
    Center --> L8[8 o'clock - Level Conn.]
    Center --> L10[10 o'clock - Pressure Conn.]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Nozzle schedule always lists **tag, size, service, clock-position, projection, flange rating**. This is the **Nozzle Orientation List (NOL)** and is graded in KTU drawing questions.
